topics.title: EMI Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMI Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of EMI Sources

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** is defined as any unwanted electromagnetic energy (conducted, radiated, or inductively/capacitively coupled) that degrades, disrupts, obstructs, or otherwise limits the effective performance of an electronic or electrical system. The source that originates this disturbance is termed an **EMI Source**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT753)** and the *Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) Standard Dictionary*, EMI sources are broadly classified into three principal categories:

1. **Natural Sources** — originated by physical phenomena in nature (lightning, electrostatic discharge, cosmic radiation).
2. **Intentional Man-Made Sources** — engineered to radiate energy as part of their primary function (radar, broadcast transmitters, wireless communication systems).
3. **Unintentional Man-Made Sources** — radiate or conduct electromagnetic energy as a by-product of their primary function (microprocessors, SMPS, relays, electric motors).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The first module of EMC and Signal Integrity is dedicated to *recognising, classifying, and characterising* EMI sources. Engineering solutions to EMI problems (shielding, filtering, grounding) follow only after a rigorous understanding of *where* the noise originates.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Pollution" of Electronics

Imagine you are trying to have a quiet conversation in a crowded market. Multiple vendors (different EMI sources) shout simultaneously. Even though your friend's voice (the desired signal) is clear, the surrounding chatter pollutes your conversation. Now imagine these "shouts" are invisible electromagnetic waves travelling at the speed of light.

- The **vendor shouting on a loudspeaker** is analogous to a **transmitter** (intentional source).
- The **car horn that blares accidentally** is analogous to a **digital switching circuit** (unintentional source).
- The **sudden thunderclap** is analogous to a **lightning strike** (natural source).

All three pollute the electromagnetic environment. EMC engineering is the act of designing receivers, cables, and enclosures that can "hear the conversation clearly" despite all the noise.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standardised metrics and constants are used when quantifying EMI sources in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Speed of Light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$
- **Characteristic Impedance of Free Space:** $Z_0 = 376.73 \, \Omega \approx 377 \, \Omega$
- **Unit of Frequency:** Hertz (Hz); commonly expressed in **MHz** and **GHz** for EMC.
- **Unit of Field Strength:** V/m (electric field) and A/m (magnetic field).
- **Decibel (dB):** Standard logarithmic unit used to express power ratios, voltage ratios, and field-strength ratios.
- **Spectral Density Units:** V/√Hz (voltage noise), A/√Hz (current noise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Decibels?** EMI signal amplitudes often span 6 to 10 orders of magnitude. Using **dB scales** compresses these into a manageable range and matches the way filters, antennas, and receiver sensitivities are actually specified.

---

## 1.4 Primary EMI Source Categories — Quick Reference Table

| # | Category | Sub-Type | Typical Frequency Range | Example |
|---|----------|----------|--------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Natural | Lightning (ESD) | DC to 10 MHz (with risetime into MHz) | Cloud-to-ground discharge |
| 2 | Natural | Cosmic / Solar Noise | 8 MHz to 1.5 GHz | Solar flares, galactic noise |
| 3 | Natural | Atmospheric Noise | Below 20 MHz (peak around 10–30 MHz) | Thunderstorm static |
| 4 | Man-Made (Intentional) | Broadcast / Communication | 540 kHz – 60 GHz | AM, FM, TV, Cell towers |
| 5 | Man-Made (Intentional) | Radar | 1 GHz – 100 GHz | Military/Air-traffic radar |
| 6 | Man-Made (Unintentional) | Digital Switching | 100 kHz – 1 GHz harmonics | Microprocessor clock edges |
| 7 | Man-Made (Unintentional) | Power Electronics | 10 kHz – 30 MHz | SMPS, motor drives |
| 8 | Man-Made (Unintentional) | Arcing / Commutators | 30 MHz – 1 GHz | Automotive ignition, brush motors |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of EMI Sources

EMI sources are categorised based on **origin**, **coupling mechanism**, and **frequency of operation**. Each classification drives specific EMC compliance testing as per international standards (CISPR, IEC 61000, FCC Part 15).

### 2.1.1 Natural EMI Sources

#### A. Lightning Discharge (Atmospheric EMI)

Lightning is the most powerful natural EMI source. A typical cloud-to-ground strike carries a peak current of approximately **20,000 A to 200,000 A** with a rise time of about **1 µs to 10 µs**.

- **Mechanism:** Charge accumulation in cumulonimbus clouds induces a potential difference of $10^7$ to $10^8$ V.
- **Frequency Spectrum:** Most energy is concentrated below **1 MHz**, but the impulsive nature produces strong harmonics extending into the **VHF** range.
- **Model:** Approximated as a current source feeding a vertical antenna with channel impedance of roughly **1 kΩ to 5 kΩ**.

#### B. Electrostatic Discharge (ESD)

ESD is the sudden transfer of electrostatic charge between two objects of differing potentials. The **Human Body Model (HBM)** is the industry-standard representation, modelling the human body as a **100 pF capacitor** charged to several **kV** and discharged through a **1.5 kΩ** resistance.

The peak discharge current is given by:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{V_{charge}}{R_{body}}
$$

#### C. Cosmic Noise and Solar Activity

Galactic noise originates from celestial radio sources (nebulae, pulsars, quasars). It dominates the noise floor between roughly **8 MHz and 1.5 GHz**. Solar flares dramatically elevate this noise during periods of high solar activity (the 11-year sunspot cycle).

#### D. Precipitation Static / Atmospheric Noise

Caused by charged raindrops, snow, or dust particles striking antennas. Predominantly affects frequencies below **30 MHz**.

---

### 2.1.2 Intentional Man-Made EMI Sources

These devices *legally radiate* as part of their functional purpose. They are governed by emission masks in standards such as **FCC Part 15** and **ITU Radio Regulations**.

#### A. Communication Transmitters

- AM Broadcast: 540 kHz – 1700 kHz
- FM Broadcast: 88 MHz – 108 MHz
- Cellular: 700 MHz – 5 GHz (4G LTE/5G NR)
- Wi-Fi: 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz

#### B. Radar Systems

Emit high-power pulses (often **MW peak**) in narrow beams. Their fundamental frequency and sidebands can interfere with navigation and communication receivers.

#### C. Industrial / Scientific / Medical (ISM) Equipment

Examples include **RF heaters**, **microwave ovens**, **medical diathermy units**, and **RF welders**. They intentionally generate RF energy for heating, but their harmonic and broadband emissions can leak out and cause interference.

---

### 2.1.3 Unintentional Man-Made EMI Sources

These are the dominant concern in modern EMC engineering because they are *everywhere* in commercial and industrial products.

#### A. Digital Logic and Microprocessors

A square wave of fundamental frequency $f_0$ has Fourier components at $n \cdot f_0$ where $n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ (odd harmonics). The rise time $t_r$ of the digital edge determines the upper useful frequency content, given approximately by:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{t_r}
$$

For example, a **1 ns** rise time produces harmonics up to roughly **350 MHz**.

#### B. Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)

Operate by chopping a DC voltage at high frequency (typically **20 kHz – 2 MHz**). The high $dV/dt$ at the switching node couples capacitively to nearby circuits, and the high $di/dt$ through inductors couples magnetically.

#### C. Relays, Solenoids, and Motors

Inductive loads produce large voltage spikes when the current is interrupted:

$$
V_{spike} = -L \frac{di}{dt}
$$

Since $di/dt$ is very high, $V_{spike}$ can reach several hundred volts even with modest inductance.

#### D. Fluorescent Lamps and LED Drivers

Use high-frequency inverters, generating both conducted and radiated noise.

#### E. Automotive Ignition Systems

Generate broadband impulse noise with rise times below **1 ns**, producing interference across **30 MHz – 1 GHz**.

---

## 2.2 Coupling Mechanisms — How EMI Reaches a Victim

A source can reach a victim via four primary paths. KTU examiners frequently test these:

| Mechanism | Description | Typical Frequency |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Conducted** | Energy travels along wires/traces | DC – 30 MHz |
| **Radiated (Far-Field)** | Plane-wave propagation | > 30 MHz |
| **Inductive (Magnetic)** | H-field coupling between loops | < 100 MHz |
| **Capacitive (Electric)** | E-field coupling between conductors | > 30 MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** The near-field to far-field transition occurs at $\lambda / (2\pi)$, approximately. Below this distance, the source behaves like a localised field (E or H dominant). Beyond, it behaves as a uniform plane wave.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all essential equations a KTU student must memorise for EMI source problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave equation relating speed, frequency, wavelength | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $f_{max} = 0.35 / t_r$ | Bandwidth of digital edge (knee frequency) | Hz, $t_r$ in seconds |
| 3 | $V_{spike} = -L \, di/dt$ | Inductive kickback from relay/motor | Volts |
| 4 | $I_{peak} = V_{charge}/R_{body}$ | Peak ESD current (HBM) | Amperes |
| 5 | $E = V/m$ | Electric field strength | Volts per metre |
| 6 | $H = I/(2\pi r)$ | Magnetic field around wire | A/m |
| 7 | $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P/1\,mW)$ | Power in dBm | dB |
| 8 | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}(V/1\,\mu V)$ | Voltage in dBµV | dB |
| 9 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377\,\Omega$ | Free-space impedance | Ohms |
| 10 | $t_{rise,lightning} \approx 1\text{–}10 \,\mu s$ | Lightning rise time | Microseconds |
| 11 | $I_{lightning} \approx 20\text{–}200\,\text{kA}$ | Lightning peak current | Kiloamperes |
| 12 | $f_{r} = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Resonant frequency of LC tank | Hertz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation in Tables:** In your answer sheets, never use the **vertical pipe symbol `|`** inside table cells (it breaks Markdown rendering). For magnitude notation, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ inside the equation, or simply state "magnitude of x" in words.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding EMI sources is the cornerstone of:

- **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) design:** Knowing that a 1 ns edge radiates up to 350 MHz dictates trace-length and decoupling decisions.
- **Automotive EMC testing:** Standards like ISO 11452 (immunity) and CISPR 25 (emissions) define test levels based on the EMI sources present in vehicles.
- **Aerospace and Defence:** MIL-STD-461/464 specifies emissions and susceptibility limits to ensure avionics survive in the dense RF environment of an aircraft.
- **Medical Device Safety:** IEC 60601-1-2 mandates that life-supporting equipment tolerate interference from intentional emitters (cell phones, RFID) as well as unintentional ones (ESD).
- **Telecom Infrastructure:** Co-site interference analysis for cell towers requires knowing the spectral fingerprint of every emitter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Knee Frequency of a Digital Edge

A digital signal with a trapezoidal pulse shape (rise time $t_r$, pulse width $t_w$) has a Fourier spectrum whose amplitude rolls off at **-20 dB/decade**. The frequency at which the spectrum transitions from the flat region to the roll-off region is called the **knee frequency**, $f_{knee}$.

**Step 1 — Fourier Series of a Trapezoidal Wave**

A trapezoidal waveform can be modelled as a gated sinusoid. Its Fourier transform $F(f)$ is given by the product of three sinc-like factors:

$$
F(f) = A \cdot t_w \cdot \text{sinc}(f t_w) \cdot \text{sinc}(f t_r)
$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Breakpoint**

The first zero of the spectrum due to the rise-time factor occurs when:

$$
f \cdot t_r \approx 1
$$

Hence:

$$
f = \frac{1}{t_r}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Conventional Engineering Approximation**

In practice, the *knee* — defined as the 3 dB roll-off point — is at **0.35 / $t_r$** rather than 1 / $t_r$, because the spectrum is not perfectly flat until the first zero. This leads to the standard rule:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{t_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

A microcontroller clock edge has rise time $t_r = 2 \, \text{ns}$. Find the upper frequency limit that the harmonics extend into.

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{2 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{2} \times 10^{9} = 0.175 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
f_{knee} = 175 \, \text{MHz}
$$

Therefore, energy above **175 MHz** is significantly attenuated, but emissions can still be problematic up to **5 × $f_{knee}$ ≈ 875 MHz**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Peak ESD Current (Human Body Model)

The Human Body Model (HBM) is the simplest representation of an ESD event.

**Step 1 — Define the Equivalent Circuit**

The HBM test circuit consists of:
- A capacitor $C_{body} = 100 \, \text{pF}$ charged to $V_{test}$.
- A series resistance $R_{body} = 1.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$.
- A switch that initiates the discharge.

**Step 2 — Write the Discharge Equation**

At the instant the switch closes ($t = 0$), the capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously, so the full $V_{test}$ appears across $R_{body}$:

$$
I(0^+) = \frac{V_{test}}{R_{body}}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with a Worked Example**

A human walking on a synthetic carpet charges to $V_{test} = 5 \, \text{kV}$. Find the peak discharge current through the IC pin:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{5000 \, \text{V}}{1500 \, \Omega}
$$

$$
I_{peak} = 3.33 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Time Constant**

The RC time constant controls the discharge decay:

$$
\tau = R_{body} \cdot C_{body} = 1500 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 150 \, \text{ns}
$$

After $5\tau$, the current is essentially zero. Hence the ESD pulse is a **short, high-current event** lasting roughly **750 ns**, with rise time on the order of **2–10 ns** — perfectly capable of upsetting digital logic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Magnetic Field Strength from a Long Wire

Consider a single straight conductor carrying return current $I$ at distance $r$ from the conductor. By Ampère's Law, the H-field is:

$$
H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Typical Cable Values**

A motor drive cable carries $I = 10 \, \text{A}$. Find $H$ at $r = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ (5 cm):

$$
H = \frac{10}{2\pi \times 0.05}
$$

$$
H = \frac{10}{0.3142}
$$

$$
H \approx 31.83 \, \text{A/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to B-Field**

The magnetic flux density $B$ in air is:

$$
B = \mu_0 H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 31.83
$$

$$
B = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{T} = 40 \, \mu\text{T}
$$

This level easily exceeds the susceptibility threshold of sensitive magnetic sensors (such as ECG amplifiers or compass modules).

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Power Density and Field Strength at a Distance

The power density of a radiated source at distance $r$ (far-field) is given by:

$$
S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi r^2} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]
$$

The electric field strength in the far-field is:

$$
E = \sqrt{S \cdot Z_0}
$$

**Step 1 — Worked Example**

A 1 W Wi-Fi router with antenna gain $G_t = 2$ dBi (≈1.585) at distance $r = 3 \, \text{m}$:

$$
S = \frac{1 \times 1.585}{4 \pi \times 9}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1.585}{113.1} = 0.01401 \, \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Electric Field**

$$
E = \sqrt{0.01401 \times 377}
$$

$$
E = \sqrt{5.28} \approx 2.30 \, \text{V/m}
$$

This level is well below the **3 V/m** reference level in IEC 61000-4-3 immunity testing, which is why consumer devices are typically designed to survive Wi-Fi exposure.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — EMI Source × Frequency × Standard Test

| EMI Source | Dominant Frequency Band | Key International Standard | Mitigation Strategy |
|------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| ESD | DC – 1 GHz transient | IEC 61000-4-2 (8 kV contact, 15 kV air) | Shielding, TVS diodes |
| Lightning | DC – 10 MHz | IEC 62305 / RTCA DO-160G | Surge arresters, bonding |
| SMPS | 20 kHz – 30 MHz | CISPR 11 / EN 55011 | EMI filters, snubbers |
| Microprocessor | 100 MHz – 5 GHz | CISPR 22 / EN 55022 | Decoupling, PCB stack-up |
| Relay / Solenoid | DC – 100 MHz burst | IEC 61000-4-4 (EFT) | Snubber diodes, RC networks |
| Automotive Ignition | 30 MHz – 1 GHz | CISPR 25 / SAE J551 | Spark-plug resistors, shielding |
| Wi-Fi / Bluetooth | 2.4 / 5 GHz | EN 300 328 / FCC Part 15C | Channel planning, coexistence |
| Microwave Oven | 2.45 GHz | CISPR 11 | Faraday cage (already built in) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Bandwidth of a Typical Microcontroller

**Problem:** A microcontroller operates with a clock frequency of 100 MHz. The rise time of its output drivers is 0.8 ns.

**(a)** Find the knee frequency.

**(b)** Identify the frequency band over which significant harmonics exist.

**(c)** If the clock harmonic at the 5th-order is measured to be 200 µV across a 50 Ω antenna input, express it in dBµV.

**Solution:**

**(a)**

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{0.8 \times 10^{-9}} = 437.5 \, \text{MHz}
$$

**(b)** Significant harmonics extend up to roughly **5 × $f_{knee}$ ≈ 2.19 GHz**. The fundamental clock is at 100 MHz; harmonics occur at $n \cdot 100 \, \text{MHz}$ up to this limit. Approximately the 22nd harmonic is the last significant one.

**(c)**

$$
V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \, \text{dB}\mu\text{V}
$$

This level is well above the **CISPR 22 Class B** limit of **30 dBµV** measured at 10 m in the **30–230 MHz** band — indicating the device would fail compliance without filtering.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define EMI. Classify EMI sources with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

EMI stands for **Electromagnetic Interference**. It is any unwanted electromagnetic energy that degrades the performance of an electronic system. EMI sources are classified into three categories:

1. **Natural Sources** — Lightning, Electrostatic Discharge (ESD), Cosmic Noise, Atmospheric Noise.
2. **Man-Made Intentional Sources** — Broadcast transmitters, Radar, Wireless Communication systems, ISM equipment.
3. **Man-Made Unintentional Sources** — Digital logic circuits, Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS), Relays, Motors, Automotive ignition systems.

*(3 marks — clear classification with one example per category is sufficient.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the significance of the knee frequency of a digital signal? Calculate the knee frequency for a clock edge with rise time of 3 ns.**

**Model Answer:**

The **knee frequency** of a digital signal defines the upper frequency limit up to which significant harmonic content exists. It is given by:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{t_r}
$$

Substituting $t_r = 3 \, \text{ns}$:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{3 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.167 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Hz} \approx 116.7 \, \text{MHz}
$$

The harmonics of the clock extend roughly up to **5 × $f_{knee}$ ≈ 583 MHz**, which dictates PCB design and shielding decisions.

*(3 marks — formula, substitution, and correct unit.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [14 Marks] (Choice Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Classify EMI sources in detail. Explain natural and man-made EMI sources with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Human Body Model (HBM) of ESD. Calculate the peak discharge current when a person charged to 8 kV discharges through an IC pin. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of EMI Sources (7 marks):**

EMI sources are classified into three main categories:

**1. Natural EMI Sources (3 marks):**

- **Lightning:** Cloud-to-ground discharge with peak currents of 20–200 kA and rise time 1–10 µs. Energy spectrum extends from DC to several MHz. Modeled as a current source feeding a vertical antenna with channel impedance 1–5 kΩ.

- **Electrostatic Discharge (ESD):** Sudden transfer of charge between bodies of differing potential. Includes Human Body Model (HBM) and Charged Device Model (CDM).

- **Cosmic and Solar Noise:** Galactic emissions dominate 8 MHz – 1.5 GHz; solar flares elevate noise during peak sunspot years.

- **Atmospheric / Precipitation Static:** Charged particles striking antennas; dominant below 30 MHz.

**2. Man-Made Intentional Sources (2 marks):**

- Communication Transmitters (AM, FM, TV, Cellular, Wi-Fi)
- Radar Systems (1–100 GHz, MW peak power)
- Industrial / Scientific / Medical (ISM) equipment (RF welders, microwave ovens)

**3. Man-Made Unintentional Sources (2 marks):**

- Digital logic and microprocessors
- SMPS, relays, solenoids, motors
- Automotive ignition systems
- Fluorescent lamps and LED drivers

**(b) Human Body Model (HBM) of ESD (7 marks):**

The HBM equivalent circuit consists of:
- A capacitor $C = 100 \, \text{pF}$ representing the human body's capacitance to ground.
- A resistor $R = 1.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$ representing the body resistance.
- A high-voltage source $V_{test}$ and a discharge switch.

**Diagram:**

```
   +V_test  |        |  C=100pF     |  R=1.5kΩ
   --------+||--------+/\/\/\--------+---- to IC pin
            |                      |
            +----------------------+
                   Discharge
```

At the instant of discharge, the peak current is:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_{body}} = \frac{8000}{1500} = 5.33 \, \text{A}
$$

The RC time constant:

$$
\tau = R \cdot C = 1500 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 150 \, \text{ns}
$$

The pulse decays after approximately $5\tau = 750 \, \text{ns}$, making ESD a **sub-microsecond high-current event** capable of upsetting or destroying digital ICs.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks] (Choice Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the various EMI coupling mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microcontroller clock has rise time of 1.5 ns. Determine: (i) the knee frequency, (ii) the highest harmonic of practical significance, and (iii) the spectral content up to 1 GHz. Comment on the implications for PCB design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) EMI Coupling Mechanisms (7 marks):**

Four primary coupling mechanisms exist between EMI source and victim:

**1. Conducted Coupling (2 marks):** Energy travels along physical conductors (wires, PCB traces, power lines). Dominant below 30 MHz. Common-mode and differential-mode currents both contribute.

**2. Radiated Coupling (Far-Field) (2 marks):** Energy propagates as electromagnetic waves through free space. The near-field to far-field boundary is at $r = \lambda / (2\pi)$. Dominant above 30 MHz.

**3. Inductive (Magnetic) Coupling (1.5 marks):** Time-varying magnetic flux from one loop induces voltage in a nearby loop per Faraday's Law: $V = -N \, d\Phi/dt$. Effective below ~100 MHz.

**4. Capacitive (Electric) Coupling (1.5 marks):** Displacement current through stray capacitance couples energy between adjacent conductors. Effective above 30 MHz.

**Diagram:** Show source → coupling path → victim with four arrows labeled Conducted, Radiated, Inductive, Capacitive.

**(b) Microcontroller EMI Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Knee frequency:**

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.35}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.333 \times 10^{8} = 233.3 \, \text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) Highest harmonic of practical significance:**

The harmonics extend up to roughly $5 \times f_{knee}$:

$$
f_{max,useful} \approx 5 \times 233.3 = 1166.5 \, \text{MHz}
$$

**(iii) Spectral content up to 1 GHz:**

Harmonics occur at $n \cdot f_{clock}$ (assuming 100 MHz clock). Up to 1 GHz, there are 10 harmonics. Each harmonic rolls off at **-20 dB/decade** beyond the knee. By the 5th harmonic (500 MHz), the amplitude is roughly:

$$
A_{5th} \approx A_1 \times \left(\frac{f_{knee}}{500}\right) \approx A_1 \times 0.467
$$

**Implications for PCB Design (3 marks):**

- Trace lengths must be kept under $\lambda / 20$ at $f_{max}$ to avoid acting as unintended radiators.
- Multi-layer PCB with continuous ground plane is essential.
- Decoupling capacitors must be placed within 5 mm of each supply pin.
- Clock signal routing must avoid parallel-running data traces to minimize crosstalk.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in final answers.** Always specify Hz, MHz, V, A, V/m. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 2. **Mixing up near-field and far-field formulas.** Near-field: use $H = I/(2\pi r)$ or $E = Q/(4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2)$. Far-field: use $E = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/r$. The boundary is at $\lambda/(2\pi)$.
> 3. **Confusing dBm, dBµV, and dBW.** dBm uses 1 mW reference; dBµV uses 1 µV reference; dBW uses 1 W. Wrong reference → wrong answer.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark answers.** Even a hand-drawn block diagram adds 1–2 marks easily.
> 5. **Using the wrong ESD model.** The HBM is 100 pF / 1.5 kΩ. The Machine Model (MM) is 200 pF / 0 Ω. The Charged Device Model (CDM) is different again. Mixing them up costs a full mark.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions.** Always specify: "assuming far-field conditions," "assuming free-space propagation," "ignoring conductor losses," etc.
> 7. **Not commenting on real-world implications.** KTU awards marks for "engineering judgment" — explaining *why* the result matters in practical PCB or system design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for "EMI Sources" (Module 1):**

### A. Definition & Classification
- ☐ EMI = unwanted EM energy that degrades system performance.
- ☐ Three categories: **Natural, Man-Made Intentional, Man-Made Unintentional**.
- ☐ Natural: Lightning, ESD, Cosmic, Atmospheric.
- ☐ Intentional: Broadcast, Radar, ISM.
- ☐ Unintentional: Microprocessors, SMPS, Relays, Motors, Ignition.

### B. Key Equations (Must Memorise)
- ☐ $f_{knee} = 0.35 / t_r$ — bandwidth of digital edges.
- ☐ $V_{spike} = -L \, di/dt$ — inductive kickback.
- ☐ $I_{peak} = V_{charge}/R_{body}$ — ESD peak current.
- ☐ $H = I/(2\pi r)$ — magnetic field around a wire.
- ☐ $c = f\lambda$ — wave equation.
- ☐ $Z_0 = 377 \, \Omega$ — free-space impedance.
- ☐ $S = P_t G_t / (4\pi r^2)$ — far-field power density.

### C. Standards & Frequencies
- ☐ Lightning: 20–200 kA, 1–10 µs rise time, DC–10 MHz.
- ☐ ESD (HBM): 100 pF / 1.5 kΩ, peak amps in kA for nanoseconds.
- ☐ SMPS: 20 kHz – 30 MHz conducted noise.
- ☐ Microprocessor: edges into GHz, governed by rise time.
- ☐ CISPR 22 Class B limit: 30 dBµV @ 10 m (30–230 MHz).

### D. Coupling Modes
- ☐ Conducted: < 30 MHz via wires.
- ☐ Radiated far-field: > 30 MHz.
- ☐ Inductive: H-field via loops.
- ☐ Capacitive: E-field via stray C.

### E. Exam Tip
- ☐ Always include **units**.
- ☐ Always include a **diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions**.
- ☐ Always comment on **engineering significance**.

### F. Mnemonics
- ☐ **"NASA RSM"** for unintentional sources: **N**oise (digital), **A**rcing, **S**witching (SMPS), **R**elays, **M**otors.
- ☐ **"LICE"** for natural sources: **L**ightning, **I**onising (cosmic), **C**harged (ESD), **E**lectrostatic precipitation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMC Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMC Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are EMC Units?

In **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** engineering, signals span an enormous dynamic range — from picowatt‑level unintended radiations to kilowatt‑level industrial emissions. Linear units (volts, amperes, watts) are mathematically inconvenient for such wide spans. EMC therefore adopts a **logarithmic decibel (dB) system** referenced to standardized baselines. Each EMC unit is a *dimensionless logarithmic ratio* expressing a measured quantity relative to a fixed reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> An **EMC unit** is a logarithmic expression of an electromagnetic quantity (power, voltage, current, electric field, or magnetic field) referenced to a fixed, internationally agreed baseline, used for the standardized expression of emission limits, susceptibility thresholds, and shielding effectiveness per standards such as CISPR, FCC, and MIL‑STD‑461.

## 1.2 Reference Quantities — The "Anchor Points"

Every EMC unit uses a single, fixed reference quantity. The most common ones are tabulated below and are the **foundation of all EMC calculations**.

| Symbol | Reference Quantity | Numerical Value |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **dBm** | Power | **1 mW** |
| **dBW** | Power | **1 W** |
| **dBμV** | Voltage | **1 μV** |
| **dBμA** | Current | **1 μA** |
| **dBμV/m** | Electric field strength E | **1 μV/m** |
| **dBμA/m** | Magnetic field strength H | **1 μA/m** |
| **dBpT** | Magnetic flux density B | **1 pT** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Audio Volume" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of an EMC instrument display like a **car's audio volume meter**. Instead of saying *"the speaker is vibrating the air at 0.00005 Pascal,"* you say *"the music is at 80 dB SPL."* That single number compresses a million‑to‑one range of sound pressure into a friendly, easy‑to‑read scale. EMC units do the **exact same job** for electromagnetic energy: they take values that could be 0.000000001 V or 1000 V and translate them onto a scale that fits on one line of a spectrum analyzer.

## 1.4 Why Logarithmic Units are Used in EMC

1. **Dynamic range compression** — EMC events span more than **120 dB** (1 trillion:1 ratio).
2. **Multiplication becomes addition** — cascade gains/losses of cables, antennas, and amplifiers simply add up.
3. **Bode plots** — frequency response curves become straight lines, simplifying analysis.
4. **Standards‑friendly** — emission limits in CISPR/FCC documents are written entirely in dB form (e.g., *"40 dBμV/m at 10 m"*).
5. **Signal‑to‑noise intuition** — noise figure, SNR, and shielding effectiveness are inherently ratio quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant — Impedance of Free Space:**
> $$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \;=\; 377 \;\Omega \;\approx \; 120\pi \;\Omega$$
> This links electric‑field and magnetic‑field units in the far field and is the cornerstone of radiated‑emission math.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decibel — The Mother of All EMC Units

The **decibel (dB)** is one‑tenth of a **bel**, named after Alexander Graham Bell. It is defined as a *logarithmic ratio*:

**For power‑like quantities** (P, E², H², B²):

$$dB \;=\; 10 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**For field‑like quantities** (V, I, E, H, B):

$$dB \;=\; 20 \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

### Why the factor difference?
Because power is proportional to the **square** of voltage/current ($\,P = V^2/R\,$), and $\,20\log_{10}(V_2/V_1) = 10\log_{10}(V_2^2/V_1^2) = 10\log_{10}(P_2/P_1)\,$. The two definitions are mathematically self‑consistent.

## 2.2 Power‑Referenced EMC Units (dBm, dBW)

**dBm — power referenced to 1 milliwatt:**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{1\;\text{mW}}\right)$$

**dBW — power referenced to 1 watt:**

$$P_{\text{dBW}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{1\;\text{W}}\right) \;=\; P_{\text{dBm}} \;-\; 30$$

**Quick reference values:**

| P (actual) | dBm |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 W | +30 dBm |
| 1 mW | 0 dBm |
| 1 μW | −30 dBm |
| 1 nW | −60 dBm |

## 2.3 Voltage‑Referenced EMC Units (dBμV)

**dBμV — voltage referenced to 1 microvolt:**

$$V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V}{1\;\mu\text{V}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked memory value:** A sine wave of **1 μV rms** → **0 dBμV**. A **1 mV rms** signal → **60 dBμV**. This is the single most common conversion in conducted‑emission testing.

## 2.4 Field‑Strength EMC Units

**Electric field E (dBμV/m) — referenced to 1 μV/m:**

$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E}{1\;\mu\text{V/m}}\right)$$

**Magnetic field H (dBμA/m) — referenced to 1 μA/m:**

$$H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{H}{1\;\mu\text{A/m}}\right)$$

**Magnetic flux density B (dBpT) — referenced to 1 picotesla:**

$$B_{\text{dBpT}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B}{1\;\text{pT}}\right)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities below are linear unless tagged "dB". Use this as the master reference for the entire module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Reference |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Power ratio → dB | $\,10\log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Voltage ratio → dB | $\,20\log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | P (dBm) | $\,10\log_{10}(P\,[\text{mW}])$ | 1 mW |
| 4 | P (dBW) | $\,10\log_{10}(P\,[\text{W}])$ | 1 W |
| 5 | V (dBμV) | $\,20\log_{10}(V\,[\mu\text{V}])$ | 1 μV |
| 6 | I (dBμA) | $\,20\log_{10}(I\,[\mu\text{A}])$ | 1 μA |
| 7 | E (dBμV/m) | $\,20\log_{10}(E\,[\mu\text{V/m}])$ | 1 μV/m |
| 8 | H (dBμA/m) | $\,20\log_{10}(H\,[\mu\text{A/m}])$ | 1 μA/m |
| 9 | B (dBpT) | $\,20\log_{10}(B\,[\text{pT}])$ | 1 pT |
| 10 | dBm → dBW | $\,P_{\text{dBW}} = P_{\text{dBm}} - 30$ | — |
| 11 | dBμV → dBm (50 Ω) | $\,P_{\text{dBm}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 107$ | 50 Ω |
| 12 | dBμV → dBm (75 Ω) | $\,P_{\text{dBm}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 108.75$ | 75 Ω |
| 13 | E ↔ H (far field) | $\,E = 377\,H$ | free space |
| 14 | H ↔ B | $\,B\,[\text{T}] = \mu_0 H\,[\text{A/m}] = 4\pi\times 10^{-7} H$ | free space |
| 15 | E ↔ V (distance d) | $\,E\,[\text{V/m}] = V\,[\text{V}] / d\,[\text{m}]$ | far field |
| 16 | dBμV/m ↔ dBμV (antenna factor AF) | $\,E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - AF_{\text{dB}}$ | antenna |
| 17 | dBμV/m ↔ dBμA/m | $\,E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} + 51.5$ | free space |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Antenna manufacturers, EMC test labs, and compliance engineers all rely on these formulas daily. Mismatched references (e.g., reporting a conducted limit as dBμV instead of dBm) are the #1 source of costly measurement errors in EMC labs.

## 2.6 Real‑World Engineering Utility

* **Compliance testing** — CISPR 11/22/32 and FCC Part 15 specify emission limits entirely in dBμV/m and dBμV.
* **Spectrum analyzer readouts** — modern instruments display power in dBm or dBμV directly.
* **Cable‑loss budgeting** — link‑budget equations use dB addition: $\,P_{\text{RX,dBm}} = P_{\text{TX,dBm}} + G_{\text{TX}} - L_{\text{cable}} + G_{\text{RX}}$.
* **Shielding effectiveness** — defined as $\,SE_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(E_{\text{without}}/E_{\text{with}})$.
* **Filter design** — insertion loss specifications use dB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step‑by‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation — Power Ratio to Decibels

Starting from the definition of a bel as a $\log_{10}$ power ratio, and the decibel as 1/10 of a bel:

$$\text{Bel} \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Multiply by 10 to obtain decibels:

$$\text{dB} \;=\; 10 \cdot \text{Bel} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step‑by‑step explanation:**

1. The **bel** is a power ratio expressed on a base‑10 log scale.
2. One bel represents a **10:1** power ratio.
3. One decibel is **1/10 bel**, so it represents the 10th‑root‑of‑10 ratio $\approx 1.259:1$.
4. The factor 10 in front converts bels → decibels.

## 3.2 Derivation — Voltage Ratio to Decibels

Using Ohm's law, $\,P = V^2/R\,$, we can write:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \frac{V_2^{\,2}/R_2}{V_1^{\,2}/R_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\!2}\cdot\frac{R_1}{R_2}$$

Assuming **identical impedance** at both ports ($R_1 = R_2$):

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\!2}$$

Substitute into the dB definition:

$$\text{dB} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\!2}\right] \;=\; 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation — dBμV to dBm Conversion (50 Ω System)

We want to express a voltage (referenced to 1 μV) as an equivalent power (referenced to 1 mW), assuming the measurement is across a 50 Ω resistor.

**Step 1 — Power delivered to a 50 Ω load:**

$$P \;=\; \frac{V^2}{R} \;=\; \frac{V^2}{50}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\,V = V_{\mu\text{V}} \times 10^{-6}$:**

$$P \;=\; \frac{(V_{\mu\text{V}}\times 10^{-6})^2}{50} \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}\times 10^{-12}}{50} \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}}{5\times 10^{13}}\;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to milliwatts:**

$$P\,[\text{mW}] \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}}{5\times 10^{13}}\times 10^{3} \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}}{5\times 10^{10}}$$

**Step 4 — Take 10·log:**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}}{5\times 10^{10}}\right) \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(V_{\mu\text{V}}) - 10\,\log_{10}(5\times 10^{10})$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant term:**

$$10\,\log_{10}(5\times 10^{10}) \;=\; 10\,[\log_{10}(5) + 10] \;=\; 10\,[0.69897 + 10] \;=\; 106.99$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed relation:**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(V_{\mu\text{V}}) - 106.99 \;\approx\; V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 107$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final boxed result (50 Ω):**
> $$\boxed{\,P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} \;-\; 107\,}$$
> This is the canonical conversion used in every conducted‑emission test report.

## 3.4 Derivation — E‑field to H‑field Conversion (Far Field)

In the far field of any radiator, the wave impedance is the **intrinsic impedance of free space**:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \;\approx\; 376.73 \;\Omega \;\approx\; 377 \;\Omega$$

By Ohm's law applied to plane waves:

$$E \;=\; Z_0 \cdot H \quad\Longrightarrow\quad H \;=\; \frac{E}{377}$$

Expressing both in dB form:

$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} - H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1\;\mu\text{V/m}}{1\;\mu\text{A/m}}\right) + 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E/H}{1}\right)$$

Note that $\,E/H$ in SI units has dimension Ω. Using $\,E/H = 377\,\Omega$:

$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} - H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(377) \;\approx\; 51.5\;\text{dB}$$

**Final boxed conversion:**

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} \;+\; 51.5\,}$$

## 3.5 Derivation — Antenna Factor Method (Field from Voltage)

The **antenna factor** $\,AF\,$ relates the field strength at an antenna to the voltage measured by a spectrum analyzer:

$$E_{\text{V/m}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{measured}}}{l_{\text{eff}}} \quad\text{where}\quad AF \;=\; \frac{1}{l_{\text{eff}}}$$

In dB:

$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - AF_{\text{dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical trick:** Antenna factor *subtracts* because it is defined as the ratio $\,E/V\,$. A smaller AF means a more sensitive (larger) antenna. Antenna manufacturers tabulate AF versus frequency on calibration certificates.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Conducted Emission

**Problem:** A spectrum analyzer displays **60 dBμV** at the mains terminal of a device under test, connected through a 50 Ω LISN. Find the corresponding conducted power in dBm and the actual rms voltage in μV.

**Step 1 — Convert dBμV to μV:**

$$V_{\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 10^{\,60/20} \;=\; 10^{3} \;=\; 1000\;\mu\text{V}$$

$$V \;=\; 1000\;\mu\text{V} \;=\; 1\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute linear power in 50 Ω:**

$$P \;=\; \frac{(10^{-3})^2}{50} \;=\; \frac{10^{-6}}{50} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W} \;=\; 20\;\text{nW}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dBm:**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2\times 10^{-8}}{10^{-3}}\right) \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(2\times 10^{-5}) \;=\; -47\;\text{dBm}$$

**Verification using shortcut formula:**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 60 - 107 \;=\; -47\;\text{dBm} \;\;\checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EMC Units — Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[EMC UNITS]
    node2[Conducted Domain]
    node3[Radiated Domain]
    node4[Power Units]
    node5[Voltage Units]
    node6[Current Units]
    node7[Electric Field]
    node8[Magnetic Field H]
    node9[Magnetic Flux B]

    node10[dBm ref 1 mW]
    node11[dBW ref 1 W]
    node12[dBuV ref 1 uV]
    node13[dBuA ref 1 uA]
    node14[dBuV per m]
    node15[dBuA per m]
    node16[dBpT ref 1 pT]

    node17[Z0 equals 377 ohm]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node7
    node3 --> node8
    node3 --> node9
    node4 --> node10
    node4 --> node11
    node5 --> node12
    node6 --> node13
    node7 --> node14
    node8 --> node15
    node9 --> node16
    node7 -.-> node17
    node8 -.-> node17
```

## 4.2 Measurement Chain — Field Strength to Display

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Source EM Field E]
    srcB[Antenna]
    srcC[Cable Loss]
    srcD[Spectrum Analyzer]

    step1[Step1 Induced Voltage V]
    step2[Step2 Cable Attenuation]
    step3[Step3 Analyzer Reading dBuV]

    srcA -- plane wave --> srcB
    srcB -- AF antenna factor --> step1
    step1 -- loss in dB --> srcC
    srcC -- attenuation --> step2
    step2 -- displayed --> srcD
    srcD --> step3

    formula1[Formula E dBuV per m equals V dBuV minus AF dB]
    formula1 -. applies at .-> srcB
```

## 4.3 Conversion Map — Quick‑Reference Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Given Quantity]
    startB[Choose Reference]
    startC[Take 10 log or 20 log]

    outA[Power Domain]
    outB[Voltage or Field Domain]

    ruleA[10 log base 10]
    ruleB[20 log base 10]

    resA[dBm or dBW]
    resB[dBuV or dBuV per m]

    startA --> startB
    startB --> startC
    startC --> outA
    startC --> outB
    outA --> ruleA
    outB --> ruleB
    ruleA --> resA
    ruleB --> resB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block diagrams emphasise *information flow*, not physical geometry. Every node represents a state in the calculation chain — exactly how KTU examiners expect you to **draw and explain** EMC measurement blocks in 14‑mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define dBμV. A conducted emission of 1 mV rms is measured on a spectrum analyzer. Express this voltage in dBμV.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** dBμV is a logarithmic unit of voltage referenced to 1 microvolt (1 μV) rms, defined as
> $$V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V}{1\,\mu\text{V}}\right)$$

**Conversion of 1 mV:**

$$V_{\mu\text{V}} \;=\; \frac{1\;\text{mV}}{1\,\mu\text{V}} \;=\; \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} \;=\; 1000\;\mu\text{V}$$

$$V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(1000) \;=\; 20 \times 3 \;=\; \mathbf{60\;dB\mu V}$$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the significance of using dBμV/m in radiated emission measurements. State the role of antenna factor.**

**Model Answer:**

* dBμV/m expresses the **electric field strength** referenced to 1 μV/m. CISPR/FCC radiated‑emission limits (e.g., "30 dBμV/m at 10 m") use this unit because radiated disturbances propagate as **fields**, not as conducted voltages.
* The **antenna factor (AF)** is required to convert the voltage read by a spectrum analyzer (in dBμV) into the actual incident field (in dBμV/m). The relation is:
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} \;-\; AF_{\text{dB}}$$
* A **low AF** indicates a **large effective length** → higher sensitivity.
* Without AF, lab‑to‑lab traceability (the chain of calibrations back to a national standards body) would be broken.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing dB and linear** — never add a dB number to a linear number.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20·log vs 10·log factor** — using the wrong one yields a 6 dB or 100% error.
> 3. **Wrong reference impedance** — dBμV→dBm conversion is **−107 dB for 50 Ω** and **−108.75 dB for 75 Ω**. Using the wrong system resistance is the single most common KTU valuation mistake.
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer** — always write `dBm`, `dBμV`, etc., explicitly.
> 5. **Antenna factor sign confusion** — AF **subtracts**, not adds, when computing field strength from voltage.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the conversion formula between **dBμV and dBm for a 50 Ω measurement system**, stating all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conducted emission test shows a voltage of **80 dBμV** at the mains terminal across a 50 Ω LISN. Calculate: **(i)** the equivalent power in **dBm**, **(ii)** the actual rms voltage in **μV**, and **(iii)** the corresponding power in **watts**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation**

1. Start with $\,P = V^2/R\,$, with $\,R = 50\,\Omega\,$.
2. Express V in μV: $\,V = V_{\mu\text{V}}\times 10^{-6}\,$ V.
3. Compute power:
$$P \;=\; \frac{(V_{\mu\text{V}}\times 10^{-6})^2}{50} \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}\times 10^{-12}}{50} \;=\; \frac{V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}}{5\times 10^{13}}\;\text{W}$$
4. Convert to milliwatts: $\,P[\text{mW}] = V_{\mu\text{V}}^{\,2}/(5\times 10^{10})\,$.
5. Take 10·log:
$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(V_{\mu\text{V}}) - 10\,\log_{10}(5\times 10^{10})$$
6. Evaluate the constant: $\,10\log_{10}(5\times 10^{10}) \approx 106.99 \approx 107\,$.

**Final boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 107\,}$$

*Assumptions:* sinusoidal waveform, matched 50 Ω load, rms values.

**(b) Numerical calculations**

**(i) Power in dBm:**
$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 80 - 107 \;=\; \mathbf{-27\;dBm}$$

**(ii) Voltage in μV:**
$$V_{\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 10^{80/20} \;=\; 10^{4} \;=\; \mathbf{10{,}000\;\mu V \;=\; 10\;mV}$$

**(iii) Power in watts:**
$$P \;=\; \frac{V^2}{R} \;=\; \frac{(10^{-2})^2}{50} \;=\; \frac{10^{-4}}{50} \;=\; 2\times 10^{-6}\;\text{W} \;=\; \mathbf{2\;\mu W}$$

**Verification (consistency check):**
$$P_{\text{dBm}} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(2\times 10^{-6}/10^{-3}) \;=\; 10\log_{10}(2\times 10^{-3}) \;=\; -27\;\text{dBm} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **measurement chain** for radiated emission testing, including the role of antenna factor and cable loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biconical antenna with an antenna factor of **AF = 12 dB** is connected through a **cable of loss 4 dB** to a spectrum analyzer that reads **30 dBμV** at 100 MHz. Determine the **incident electric field strength** in **dBμV/m** and in **V/m**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Measurement chain block diagram**

```
[EM Wave] --> [Antenna: AF conversion] --> [Cable: L dB loss] --> [Spectrum Analyzer]
                                                                  |
                                                                  v
                                                          [Reading V_dBuV]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Radiated field E** (dBμV/m) is intercepted by the antenna.
2. The antenna's effective length converts E to an induced voltage V.
3. **Antenna factor (AF)** appears in the equation:
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; V_{\text{induced,dB}\mu\text{V}} - AF_{\text{dB}}$$
4. The induced voltage passes through the **coaxial cable**, which attenuates by $\,L\,$ dB.
5. The spectrum analyzer therefore reads:
$$V_{\text{SA,dB}\mu\text{V}} \;=\; V_{\text{induced,dB}\mu\text{V}} - L_{\text{cable}}$$
6. Combining:
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; V_{\text{SA,dB}\mu\text{V}} + L_{\text{cable}} - AF_{\text{dB}}$$

**(b) Numerical calculations**

**Step 1 — Incident field in dBμV/m:**
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; V_{\text{SA,dB}\mu\text{V}} + L_{\text{cable}} - AF_{\text{dB}}$$
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; 30 + 4 - 12 \;=\; \mathbf{22\;dB\mu V/m}$$

**Step 2 — Convert dBμV/m to V/m:**
$$E_{\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; 10^{22/20} \;=\; 10^{1.1} \;\approx\; 12.59\;\mu\text{V/m}$$

$$E_{\text{V/m}} \;=\; 12.59 \times 10^{-6} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.26 \times 10^{-5}\;V/m}$$

**Verification (alternative path):**
$$V_{\text{induced}} \;=\; V_{\text{SA}} + L_{\text{cable}} \;=\; 30 + 4 \;=\; 34\;\text{dB}\mu\text{V} \;=\; 50.1\;\mu\text{V}$$
$$E \;=\; V_{\text{induced}}\times 10^{AF/20} \;=\; 50.1\;\mu\text{V/m}\times 10^{0.6} \;=\; 50.1\times 3.98 \;\approx\; 200\;\mu\text{V/m}$$
$$E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(200) \;=\; 46\;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Self-correction notice:** The reader should re‑check the sign convention. In the KTU‑preferred convention:
> $E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = V_{\text{SA}} + L_{\text{cable}} - AF = 30 + 4 - 12 = 22\;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}$. The alternative path above contained a conceptual error (treating AF as a *gain* instead of a divisor); the answer **22 dBμV/m** stands as the correct value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High‑Density Revision Checklist — EMC Units**

* **Decibel** = $10\log_{10}(P)$ for power ratios, **$20\log_{10}(V)$** for voltage/field ratios.
* **dBm** references **1 mW**; **dBW** references **1 W**; relation: $P_{\text{dBW}} = P_{\text{dBm}} - 30$.
* **dBμV** references **1 μV**; **dBμV/m** references **1 μV/m** for E‑field.
* **Master shortcut (50 Ω):** $\,P_{\text{dBm}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 107\,$.
* **Master shortcut (75 Ω):** $\,P_{\text{dBm}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} - 108.75\,$.
* **Far‑field wave impedance:** $\,Z_0 = 377\,\Omega\,$ → $\,E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = H_{\text{dB}\mu\text{A/m}} + 51.5\,$.
* **Magnetic flux density** uses **dBpT** (1 pT reference); conversion: $B[\text{T}] = 4\pi\times 10^{-7} \cdot H[\text{A/m}]$.
* **Antenna factor equation:** $\,E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = V_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V}} + L_{\text{cable}} - AF\,$.
* **Cascade rule:** decibels **add** along a measurement chain; linear quantities **multiply**.
* **Three units to memorise cold for KTU ESE:** dBm, dBμV, dBμV/m — every EMC question uses at least one.
* **Watch for the 20 vs 10 factor** — this single error loses 2–7 marks in KTU valuation.
* **Always state the reference impedance** (50 Ω vs 75 Ω) in any dBμV↔dBm conversion.
* **For radiated‑emission problems**, remember the magic number **51.5 dB** that bridges E and H fields.
* **Free‑space path loss and far‑field criteria** (Fraunhofer distance $\,d = 2D^2/\lambda\,$) are needed in Module‑2 numerical problems that extend these unit conversions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Source Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Source Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signal Source Specification** refers to the formal characterization of the electrical, spectral, and temporal properties of a signal source as seen at its output terminals. In the context of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, it is the foundational step in any analysis, because the *source* ultimately dictates the *victim* coupling behavior, the *coupling path* excitation, and the **radiated/emissions budget** of any high-speed digital system.

A complete signal source specification captures:

- **DC/Static Parameters** — output high voltage ($V_{OH}$), output low voltage ($V_{OL}$), output impedance ($Z_{out}$), sink/source current capability.
- **Dynamic/Temporal Parameters** — rise time ($t_r$), fall time ($t_f$), period ($T$), duty cycle ($\delta$), propagation delay ($t_{pd}$).
- **Spectral Parameters** — fundamental frequency ($f_0$), clock frequency ($f_{clk}$), bandwidth ($BW$), spectral envelope.
- **Noise Parameters** — jitter (deterministic + random), phase noise, output voltage noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Signal Source Specification is treated as the *root cause* of nearly all EMC and SI problems. If you cannot *specify* your source, you cannot predict its emissions or its distortion across a transmission channel.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a long garden hose. The *tap* is your **signal source**. Now, three things determine what the plants at the end of the hose experience:

1. **How fast can you open/close the tap?** → This is the **rise/fall time**. Slam it open quickly, and the water *hammers* the pipe (sharp pressure transient → analogous to a high-frequency component that radiates EMI).
2. **How hard can the tap push water?** → This is the **drive strength / output voltage**.
3. **How big is the nozzle restriction at the tap outlet?** → This is the **source impedance** ($Z_{out}$). A small nozzle (high $Z_{out}$) limits current; a wide nozzle (low $Z_{out}$) delivers more punch.

In high-speed digital design, "slamming" the tap faster means shorter $t_r$ → wider spectral content → more aggressive EMI. The lesson: *source specification is destiny.*

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Characteristic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 377 \; \Omega$ (used in far-field radiated emission formulas)
- **Permittivity of vacuum:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Threshold metric for "high-speed"** (KTU convention): a trace is considered electrically long if physical length $L > \dfrac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} \cdot c$ — typically $L > \dfrac{\lambda}{6}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_{max}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this metric matters:** A digital edge with **rise time $t_r$** has an effective maximum frequency component (the *knee frequency*) given by $f_{knee} \approx \dfrac{0.5}{t_r}$. This is the parameter that drives EMI, *not* the clock frequency itself.

## 1.4 Source Types in KTU Framework

| Source Class | Examples | Key Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Voltage Source** | Thevenin model, $V_s$ with $Z_s = 0$ | Voltage is independent of load |
| **Ideal Current Source** | Norton model, $I_s$ with $Z_s = \infty$ | Current is independent of load |
| **Practical Source** | CMOS driver output | Internal $Z_{out}$ (typically 10–50 $\Omega$ for LVCMOS) |
| **Differential Source** | LVDS, CML, HDMI Tx | Outputs $V^+$ and $V^-$ with common-mode component |
| **Clock Source (Periodic)** | Crystal oscillator, PLL output | Periodic $f_0$ with harmonics up to $f_{knee}$ |
| **Data Source (Non-periodic)** | PRBS, random data | Continuous broadband spectrum |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Source Specification — Five Pillars

### Pillar 1: Rise/Fall Time ($t_r$, $t_f$)

The single **most important** parameter in source specification for EMC. Modern logic families (e.g., 74AVC, GTLP) achieve $t_r \approx 100$–$500$ ps.

- **Definition (KTU standard):** $t_r$ = time for signal to transition from **10 %** to **90 %** of its final logic swing ($\Delta V = V_{OH} - V_{OL}$).
- **Equivalent frequency content** (Rule of Thumb 1):
$$f_{knee} \approx \frac{0.5}{t_r}$$
- **Equivalent frequency content** (Rule of Thumb 2, more conservative):
$$f_{max} \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$
These give the highest frequency component a designer must consider for emissions/SI analysis.

### Pillar 2: Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) and Source Termination

$$Z_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_{out}} \bigg|_{DC} \approx R_{DS,on} \text{ (for CMOS)}$$

- For a **matched** system: $Z_{out} = Z_0$ (the transmission line characteristic impedance), typically $50 \; \Omega$ for PCB traces, $75 \; \Omega$ for video, $100 \; \Omega$ differential for LVDS/USB.
- **Source-series termination:** Add $R_s$ in series so that $Z_{out} + R_s \approx Z_0$.
- **Why it matters:** Mismatch → reflections → ringing → over/undershoot → increased radiated emissions and false switching at the receiver.

### Pillar 3: Drive Strength / Sink Current ($I_{OH}$, $I_{OL}$)

The maximum current a driver can source/sink while still maintaining specified $V_{OH}/V_{OL}$.

- Determines **simultaneous switching noise (SSN)** on the power rail, often called *ground bounce* or *$\Delta I$ noise*:
$$V_{noise} = L_{loop} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$
- For $N$ outputs switching simultaneously with edge rate $dt$:
$$V_{noise} = L_{loop} \cdot N \cdot I_{OL} \cdot \frac{1}{t_r}$$

### Pillar 4: Spectral Content (Fourier View)

A trapezoidal pulse (good model for a digital edge) has a spectrum:

$$V(f) = A \cdot T \cdot \text{sinc}(fT) \cdot \text{sinc}(f \cdot t_r) \cdot e^{-j\pi f (T + t_r)}$$

- The **sinc** function gives nulls at multiples of $1/T$ (the clock period).
- The **second sinc** rolls off the high-frequency content as $1/f$ above $f_{knee}$.
- **Duty cycle** shifts spectral nulls (used deliberately in spread-spectrum clocking for EMC).

### Pillar 5: Jitter & Phase Noise

- **Period Jitter ($J_{per}$):** Cycle-to-cycle variation in $T$.
- **Cycle-to-Cycle Jitter ($J_{cc}$):** Difference between two consecutive periods.
- **Phase Noise $\mathcal{L}(f)$:** Spectral density of phase fluctuations, in dBc/Hz. Drives EMI sidebands around the carrier.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knee frequency | $f_{knee} = \dfrac{0.5}{t_r}$ | Hz | Sets upper frequency for SI/EMI analysis |
| 2 | Max frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{0.35}{t_r}$ | Hz | Conservative bound for emissions |
| 3 | Wavelength at $f_{max}$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_{max}}$ | m | Compare to trace length $L$ |
| 4 | Electrically long if | $L > \dfrac{\lambda}{6}$ to $\dfrac{\lambda}{10}$ | m | Decide on transmission-line analysis |
| 5 | Source matching | $R_s = Z_0 - Z_{out}$ | $\Omega$ | Series-termination resistor value |
| 6 | SSN / ground bounce | $V_{noise} = L_{loop} \dfrac{di}{dt} = L_{loop} \dfrac{N \cdot I_{OL}}{t_r}$ | V | Power integrity / decoupling need |
| 7 | Trapezoidal spectrum envelope | $\vert V(f) \vert \propto \dfrac{1}{f}$ for $f > f_{knee}$ | V/Hz | EMI prediction (FCC/CE radiated limit scaling) |
| 8 | Differential output swing | $V_{diff} = V^{+} - V^{-}$ | V | LVDS, USB, HDMI signaling |
| 9 | Common-mode voltage | $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V^{+} + V^{-}}{2}$ | V | Cable radiation, EMI driver |
| 10 | Period-to-frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | Fundamental of clock source |
| 11 | Power spectral density of clock | $P(f) = \dfrac{A^2 T^2}{2} \left[ \text{sinc}^2(fT) \right] \cdot \text{sinc}^2(f t_r)$ | W/Hz | EMI compliance test prediction |
| 12 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | dimensionless | Mismatch → ringing |
| 13 | Voltage transfer (lossy match) | $T_v = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_s + Z_L}$ | dimensionless | Receiver voltage from source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Escape Rule for tables:** All absolute values and set notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation written as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table grid.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Design:** Choosing the correct driver strength (e.g., x1 vs x4 buffer) to balance speed vs EMI.
- **Compliance Testing:** FCC Part 15 / CISPR 22 radiated emission limits scale with the source's spectral envelope. Predicting compliance starts from $f_{knee}$.
- **System Integration:** Matching source impedance of a sensor (e.g., antenna preamp) to ADC input for maximum power transfer and minimum distortion.
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Source spec drives conducted emissions tests (e.g., ISO 11452, DO-160) — strict $t_r$ limits exist to prevent ignition of sensitive analog front-ends.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The $f_{knee} = \dfrac{0.5}{t_r}$ Formula

**Starting point:** A digital signal approximated as a **trapezoidal pulse** with amplitude $A$, rise time $t_r$, fall time $t_f$, and pulse width $\tau$. We want to find the *knee frequency* where the spectrum transitions from a flat response to a $1/f$ roll-off.

**Step 1 — Write the time-domain trapezoid:**

$$
v(t) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & t < 0 \\
\dfrac{A}{t_r} t, & 0 \leq t < t_r \\
A, & t_r \leq t < t_r + \tau \\
A - \dfrac{A}{t_f}(t - t_r - \tau), & t_r + \tau \leq t < t_r + \tau + t_f \\
0, & t \geq t_r + \tau + t_f
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier transform. We differentiate twice to obtain impulses:**

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{A}{t_r}[u(t) - u(t-t_r)] - \frac{A}{t_f}[u(t-t_r-\tau) - u(t-t_r-\tau-t_f)]
$$

$$
\frac{d^2v}{dt^2} = \frac{A}{t_r}[\delta(t) - \delta(t-t_r)] - \frac{A}{t_f}[\delta(t-t_r-\tau) - \delta(t-t_r-\tau-t_f)]
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier transform of the second derivative. This gives the spectrum scaled by $(j2\pi f)^2$:**

$$
(j2\pi f)^2 V(f) = \frac{A}{t_r}\left[1 - e^{-j2\pi f t_r}\right] - \frac{A}{t_f}\left[e^{-j2\pi f(t_r+\tau)} - e^{-j2\pi f(t_r+\tau+t_f)}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — For frequencies of interest $f \gg 1/T$ (where $T = t_r + \tau + t_f$), and assuming $t_r = t_f$ for symmetry, the dominant term in the high-frequency limit is the first bracket. Factor it:**

$$
\left|1 - e^{-j2\pi f t_r}\right| = 2\left|\sin(\pi f t_r)\right|
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the magnitude of the original spectrum. The $(j2\pi f)^2$ factor in the denominator contributes a $1/f^2$ roll-off, but the numerator's $\sin(\pi f t_r)$ provides a numerator that grows linearly with $f$ for small $f t_r$:**

$$
\left| \sin(\pi f t_r) \right| \approx \pi f t_r \quad \text{for} \quad f t_r \ll 1
$$

**Step 6 — Combine. For $f t_r \ll 1$, the spectrum amplitude is approximately constant (flat). For $f t_r \gg 1$, the $\sin$ saturates near $\pm 1$, and the $1/f^2$ term dominates, giving a $1/f$ envelope:**

$$
|V(f)| \approx \frac{A}{2 \pi f} \cdot 2 = \frac{A}{\pi f} \quad \text{for} \quad f t_r \gg 1
$$

**Step 7 — The transition between flat-region and $1/f$-rolloff happens when $f t_r \approx 0.5$, giving the KTU knee frequency:**

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r}
$$

**Textual Logic:** We have shown that the spectrum of a trapezoidal pulse is **flat (white-noise-like)** up to $f_{knee}$ and then rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade (a $1/f$ envelope). This is why digital sources have predictable, predictable-spectra EMI signatures.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ground Bounce (SSN) Voltage on a Multi-Driver Bus

**Starting point:** $N$ CMOS outputs switch simultaneously from high to low. Each pin sources/sinks a current $I_{OL}$ through a non-zero power-rail inductance $L_{loop}$.

**Step 1 — Total current transition:**

$$
\Delta I_{total} = N \cdot I_{OL}
$$

**Step 2 — Time over which this transition occurs = the rise/fall time of the edges:**

$$
\Delta t = t_r \quad (\text{assuming all edges aligned})
$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of current:**

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{N \cdot I_{OL}}{t_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the fundamental inductor voltage equation $v = L \, \dfrac{di}{dt}$:**

$$
V_{noise} = L_{loop} \cdot \frac{N \cdot I_{OL}}{t_r}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute typical values for KTU problem-solving (e.g., 32-bit data bus, $I_{OL} = 24$ mA, $t_r = 1$ ns, $L_{loop} = 5$ nH):**

$$
V_{noise} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot \frac{32 \cdot 24 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 7.68 \times 10^{8} = 3.84 \text{ V}
$$

**Textual Logic:** A 3.84 V glitch on a 3.3 V rail is catastrophic — it would invert the logic level. This is why multi-driver buses need either controlled slew rate, fewer simultaneously switching outputs, or reduced package inductance (e.g., flip-chip BGA instead of QFP).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reflection at Source-Side Termination

**Setup:** A line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is driven by a source with output impedance $Z_{out}$ and a series termination resistor $R_s$. A step $V_s$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Initial forward wave launched into the line (before any reflection):**

$$
V_{forward} = V_s \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_{out} + R_s + Z_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient at the source (load = $Z_0$ seen from the line):**

$$
\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_{out} + R_s - Z_0}{Z_{out} + R_s + Z_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Reflection coefficient at the far end (open circuit, worst case):**

$$
\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = +1 \quad \text{(open)}
$$

**Step 4 — Round-trip voltage at the source after first reflection returns:**

$$
V_{received} = V_{forward} \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) \cdot \Gamma_S = 2 V_{forward} \cdot \Gamma_S
$$

**Step 5 — For **perfect source matching**, we require $Z_{out} + R_s = Z_0$, hence $\Gamma_S = 0$. The wave is fully absorbed at the source, no ringing. Hence KTU condition:**

$$
R_s = Z_0 - Z_{out}
$$

**Textual Logic:** This is why a **series-terminated driver** is preferred for point-to-point high-speed links (e.g., DDR address/command, SPI at high speed). The wave reflects once at the open far end, doubling to full swing at the load, and is *not* re-reflected because $\Gamma_S = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Signal Source Specification Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *specification pipeline* a designer goes through, from the abstract signal properties to the physical emission prediction:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Signal Source: Clock or Data] --> B[Time-Domain Characterization]
    B --> C[Rise Time, Fall Time, Period, Amplitude]
    C --> D[Knee Frequency Calculation]
    D --> E[Source Impedance Profile]
    E --> F[Spectral Domain Model]
    F --> G[Harmonics and Envelope]
    G --> H[Coupling Path Identification]
    H --> I[Radiated Emissions Prediction]
    H --> J[Conducted Emissions Prediction]
    I --> K[EMI Compliance Check]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Compliant?}
    L -- No --> M[Add Filtering or Slow Edge]
    M --> A
    L -- Yes --> N[Design Validated]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Source Impedance Matching Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Side]
        VDRV[Driver Z out] --> RS[Series R s]
    end
    RS --> TL[Transmission Line Z0]
    TL --> RL[Far End Load ZL]
    subgraph TERM[Termination Options]
        T1[Series Term: Rs = Z0 - Zout]
        T2[Parallel Term: R to VTT at Rx]
        T3[Differential Term: Rdiff = 100 ohm]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Source Categories in Modern Electronics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Signal Source Universe] --> IDL[Ideal: V or I with Z = 0 or Infinity]
    SRC --> LIN[Linear Drivers: Op-amp output, BJT/FET analog]
    SRC --> DIG[Digital Logic: CMOS TTL LVDS]
    SRC --> CLK[Clock Generators: XO VCXO PLL]
    SRC --> SENS[Sensor Transducers: Thermocouple Photodiode Antenna]
    DIG --> CMOS_FAM[CMOS Families: HC LVTTL LVCMOS GTLP]
    CLK --> CLK_CHAR[Characterized by: f0 Jitter Phase Noise]
    SENS --> SEN_OUT[High Z or Specific Source Impedance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `SRC`, `IDL`, `CMOS_FAM`) and labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italics inside quoted labels — to ensure successful Mermaid rendering.

## 4.4 Schematic Equivalents (As Textual Block)

| Source Type | Equivalent Circuit | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Practical Voltage Source | $V_s$ in series with $Z_{out}$ | $Z_{out}$ is $R_{DS,on}$ for CMOS, $r_o \parallel R_L$ for op-amp |
| Practical Current Source | $I_s$ in parallel with $Z_{out}$ | $Z_{out}$ is high (Mohm range) for ideal current source |
| Differential Driver | Two Thevenin sources: $V^+$ and $V^-$ with common-mode $V_{cm}$ | Used in LVDS, USB 2.0, HDMI |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.a]

**Define the term "knee frequency" of a digital signal and explain its significance in EMC analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **knee frequency** $f_{knee}$ of a digital signal is the frequency at which the spectral envelope of the signal transitions from a *flat* (white-noise-like) region to a *$-20$ dB/decade* (i.e., $1/f$) roll-off. It is given by:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r}
$$

where $t_r$ is the **10–90 % rise time** of the signal. *(1 mark for definition + formula)*

**Significance in EMC:** *(2 marks)*
1. It determines the **upper bound of the frequency content** that must be considered in SI and EMI analysis.
2. Conducted and radiated emissions tests (e.g., FCC, CISPR) require system behavior to be characterized up to at least $5 \cdot f_{knee}$ to capture the energy in the roll-off region.
3. Faster edges (smaller $t_r$) push $f_{knee}$ higher, increasing the difficulty of meeting EMC compliance.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q1.c]

**What is the difference between an ideal voltage source and a practical voltage source? How is this relevant to signal source specification in high-speed digital design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- An **ideal voltage source** maintains a fixed output voltage independent of the load current, modeled as $V_s$ in series with $Z_{out} = 0 \; \Omega$. *(1 mark)*
- A **practical voltage source** has a non-zero internal/output impedance $Z_{out} \neq 0 \; \Omega$, so the voltage at the load terminals changes with current draw. For a CMOS digital driver, $Z_{out}$ is approximately equal to the channel on-resistance $R_{DS,on}$ (typically 10–50 $\Omega$). *(1 mark)*
- **Relevance:** In high-speed design, $Z_{out}$ must be deliberately specified (and often a series termination $R_s$ added) so that the source matches the line impedance $Z_0$. Mismatch causes reflections, ringing, and EMI. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2.a Module 1 Internal Choice]

**A CMOS driver with output impedance $Z_{out} = 12 \; \Omega$ drives a 50 $\Omega$ microstrip trace. The driver switches a 3.3 V logic signal with rise time $t_r = 1.2$ ns.**

**(a)** Calculate the **knee frequency** and the **maximum frequency** component. Identify which frequency should be used for compliance testing against a CISPR 22 limit that begins at 1 GHz. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **source-series termination** to suppress reflections. Compute the value of the termination resistor. Also, compute the **peak ground-bounce voltage** if 16 outputs switch simultaneously with $I_{OL} = 16$ mA each, into a power-rail inductance of 4 nH. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Knee and Max Frequency

**Step 1 — Knee frequency using the KTU rule of thumb:**

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r} = \frac{0.5}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.17 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz} = 417 \text{ MHz}
$$

*(2 marks for correct formula and substitution)*

**Step 2 — Maximum frequency (more conservative):**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{t_r} = \frac{0.35}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.92 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz} = 292 \text{ MHz}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Selection for compliance:** Since the CISPR 22 limit is meaningful from 1 GHz onwards, and *both* $f_{knee} = 417$ MHz and $f_{max} = 292$ MHz are below 1 GHz, the test must include at least **3rd to 5th harmonic of $f_{knee}$**, i.e., we should design assuming emissions are significant up to $5 \cdot f_{knee} \approx 2.08$ GHz. For compliance, $f_{knee}$ is the correct choice because it marks the *start* of the $1/f$ roll-off. *(3 marks for engineering judgement)*

#### Part (b) — Source Termination and Ground Bounce

**Step 1 — Required source impedance for matching:**

$$
Z_{out} + R_s = Z_0 \implies R_s = 50 - 12 = 38 \; \Omega
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Use the closest standard E12 value: $R_s = 39 \; \Omega$** *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Ground-bounce voltage:**

$$
V_{noise} = L_{loop} \cdot \frac{N \cdot I_{OL}}{t_r} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \cdot \frac{16 \cdot 16 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
V_{noise} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \cdot \frac{256 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2.133 \times 10^{8} = 0.853 \text{ V}
$$

*(3 marks for correct formula and arithmetic)*

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** A 0.85 V noise spike on a 3.3 V rail represents a **~26 % supply disturbance** — significant, and likely to cause logic errors unless the package inductance is reduced (e.g., using an array with lower $L_{loop}$ or splitting the bus into banks). *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q2.b Module 1 Internal Choice]

**(a)** With a labeled spectrum diagram, explain the **spectral content of a periodic trapezoidal pulse train** and identify three parameters of the source specification that control its harmonic envelope. (7 marks)

**(b)** An **LVDS driver** has $V_{OD} = 350$ mV differential swing and $V_{cm} = 1.25$ V common-mode. Compute the **worst-case peak-to-peak output voltage** and the **emitted common-mode voltage range** that could drive a long cable acting as an antenna. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Spectrum of a Trapezoidal Pulse Train

A periodic train of trapezoidal pulses (period $T$, pulse width $\tau$, rise time $t_r$, fall time $t_f$) has a discrete line spectrum with amplitudes at harmonics $f_n = n/T$:

$$
V_n = \frac{A \cdot \tau}{T} \cdot \text{sinc}(n \tau / T) \cdot \text{sinc}(n t_r / T)
$$

The amplitude of the *envelope* is given by:

$$
\text{Envelope}(f) = A \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f t_r)}{\pi f} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}
$$

*(3 marks for correctly writing or sketching the spectrum expression)*

**Three source parameters controlling the envelope:**

1. **Rise time $t_r$** — controls the high-frequency roll-off. Smaller $t_r$ → spectrum extends further. *(1.3 marks)*
2. **Duty cycle $\delta = \tau/T$** — controls the location of the first spectral null at $f = 1/\tau$. Changing duty cycle shifts nulls and can be used to move energy away from compliance bands (spread-spectrum clocking). *(1.3 marks)*
3. **Amplitude $A$** — scales the entire spectrum linearly. Lower amplitude → lower emissions (used in LVPECL vs LVDS, for example). *(1.4 marks)*

#### Part (b) — LVDS Output Levels

**Step 1 — Differential output (peak-to-peak):**

$$
V_{diff,pp} = 2 \cdot V_{OD} = 2 \cdot 350 \text{ mV} = 700 \text{ mV}_{pp}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Common-mode range:**

The common-mode voltage is centered at $V_{cm} = 1.25$ V. The common-mode component is nominally constant, but small variations exist due to mismatch between the two halves of the driver. The **peak common-mode voltage** can swing approximately $\pm 25$ mV around the nominal (per TIA/EIA-644 spec). Hence:

$$
V_{cm,max} \approx 1.275 \text{ V}, \quad V_{cm,min} \approx 1.225 \text{ V}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Cable radiation impact:** Even though the differential signal cancels in a well-balanced twisted pair, any **common-mode voltage on a cable** radiates efficiently because it is the *common-mode* current that drives cable-as-antenna EMI. A 1.25 V common-mode potential at 100 MHz through a 1 m cable can violate CISPR 22 Class B at 3 m by 10–20 dB. Hence LVDS systems need **common-mode chokes** or **shielded cables**. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 0.5 in $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$.** Some texts use $f_{max} = 0.35/t_r$. Using the wrong constant can cost **2 of 7 marks** in a problem.
> 2. **Mixing rise time and fall time definitions** (e.g., using 20–80 % instead of 10–90 %). KTU follows the 10–90 % convention; clarify in your answer.
> 3. **Dropping the units in SSN calculations.** $L$ in henries, $di/dt$ in A/s — show the unit cancellation explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing $V_{OD}$ (differential) and $V_{cm}$ (common-mode).** Many students write $V_{OD} = V_{cm}$, losing **full 3 marks** in any LVDS question.
> 5. **Forgetting to state engineering judgement** — KTU values *interpretation* marks (1–2 per sub-question). Always conclude with a sentence on real-world impact.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Knee frequency formula:** $f_{knee} = \dfrac{0.5}{t_r}$ — *the* most-tested equation in Module 1.
- [ ] **Conservative bound:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{0.35}{t_r}$ — used for *worst-case* compliance analysis.
- [ ] **Electrically long trace criterion:** $L > \lambda/6$ where $\lambda = c/f_{knee}$.
- [ ] **Source matching condition (series termination):** $R_s = Z_0 - Z_{out}$.
- [ ] **Ground-bounce formula:** $V_{noise} = L_{loop} \cdot N \cdot I_{OL} / t_r$.
- [ ] **Spectrum of trapezoidal pulse:** flat up to $f_{knee}$, then $1/f$ roll-off.
- [ ] **Differential vs Common-mode:** $V_{OD} = V^+ - V^-$; $V_{cm} = (V^+ + V^-)/2$ — *common-mode radiates*.
- [ ] **Jitter types:** Period jitter, cycle-to-cycle jitter, phase noise $\mathcal{L}(f)$.
- [ ] **Spread-spectrum clocking:** Modulates $f_0$ to spread harmonics, reducing peak EMI.
- [ ] **Drive strength ↔ EMI:** Stronger driver = faster $t_r$ = higher $f_{knee}$ = *worse* EMI.
- [ ] **Termination choices:** Series (point-to-point), Parallel (multi-drop), Differential (100 $\Omega$ for LVDS).
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ — $+\!1$ for open, $-1$ for short, $0$ for matched.
- [ ] **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [ ] **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 = 377 \; \Omega$ — relevant for radiated emissions and antenna theory.
- [ ] **Key KTU exam mantra:** "Spec the source first — then everything else (channel, coupling, radiation) follows."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of EMC Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages of EMC Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
