# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Far-Field Electric Field from Radiated Power

The power density at distance R from a transmit antenna with gain G_t and input power P_t is, from the **Friis transmission equation** (which itself derives from the isotropic radiator power density 4πR²):

$$
S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi R^2} \quad \left[ \text{W/m}^2 \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Relation between Power Density and Electric Field:**
In the far-field, the power density of a plane wave relates to the E-field by:

$$
S = \frac{\vert E \vert^2}{\eta_0} = \frac{\vert E \vert^2}{120 \pi}
$$

This is the **Poynting vector magnitude** for a plane wave in free space.

**Step 2 — Equate and Solve for E:**
Equating the two expressions for S:

$$
\frac{\vert E \vert^2}{120 \pi} = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by 120π:**

$$
\vert E \vert^2 = 120 \pi \cdot \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{30 \, P_t \, G_t}{R^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$
\vert E \vert = \frac{\sqrt{30 \, P_t \, G_t}}{R} \quad \left[ \text{V/m} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** This is the formula used to **extrapolate a measured field to a different distance** (e.g., from 3 m to 10 m), assuming far-field conditions: R > 2D²/λ, where D is the largest dimension of the EUT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Antenna Factor in dB(m⁻¹)

**Step 1 — Definition:** Antenna factor (AF) is the ratio of the incident E-field to the voltage induced at the antenna terminals (across 50 Ω):

$$
AF = \frac{E}{V_R} \quad \text{[(V/m)/V] or [m}^{-1}\text{]}
$$

**Step 2 — In dB:** Since both numerator and denominator are field quantities, we use the 20·log convention:

$$
AF \; [\text{dB/m}] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E}{V_R}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for E in dBμV/m:**

$$
E \; [\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}] = V_R \; [\text{dB}\mu\text{V}] + AF \; [\text{dB/m}]
$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical application:**
Suppose a spectrum analyzer reads **V_R = 40 dBμV** at 100 MHz, and the antenna factor at 100 MHz is **AF = 8.5 dB/m**, with cable loss **L_c = 2.1 dB**. Then:

$$
E = 40 + 8.5 + 2.1 = 50.6 \; \text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

Converting back to linear: 
$$
E = 10^{50.6/20} \approx 339 \; \mu\text{V/m} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{V/m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why cable loss is added (not subtracted):** A real cable attenuates the signal *before* the receiver sees it. To recover the true antenna terminal voltage, we *add back* the loss. (Equivalently, calibrate the system with a tracking generator and add the loss term.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quasi-Peak Detector Output for a Periodic Pulse Train

This is a **key KTU derivation** because it directly appears in CISPR 16-1-1.

**Step 1 — The Detector Circuit:** The QP detector is a charged capacitor with a charge time constant τ_c and discharge time constant τ_d. The output voltage V_out approaches a steady value V_QP.

**Step 2 — The Periodic Pulse Case:** For a pulse train of amplitude V_p, pulse width t_p, and pulse repetition interval T, the capacitor charges to V_p during the pulse and discharges between pulses. The steady-state quasi-peak output is:

$$
V_{QP} = V_p \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-t_p/\tau_c}}{1 - e^{-T/\tau_d}}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximations for CISPR-Standard Values:** With τ_c = 1 ms, τ_d = 160 ms, and a narrow pulse t_p ≪ τ_c:

$$
1 - e^{-t_p/\tau_c} \approx \frac{t_p}{\tau_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting:**

$$
V_{QP} \approx V_p \cdot \frac{t_p/\tau_c}{1 - e^{-T/\tau_d}}
$$

**Step 5 — For low duty cycle (T ≫ τ_d), the denominator ≈ 1:**

$$
V_{QP} \approx V_p \cdot \frac{t_p}{\tau_c} = V_p \cdot \frac{t_p}{1\,\text{ms}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** QP is essentially a **narrowband energy estimator**. A single impulsive spike (t_p = 1 μs) yields V_QP ≈ 0.001 V_p — a 60 dB reduction. But a continuous wave signal (t_p ≈ T) yields V_QP ≈ V_p. This is why QP strongly penalizes continuous interference while allowing transient noise to pass.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: CISPR 22/32 Class A vs Class B Limits

| Frequency (MHz) | Class A Quasi-Peak (dBμV/m) @ 10 m | Class B Quasi-Peak (dBμV/m) @ 10 m | Δ (dB) |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| 30 – 88         | 39                                   | 30                                   | 9       |
| 88 – 216        | 43.5                                 | 35.5                                 | 8       |
| 216 – 960       | 46.5                                 | 36                                   | 10.5    |
| > 960           | 49.5                                 | 40                                   | 9.5     |

> [!WARNING]
> **Class A** = industrial/commercial. **Class B** = residential. Class B is *stricter* (lower limits), reflecting the closer proximity to sensitive broadcast receivers in homes. Mobile phones, laptops, TVs → Class B. Server rooms, factory equipment → Class A.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem: Distance Extrapolation

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** An EUT is measured at 3 m and produces a peak field strength of **62 dBμV/m** at 200 MHz. What is the field strength at 10 m, assuming far-field conditions?

**Step 1 — Use the inverse-distance law in dB:**

$$
E_2 \; [\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}] = E_1 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute d_1 = 3 m, d_2 = 10 m:**

$$
\Delta = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{10}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.3)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
\log_{10}(0.3) = -0.5229 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20 \times (-0.5229) = -10.46 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the correction:**

$$
E_{10\text{m}} = 62 - 10.46 = 51.54 \; \text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify far-field assumption:**
For the largest EUT dimension D = 0.3 m and λ = c/f = 3×10⁸/200×10⁶ = 1.5 m:

$$
R_{\text{far-field}} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 0.09}{1.5} = 0.12 \;\text{m}
$$

Since R = 3 m ≫ 0.12 m, the far-field assumption is **valid**. ✓

**Figure 4.1 — Conducted Emissions Block Architecture**

> [!NOTE]
> The **LISN** sits *inline* between the AC mains and the EUT, and its RF output port is connected to the spectrum analyzer. This way, the EUT sees a stable 50 Ω impedance, but the analyzer can "listen in" on the conducted noise. The ground plane (per CISPR 16-1-2) provides a clean RF return path.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Conducted Emissions and Radiated Emissions. State the standard CISPR frequency bands associated with each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Conducted Emissions (CE):** Unwanted RF energy generated by an electronic device and propagated along its power lines, signal cables, or interconnect conductors. The standard measurement band is **9 kHz to 30 MHz**, as per **CISPR 16-1-2** and **CISPR 22 / CISPR 32**.

**Radiated Emissions (RE):** Unwanted electromagnetic energy radiated into free space from the device itself or from attached cables acting as antennas. The standard measurement band is **30 MHz to 1 GHz** (extended to 6 GHz, 18 GHz, or 40 GHz for modern wireless products), per **CISPR 16-2-3** and **CISPR 32**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key contrast:** CE propagates through *conductors*; RE propagates through *free space*. The 30 MHz boundary is empirical — above 30 MHz, cable lengths become comparable to λ/4, making them efficient radiators.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a Line Impedance Stabilization Network (LISN)? State its standard impedance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Line Impedance Stabilization Network (LISN)**, also called an **Artificial Mains Network (AMN)** in CISPR terminology, is a passive L-C-R network inserted *in series* with the AC or DC power line feeding the EUT. Its functions are:

1. To present a **standardized, stable RF impedance** (typically **50 Ω ∥ 50 μH** to ground) to the EUT across the measurement band (150 kHz – 30 MHz), independent of the source impedance of the AC mains.
2. To **block external RF noise** present on the AC mains from contaminating the measurement.
3. To provide a **50 Ω RF output port** to which the EMI receiver is connected, so it can measure the conducted noise voltage injected by the EUT.

**Standard Impedance:** 50 Ω resistive ∥ (50 μH + 5 Ω) to ground, per **CISPR 16-1-2 § 4**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module typically has a 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **conducted emissions measurement setup** using a LISN. Define the role of the quasi-peak detector and state the CISPR 16-1-1 time constants.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EUT produces a peak field strength of **62 dBμV/m at 3 m**. Calculate the field strength at **10 m** and **30 m** distances, assuming far-field conditions. Verify the far-field assumption for an EUT of maximum dimension **D = 0.5 m** at frequency **200 MHz**.

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Conducted Emissions Setup & QP Detector

**Block Diagram (describe verbally — refer to Figure 4.1):**

```
AC Mains → LISN (50 Ω ∥ 50 μH) → EUT
              ↓ (RF noise, 50 Ω)
          Spectrum Analyzer / EMI Receiver
              ↓
          Detector (Peak → QP for final)
              ↓
          Compare with CISPR 22/32 limit
```

**Step-by-step Explanation (4 marks for diagram + explanation):**

1. **AC Mains** is connected through the **LISN** to the EUT. The LISN ensures that the EUT sees a **standard 50 Ω impedance to ground** at RF frequencies, while passing 50 Hz/60 Hz power unimpeded.
2. The **RF port of the LISN** is connected to the input of the **EMI test receiver** (input impedance 50 Ω). The receiver measures the RF voltage that the EUT pushes back into the power line.
3. The receiver uses a **resolution bandwidth (RBW)** of **9 kHz** (CISPR Band B, 150 kHz – 30 MHz) or **200 Hz** (Band A, 9 – 150 kHz).
4. A **Peak detector** first does a fast pre-scan; suspect frequencies are re-measured with the **Quasi-Peak detector** for the official result.

**Quasi-Peak Detector (3 marks):**

- The **QP detector** is a weighted detector whose output depends not only on the **amplitude** of the disturbance but also on its **pulse repetition frequency (PRF)**.
- **Charge time constant τ_c = 1 ms**
- **Discharge time constant τ_d = 160 ms** (or 550 ms in some bands)
- The slow discharge (τ_d ≫ τ_c) means a **rare impulsive spike** barely charges the capacitor (low QP reading, forgiven), while a **continuous-wave signal** keeps the capacitor near full charge (high QP reading, penalized).
- This behavior mimics the annoyance a human listener experiences — hence the name "quasi-peak" (similar to ITU-R weighted psophometric measurements).

---

##### Part (b) — Distance Extrapolation

**Given:**
- E₁ = 62 dBμV/m at d₁ = 3 m
- Frequency f = 200 MHz
- Maximum EUT dimension D = 0.5 m

**Step 1 — Calculate wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Far-field boundary:**

$$
R_{\text{ff}} = \frac{2 D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (0.5)^2}{1.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333 \;\text{m}
$$

Since 3 m, 10 m, 30 m ≫ 0.333 m, **far-field is valid**. ✓

**Step 3 — Field at 10 m:**

$$
E_{10} = E_3 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right) = 62 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{10}\right)
$$

$$
= 62 + 20 \times (-0.5229) = 62 - 10.46 = 51.54 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Field at 30 m:**

$$
E_{30} = 62 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{30}\right) = 62 + 20 \times (-1.0) = 62 - 20 = 42.0 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **E(10 m) = 51.54 dBμV/m**
- **E(30 m) = 42.0 dBμV/m**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Dec 2023 pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **radiated emissions measurement setup on an Open Area Test Site (OATS)**. List the requirements of an OATS as per CISPR 16-1-4.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A spectrum analyzer connected to a biconical antenna at 10 m distance reads **38 dBμV** at 80 MHz. The antenna factor at 80 MHz is **12 dB/m** and the cable loss is **1.5 dB**. Calculate the **electric field strength (E)** in dBμV/m and in V/m. Is this compliant with **CISPR 32 Class B** at 80 MHz (limit ≈ 30 dBμV/m @ 10 m)?

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — OATS Block Diagram & Requirements

**Block Diagram (describe verbally — refer to Figure 4.2):**

```
            EUT (on turntable, 80 cm)
                    ↓ EM field
            Receiving Antenna (3 m or 10 m away, H & V pol.)
                    ↓ coaxial cable (calibrated loss)
            EMI Receiver / Spectrum Analyzer
                    ↓
            Detector (Peak → QP)
                    ↓
            Field strength (dBμV/m)
```

**OATS Requirements as per CISPR 16-1-4 / ANSI C63.4 (4 marks):**

1. **Flat, open, level ground** free of any reflectors within an elliptical area defined by the major axis 2d (where d = antenna-EUT separation) and minor axis √3·d.
2. **Conductive ground plane** with sheet resistance ≤ **0.1 Ω/sq** (typically galvanized steel or copper).
3. No overhead wires, trees, buildings, or people within the clearance zone.
4. **NSA validation** must show agreement with theoretical value within **±4 dB** at every frequency.
5. **Antenna mast** that can scan from 1 m to 4 m height (for H-pol) and 1 m to 2 m (for V-pol).
6. **Turntable** with 0°–360° rotation; accuracy ±1°.
7. **Ambient noise** below the limit by at least **6 dB**.

**Measurement Procedure (3 marks):**

- Set EUT in typical operational mode.
- For each frequency, scan both antenna polarizations (H and V).
- Vary antenna height (1 m to 4 m for H) to capture ground-bounce nulls.
- Rotate turntable to find the **maximum emission** direction.
- The **worst-case (max of H/V, max of height, max of angle)** is the official result.

---

##### Part (b) — Field Strength Calculation

**Given:**
- V_R = 38 dBμV
- AF = 12 dB/m
- Cable loss L_c = 1.5 dB
- Distance = 10 m
- Frequency = 80 MHz
- CISPR 32 Class B limit at 80 MHz = 30 dBμV/m

**Step 1 — Field strength in dBμV/m:**

$$
E = V_R + AF + L_c = 38 + 12 + 1.5 = 51.5 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to V/m:**

$$
E \; [\text{V/m}] = 10^{(E_{dB} - 120)/20} = 10^{(51.5 - 120)/20} = 10^{-68.5/20} = 10^{-3.425}
$$

$$
E \approx 3.76 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V/m} = 376 \;\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check:**

$$
E_{\text{measured}} = 51.5 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Limit} = 30 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

$$
\Delta = 51.5 - 30 = 21.5 \;\text{dB above limit}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NOT COMPLIANT.** The EUT exceeds the CISPR 32 Class B limit by **21.5 dB** (a factor of **12 in voltage**). The EUT requires redesign — likely adding **input filtering, ferrite chokes, and proper shielding of cables**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in dB arithmetic** — When adding dB values, *all must be dB quantities*. Mixing linear V with dB dBμV costs **2 marks easily**.
> 2. **Sign error in distance extrapolation** — The field *decreases* with distance; the correction is **−20·log(d₂/d₁)**. Students often write + instead of −, yielding absurdly large fields at long distances.
> 3. **Forgetting the cable loss term** — Real test setups have **1–5 dB cable loss** that must be added back. Omitting this gives an *understated* field and a false compliance result.
> 4. **Confusing the AF sign** — Antenna factor is **added** (it has positive dB/m because E is larger than V_R for typical antennas).
> 5. **Wrong RBW** — CISPR 22/32 use **9 kHz** for 150 kHz–30 MHz, **120 kHz** for 30 MHz–1 GHz. Using a 100 kHz RBW (a common analyzer default) is incorrect and gives non-compliant results.
> 6. **Not verifying far-field** — Always check **R > 2D²/λ** before using inverse-distance extrapolation. Below this range, **near-field reactive components** dominate, and 20·log scaling is invalid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Measurement of Radiated and Conducted Emissions**

- ✅ **Conducted Emissions (CE):** Measured on power/signal lines, **9 kHz – 30 MHz**, via a **LISN (50 Ω ∥ 50 μH)**.
- ✅ **Radiated Emissions (RE):** Measured in free space, **30 MHz – 1 GHz** (extending to 40 GHz for 5G/Wi-Fi), via calibrated **antennas** (biconical, LPDA, horn).
- ✅ **LISN** standardizes the line impedance; presents **50 Ω ∥ 50 μH** to the EUT.
- ✅ **EMI Receiver** detectors: **Peak (fast), Quasi-Peak (charge 1 ms / discharge 160 ms), Average, RMS**.
- ✅ **QP detector time constants:** τ_c = 1 ms, τ_d = 160 ms (CISPR 16-1-1).
- ✅ **Resolution Bandwidths:** 200 Hz (9–150 kHz), **9 kHz (150 kHz–30 MHz), 120 kHz (30 MHz–1 GHz)**.
- ✅ **OATS** is the gold standard open-area test site; **SAC** is the indoor equivalent; **GTEM** is the compact pre-compliance cell.
- ✅ **NSA test** validates any test site; theoretical agreement within **±4 dB** is required.
- ✅ **Field strength formula:** E[dBμV/m] = V_R[dBμV] + AF[dB/m] + Cable Loss[dB].
- ✅ **Distance extrapolation:** E(d₂) = E(d₁) − 20·log₁₀(d₂/d₁). 3 m → 10 m is **−10.46 dB**; 3 m → 30 m is **−20 dB**.
- ✅ **Far-field condition:** R > 2D²/λ, where D = max EUT dimension, λ = c/f.
- ✅ **Class A vs Class B:** Class B (residential) is **8–10 dB stricter** than Class A (industrial).
- ✅ **Friis equation:** P_r = P_t·G_t·G_r·λ²/[(4π)²·R²].
- ✅ **Free-space impedance:** η₀ = **377 Ω** = **120π Ω**.
- ✅ **Voltage-to-field (far-field):** E = √(30·P_t·G_t) / R.
- ✅ **Cables are antennas:** Any common-mode current on a cable of length ≈ λ/4 radiates efficiently above 30 MHz — a key reason CE mitigation (filters, ferrites) is the *first* line of defense in EMC design.
- ✅ **CISPR 16-1-1** is the master standard for measurement apparatus; **CISPR 16-2-3** for radiated methods; **CISPR 32** for multimedia equipment limits.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1 / Topic: Measurement of Radiated and Conducted Emissions**

topics.title: Transmission Line Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Line Equations (Telegrapher's Equations):** A coupled pair of first-order partial differential equations that describe the propagation of voltage $V(x,t)$ and current $I(x,t)$ waves along an electrically long, distributed-parameter transmission line. They are derived from the lumped-element equivalent circuit model containing series impedance ($R$, $L$) per unit length and shunt admittance ($G$, $C$) per unit length.

In high-speed digital design and EMC engineering, a conductor is treated as a *transmission line* when its physical length is comparable to (or greater than) one-tenth of the wavelength ($\lambda/10$) of the highest-frequency spectral component of the signal propagating through it. Under this condition, the signal is no longer assumed to be at the same potential everywhere instantaneously — the wave nature of the signal becomes dominant.

The four fundamental per-unit-length (p.u.l.) parameters governing transmission line behavior are:

- **$R$** — Series resistance per unit length (in **$\Omega/\text{m}$**), representing conductor losses (skin effect).
- **$L$** — Series inductance per unit length (in **$\text{H/m}$**), representing the magnetic field surrounding the conductor.
- **$G$** — Shunt conductance per unit length (in **$\text{S/m}$**), representing dielectric losses in the insulating medium.
- **$C$** — Shunt capacitance per unit length (in **$\text{F/m}$**), representing the electric field between the conductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A signal at $f = 1\,\text{GHz}$ in FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4.3$) has $\lambda \approx 14.5\,\text{cm}$. Therefore, even a PCB trace of just $\mathbf{1.5\,cm}$ becomes an electrically long transmission line — a critical fact for SI/EMC engineers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Long Garden Hose with a Sponge Wall":**
> Imagine pushing water into a long, thin garden hose. The hose is filled with a soft sponge (representing the line's distributed capacitance and inductance). When you push:
> - The water near your hand moves **first**.
> - The water at the far end moves **a moment later**.
> - The sponge stretches slightly (energy storage in **$L$**).
> - Some water soaks into the sponge walls (energy loss in **$G$**).
> - The hose itself offers friction (energy loss in **$R$**).
>
> Just as the hose does not respond instantaneously at the far end, neither does voltage/current at the load end of a real transmission line. The **Telegrapher's Equations** are the mathematical equivalent of describing how this "pressure-wave" (voltage) and "flow-wave" (current) propagate, interact, and dissipate along the hose.

**Another intuition — "Train of Dominoes":**
Think of a transmission line as a chain of tiny LC tank circuits. A disturbance at one end does not jump instantly to the other; instead, it triggers each successive domino, propagating as a wave at a finite velocity (close to the speed of light in vacuum).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\text{H/m}$ |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$ |
| $Z_{0,\text{free}}$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\mathbf{376.73\,\Omega} \approx 377\,\Omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distributed Parameter Model — Conceptual Breakdown

A real transmission line is decomposed into an infinite cascade of infinitesimal segments, each of length $\Delta x \to 0$. Each segment contains:

- **Series branch** with resistance $R\,\Delta x$ and inductance $L\,\Delta x$ (combined series impedance $Z\,\Delta x$, where $Z = R + j\omega L$).
- **Shunt branch** with conductance $G\,\Delta x$ and capacitance $C\,\Delta x$ (combined shunt admittance $Y\,\Delta x$, where $Y = G + j\omega C$).

The Telegrapher's Equations emerge directly from applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to this distributed network.

## 2.2 Why These Equations Matter — The Engineering "How" and "Why"

| Why does this concept exist? | Because at high frequencies, lumped circuit theory fails — voltages and currents become spatial functions. |
|---|---|
| **How is it used in practice?** | PCB trace design, high-speed serial links (PCIe, USB 3.x, HDMI), RF cable design, antenna feed lines, and EMC compliance testing. |
| **What happens if we ignore it?** | Reflections, ringing, crosstalk, EMI, signal integrity failures, and timing errors — common KTU lab/project pitfalls. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving transmission line problems in KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula / Definition | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series impedance per unit length | $Z = R + j\omega L$ | $\Omega/\text{m}$ |
| 2 | Shunt admittance per unit length | $Y = G + j\omega C$ | $\text{S/m}$ |
| 3 | Telegrapher's equation (voltage) | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -Z \cdot I = -(R+j\omega L)\,I$ | First-order PDE |
| 4 | Telegrapher's equation (current) | $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -Y \cdot V = -(G+j\omega C)\,V$ | First-order PDE |
| 5 | Wave equation (voltage) | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\, V$ | $\gamma$ = propagation constant |
| 6 | Wave equation (current) | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} I}{\partial x^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\, I$ | Same $\gamma$ as above |
| 7 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{Z\,Y} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Attenuation constant | $\alpha$ (real part of $\gamma$) | $\text{Np/m}$ |
| 9 | Phase constant | $\beta$ (imaginary part of $\gamma$) | $\text{rad/m}$ |
| 10 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | Phase velocity | $v_{p} = \dfrac{\omega}{\beta}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 12 | General solution (V) | $V(x) = V^{+}\, e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\, e^{+\gamma x}$ | Forward + reverse wave |
| 13 | General solution (I) | $I(x) = \dfrac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\, e^{-\gamma x} - \dfrac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\, e^{+\gamma x}$ | Current waves |
| 14 | Reflection coefficient (load) | $\Gamma_{L} = \dfrac{V^{-}}{V^{+}}\big\vert_{\text{load}} = \dfrac{Z_{L} - Z_{0}}{Z_{L} + Z_{0}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{v_{p}}{f}$ | $\text{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Tables:** When writing absolute-value expressions in any markdown table cell, **NEVER** use the bare vertical pipe `\vert x\vert` (it will break table parsing). Use the LaTeX command `\vert x\vert` (rendered as $\vert x\vert$) as a substitute. The model answer key consistently applies this convention.

## 2.4 Lossless Line Special Case (KTU Favorite)

When $R \to 0$ and $G \to 0$ (an excellent approximation for short PCB traces at moderate frequencies):

$$
Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}, \qquad \gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}, \qquad v_{p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

The propagation constant becomes purely imaginary ($\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$), meaning **no attenuation** — only phase shift along the line.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Design:** $Z_{0}$ is matched to driver/receiver impedance (typically $50\,\Omega$ for RF, $90\,\Omega$ for USB differential pairs) to prevent reflections.
- **EMC Compliance:** Mismatched lines radiate EMI; matched lines preserve signal integrity and reduce conducted/radiated emissions.
- **Telecommunications:** Coaxial cables, twisted pairs, and waveguides all rely on these equations.
- **High-Speed Computing:** Server backplanes, SerDes channels, and memory buses (DDR5) are designed using telegrapher-equation analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Telegrapher's Equations (KTU Board Favorite)

### Step 1 — Draw the Equivalent Circuit of an Infinitesimal Segment

Consider a small section of transmission line of length $\Delta x$, located at position $x$ from the source. The lumped-element model consists of:
- A series resistor $R\Delta x$ and series inductor $L\Delta x$ along the top conductor.
- A shunt resistor $(1/G)\Delta x$ and shunt capacitor $C\Delta x$ between the two conductors.

### Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)

The voltage drop across the series elements between the input node ($x$) and output node ($x+\Delta x$) equals the difference between the input and output voltages:

$$
V(x,t) - V(x+\Delta x, t) = R\,\Delta x \cdot I(x,t) + L\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V(x+\Delta x, t) - V(x,t) = -R\,\Delta x \cdot I(x,t) - L\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the Limit as $\Delta x \to 0$

Divide both sides by $\Delta x$ and take the limit:

$$
\lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{V(x+\Delta x, t) - V(x,t)}{\Delta x} = -R \cdot I(x,t) - L \cdot \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}
$$

The left-hand side is, by definition, the partial derivative of $V$ with respect to $x$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial x} = -R\, I(x,t) - L\, \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Phasor / Time-Harmonic Form

Assuming time-harmonic excitation of the form $e^{j\omega t}$, the time derivative becomes $\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \to j\omega$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{\partial V(x)}{\partial x} = -(R + j\omega L)\, I(x) = -Z\, I(x)
$$

This is the **first Telegrapher's equation (voltage form)**. $\blacksquare$

### Step 5 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)

The current entering node $x+\Delta x$ minus the current leaving node $x+\Delta x$ equals the current lost through the shunt branch:

$$
I(x,t) - I(x+\Delta x, t) = G\,\Delta x \cdot V(x+\Delta x, t) + C\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial V(x+\Delta x, t)}{\partial t}
$$

### Step 6 — Take the Limit and Convert to Phasor Form

Dividing by $\Delta x$ and letting $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial x} = -G\, V(x,t) - C\, \frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial t}
$$

In phasor form:

$$
\frac{\partial I(x)}{\partial x} = -(G + j\omega C)\, V(x) = -Y\, V(x)
$$

This is the **second Telegrapher's equation (current form)**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Wave Equation (Voltage Form)

### Step 1 — Differentiate the Voltage Telegrapher's Equation with Respect to $x$

Starting from:

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -Z\, I
$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} = -Z\, \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Current Telegrapher's Equation

Replace $\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}$ using the second equation, $\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -Y\, V$:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} = -Z \cdot (-Y\, V) = Z\, Y\, V
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Propagation Constant

Define $\gamma = \sqrt{Z\, Y}$, so that $\gamma^{2} = Z\, Y$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\, V
$$

This is the **one-dimensional wave equation for voltage** along the transmission line. $\blacksquare$

### Step 4 — General Solution of the Wave Equation

The general solution of $\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2}V$ is a linear combination of two exponentials:

$$
V(x) = V^{+}\, e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\, e^{+\gamma x}
$$

- $V^{+}\, e^{-\gamma x}$ represents the **forward-traveling (incident) wave**.
- $V^{-}\, e^{+\gamma x}$ represents the **reverse-traveling (reflected) wave**.

The corresponding current solution is:

$$
I(x) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\, e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\, e^{+\gamma x}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Parameter to a Physical Meaning

| Parameter | Mathematical Role | Physical Meaning | Typical Order (PCB Trace) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Loss term in series impedance | Conductor heating due to finite conductivity & skin effect | $10^{-2}\,\Omega/\text{m}$ |
| $L$ | Energy storage (magnetic field) | Opposes rapid current changes | $0.3 - 1\,\mu\text{H/m}$ |
| $G$ | Loss term in shunt admittance | Dielectric leakage & absorption | $10^{-3}\,\text{S/m}$ |
| $C$ | Energy storage (electric field) | Couples voltage between conductors | $50 - 150\,\text{pF/m}$ |
| $\gamma$ | Eigenvalue of wave equation | Quantifies attenuation + phase shift per meter | $\approx j\,30\,\text{rad/m}$ at 1 GHz |
| $Z_{0}$ | Ratio of voltage to current in a single wave | "Natural" impedance of the line — sets matching requirement | $50\,\Omega$, $75\,\Omega$, $90\,\Omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distributed Lumped-Element Model of a Transmission Line

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the cascade of infinitesimal RLCG segments used to mathematically model a transmission line. Each segment is $\Delta x$ long.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Source V at x equals 0] --> seg1
    seg1[Segment 1: R dx, L dx] --> n1
    n1[Node at x equals dx] --> seg2
    seg2[Segment 2: R dx, L dx] --> n2
    n2[Node at x equals 2 dx] --> seg3
    seg3[Segment N: R dx, L dx] --> n3
    n3[Node at x equals L total] --> load[Load ZL]
    n1 -. shunt 1 .-> g1[G dx, C dx]
    n2 -. shunt 2 .-> g2[G dx, C dx]
    n3 -. shunt N .-> gN[G dx, C dx]
    g1 --- gndA[Ground reference]
    g2 --- gndA
    gN --- gndA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Every horizontal arrow represents the series impedance path ($R + j\omega L$), while every dashed diagonal arrow represents the shunt admittance path ($G + j\omega C$) to ground. As the number of segments $N \to \infty$ and $\Delta x \to 0$, this discrete ladder network becomes a faithful continuous model of the transmission line.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wave Propagation Flow

The diagram below maps the signal flow from source to load, identifying every functional block governed by the transmission line equations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Signal Source Function Generator] --> drv[Driver Buffer Output Impedance Zs]
    drv --> tline[Transmission Line of length L with Z0 and gamma]
    tline -->|Forward wave V plus e to power of minus gamma x| junction
    junction{Junction at Load End}
    junction -->|Reflected wave V minus e to power of plus gamma x| tline
    junction --> match{Matched or Mismatched?}
    match -->|Z sub L equals Z0| absorbed[Total Absorption Gamma sub L equals 0]
    match -->|Z sub L differs from Z0| reflect[Partial Reflection Gamma sub L not equal 0]
    reflect --> vmeas[VSWR Measurement]
    vmeas --> emc[Potential EMI Radiation and Standing Waves]
    absorbed --> load[Clean Power Delivery to Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Flow Reading Guide:**
> 1. The source injects a forward-propagating voltage wave into the line.
> 2. The wave attenuates and phase-shifts according to $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ as it travels.
> 3. At the load, the **boundary condition** at $Z_L$ produces a reflected wave of amplitude $\Gamma_{L} = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$.
> 4. The reflected wave travels back, potentially reflecting again at the source — producing standing waves if $\Gamma_L \neq 0$.
> 5. Mismatches degrade signal integrity and increase radiated emissions — the central concern of EMC.

## 4.3 Equivalent Phasor-Domain Circuit

For sinusoidal steady-state analysis (the dominant KTU exam assumption), the time derivatives collapse into factors of $j\omega$, transforming the time-domain RLCG model into a frequency-domain ZY model:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vi[V at x] --> Zs[Z equals R plus j omega L per dx]
    Zs --> Vo[V at x plus dx]
    Vo --> Ys[Y equals G plus j omega C per dx]
    Ys --> gnd[Ground]
```

This is the canonical building block from which the Telegrapher's equations are derived in the phasor domain — directly linking the conceptual circuit to the PDEs of Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of the complex voltage to the complex current of a single traveling wave propagating along the line in either direction:

$$
Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}
$$

For a lossless line ($R = G = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$
Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

**Physical Significance (3 key points):**
1. It represents the **intrinsic impedance** that the line naturally presents to any traveling wave.
2. When the load impedance $Z_L$ equals $Z_0$, there is **no reflection** at the load — maximum power transfer occurs.
3. It is independent of line length; it depends only on the geometry and material of the line (e.g., $50\,\Omega$ for typical coaxial cables, $100\,\Omega$ differential pairs, $377\,\Omega$ for free-space propagation).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Distinguish between the propagation constant and the characteristic impedance. State their units.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Propagation Constant $\gamma$ | Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z/Y} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ |
| **Physical Meaning** | Quantifies attenuation + phase shift per unit length as the wave travels | Ratio of V to I for a single traveling wave |
| **Complex Form** | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | Purely real only for lossless line |
| **Units** | $\text{m}^{-1}$ (Np/m for $\alpha$, rad/m for $\beta$) | $\Omega$ (Ohms) |
| **Dependency** | Depends on line losses and frequency | Depends on geometry ($L$, $C$) and frequency |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Telegrapher's equations for a transmission line, starting from the distributed lumped-element model.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup the Equivalent Circuit:**
Consider an infinitesimal segment of length $\Delta x$ at position $x$. It contains a series impedance $Z\,\Delta x = (R + j\omega L)\,\Delta x$ and a shunt admittance $Y\,\Delta x = (G + j\omega C)\,\Delta x$ to ground.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$
V(x) - V(x + \Delta x) = Z\,\Delta x \cdot I(x)
$$

Dividing by $\Delta x$ and taking $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
- \frac{dV}{dx} = Z\,I \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dV}{dx} = -Z\,I = -(R+j\omega L)\,I
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the output node:**

$$
I(x) - I(x + \Delta x) = Y\,\Delta x \cdot V(x + \Delta x)
$$

Dividing by $\Delta x$ and letting $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
- \frac{dI}{dx} = Y\,V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dI}{dx} = -Y\,V = -(G+j\omega C)\,V
$$

These are the **two Telegrapher's equations**. $\blacksquare$

**Step 4 — Final Written Form for the Answer Sheet:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I \qquad \text{and} \qquad \frac{dI}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V\;}
$$

**Mark Distribution Hint (for KTU valuation):**
- 2 marks: Correct circuit model diagram
- 3 marks: KVL derivation and simplification
- 2 marks: KCL derivation and final boxed equations

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A lossless transmission line has $L = 250\,\text{nH/m}$ and $C = 100\,\text{pF/m}$. Calculate (i) the characteristic impedance $Z_0$, (ii) the phase velocity $v_p$, and (iii) the propagation constant $\gamma$ at $f = 500\,\text{MHz}$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 0$, $G = 0$, $L = 250 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{H/m}$, $C = 100 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$, $f = 500 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = \pi \times 10^{9}\,\text{rad/s}$.

**(i) Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{250 \times 10^{-9}}{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\,\Omega
$$

**(ii) Phase Velocity:**

$$
v_{p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L\,C}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(250 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

$$
v_{p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-17}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}
$$

**(iii) Propagation Constant:**

For a lossless line, $\alpha = 0$, so:

$$
\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{L\,C} = j \cdot (\pi \times 10^{9}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9}) = j\,5\pi\,\text{rad/m}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\gamma \approx j\,15.71\,\text{rad/m}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\;Z_{0} = 50\,\Omega,\quad v_{p} = 2 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s},\quad \gamma = j\,5\pi\,\text{rad/m}\;}
$$

**Mark Distribution Hint:**
- 2 marks: $Z_0$ calculation
- 2 marks: $v_p$ calculation
- 3 marks: $\gamma$ calculation with correct identification of $\alpha = 0$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the one-dimensional wave equation for voltage on a transmission line. State the general solution and identify each term.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start from the Telegrapher's Equations:**

$$
\frac{dV}{dx} = -Z\,I \qquad (1) \qquad \frac{dI}{dx} = -Y\,V \qquad (2)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate Equation (1) with respect to $x$:**

$$
\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,\frac{dI}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation (2) into the result:**

$$
\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,(-Y\,V) = Z\,Y\,V
$$

**Step 4 — Define the propagation constant $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\,V\;}
$$

This is the **one-dimensional wave equation** for voltage along a transmission line.

**Step 5 — General Solution:**

$$
V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}
$$

**Identification of Terms:**
- $V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x}$ — **forward-traveling (incident) wave** propagating in the $+x$ direction.
- $V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}$ — **reverse-traveling (reflected) wave** propagating in the $-x$ direction.
- $V^{+}$, $V^{-}$ — complex amplitudes determined by boundary conditions at source and load.

**Mark Distribution Hint:**
- 3 marks: Derivation from Telegrapher's equations
- 2 marks: Defining $\gamma$ and writing the wave equation
- 2 marks: General solution + identification of forward/reverse waves

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a low-loss transmission line ($R \ll \omega L$ and $G \ll \omega C$), derive approximate expressions for the attenuation constant $\alpha$, phase constant $\beta$, and characteristic impedance $Z_0$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the exact propagation constant:**

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand for low-loss conditions** ($R \ll \omega L$, $G \ll \omega C$). The product becomes:

$$
(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C) = RG + j\omega RC + j\omega LG + j^{2}\omega^{2}LC
$$

$$
= (RG - \omega^{2}LC) + j\omega(RC + LG)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the low-loss approximation** ($\omega^{2}LC \gg RG$):

$$
\gamma \approx \sqrt{-\omega^{2}LC\,(1 - \tfrac{RG}{\omega^{2}LC}) + j\omega(RC + LG)}
$$

Using the approximation $\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - x/2$ for small $x$:

$$
\gamma \approx j\omega\sqrt{LC}\left(1 - \frac{RG}{2\omega^{2}LC}\right) + \sqrt{j\omega RC \cdot \text{(real terms)}}
$$

After detailed expansion (standard low-loss derivation):

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha \approx \frac{1}{2}\left(R\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} + G\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\right) = \frac{R}{2Z_{0}} + \frac{G\,Z_{0}}{2}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{LC}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Z_{0} \approx \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $\alpha$ has **two contributions**: conductor loss ($R/Z_0$) and dielectric loss ($G Z_0$).
- $\beta$ is identical to the lossless case — the phase velocity is unchanged to first order.
- $Z_0$ is approximately real (resistive) for a low-loss line — making impedance matching straightforward.

**Mark Distribution Hint:**
- 3 marks: Algebraic expansion of $(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)$
- 2 marks: Application of low-loss approximation
- 2 marks: Final boxed expressions + physical interpretation

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Transmission Line Equations (KTU Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in KVL:** Students often write $V(x+\Delta x) - V(x) = +Z\Delta x \cdot I$. The correct form has the **negative sign** because voltage *decreases* in the direction of current flow.
>
> 2. **Confusing $Z$ and $Y$ in the final equations:** Many scripts write $\frac{dV}{dx} = -Y\,I$ — this is a fatal error. Voltage is governed by series impedance $Z$; current is governed by shunt admittance $Y$.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $\alpha$ is the **attenuation constant** (units: Np/m), $\beta$ is the **phase constant** (units: rad/m). Examiners deduct full marks if swapped.
>
> 4. **Omitting the boundary condition discussion:** When asked to find the general solution, always mention that $V^{+}$ and $V^{-}$ are determined by **boundary conditions** at the source ($x=0$) and load ($x=L$).
>
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer:** Always state units explicitly ($\Omega$, m/s, rad/m). A numerically correct answer without units loses **0.5 to 1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Using $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) instead of magnitude in tables:** As noted in Section 2.3, use the LaTeX form `\vert x\vert` to avoid markdown rendering failures.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Line Equations — Rapid-Revision Summary**
>
> - **Telegrapher's Equations** — the two coupled first-order PDEs:
>   - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I$
>   - $\dfrac{dI}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V$
> - **Wave Equation:** $\dfrac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2}V$, where $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{ZY}$.
> - **General Voltage Solution:** $V(x) = V^{+} e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-} e^{+\gamma x}$.
> - **Characteristic Impedance:** $Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$.
> - **Lossless Special Case:** $Z_{0} = \sqrt{L/C}$, $\alpha = 0$, $v_{p} = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
> - **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma_{L} = (Z_{L} - Z_{0})/(Z_{L} + Z_{0})$ — zero when matched.
> - **Phase Velocity:** $v_{p} = \omega/\beta$.
> - **Units to Memorize:** $R$ in $\Omega/\text{m}$, $L$ in $\text{H/m}$, $G$ in $\text{S/m}$, $C$ in $\text{F/m}$, $\gamma$ in $\text{m}^{-1}$, $Z_0$ in $\Omega$.
> - **Physical Constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$, $Z_{0,\text{free}} \approx 377\,\Omega$.
> - **Engineering Significance:** Impedance matching prevents reflections; mismatched lines cause EMI and signal-integrity failures.
> - **Common PCB Values:** $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ (RF/single-ended), $90\,\Omega$ (USB differential), $100\,\Omega$ (Ethernet).
> - **Lossless vs Low-Loss:** Lossless uses exact $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$; low-loss uses same form but with finite $\alpha$.
> - **Always include boundary conditions** when solving wave-equation problems for full marks.
> - **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** — *Analyze wave propagation on transmission lines using Telegrapher's equations and derive characteristic impedance / propagation constants* (Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High-Speed Digital Interconnects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** High-Speed Digital Interconnects # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **High-Speed Digital Interconnect** is a conducting pathway—typically a trace on a printed circuit board (PCB), a cable, a connector, or a package lead—used to propagate digital signals between active devices (ICs, ASICs, FPGAs) whose signal edge rates are fast enough that the interconnect's physical length is electrically comparable to (or greater than) the wavelength of the highest significant frequency component of the digital signal. Under such conditions, the interconnect can no longer be treated as a simple wire (a short circuit at all frequencies); it must instead be analyzed as a **distributed parameter transmission line**, exhibiting frequency-dependent behavior, propagation delay, characteristic impedance, reflections, losses, and crosstalk. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):** > "An interconnect is classified as *electrically long* (and therefore 'high-speed') when its physical length $l$ exceeds (or is a non-negligible fraction of) the *critical length* $l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$, where $t_r$ is the 10–90 % rise time of the driver and $t_{pd}$ is the per-unit-length propagation delay of the medium." The two governing metrics that gate the entire high-speed domain are: - **Propagation Velocity:** $v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. - **Characteristic Impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_0}{C_0}}$ (typically $\mathbf{50\ \Omega}$ for general PCB/coaxial practice and $\mathbf{75\ \Omega}$ for video/RF). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose" Imagine you are filling a long garden hose with water from a tap. At low flow rates, you barely notice the hose. If you suddenly slam the tap shut, a pressure wave reverberates back up the hose and bangs the pipe. Why? Because the water has mass (inductance-like behavior) and the water column is compressible (capacitance-like behavior), and the disturbance takes time to travel down the length of the hose. A high-speed digital edge is exactly this phenomenon, but with **electrons and electric fields** instead of water: | Hose Element | Electrical Equivalent | |---|---| | Water mass / inertia | Series inductance $L_0$ (H/m) | | Hose elasticity / water compressibility | Shunt capacitance $C_0$ (F/m) | | Pipe wall friction | Series resistance $R_0$ (Ω/m) | | Water leaking through porous wall | Shunt conductance $G_0$ (S/m) | | Faucet sudden shut-off | Fast digital edge (small $t_r$) | | Pressure wave reverberation | Signal reflection | If the hose is very short (small $l$) compared to the surge travel time, the wave bounces back before you notice. If the hose is long, the bounce-back **causes visible ringing, overshoot, undershoot, and timing errors**—the same symptoms we call *signal integrity* failures on a PCB. ## 1.3 Why This Matters in Modern Electronics > [!NOTE] > **The "High-Speed" Threshold Has Dropped Sharply.** > - In the 1990s, only clock frequencies above ~50 MHz were considered "high speed." > - In KTU 2024 board examinations, the **rule of thumb** is: a digital interconnect is high-speed if $t_r < 2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot l$, OR equivalently, if the **third harmonic** of the edge lies above $\sim 1$ GHz, OR if the **edge spatial extent** $l_{edge} = v_p \cdot t_r$ is comparable to the trace length. The rise times of modern CMOS drivers are typically in the range $\mathbf{50\ ps}$ to $\mathbf{500\ ps}$, so even a **5 cm** PCB trace can be electrically long. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for KTU Reference) - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - Vacuum permeability: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m - Characteristic impedance of free space: $Z_{0,FS} \approx 376.7\ \Omega \approx 377\ \Omega$ - Standard logic termination: $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (most common), $75\ \Omega$ (video), $100\ \Omega$ (differential pairs, e.g., USB, Ethernet) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Lumped vs. Distributed Regime — The Central Distinction The single most important theoretical gate in Module 2 is recognizing **when** an interconnect stops behaving as a lumped element. ### 2.1.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step 1. A digital driver produces a trapezoidal pulse with **rise time** $t_r$ (10 %–90 %). 2. The spectral content of that edge extends up to a **knee frequency** $f_{knee} \approx \frac{0.5}{t_r}$ (often simplified as $f_{knee} \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}$). 3. Each spectral component travels down the interconnect as a wave of wavelength $\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f}$. 4. If the trace is much shorter than the shortest wavelength, **all frequencies see the same voltage at the same instant** (lumped). If comparable, different frequencies arrive at the far end at different phases (distributed). 5. The **boundary between regimes** is the *critical length* $l_c$. > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (Board-Exam Standard):** > - **Lumped (wire-like):** $l < \frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}}$ > - **Distributed (transmission line):** $l \geq \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ > - **Marginal (use caution):** $\frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}} \leq l < \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ ## 2.2 Transmission Line Distributed Model A high-speed interconnect is modeled as an infinite cascade of infinitesimal sections, each containing: - $R_0$ — series resistance per unit length (Ω/m), from conductor skin-effect loss - $L_0$ — series inductance per unit length (H/m), from magnetic energy storage - $G_0$ — shunt conductance per unit length (S/m), from dielectric loss - $C_0$ — shunt capacitance per unit length (F/m), from electric energy storage The **Telegrapher's Equations** govern the voltage and current along the line: $$\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial x} = -R_0 I(x,t) - L_0 \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial x} = -G_0 V(x,t) - C_0 \frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial t}$$ For a **lossless** line ($R_0 = G_0 = 0$), these reduce to the classical wave equation with the solution: $$V(x,t) = V^+ f\!\left(t - \frac{x}{v_p}\right) + V^- f\!\left(t + \frac{x}{v_p}\right)$$ where $V^+$ is the forward-traveling wave and $V^-$ is the reverse-traveling (reflected) wave. ## 2.3 Reflection Phenomena When a wave traveling on a line of impedance $Z_0$ encounters a termination impedance $Z_L$ (load) or source impedance $Z_S$, a portion is reflected. The **reflection coefficients** are: $$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} \quad \text{(load end)}$$ $$\Gamma_S = \frac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} \quad \text{(source end)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Cases (Must Memorize for KTU Board):** > - **Open circuit** ($Z_L \to \infty$): $\Gamma_L = +1$ → wave inverts (well, doubles at the load, but conventional analysis shows full positive reflection). > - **Short circuit** ($Z_L = 0$): $\Gamma_L = -1$ → wave reflects with **inversion** (negative reflection). > - **Matched load** ($Z_L = Z_0$): $\Gamma_L = 0$ → no reflection. > - **Source-matched driver** ($Z_S = Z_0$): $\Gamma_S = 0$ → no re-reflection from source. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board-Hot. Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` in tables to prevent Markdown breakage.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Propagation velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}$ | m/s | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | | 2 | Per-unit-length propagation delay | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{1}{v_p} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{c}$ | s/m | ns/m for FR4: ~5–7 ns/m | | 3 | Critical length | $l_c = \dfrac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ | m | Threshold for transmission-line behavior | | 4 | Knee frequency | $f_{knee} \approx \dfrac{0.5}{t_r}$ | Hz | Spectral content of edge | | 5 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | m | For highest significant frequency | | 6 | Characteristic impedance (lossless) | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L_0}{C_0}}$ | Ω | Independent of length & frequency (lossless) | | 7 | Characteristic impedance (lossy) | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_0 + j\omega L_0}{G_0 + j\omega C_0}}$ | Ω | Frequency-dependent when lossy | | 8 | Load reflection coefficient | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | unitless | $-1 \leq \Gamma_L \leq +1$ for passive loads | | 9 | Source reflection coefficient | $\Gamma_S = \dfrac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$ | unitless | At the driver side | | 10 | Transmission coefficient | $T = 1 + \Gamma = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | unitless | Voltage seen by load | | 11 | Forward wave voltage | $V^+ = V_S \cdot \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$ | V | Initial incident wave from source | | 12 | Microstrip $Z_0$ (approx.) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8h}{w} + \dfrac{w}{4h}\right)$ | Ω | For $w/h \leq 1$ | | 13 | Stripline $Z_0$ (approx.) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4b}{0.67\pi w + t}\right)$ | Ω | Symmetric stripline | | 14 | PCB per-unit $L_0, C_0$ (microstrip) | $L_0 C_0 = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff}$ | — | Fundamental LC relation | | 15 | Near-end crosstalk (NEXT) | $V_{NEXT} = k_b \cdot V_0$ | V | $k_b$ = backward coupling coef. | | 16 | Far-end crosstalk (FEXT) | $V_{FEXT} = k_f \cdot l \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | V | Proportional to coupling length | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PCB Design:** Determining trace width and stack-up so that $Z_0$ matches driver/receiver (e.g., $50\ \Omega$ for most digital, $90\ \Omega$ differential for USB). - **Cable Design:** Coaxial cables (TV, Ethernet) engineered to maintain $Z_0$ along the entire length to prevent reflections. - **High-Speed Serial Links:** PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x, HDMI, SerDes—all rely on controlled-impedance interconnects. - **EMC Compliance:** Unmatched traces radiate strongly (acting as antennas) and fail CE/FCC radiated emissions tests. - **SI/PI Co-design:** Power delivery networks (PDN) and signal traces must be co-optimized for noise and ringing. ## 2.6 Termination Strategies (KTU Board Favourite) | Strategy | Termination Resistor | When to Use | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Series (Source) Termination** | $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{driver}$ | Unidirectional, point-to-point | Single resistor, no DC load | Requires $R_S$ matched; first incident is half-amplitude | | **Parallel (End) Termination** | $R_T = Z_0$ to $V_{TT}$ | Point-to-point, multidrop | Full amplitude at receiver | DC current draw; needs reference voltage | | **Thevenin Termination** | $R_1 \Vert R_2 = Z_0$, biased at switch point | CMOS/TTL buses | Logic-level threshold control | Static current consumption | | **AC Termination** | $R_T = Z_0$ in series with capacitor | Buses, when DC current is undesirable | No DC draw | Slow settling, low-frequency issues | | **Diode Termination** | Clamp diodes at receiver | ESD / overshoot protection | Compact | Non-linear, not for SI control | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Length of an Interconnect **Goal:** Derive the condition under which an interconnect must be treated as a transmission line. ### 3.1.1 Setup - Driver rise time: $t_r$ (seconds) - Per-unit-length propagation delay: $t_{pd}$ (seconds per meter) - Interconnect physical length: $l$ (meters) - One-way flight time: $t_{flight} = t_{pd} \cdot l$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the round-trip time.** A reflection travels from the source to the load and back to the source, so the total round-trip time is: $$\tau_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{flight} = 2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot l$$ *Explanation:* The forward wave takes $t_{pd} \cdot l$ to reach the load, reflects, and takes another $t_{pd} \cdot l$ to return. The factor of 2 captures both traversals. **Step 2 — State the lumped assumption.** The interconnect behaves as a lumped element (i.e., a simple wire) if the line settles to its final value before any reflection returns. This requires: $$\tau_{RT} \ll t_r$$ The conventional boundary, **used by Howard Johnson's "High-Speed Digital Design"** and adopted by KTU board examiners, sets equality at the 50 % point: $$2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot l_c = t_r$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the critical length $l_c$.** Rearranging for $l$: $$l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$ *Explanation:* This is the longest trace for which the round-trip is shorter than the rise time. Beyond $l_c$, the edge has not finished transitioning before the first reflection arrives, and the line must be analyzed as distributed. **Step 4 — Conservative threshold.** Many texts (and KTU conservative practice) use a stricter bound of $l_c/3$ for absolutely safe lumped analysis: $$l_{lumped} \leq \frac{t_r}{6 \cdot t_{pd}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style):** > Given: $t_r = 1\ \text{ns}$, FR4 microstrip with $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = 4.2$. > Compute: $t_{pd} = \dfrac{\sqrt{4.2}}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.81\ \text{ps/mm} = 6.81\ \text{ns/m}$. > Then: $l_c = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 6.81 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 0.0734\ \text{m} \approx 7.34\ \text{cm}$. > **Conclusion:** Any FR4 trace longer than ~7 cm is electrically long for a 1 ns edge. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reflection Coefficient at a Load **Goal:** Derive the voltage reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L$ for a lossless line terminated in an arbitrary impedance $Z_L$. ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Express the total voltage and current at the load.** At any point on the line, the total voltage is the sum of incident and reflected waves, and the total current is their difference (with $V^+/Z_0$ in the forward direction, $-V^-/Z_0$ in the reverse): $$V = V^+ + V^-$$ $$I = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition at the load.** By Ohm's law at the load terminals: $$V_L = Z_L \cdot I_L$$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** Plug in $V_L = V^+ + V^-$ and $I_L = (V^+ - V^-)/Z_0$: $$V^+ + V^- = Z_L \cdot \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the ratio $V^-/V^+$.** Multiply both sides by $Z_0$: $$Z_0 (V^+ + V^-) = Z_L (V^+ - V^-)$$ Expand: $$Z_0 V^+ + Z_0 V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_L V^-$$ Collect $V^-$ on the left and $V^+$ on the right: $$Z_0 V^- + Z_L V^- = Z_L V^+ - Z_0 V^+$$ $$V^-(Z_0 + Z_L) = V^+(Z_L - Z_0)$$ **Step 5 — Define the reflection coefficient.** By definition, $\Gamma_L = V^-/V^+$: $$\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ *Explanation:* This is the KTU board's most-tested equation in Module 2. When $Z_L = Z_0$, $\Gamma_L = 0$ (matched, no reflection). When $Z_L \to \infty$ (open), $\Gamma_L = +1$. When $Z_L = 0$ (short), $\Gamma_L = -1$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Initial Incident Wave from a Source **Goal:** Compute the magnitude of the forward wave launched by a real source with series impedance $Z_S$ into a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$. ### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Model the source.** The driver is modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_S$ in series with internal impedance $Z_S$. At $t = 0^+$, the source is connected to the line of impedance $Z_0$. **Step 2 — Apply voltage divider.** Before any reflection returns, the line behaves as a resistor of value $Z_0$. The voltage launched on the line is the result of a simple voltage divider between $Z_S$ and $Z_0$: $$V^+ = V_S \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$$ **Step 3 — Voltage at the load (incident).** The voltage that the load sees on the first arrival is: $$V_{load,1} = V^+ \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) = V^+ \cdot T$$ where $T = \dfrac{2Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$ is the transmission coefficient. **Step 4 — Voltage at the load (after multiple reflections).** The total voltage at the load after $n$ round trips is the infinite geometric sum: $$V_{load,\infty} = V^+ \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\Gamma_L \Gamma_S)^k = V^+ \cdot \frac{1 + \Gamma_L}{1 - \Gamma_L \Gamma_S}$$ **Step 5 — Simplification for matched source ($\Gamma_S = 0$):** $$V_{load,\infty} = V^+ \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L) = V_S \cdot \frac{Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0} \cdot \frac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ *Explanation:* This is the well-known "double voltage-divider" formula. When $Z_S = Z_0 = Z_L$ (full matching), $V_{load,\infty} = V_S / 2$, and there is no ringing. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Per-Unit-Length Inductance and Capacitance of a Microstrip **Goal:** Derive the LC parameters for a wide microstrip on a dielectric substrate. ### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define geometry.** A microstrip has: - Trace width: $w$ (m) - Dielectric height: $h$ (m) - Dielectric constant: $\varepsilon_r$ - Effective dielectric constant (accounting for the air above): $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 h/w}}$ **Step 2 — Capacitance per unit length.** For $w/h \geq 1$ (wide trace): $$C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff} \cdot \frac{w}{h} \quad \text{(approximate, with corrections)}$$ **Step 3 — Use the fundamental identity $L_0 C_0 = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff}$.** This is a Maxwell-equation result for TEM-like propagation on a microstrip: $$L_0 = \frac{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff}}{C_0}$$ **Step 4 — Characteristic impedance.** Substituting $L_0$ and $C_0$ into $Z_0 = \sqrt{L_0/C_0}$: $$Z_0 = \frac{\sqrt{L_0}}{\sqrt{C_0}} = \frac{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,eff}} \cdot \sqrt{w/h}} = \frac{1}{c \cdot \varepsilon_0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{h}{w}}$$ After substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m: $$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{h}{w}} \quad \text{(for } w/h \geq 1\text{)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the standard KTU board formula for wide-microstrip $Z_0$.** For narrow traces ($w/h < 1$), the alternative logarithmic form applies: > $$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} \ln\!\left(\frac{8h}{w} + \frac{w}{4h}\right)$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Reflection Behavior for Common Loads | Load Type | $Z_L$ | $\Gamma_L$ | Effect on Step Edge | |---|---|---|---| | Open circuit | $\infty$ | $+1$ | Voltage doubles at load (no inversion) | | Short circuit | $0$ | $-1$ | Voltage goes to zero, inverts on return | | Matched | $Z_0$ | $0$ | No reflection; clean step | | Higher than $Z_0$ | $Z_L > Z_0$ | $0 < \Gamma_L < 1$ | Partial positive reflection | | Lower than $Z_0$ | $0 < Z_L < Z_0$ | $-1 < \Gamma_L < 0$ | Partial negative reflection | | Capacitive (HF) | $1/(j\omega C)$ | $\to -1$ as $\omega \to \infty$ | Acts like short at high freq | | Inductive (HF) | $j\omega L$ | $\to +1$ as $\omega \to \infty$ | Acts like open at high freq | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lumped vs. Distributed Equivalence Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Driver t_r edge] --> B{Is l greater than l_c?} B -- No --> C[Lumped model
Simple wire
No reflections] B -- Yes --> D[Distributed model
Transmission line
Treated with Telegrapher equations] C --> E[Single node analysis
V=IR sufficient] D --> F[Bounce diagram analysis
Required] ``` ## 4.2 Transmission Line Distributed RLCG Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input port V_in, I_in] --> B[R_0 per length] B --> C[L_0 per length] C --> D[Cascade to next section] D --> E[R_0 per length] E --> F[L_0 per length] F --> G[Output to next stage] A2[G_0 per length] --- B C2[G_0 per length] --- C E2[G_0 per length] --- E F2[G_0 per length] --- F G2[C_0 per length] --- D H[Return path
ground] --- A2 H --- C2 H --- E2 H --- F2 H --- G2 ``` ## 4.3 Bounce Diagram — Three-Round-Trip Reflection Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD T0[Time t=0
V+ launched from source
Magnitude V_S times Z_0 over Z_S plus Z_0] T1[Time t = t_flight
V+ reaches load Z_L
Reflected: V- = V+ times Gamma_L] T2[Time t = 2 times t_flight
V- returns to source Z_S
Re-reflected: V+ prime = V- times Gamma_S] T3[Time t = 3 times t_flight
V+ prime arrives at load
Reflected again by Gamma_L] T4[Time t = 4 times t_flight
Back at source
Cycle continues] T0 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5[Steady state
V_load = V_S times Z_L over Z_S plus Z_L] ``` ## 4.4 Termination Strategy Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Choose termination strategy] --> Q1{Is link
point-to-point?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is DC current
permitted?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use parallel or Thevenin
termination at receiver] Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use series termination
at source only] Q1 -- No --> Q5{Multidrop bus?} Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Thevenin or AC
termination at both ends] Q5 -- No --> Q7[Differential pair?
Use differential
termination 100 ohm] ``` ## 4.5 Crosstalk Coupling Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Aggressor line
Driver A] -->|Capacitive coupling Cm| B[Victim line
Quiet receiver B] A -->|Inductive coupling Lm| B B --> C[Near end NEXT
= kb times V0
appears at near end] B --> D[Far end FEXT
= kf times l times dV by dt
appears at far end] ``` ## 4.6 Signal Integrity Degradation Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ideal step edge from driver] --> B[Transmission line effects] B --> B1[Attenuation
due to skin effect and dielectric loss] B --> B2[Dispersion
different frequencies travel at different speeds] B --> B3[Reflections
from impedance mismatch] B --> B4[Crosstalk
from adjacent lines] B1 --> C[Distorted waveform at receiver] B2 --> C B3 --> C B4 --> C C --> D{Within timing
and voltage
margins?} D -- Yes --> E[Functional system] D -- No --> F[SI failure
setup or hold violation
or false triggering] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define a high-speed digital interconnect. State the condition for a digital interconnect to behave as a transmission line.** **Model Answer:** A high-speed digital interconnect is a conducting pathway on a PCB, package, or cable whose physical length is electrically comparable to the wavelength of the highest significant frequency component of the propagating digital signal. Under such conditions, the interconnect must be modeled as a **distributed parameter transmission line**, exhibiting propagation delay, characteristic impedance, reflections, and crosstalk — rather than as a simple lumped wire. **Condition (KTU Board-Standard Phrasing):** An interconnect behaves as a transmission line when its physical length $l$ exceeds the *critical length*: $$l \geq l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$ where $t_r$ is the 10–90 % rise time of the driver and $t_{pd}$ is the per-unit-length propagation delay (typically 5–7 ns/m for FR4 microstrip). For $l < t_r/(6 t_{pd})$, the interconnect can be safely treated as a lumped element. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (KTU Examiner's Pattern):** > - Definition — 1 mark > - Critical length formula and explanation — 1 mark > - Numerical example or typical $t_{pd}$ value — 1 mark --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. What is its value for a matched termination?** **Model Answer:** The **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ of a transmission line is the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single forward-traveling wave on the line, with no reflections present. For a lossless line, it depends only on the per-unit-length inductance and capacitance: $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_0}{C_0}}$$ For a **lossy** line (with $R_0$ and $G_0$ non-zero), $Z_0$ becomes frequency-dependent: $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R_0 + j \omega L_0}{G_0 + j \omega C_0}}$$ **Matched Termination Condition:** A termination is said to be matched when the load impedance $Z_L$ (or source impedance $Z_S$) equals the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of the line. In that case, the load reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L = 0$ and the source reflection coefficient $\Gamma_S = 0$, eliminating all reflections. **Standard value used in PCB design is $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$** (single-ended) or **$100\ \Omega$** (differential). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and must be split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B (full-or-none choice). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the critical length of a high-speed digital interconnect. A CMOS driver has a rise time of 2 ns driving an FR4 microstrip trace. The effective dielectric constant is 4.2. Determine the maximum trace length for lumped-element analysis.** **(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the source-series termination scheme. What is its main disadvantage, and how can it be overcome?** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Derivation:** Let $t_r$ be the rise time of the driver, $t_{pd}$ be the per-unit-length propagation delay, and $l$ be the trace length. - One-way flight time: $t_{flight} = t_{pd} \cdot l$ - Round-trip flight time: $\tau_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot l$ For the interconnect to be treated as a lumped element, the round-trip time must be much smaller than the rise time. The **standard critical length** is defined at the boundary $\tau_{RT} = t_r$: $$2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot l_c = t_r \quad \Longrightarrow \quad l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$ **Numerical Computation:** - $t_r = 2\ \text{ns} = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$ - $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = 4.2$ - $t_{pd} = \dfrac{\sqrt{4.2}}{3 \times 10^8}\ \text{s/m} = \dfrac{2.049}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.83 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s/m} = 6.83\ \text{ns/m}$ Substitute: $$l_c = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 6.83 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2}{13.66} \approx 0.1464\ \text{m} \approx 14.6\ \text{cm}$$ For **conservative lumped analysis** (using $l_{lumped} \leq l_c/3$): $$l_{lumped} = \frac{l_c}{3} = \frac{14.6}{3} \approx 4.88\ \text{cm}$$ **Result:** The maximum trace length for safe lumped analysis is approximately **4.9 cm**. Beyond 4.9 cm, the line must be analyzed as a transmission line. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Source-Series Termination Scheme:** In source-series termination, a resistor $R_S$ is placed **in series with the driver output**, between the driver's internal output pin and the beginning of the transmission line. The value of $R_S$ is chosen such that the **total series resistance** at the source equals the characteristic impedance of the line: $$R_S + Z_{driver,internal} = Z_0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_S = Z_0 - Z_{driver,internal}$$ **Operation:** 1. When the driver switches, the initial voltage launched on the line is: $$V^+ = V_S \cdot \frac{Z_0}{R_S + Z_{driver,internal} + Z_0} = \frac{V_S}{2}$$ (when the total source impedance equals $Z_0$). 2. This half-amplitude wave travels to the load. If the load is **high-impedance** (typical of a CMOS receiver, $Z_L \gg Z_0$), then $\Gamma_L \approx +1$, and the wave reflects with full amplitude back toward the source. 3. At the source, the source is now matched ($\Gamma_S = 0$), so the reflected wave is **absorbed** and does not re-reflect. 4. The voltage at the load therefore rises from $V_S/2$ to $V_S$ once the reflection returns — total time $\approx 2 \cdot t_{flight}$. **Disadvantage:** - The first-incident wave is only **half amplitude**, so the receiver sees a slow-rising intermediate step. This can cause **double-clocking or metastability** in high-speed logic. - The scheme is **unsuitable for bidirectional or multidrop buses**, where waves travel in both directions. - It works only when the load is **strictly high-impedance** (point-to-point, e.g., a single CMOS input). **How the Disadvantage is Overcome:** - Use **parallel termination at the receiver** instead, which gives full amplitude on the first incident wave. This is preferred for high-speed serial links. - For point-to-point unidirectional links (e.g., clock distribution, source-synchronous interfaces), series termination is acceptable because the half-amplitude step has time to settle to full amplitude before the next switching event. - A small **damping resistor** at the receiver (in addition to source-series termination) can reduce the half-amplitude ringing on multiple-reflection links. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $l_c = t_r/(2t_{pd})$ with $l_c = t_r/t_{pd}$.** The factor of 2 is from the round-trip — losing this is a **2-mark penalty** under KTU strict marking. > 2. **Forgetting to compute $t_{pd}$ explicitly** in numerical problems. Students often write $t_{pd} \approx 5\ \text{ns/m}$ as a magic number without showing $t_{pd} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}/c$. This costs the derivation marks. > 3. **In Part (b), writing only "matched load = no reflection"** without explaining the half-amplitude first step — losing 2–3 marks. > 4. **Not drawing the schematic** for the termination scheme. KTU board examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for the diagram. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a) [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the Telegrapher's equations for a transmission line. Derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a lossless line.** **(b) [7 Marks] A clock signal with 200 MHz frequency and 80 ps rise time propagates on an FR4 microstrip ($\varepsilon_{r,eff} = 4.0$). Calculate (i) the knee frequency, (ii) the critical length, (iii) the propagation velocity, and (iv) the wavelength at the knee frequency.** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Telegrapher's Equations — Setup and Derivation:** Consider an infinitesimal section $dx$ of a transmission line containing: - $R_0\, dx$ — series resistance - $L_0\, dx$ — series inductance - $G_0\, dx$ — shunt conductance - $C_0\, dx$ — shunt capacitance Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop: $$V(x,t) - R_0\, dx \cdot I(x,t) - L_0\, dx \cdot \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t} - V(x+dx, t) = 0$$ Rearranging and dividing by $dx$: $$-\frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial x} = R_0 I(x,t) + L_0 \frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial t}$$ Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the node: $$I(x,t) - G_0\, dx \cdot V(x+dx, t) - C_0\, dx \cdot \frac{\partial V(x+dx, t)}{\partial t} - I(x+dx, t) = 0$$ Rearranging and dividing by $dx$: $$-\frac{\partial I(x,t)}{\partial x} = G_0 V(x,t) + C_0 \frac{\partial V(x,t)}{\partial t}$$ These are the **Telegrapher's Equations**. **Lossless Case:** For a lossless line, $R_0 = G_0 = 0$: $$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -L_0 \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \qquad \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -C_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$ **Derivation of Characteristic Impedance:** Differentiating the first equation with respect to $x$ and substituting the second: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = -L_0 \frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial x \partial t} = L_0 C_0 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$$ This is the classical wave equation with solution: $$V(x,t) = V^+ e^{-j \beta x} + V^- e^{+j \beta x}$$ where $\beta = \omega \sqrt{L_0 C_0}$. Substituting back into the lossless KVL equation: $$-j \beta V = -j \omega L_0 I \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{V}{I} = \frac{\omega L_0}{\beta} = \frac{L_0}{\sqrt{L_0 C_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{L_0}{C_0}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_0}{C_0}}}$$ which is **independent of frequency** for a lossless line. For a lossy line, $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R_0 + j\omega L_0)/(G_0 + j\omega C_0)}$ becomes frequency-dependent. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $f_{clock} = 200\ \text{MHz}$ - $t_r = 80\ \text{ps} = 80 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{s}$ - $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = 4.0$ **(i) Knee Frequency:** $$f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r} = \frac{0.5}{80 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.25 \times 10^9\ \text{Hz} = 6.25\ \text{GHz}$$ **(ii) Critical Length:** First, compute the per-unit-length propagation delay: $$t_{pd} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{c} = \frac{\sqrt{4.0}}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{2}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.67 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s/m} = 6.67\ \text{ns/m}$$ Then: $$l_c = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{80}{13340} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 6.0\ \text{mm}$$ **(iii) Propagation Velocity:** $$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.0} = 1.5 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$ **(iv) Wavelength at Knee Frequency:** $$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f_{knee}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{6.25 \times 10^9} = 0.024\ \text{m} = 24\ \text{mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Part (b):** > 1. **Using $f_{clock}$ instead of $f_{knee}$** for the wavelength calculation. The question explicitly asks for "wavelength at the knee frequency" — using 200 MHz gives $\lambda = 75\ \text{cm}$, which is wrong by a factor of 30. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 0.5** in the knee frequency formula. Some texts use $0.35/t_r$, others $0.5/t_r$. KTU boards accept $0.5/t_r$ as the standard. > 3. **Mixing up $c$ with $v_p$** in the wavelength formula.
