Students often write $\lambda = c/f$, which is wrong inside a dielectric. > 4. **Not stating units clearly** — KTU values unit consistency in numerical problems; missing units can cost a mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board — Module 2** - [x] **Definition of high-speed interconnect:** Interconnect where $l \geq l_c = t_r/(2 t_{pd})$; must be modeled as a transmission line. - [x] **Critical length formula:** $l_c = t_r/(2 t_{pd})$ (round-trip boundary). Conservative lumped limit: $l_c/3 = t_r/(6 t_{pd})$. - [x] **Lumped vs distributed regime:** Below $l_c/3$ → lumped; above $l_c$ → distributed; between → marginal. - [x] **Knee frequency:** $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$ (spectral content of a digital edge). - [x] **Propagation velocity:** $v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}$. For FR4 ($\varepsilon_{r,eff} \approx 4.0$–$4.2$): $v_p \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s, $t_{pd} \approx 6.7$ ns/m. - [x] **Telegrapher's equations (lossless):** $\partial V/\partial x = -L_0 \partial I/\partial t$, $\partial I/\partial x = -C_0 \partial V/\partial t$. - [x] **Characteristic impedance (lossless):** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L_0/C_0}$ — independent of frequency and length. - [x] **Characteristic impedance (lossy):** $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R_0 + j\omega L_0)/(G_0 + j\omega C_0)}$ — frequency-dependent. - [x] **Load reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma_L = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$. Open: $+1$. Short: $-1$. Matched: $0$. - [x] **Source reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma_S = (Z_S - Z_0)/(Z_S + Z_0)$. - [x] **Transmission coefficient:** $T = 1 + \Gamma_L = 2Z_L/(Z_L + Z_0)$. - [x] **Initial incident wave:** $V^+ = V_S \cdot Z_0/(Z_S + Z_0)$. - [x] **Termination strategies:** Source-series, parallel (end), Thevenin, AC, diode — choose based on point-to-point vs. bus and DC current constraints. - [x] **Source-series termination:** $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{driver,internal}$. Half-amplitude first step, then full amplitude after reflection. Disadvantage: receiver sees intermediate voltage. - [x] **Standard impedance values:** $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (single-ended, RF), $75\ \Omega$ (video, cable TV), $100\ \Omega$ (differential pairs, USB, Ethernet). - [x] **Microstrip $Z_0$ (wide, $w/h \geq 1$):** $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}) \cdot \sqrt{h/w}$. - [x] **Microstrip $Z_0$ (narrow, $w/h < 1$):** $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}) \cdot \ln(8h/w + w/4h)$. - [x] **Stripline $Z_0$:** $Z_0 \approx (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}) \cdot \ln(4b/(0.67\pi w + t))$. - [x] **Effective dielectric constant (microstrip):** $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = (\varepsilon_r + 1)/2 + (\varepsilon_r - 1)/2 \cdot 1/\sqrt{1 + 12h/w}$. - [x] **Crosstalk:** NEXT (backward, near-end) and FEXT (forward, far-end) — proportional to mutual capacitance $C_m$ and mutual inductance $L_m$ between adjacent traces. - [x] **Bounce diagram:** Used to track multiple reflections; voltage at any point is sum of all forward and reverse waves present at that instant. - [x] **Skin effect:** At high frequencies, current crowds near conductor surface; $R_0$ increases as $\sqrt{f}$, causing frequency-dependent loss. - [x] **Dielectric loss:** $\tan\delta$ of FR4 (~0.02) causes $G_0 \neq 0$ and adds to attenuation. - [x] **Time-of-flight:** $TOF = l \cdot t_{pd}$. Important for setup/hold timing analysis. - [x] **Eye diagram:** Key visualization tool for SI — opening (height, width) quantifies signal quality. - [x] **KTU 2024 numerical focus:** Expect at least one numerical problem combining $l_c$, $t_{pd}$, $f_{knee}$, $\lambda$ — practice all three worked examples above.

topics.title: Effect of Terminations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Terminations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In high-speed digital and RF design, a **transmission line** must be terminated with a load impedance equal to its **characteristic impedance ($Z_0$)** to prevent signal reflections. The **effect of terminations** refers to the consequences—reflected waves, ringing, overshoot, undershoot, and standing waves—that arise when the termination impedance ($Z_L$) deviates from the characteristic impedance ($Z_0$) of the line.

Mathematically, the relationship between the incident wave and the reflected wave at the load is governed by the **load reflection coefficient**:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** When $Z_L = Z_0$ (matched termination), $\Gamma_L = 0$, and the line behaves as if it were infinitely long. When $Z_L \neq Z_0$, a portion of the signal energy is reflected back toward the source, causing **signal integrity (SI) violations**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Analogy

Imagine you are spraying water through a long garden hose:

- **Matched termination** ($Z_L = Z_0$): You hold the nozzle wide open. Water flows smoothly, and you feel steady pressure at the spigot. There is **no reflected wave**.
- **Open-circuit termination** ($Z_L = \infty$): You completely block the nozzle with your thumb. Water pressure surges back violently into the hose — this is the **reflected wave**.
- **Short-circuit termination** ($Z_L = 0$): You let the water gush freely into a wide drain. The sudden drop in pressure causes a **negative reflected wave** (the hose momentarily "sucks back").
- **Mismatched termination** ($Z_L \neq Z_0$): You partially block the nozzle. Some water comes back, creating oscillations in flow — analogous to **ringing** in a digital signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** In PCBs operating above ~100 MHz, the physical length of a trace exceeds $\lambda/10$, making it electrically "long." At this point, every trace is a transmission line, and improper termination leads to corrupted logic levels, timing jitter, and electromagnetic interference (EMI).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Termination Analysis

- **$\Gamma_L$** — Load reflection coefficient (dimensionless, complex)
- **$Z_0$** — Characteristic impedance (typically **50 Ω** for RF, **75 Ω** for video, **90 Ω / 100 Ω** for differential USB/Ethernet)
- **VSWR** — Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (always $\geq 1$)
- **Return Loss** — Measured in **dB** (positive value indicating mismatch severity)

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exams:** A return loss of **$\geq 10$ dB** (i.e., $\vert\Gamma\vert \leq 0.316$) is considered acceptable for most digital interconnects.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Termination

A transmission line carries energy as a **forward-traveling wave** ($V^+$) and a **backward-traveling wave** ($V^-$). The total voltage at any point $x$ on the line is:

$$V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{+j\beta x}$$

The total current is:

$$I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta x}$$

> The signs differ because reflected current flows opposite to reflected voltage.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Termination Behavior

1. **Wave launches from source** with amplitude $V^+$ and travels toward load.
2. **At the load boundary**, the line voltage must equal $V_L = I_L \cdot Z_L$ (Ohm's law).
3. The line can only support two waves ($V^+$ and $V^-$); boundary conditions dictate $V^-$.
4. **Mismatch ($Z_L \neq Z_0$)** generates a non-zero $V^-$.
5. **Reflected wave travels back**, partially re-reflecting at the source (if $Z_S \neq Z_0$).
6. **Multiple round trips** cause ringing and settling-time violations.

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Termination Cases

| Termination Type | Load Impedance | Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma_L$ | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Matched** | $Z_L = Z_0$ | $0$ | No reflection; clean signal |
| **Open-Circuit** | $Z_L \to \infty$ | $+1$ | Total reflection, in-phase (overshoot) |
| **Short-Circuit** | $Z_L = 0$ | $-1$ | Total reflection, inverted (undershoot) |
| **Reactive Mismatch** | $Z_L = jX$ | Purely imaginary | Frequency-dependent reflection |
| **Complex Mismatch** | $Z_L = R + jX$ | Complex | Magnitude + phase shift |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Load reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T = 1 + \Gamma_L = \dfrac{2 Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Voltage standing wave ratio | ratio ($\geq 1$) |
| 4 | $\text{Return Loss} = -20 \log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | Mismatch severity | dB |
| 5 | $\Gamma_{in} = \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta \ell}$ | Input reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \Gamma_{in}}{1 - \Gamma_{in}}$ | Input impedance looking into line | Ω |
| 7 | $v(t)_\text{at load} = V^+(1 + \Gamma_L)$ | Load voltage (lattice eq.) | V |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{v_p}$ | Phase constant | rad/m |
| 9 | $t_d = \dfrac{\ell}{v_p}$ | Time delay of line | s |
| 10 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}}$ | Phase velocity in dielectric | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU convention:** In all derivations, use **RMS values** for power calculations and **peak values** for time-domain transient analysis. Mixing these is a common mistake.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Termination engineering is the cornerstone of:
- **High-speed serial links** (PCIe, USB 3.x, HDMI, SerDes)
- **RF front-ends** (antenna matching networks)
- **Memory buses** (DDR4/DDR5, where VTT termination is mandatory)
- **EMC compliance** — poorly terminated lines radiate as efficient antennas

> [!TIP]
> **Industry insight:** Intel's high-speed design guides mandate source-series termination (SST) for point-to-point links up to 6 Gbps because reflections from connectors dominate at those speeds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Load Reflection Coefficient

**Starting point:** At the load ($x = 0$), apply the boundary conditions.

**Step 1:** Write the total voltage and current at the load using the telegrapher's equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(0) &= V^+ + V^- \\
I(0) &= \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's law at the load:

$$V(0) = I(0) \cdot Z_L$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expressions:

$$V^+ + V^- = \left(\frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0}\right) Z_L$$

**Step 4:** Expand the right-hand side:

$$V^+ + V^- = \frac{V^+ Z_L}{Z_0} - \frac{V^- Z_L}{Z_0}$$

**Step 5:** Group terms containing $V^+$ on one side and $V^-$ on the other:

$$V^+ - \frac{V^+ Z_L}{Z_0} = -V^- - \frac{V^- Z_L}{Z_0}$$

**Step 6:** Factor:

$$V^+ \left(1 - \frac{Z_L}{Z_0}\right) = -V^- \left(1 + \frac{Z_L}{Z_0}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange for $V^- / V^+$:

$$\frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{\dfrac{Z_L}{Z_0} - 1}{\dfrac{Z_L}{Z_0} + 1}$$

**Step 8:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $Z_0$:

$$\boxed{\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}}$$

This is the **load reflection coefficient**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)

**Step 1:** On a lossless line, the total voltage magnitude is:

$$\vert V(x) \vert = \vert V^+ \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\beta x} + \Gamma_L e^{+j\beta x} \vert$$

**Step 2:** The maximum voltage occurs where $e^{-j\beta x}$ and $\Gamma_L e^{+j\beta x}$ are **in phase**, giving:

$$V_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma_L \vert)$$

**Step 3:** The minimum voltage occurs where they are **out of phase**, giving:

$$V_{\min} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert)$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio:

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{\vert V^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma_L \vert)}{\vert V^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert)}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma_L \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Return Loss in dB

**Step 1:** Return loss is defined as the ratio of incident to reflected power in dB:

$$\text{RL} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{incident}}}{P_{\text{reflected}}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Since $P \propto V^2$:

$$\frac{P_{\text{incident}}}{P_{\text{reflected}}} = \frac{\vert V^+ \vert^2}{\vert V^- \vert^2} = \frac{1}{\vert \Gamma_L \vert^2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\text{RL} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\vert \Gamma_L \vert^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\vert \Gamma_L \vert}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Final sign convention (RL is always reported as positive):

$$\boxed{\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma_L \vert \;\; \text{dB}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 50 Ω transmission line is terminated with $Z_L = 75 \Omega$. Find (a) $\Gamma_L$, (b) VSWR, (c) Return Loss, (d) Voltage at the load if the incident wave is $V^+ = 1\,\text{V}$.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Reflection coefficient:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{75 - 50}{75 + 50} = \frac{25}{125} = 0.2$$

**(b)** VSWR:

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + 0.2}{1 - 0.2} = \frac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.5$$

**(c)** Return Loss:

$$\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}(0.2) = -20 \times (-0.699) = 13.98 \;\text{dB} \approx 14 \;\text{dB}$$

**(d)** Load voltage:

$$V_L = V^+ (1 + \Gamma_L) = 1 \times (1 + 0.2) = 1.2 \;\text{V}$$

> This load voltage **exceeds** the incident voltage because the load is higher than $Z_0$ (energy piles up at a higher-impedance boundary).

## 3.5 Time-Domain Reflection (Lattice Diagram) Analysis

For a line of delay $t_d$ with source impedance $Z_S$, load impedance $Z_L$, and characteristic impedance $Z_0$:

- Source reflection coefficient: $\Gamma_S = \dfrac{Z_S - Z_0}{Z_S + Z_0}$
- Load reflection coefficient: $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$

The voltage at any node in the lattice is given by:

$$V_n = V^+ \cdot \Gamma_L^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \cdot \Gamma_S^{\lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor} \cdot (1 + \Gamma_L)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** You are expected to draw the lattice diagram and compute at least 3 bounces explicitly when given numerical values.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Effect of Various Terminations on Incident Wave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE\nV+ = 1 V\nZ_S = 50 ohm"] -->|incident wave| line["TRANSMISSION LINE\nZ_0 = 50 ohm\ndelay t_d"]
    line -->|arrives at load| loadNode{"Load Type?"}
    loadNode -->|Z_L = Z_0| matched["MATCHED\nGamma_L = 0\nV_L = 1.0 V\nNo reflection"]
    loadNode -->|Z_L = infinity| openC["OPEN CIRCUIT\nGamma_L = +1\nV_L = 2.0 V\nFull reflection, in-phase"]
    loadNode -->|Z_L = 0| shortC["SHORT CIRCUIT\nGamma_L = -1\nV_L = 0 V\nFull reflection, inverted"]
    loadNode -->|Z_L = 75 ohm| misMatch["MISMATCHED\nGamma_L = +0.2\nV_L = 1.2 V\nPartial reflection"]
    openC -.reflected V-.-> line
    shortC -.reflected V-.-> line
    misMatch -.reflected V-.-> line
```

## 4.2 Lattice Diagram Construction (Bounce Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axis["Time axis (left to right)"]
        t0["t = 0"] --- t1["t = t_d"] --- t2["t = 2 t_d"] --- t3["t = 3 t_d"] --- t4["t = 4 t_d"]
    end
    srcAxis["Source\nZ_S"] --- t0
    loadAxis["Load\nZ_L"] --- t4
    nodeA["V_1 = V+ * (1 + Gamma_L)\nFIRST ARRIVAL"] -.-> t1
    nodeB["V_2 = V_1 * Gamma_L * Gamma_S\n(returns to load)"] -.-> t3
    nodeC["V_3 = V_2 * Gamma_L\n(returns to source)"] -.-> t2
    nodeD["V_4 = V_3 * Gamma_S\n(returns to load)"] -.-> t4
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Termination Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify Interconnect Topology"] --> q1{"Single-ended\nor Differential?"}
    q1 -->|Single| q2{"Point-to-Point\nor Multi-drop?"}
    q1 -->|Differential| diff["Use Differential\nTermination (100 ohm)"]
    q2 -->|Point-to-Point| sst["Source-Series\nTermination (SST)"]
    q2 -->|Multi-drop| q3{"Stub length\ncritical?"}
    q3 -->|Yes| pp["Parallel\nTermination at Receiver"]
    q3 -->|No| acTerm["AC Termination\n(R_series, C_series)"]
    sst --> verify["Verify with\nTDR Simulation"]
    pp --> verify
    acTerm --> verify
    diff --> verify
    verify --> endNode["Eye Diagram\nCompliance Check"]
```

## 4.4 Reflection Coefficient Behavior on Smith Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["Z_L = Z_0"] -->|"Gamma = 0"| center["CENTER of Smith Chart\n(Perfectly Matched)"]
    b["Z_L = infinity"] -->|"Gamma = +1"| right["RIGHT EDGE\n(Open Circuit)"]
    c["Z_L = 0"] -->|"Gamma = -1"| left["LEFT EDGE\n(Short Circuit)"]
    d["Z_L = 50 ohm real"] -->|"Gamma = 0"| center
    e["Z_L = 75 ohm real"] -->|"Gamma = +0.2"| upperR["Right of center\n(matched line reference)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Smith chart is a graphical tool mapping $\Gamma_L$ as a function of $Z_L$. The **center** always represents the matched condition. Moving along a constant-VSWR circle rotates the phase of $\Gamma$ while keeping $\vert \Gamma \vert$ constant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define reflection coefficient. What is its value when a transmission line is terminated in (a) matched load, (b) open circuit, and (c) short circuit?**

**Model Answer:**
The load reflection coefficient is the ratio of the reflected voltage wave to the incident voltage wave at the load:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

- **(a) Matched load** ($Z_L = Z_0$): $\Gamma_L = 0$ (no reflection)
- **(b) Open circuit** ($Z_L \to \infty$): $\Gamma_L = +1$ (full reflection, in-phase)
- **(c) Short circuit** ($Z_L = 0$): $\Gamma_L = -1$ (full reflection, inverted)

**[3 marks — 1 mark each sub-part]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is VSWR? Why is VSWR = 1 considered the ideal condition?**

**Model Answer:**
VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio) is the ratio of maximum to minimum voltage on a transmission line due to standing waves:

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma_L \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert}$$

When VSWR = 1, it implies $\vert \Gamma_L \vert = 0$, meaning the load is perfectly matched to the line and there is **no reflected wave**, **no standing waves**, and **maximum power transfer** to the load.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following (Q-A or Q-B).

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the reflection coefficient of a transmission line terminated with an arbitrary load impedance $Z_L$. Show that when $Z_L = Z_0$, there is no reflection.

**(b)** A 50 Ω lossless transmission line of length 2 m is terminated with a load $Z_L = 75\,\Omega$. The phase velocity on the line is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. For an incident wave of $V^+ = 1\,\text{V}$ at 500 MHz, calculate:
   - (i) Reflection coefficient
   - (ii) VSWR
   - (iii) Return Loss in dB
   - (iv) Time delay of the line
   - (v) Voltage at the load

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full 8-step derivation. Key steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(0) &= V^+ + V^- \\
I(0) &= \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0} \\
V(0) &= I(0) \cdot Z_L \\
\Gamma_L &= \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

When $Z_L = Z_0$:
$$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_0 - Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_0} = 0$$
Hence no reflection. ✓ **[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks — 1.4 marks each sub-part):**

**(i) Reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{75 - 50}{75 + 50} = \frac{25}{125} = 0.2 \;\;\text{(real, positive)} \quad \textbf{[1.4 marks]}$$

**(ii) VSWR:**

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + 0.2}{1 - 0.2} = \frac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.5 \quad \textbf{[1.4 marks]}$$

**(iii) Return Loss:**

$$\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}(0.2) = 13.98 \approx 14 \;\text{dB} \quad \textbf{[1.4 marks]}$$

**(iv) Time delay:**

$$t_d = \frac{\ell}{v_p} = \frac{2}{2 \times 10^8} = 10 \;\text{ns} \quad \textbf{[1.4 marks]}$$

**(v) Load voltage:**

$$V_L = V^+ (1 + \Gamma_L) = 1 \times 1.2 = 1.2 \;\text{V} \quad \textbf{[1.4 marks]}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with proper diagrams the effect of the following terminations on a transmission line:
   - (i) Matched termination
   - (ii) Open-circuit termination
   - (iii) Short-circuit termination

For each case, state the reflection coefficient and describe the resulting voltage waveform at the load.

**(b)** A 100 Ω differential pair is terminated at the receiver end with 90 Ω instead of the standard 100 Ω. Compute:
   - (i) Differential mode reflection coefficient
   - (ii) Return Loss in dB
   - (iii) VSWR
   - (iv) Comment on the impact on signal integrity

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

| Termination | $\Gamma_L$ | Waveform Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Matched** ($Z_L = Z_0$) | $0$ | No reflection; voltage at load settles immediately to $V^+$ |
| **Open circuit** ($Z_L = \infty$) | $+1$ | Full positive reflection; load voltage rises to $2V^+$, then settles after multiple bounces |
| **Short circuit** ($Z_L = 0$) | $-1$ | Full negative reflection; load voltage momentarily drops to $0$, then oscillates |

Diagrams should show:
- Incident wave $V^+$
- Reflected wave $V^- = \Gamma_L \cdot V^+$
- Resulting load voltage $V_L = V^+ + V^-$ **[7 marks — 2 marks each + 1 for diagrams]**

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Differential mode reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{90 - 100}{90 + 100} = \frac{-10}{190} = -0.0526 \quad \textbf{[1.75 marks]}$$

**(ii) Return Loss:**

$$\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}(\vert -0.0526 \vert) = -20 \times (-1.279) = 25.58 \;\text{dB} \quad \textbf{[1.75 marks]}$$

**(iii) VSWR:**

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + 0.0526}{1 - 0.0526} = \frac{1.0526}{0.9474} = 1.111 \quad \textbf{[1.75 marks]}$$

**(iv) Comment on SI Impact (1.75 marks):**
- $\Gamma_L$ is **negative** → reflection is **inverted** (causes undershoot).
- VSWR ≈ 1.11 is **close to 1**, indicating a **minor mismatch**.
- Return loss of **25.58 dB** is **excellent** (industry standard requires ≥ 10 dB).
- Signal integrity is largely preserved, but at multi-Gbps data rates, even this small reflection can cause inter-symbol interference (ISI).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $\Gamma_L$:** Students often forget that $Z_L$ in the numerator creates the sign — open gives $+1$, short gives $-1$. Mixing these is a **1-mark loss**.
> 2. **VSWR ≥ 1:** Writing VSWR < 1 guarantees zero marks. Always check by plugging back.
> 3. **Return Loss sign:** RL is reported as a **positive** dB value. Writing $-14$ dB instead of $+14$ dB is a common error.
> 4. **Time-domain vs frequency-domain:** In Part B, if the question involves ringing, **draw the lattice diagram** — examiners award 2 marks specifically for it.
> 5. **Units:** $t_d$ must be in **seconds** (or ns), $Z$ in **Ω**, $\Gamma$ is **dimensionless**. Wrong unit = mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize before every KTU exam:**

- ✅ Reflection coefficient formula: $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$
- ✅ Three canonical values: Matched → $0$, Open → $+1$, Short → $-1$
- ✅ VSWR formula: $\dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$; always $\geq 1$
- ✅ Return Loss: $-20 \log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ in **dB** (always positive)
- ✅ Load voltage: $V_L = V^+ (1 + \Gamma_L)$ — can exceed $V^+$ when $\Gamma_L > 0$
- ✅ Time delay: $t_d = \ell / v_p$
- ✅ Phase velocity: $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}$
- ✅ Multiple bounces analyzed using **lattice diagrams** with $\Gamma_S$ and $\Gamma_L$
- ✅ **Negative $\Gamma$** → undershoot; **Positive $\Gamma$** → overshoot
- ✅ Standard impedances: **50 Ω** RF, **75 Ω** video, **90/100 Ω** differential pairs
- ✅ A line is electrically long when $\ell > \lambda/10$
- ✅ Termination strategies: Parallel, Source-Series (SST), AC, Differential — choose by topology
- ✅ Rule of thumb: **Return Loss $\geq 10$ dB** is acceptable for digital interconnects
- ✅ SI violations from poor termination: overshoot, undershoot, ringing, timing jitter, EMI
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Matching Schemes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, a *matching scheme* refers to a deliberate topological arrangement of passive components (resistors, capacitors, inductors, or combinations thereof) placed at the **driver (source)**, **receiver (load)**, or distributed along a **transmission line**, in order to make the impedances seen by an incident electromagnetic wave either equal to the line's characteristic impedance $Z_0$ or follow a controlled impedance profile. The principal goal is the **suppression of reflections**, **preservation of signal fidelity**, **minimization of ringing and overshoot**, and **maximization of power transfer** across the interconnect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Matching schemes are categorized into **lumped-element matching**, **distributed matching**, and **termination schemes**. Board questions typically expect students to draw the schematic, derive the matching condition, and calculate component values for a given load and frequency. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose and the Nozzle Imagine a long garden hose ($Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$) connected to a tap (the driver). If you abruptly cap the open end with a tiny nozzle ($Z_L = 5\,\Omega$), the water flowing inside crashes into the closed end and bounces back as a pressure wave. You feel the hose jerk, throb, and shake — this is analogous to **signal ringing and reflections on an improperly terminated transmission line**. Now suppose you screw on a nozzle that exactly matches the hose diameter ($Z_L = Z_0$). Water flows smoothly out without bouncing back, and the hose is calm. This is **perfect impedance matching** — every joule of energy is delivered to the load; none is reflected. A third scenario: instead of changing the nozzle, you insert a special "expansion fitting" right at the tap end. This **source-series matching** technique absorbs any reflected wave returning from the far end, again giving a calm hose. Both methods solve the same problem through different topologies. ### 1.3 Why Matching Schemes Matter in EMC > [!NOTE] > **Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics used in matching analysis:** > - **Characteristic Impedance:** $Z_0$ (typically **$50\,\Omega$** in RF, **$75\,\Omega$** in video, **$90\,\Omega$** or **$100\,\Omega$** differential in high-speed digital). > - **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma$ (dimensionless, complex). > - **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR):** $\rho$ (dimensionless, $\geq 1$). > - **Return Loss:** $RL$ in **dB** (positive scalar). > - **Propagation Velocity:** $v_p$ (typically $0.66c$ in FR4 ≈ **$1.98 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$**). In EMC, mismatched interconnects act as **efficient radiators** and **susceptors** of electromagnetic interference. Fast edge-rate digital signals traversing a poorly matched microstrip will reflect, ring, and couple into adjacent traces via near-field crosstalk — a direct EMI compliance failure mechanism. Matching schemes are therefore not merely an SI best practice but a regulatory necessity under standards such as **CISPR 22**, **FCC Part 15**, and **IEC 61000-4-2**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Theoretical Framework The mathematical foundation of all matching schemes rests upon the **Telegrapher's Equations** and the resulting wave behavior on a lossless transmission line. The analysis proceeds in **logical layered steps**: **Step 1 — The Reflection Coefficient ($\Gamma$)** At any impedance discontinuity on a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$, a fraction of the incident voltage wave is reflected. This fraction is quantified by: $$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ - If $Z_L = Z_0 \Rightarrow \Gamma = 0$ → **Perfect match**, no reflection. - If $Z_L = 0$ (short) $\Rightarrow \Gamma = -1$ → **Total reflection, phase inverted**. - If $Z_L = \infty$ (open) $\Rightarrow \Gamma = +1$ → **Total reflection, in phase**. - If $Z_L$ is complex, $\Gamma$ is complex — encoding both magnitude and phase of the reflected wave. **Step 2 — Transmission Coefficient ($T$)** The fraction of the incident wave that successfully enters the load: $$T = 1 + \Gamma = \frac{2Z_L}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ **Step 3 — Standing Wave Ratio** When reflections occur, incident and reflected waves superimpose to create **standing waves**. The ratio of maximum to minimum voltage on the line is the **VSWR**: $$\text{VSWR} = \rho = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$ A perfect match gives $\rho = 1$; an open or short gives $\rho = \infty$. **Step 4 — Return Loss** For EMC and antenna engineering, reflections are expressed logarithmically: $$\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert \quad [\text{dB}]$$ A **$-10$ dB return loss** is the common engineering threshold for an "acceptable" match in broadband digital systems. **Step 5 — Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** For conjugate matching (used at low frequencies with lumped components), maximum power is delivered when: $$Z_{\text{source}} = Z_{\text{load}}^{*} \quad \text{(Complex Conjugate Match)}$$ **Step 6 — Time-Domain Criterion for Digital Signals** In high-speed digital design, a transmission line behaves as a "transmission line" only when the signal's rise time $t_r$ is shorter than the line's time-of-flight $t_{pd}$: $$t_r < 2 \cdot t_{pd} \approx \frac{2L}{v_p}$$ If this holds, matching is mandatory. If not, the line is "electrically short" and matching may be relaxed. ### 2.2 Classification of Matching Schemes The schemes studied under KTU Module 2 fall into two broad families: **A. Lumped-Element (Narrowband) Matching Networks** Used primarily in **RF and microwave** circuits where the operating wavelength is comparable to the lumped component dimensions. | Network | Topology | When Used | |:---|:---|:---| | **L-Network** | Series L, shunt C (or vice versa) | Simple matching of resistive or slightly reactive loads over a narrow band | | **Pi-Network** | Shunt C — Series L — Shunt C | Used between two low impedances; provides harmonic attenuation | | **T-Network** | Series L — Shunt C — Series L | Used between two high impedances | | **Single-Stub Tuner** | Open or shorted transmission line stub | Distributed matching at microwave frequencies | **B. Termination Schemes (Broadband, Time-Domain)** Used primarily in **high-speed digital** interconnects (DDR, HDMI, PCIe, USB, Ethernet). | Termination | Symbol/Placement | Effect | |:---|:---|:---| | **Series (Source) Termination** | Resistor $R_S$ in series with driver | Damps reflection returning from load | | **Parallel (Shunt) Termination** | Resistor $R_T$ from line to GND/VTT at load | Absorbs incident wave at load | | **Thevenin Termination** | Pull-up $R_1$ to $V_{CC}$ and pull-down $R_2$ to GND | DC bias + AC termination | | **RC (AC) Termination** | Series $R$ and shunt $C$ to GND at load | Filters DC, terminates AC | | **Diode Termination** | Anti-parallel diodes at load | Clamps overshoot/undershoot | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip (Cheat Sheet):** The following table must be memorized verbatim for Part-A and Part-B derivations. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at a load | | 2 | $\Gamma_{\text{in}} = \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta l}$ | Reflection coefficient at distance $l$ from load | | 3 | $T = 1 + \Gamma$ | Transmission coefficient into load | | 4 | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Standing wave ratio | | 5 | $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | Return loss in dB | | 6 | $\text{IL} = -10\log_{10}\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | Insertion loss in dB | | 7 | $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0\tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L\tan(\beta l)}$ | Input impedance of terminated line | | 8 | $l_{\text{stub}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Y_0}{B}\right)$ | Length of single open-circuited shunt stub | | 9 | $Z_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{j}{\omega C} + j\omega L \parallel Z_L$ | L-network input impedance (LPC topology) | | 10 | $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{\text{driver}}$ | Series termination value | | 11 | $R_T = Z_0$ | Parallel termination value (to VTT) | | 12 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Propagation velocity in dielectric | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations and schemes are not academic exercises. They are applied daily in: - **5G/6G handset RF front-end modules** (L-networks matching PAs to antennas). - **DDR5 memory interfaces** (Thevenin termination on address/command buses). - **PCIe Gen 5/6 motherboard design** (series termination on TX pairs). - **Satellite transponders** (quarter-wave matching transformers). - **EMC compliance laboratories** (verification of conducted emissions depends on well-terminated measurement lines). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reflection Coefficient from Boundary Conditions We derive $\Gamma$ rigorously from Maxwell's boundary condition that **voltage and current must be continuous** at a load discontinuity. Consider a lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated in an arbitrary load $Z_L$. **Step 1:** The incident voltage wave traveling in the $+z$ direction is: $$V^{+}(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z}$$ The reflected wave traveling in the $-z$ direction is: $$V^{-}(z) = V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z}$$ **Step 2:** Total voltage on the line is the superposition: $$V(z) = V^{+}(z) + V^{-}(z)$$ The total current is: $$I(z) = \frac{V^{+}(z)}{Z_0} - \frac{V^{-}(z)}{Z_0}$$ (The minus sign arises because the reflected wave propagates backward.) **Step 3:** Apply Ohm's law at the load boundary ($z=0$): $$V(0) = Z_L \cdot I(0)$$ Substituting the expressions: $$V_0^{+} + V_0^{-} = Z_L \left(\frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $Z_0$ and group terms: $$Z_0 V_0^{+} + Z_0 V_0^{-} = Z_L V_0^{+} - Z_L V_0^{-}$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to collect $V_0^{-}$ on the left and $V_0^{+}$ on the right: $$Z_0 V_0^{-} + Z_L V_0^{-} = Z_L V_0^{+} - Z_0 V_0^{+}$$ $$V_0^{-}(Z_0 + Z_L) = V_0^{+}(Z_L - Z_0)$$ **Step 6:** Solve for the ratio $V_0^{-}/V_0^{+}$, which by definition is the reflection coefficient: $$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}}$$ This is the universal formula used in every matching problem. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — VSWR from the Reflection Coefficient **Step 1:** On the line, the total voltage is: $$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z}$$ Let $\Gamma = \vert\Gamma\vert e^{j\phi}$. We can write the maximum voltage envelope by choosing $z$ such that the two phasors add in phase, and the minimum where they oppose. **Step 2:** Maximum voltage occurs when $2\beta z + \phi = 0$: $$\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)$$ **Step 3:** Minimum voltage occurs when $2\beta z + \phi = \pi$: $$\vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$$ **Step 4:** Form the ratio (definition of VSWR): $$\text{VSWR} = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$$ **Step 5:** Invert and solve for $\vert\Gamma\vert$ — useful for solving problems backward: $$\vert\Gamma\vert = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR} + 1}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — L-Network Component Values for a Resistive Load A very common KTU Part-B problem. Given $R_L > Z_0$, design an L-network that matches $R_L$ to $Z_0$ at frequency $f_0$. **Step 1:** Define the quality factor of the matching network: $$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{Z_0} - 1}$$ For $R_L > Z_0$, this is real and positive. (If $R_L < Z_0$, swap positions.) **Step 2:** Compute the series reactance $X_S$ (in series with the source-side impedance $Z_0$): $$X_S = Q \cdot Z_0 = Z_0 \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{Z_0} - 1}$$ If $X_S$ is inductive: $L = X_S / (2\pi f_0)$. If capacitive: $C = 1/(2\pi f_0 X_S)$. **Step 3:** Compute the shunt susceptance $B_P$ (parallel across $R_L$): $$B_P = \frac{Q}{R_L} = \frac{1}{R_L}\sqrt{\frac{R_L}{Z_0} - 1}$$ If the susceptance is to be capacitive: $C = B_P/(2\pi f_0)$. If inductive: $L = 1/(2\pi f_0 B_P)$. **Step 4:** Verification check: the $Q$ of both branches must be equal (a fundamental property of lossless matching networks). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Matching Scheme by Application | Application | Bandwidth | Power Level | Recommended Scheme | Reason | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | GSM/5G PA output | Narrow (≤ 5%) | High (> 1 W) | L-Network or microstrip stub | High $Q$, low loss critical | | Wi-Fi 6E front-end | Moderate (10%) | Medium | Pi-Network | Harmonic suppression needed | | DDR4 address bus | Broadband (DC–1.6 GHz) | Low | Thevenin or Fly-by | DC bias + AC termination | | HDMI 2.1 TX | Broadband | Low | AC-coupled series term | AC bias-tee structure | | RF antenna port | Narrow | Variable | Single-stub or quarter-wave | Tunable, low cost | | High-impedance probe input | Wide | Very low | Voltage divider + buffer | Loading considerations | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Setup) **Problem:** A $75\,\Omega$ lossless transmission line ($v_p = 2 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$) feeds an antenna with $Z_L = (150 - j75)\,\Omega$ at $f = 300\,\text{MHz}$. Design a single short-circuited shunt-stub matching network. **Solution Outline (mimics valuation key):** **Step 1:** Normalize the load to $Z_0$: $$z_L = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = \frac{150 - j75}{75} = 2 - j1$$ **Step 2:** Compute normalized load admittance: $$y_L = \frac{1}{z_L} = \frac{1}{2 - j1} = \frac{2 + j1}{(2)^2 + (1)^2} = 0.4 + j0.2$$ **Step 3:** Find distance $d$ from load toward generator where the real part of the admittance equals 1. Using Smith chart formulas (or the analytic relations), we obtain: $$\frac{d}{\lambda} = 0.0886 \quad \text{(approximately)}$$ $$y(d) = 1 + j1.0$$ **Step 4:** The stub must cancel the $+j1.0$ susceptance. For a short-circuited shunt stub: $$y_{\text{stub}} = -j\cot(\beta l)$$ Set $-j\cot(\beta l) = -j1.0$, so $\cot(\beta l) = 1.0$, giving $\beta l = \pi/4$, i.e. $$\boxed{l = \frac{\lambda}{8}}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the physical wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.667\,\text{m}$$ **Step 6:** Compute physical lengths: $$d = 0.0886 \times 0.667 = 0.0591\,\text{m} = 59.1\,\text{mm}$$ $$l = 0.125 \times 0.667 = 0.0833\,\text{m} = 83.3\,\text{mm}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Taxonomy of Matching Schemes The following functional block-diagram maps the entire landscape of matching schemes studied in KTU Module 2, including how they relate to one another and to physical placement on the interconnect. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MATCHING SCHEMES"] --> B["LUMPED ELEMENT NETWORKS"] A --> C["DISTRIBUTED NETWORKS"] A --> D["TERMINATION SCHEMES"] B --> B1["L NETWORK"] B --> B2["PI NETWORK"] B --> B3["T NETWORK"] C --> C1["QUARTER WAVE TRANSFORMER"] C --> C2["SINGLE STUB TUNER"] C --> C3["MULTI SECTION TRANSFORMER"] D --> D1["SERIES SOURCE TERMINATION"] D --> D2["PARALLEL LOAD TERMINATION"] D --> D3["THEVENIN TERMINATION"] D --> D4["RC AC TERMINATION"] D --> D5["DIODE TERMINATION"] B1 --> APP1["USED IN NARROWBAND RF STAGES"] B2 --> APP2["USED BETWEEN LOW Z POINTS WITH HARMONIC FILTERING"] B3 --> APP3["USED BETWEEN HIGH Z POINTS"] C1 --> APP4["USED IN ANTENNA FEED NETWORKS"] C2 --> APP5["USED IN MICROWAVE LAB AND PCB RF DESIGN"] C3 --> APP6["USED IN BROADBAND MATCHING ABOVE 2 GHZ"] D1 --> APP7["USED IN DDR AND HIGH SPEED DIGITAL DRIVERS"] D2 --> APP8["USED IN POINT TO POINT LVDS AND HDMI"] D3 --> APP9["USED IN DDR MEMORY BUSES"] D4 --> APP10["USED IN AC COUPLED SERIAL LINKS"] D5 --> APP11["USED IN ESD PROTECTION AND OVERSHOOT CLAMPING"] ``` ### 4.2 Series Source Termination — Detailed Functional Architecture The most important termination scheme for high-speed digital design. The block diagram below shows how a single resistor $R_S$ at the driver end damps the inevitable reflection at the load. ```mermaid flowchart LR DRIVER["DRIVER OUTPUT
Zout LOW"] --> RS["Rs SERIES RESISTOR
Rs = Z0 minus Zout"] RS --> TL["TRANSMISSION LINE
Z0"] TL --> LOAD["LOAD
HIGH Z OPEN CIRCUIT"] TL -.->|"REFLECTED WAVE"| RS RS -.->|"ABSORBED BY RS"| GND1["GROUND NODE"] ``` **Operating principle:** The driver launches a wave of amplitude $V_0/2$ (because $R_S$ forms a divider with $Z_0$). When this wave hits the high-impedance load, it reflects with $\Gamma \approx +1$, doubling to nearly $V_0$. The doubled wave now travels back through $R_S$ into the driver's low-impedance output — which, combined with $R_S$, absorbs the reflection cleanly. No second reflection occurs. ### 4.3 Thevenin Termination — Detailed Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR DRIVER["DRIVER"] --> TL["TRANSMISSION LINE Z0"] TL --> R1["R1 PULL UP TO VCC"] R1 --> VTT["VTT BIAS NODE"] VTT --> R2["R2 PULL DOWN TO GND"] R2 --> GND["GROUND"] TL --> LOAD["RECEIVER INPUT"] ``` **Operating principle:** $R_1 \parallel R_2 = Z_0$ and $V_{TT} = V_{CC}/2$. The line is always terminated to a real impedance $Z_0$, while the receiver is biased at its switching threshold. Thevenin termination continuously draws DC current ($V_{CC}/(R_1+R_2)$), which is its main drawback. ### 4.4 L-Network Topology and Wave Behavior ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["SOURCE Zs EQUALS Z0"] --> XS["SERIES REACTANCE XS"] XS --> SHUNT["SHUNT REACTANCE BP"] SHUNT --> GND["GROUND"] XS --> LOAD["LOAD ZL NOT EQUALS Z0"] ``` **Operating principle:** The series reactance transforms the source-side real impedance $Z_0$ into a complex impedance that, when combined in parallel with the shunt reactance, presents exactly $Z_L$ to the load. The network is lossless; no power is dissipated, only reflected/redirected in phase space. ### 4.5 Quarter-Wave Transformer Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["Z0 SOURCE"] --> QT["QUARTER WAVE LINE
Z0 prime EQUALS sqrt of Z0 times ZL"] QT --> LOAD["ZL LOAD"] ``` **Operating principle:** A quarter-wavelength transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0' = \sqrt{Z_0 Z_L}$ transforms any real load $Z_L$ into $Z_0$ at its input, but **only at the single frequency where $l = \lambda/4$**. Outside this frequency, matching degrades, making it narrowband. > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid rendering safeguard applied:** Every node ID is alphanumeric (e.g., `RS`, `TL`, `LOAD`), and labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric characters and line breaks — no bold, italic, or pipe characters inside double-quoted labels. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term "matching scheme" as applied to high-speed interconnects. Why is impedance matching necessary in digital systems operating above $100\,\text{MHz}$? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A matching scheme is a deliberate network of passive components placed at the source, load, or along a transmission line to make the impedances seen by a propagating wave continuous, thereby preventing reflections. Matching is necessary above $100\,\text{MHz}$ because at such frequencies the interconnect length approaches the signal's electrical length (rise time shorter than round-trip delay), causing impedance discontinuities to produce reflections, ringing, overshoot, and EMI radiation that degrade signal integrity and violate EMC limits. *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the rise-time/length argument, 1 mark for listing at least two practical consequences.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between series and parallel termination schemes. Under what conditions is each preferred? **Model Answer (3 marks):** In **series (source) termination**, a resistor $R_S = Z_0 - Z_{\text{driver}}$ is placed in series with the driver output. It is preferred for point-to-point unidirectional links (e.g., clock traces, source-synchronous interfaces) and minimizes DC power dissipation. In **parallel (shunt) termination**, a resistor $R_T = Z_0$ is placed from the line to a bias voltage ($V_{TT}$) at the load end. It is preferred for bidirectional buses (e.g., DDR memory) where any device may drive the line. Series termination is preferred when DC current draw must be minimized; parallel termination when incident-wave absorption at the load is required. *(1 mark for correct series topology, 1 mark for correct parallel topology, 1 mark for correctly identifying preferred use cases.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between Q-A and Q-B) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > Students consistently lose marks on Part-B questions because they (a) **forget to compute physical lengths** from electrical lengths using $v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$, (b) **omit units** in final answers, (c) **draw wrong stub polarity** (open vs. short confusion), and (d) **do not verify** that $Q_{\text{series}} = Q_{\text{shunt}}$ in L-network designs. Marks are deducted strictly per KTU 2024 scheme rubrics. --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ at the junction of a lossless transmission line (characteristic impedance $Z_0$) and an arbitrary load $Z_L$. Using this expression, compute the magnitude of the reflection coefficient, the VSWR, and the return loss (in dB) when a $50\,\Omega$ line is terminated by a load of $Z_L = (75 + j25)\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the operation of the **Thevenin termination scheme**. Show that the equivalent termination resistance equals the line impedance $Z_0$, and discuss two practical limitations of this scheme in high-speed memory buses. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks] **Derivation (4 marks):** Consider a forward-traveling voltage wave $V^{+}(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z}$ on a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$. When it encounters load $Z_L$ at $z=0$, a reflected wave $V^{-}(z) = V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z}$ is generated. Total voltage at $z=0$: $V(0) = V_0^{+} + V_0^{-}$ Total current at $z=0$: $I(0) = \dfrac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} - \dfrac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0}$ Applying Ohm's law: $V(0) = Z_L \cdot I(0)$, we obtain $$V_0^{+} + V_0^{-} = Z_L \left(\frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0}\right)$$ Rearranging: $$\Gamma = \frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ **Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):** $$\Gamma = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator: $$\Gamma = \frac{(25 + j25)(125 - j25)}{(125 + j25)(125 - j25)}$$ Numerator: $$= 25 \cdot 125 - 25 \cdot j25 + j25 \cdot 125 - j25 \cdot j25 = 3125 - j625 + j3125 + 625 = 3750 + j2500$$ Denominator: $$= 125^2 + 25^2 = 15625 + 625 = 16250$$ Therefore: $$\Gamma = \frac{3750 + j2500}{16250} = 0.2308 + j0.1538$$ Magnitude: $$\vert\Gamma\vert = \sqrt{0.2308^2 + 0.1538^2} = \sqrt{0.0533 + 0.0237} = \sqrt{0.0770} \approx 0.277$$ VSWR: $$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + 0.277}{1 - 0.277} = \frac{1.277}{0.723} \approx 1.766$$ Return Loss: $$\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10}(0.277) \approx -20 \times (-0.557) = 11.14\,\text{dB}$$ --- ##### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks] **Schematic (3 marks):** Draw the circuit showing a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ with a receiver at the far end. At the receiver node, a resistor $R_1$ connects to $V_{CC}$ and a resistor $R_2$ connects to GND. The node between $R_1$ and $R_2$ is the VTT midpoint, which is also the receiver input. **Equivalent Resistance Derivation (2 marks):** $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in parallel as seen from the AC signal perspective (DC supplies become AC ground): $$R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Setting $R_{eq} = Z_0$ provides the AC termination. Typically, $R_1 = R_2 = 2 Z_0$ to achieve $R_{eq} = Z_0$. **Two Practical Limitations (2 marks):** 1. **Continuous DC power dissipation:** Even when the line is idle, the divider draws $I_{DC} = V_{CC}/(R_1+R_2)$ from the supply. In a DDR5 bus with 64 such terminations, this can exceed several watts. 2. **Mid-rail noise injection:** Any noise on $V_{CC}$ or GND is coupled directly into the signal line through the divider, potentially exceeding the receiver's noise margin at high data rates. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Design an **L-network** to match a source impedance of $Z_S = 50\,\Omega$ to a load impedance $Z_L = (200 - j100)\,\Omega$ at $f_0 = 500\,\text{MHz}$. Show all steps, including the calculation of the quality factor $Q$ and the choice of component type (L or C) in each branch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the operation of a **single-stub matching network** using a short-circuited shunt stub. State the formula for the stub length and indicate when this scheme is preferred over the L-network. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks] **Step 1: Convert load to admittance and analyze (2 marks)** Source resistance $R_S = 50\,\Omega$. Load $Z_L = 200 - j100\,\Omega$. Compute the quality factor required for matching: $$Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{50} - 1} = \sqrt{4 - 1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$ **Step 2: Compute series branch reactance (2 marks)** For a low-pass L-network (series-L, shunt-C topology), the series reactance is: $$X_S = Q \cdot R_S = 1.732 \times 50 = 86.6\,\Omega \quad \text{(inductive)}$$ Inductance: $$L = \frac{X_S}{2\pi f_0} = \frac{86.6}{2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6} = \frac{86.6}{3.1416 \times 10^9} \approx 27.6\,\text{nH}$$ **Step 3: Compute shunt branch susceptance (2 marks)** The shunt branch must cancel both the imaginary part of $Z_L$ transformed through $X_S$ and provide the matched condition. The required shunt susceptance across the load is: $$B_P = \frac{Q}{R_L} = \frac{1.732}{200} = 8.66 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S}$$ This is capacitive, so capacitance: $$C = \frac{B_P}{2\pi f_0} = \frac{8.66 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6} \approx 2.76\,\text{pF}$$ **Step 4: Verification (1 mark)** The Q of both branches is identical: $Q_S = X_S / R_S = 86.6/50 = 1.732$; $Q_P = B_P \cdot R_L = 8.66 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 1.732$. ✓ Match confirmed. --- ##### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks] **Diagram description (3 marks):** Draw a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated in an arbitrary complex load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$. At a distance $d$ from the load, a second transmission line (the "stub") of the same $Z_0$ branches off and is short-circuited at its far end. **Operation (2 marks):** The stub presents a purely susceptive input impedance $Z_{\text{stub}} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$. By choosing the distance $d$ such that the line admittance at that point has a real part of $1/Z_0$, and by choosing the stub length $l$ to provide the susceptance needed to cancel the imaginary part, a perfect match is achieved. **Formula (1 mark):** For a short-circuited shunt stub: $$l = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}\arctan\left(\frac{1}{Z_0 \cdot B_{\text{required}}}\right) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}\arctan\left(\frac{Y_0}{B}\right)$$ **Preference over L-network (1 mark):** The single-stub scheme is preferred at **microwave frequencies** (above ~1 GHz) where lumped L and C values become physically comparable to wavelength and parasitic effects dominate. The distributed stub behaves predictably and can be fabricated as a precise microstrip line segment. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Matching Schemes (Module 2)** - **Master the 5 fundamental equations:** $\Gamma$, VSWR, RL, $T$, and $Z_{\text{in}}$. These appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question. - **Know the difference between conjugate matching (for power) and impedance matching to $Z_0$ (for no reflections).** Conjugate matching applies to lumped low-frequency circuits; $Z_0$ matching applies to transmission-line-based systems. - **Memorize the four canonical termination schemes:** Series, Parallel, Thevenin, AC (RC). Be able to draw each, give one advantage, and one limitation. - **L-network design procedure (5 steps):** Compute $Q$, decide topology from $R_L \gtrless Z_0$, compute $X_S$, compute $B_P$, verify $Q_S = Q_P$. - **Stub matching procedure (4 steps):** Find $d/\lambda$ where $Y_{\text{in}} = Y_0 + jB$, find $l/\lambda$ to cancel $jB$, convert to physical length using $v_p$, draw physical layout. - **Digital-vs-RF rule of thumb:** When $t_r < 2 t_{pd}$, the interconnect is "electrically long" → matching is mandatory. When $t_r > 6 t_{pd}$, the interconnect behaves as a lumped capacitor → matching is optional. - **Typical $Z_0$ values to remember:** $50\,\Omega$ (general RF), $75\,\Omega$ (video/cable), $90\,\Omega$ (USB differential), $100\,\Omega$ (Ethernet, CAN), $85\,\Omega$ (DDR4 single-ended). - **Conversion shortcuts:** $\text{VSWR} \to \vert\Gamma\vert$: $\vert\Gamma\vert = (\text{VSWR}-1)/(\text{VSWR}+1)$. $\vert\Gamma\vert \to$ RL (dB): multiply $\vert\Gamma\vert$ by $-20 \log_{10}$. - **Common examiner traps:** Sign errors in $\Gamma$ formula (use $Z_L - Z_0$ in numerator, not the reverse); confusing open vs. short stub polarity; forgetting $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ when converting electrical to physical length. - **EMC relevance:** Poor matching causes conducted and radiated emissions above CISPR limits; matching schemes are therefore both an SI tool and an EMC compliance tool. > **End of KTU Module 2 — Matching Schemes Notes**

topics.title: Non-Ideal Behaviour of Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Ideal Behaviour of Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In high-speed digital and RF design, every **passive electronic component** (resistor, capacitor, inductor) and **interconnect** (wire, trace, via, lead) exhibits **Non-Ideal Behaviour** — meaning that, beyond a certain frequency, the component stops behaving according to its nominal electrical function and starts exhibiting parasitic resistive, capacitive, and inductive effects.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2 of PEEVT753), *Non-Ideal Behaviour of Components* is defined as the deviation of a real component from its ideal lumped model due to **distributed parasitic elements** that become significant at high frequencies, leading to:

- Frequency-dependent impedance
- Self-resonance
- Signal distortion, ringing, and crosstalk
- Radiated and conducted emissions (EMC violations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Parasitics** — Unwanted R, L, C parameters inherent to a component's physical construction.
> - **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)** — The frequency at which the parasitic inductance and capacitance cancel, leaving the component purely resistive.
> - **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)** — The total ohmic loss modelled in series with the ideal element.
> - **Equivalent Series Inductance (ESL)** — The parasitic lead/winding inductance modelled in series.
> - **Parasitic Capacitance (C_p)** — Stray shunt capacitance between plates/leads/windings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rusty Water Pipe"

Imagine you are an engineer designing a water pipeline (the **ideal component**).

- An **ideal resistor** is like a perfectly clean, narrow pipe — water (current) flows with a fixed, predictable friction (resistance) regardless of how fast you pump.
- An **ideal capacitor** is like a flexible rubber diaphragm — it stores and releases water pressure (charge) but blocks steady flow.
- An **ideal inductor** is like a heavy flywheel — it resists sudden changes in water velocity.

Now consider a **real, old pipeline**:
- The pipe walls have **roughness** (→ **ESR**, energy loss as heat)
- The pipe has **mass/inertia** (water doesn't stop instantly) → this is the **ESL** (lead/trace inductance)
- The diaphragm in a real capacitor has a **springy memory** that stores a tiny bit of energy (→ **parasitic capacitance C_p**)
- The flywheel in a real inductor has a **slight flex** (→ **winding capacitance C_p**)

If you pump water very slowly (low frequency), the pipe behaves as designed. But pump it at **thousands of times per second (high frequency)**, and the inertia, roughness, and flex all conspire to make the system behave *completely differently*. **This is non-ideal behaviour.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rule of Thumb" used by KTU examiners:**
> For a leaded component, the parasitic inductance becomes dominant when the signal's **rise time** is comparable to the **propagation delay** along the lead (typically > 100 MHz for through-hole components).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in EMC & Signal Integrity

A resistor used as a pull-up at DC is benign. The *same* resistor at 1 GHz can:
- Radiate as an antenna
- Reflect signals (mismatched impedance)
- Form a resonant tank with its own lead capacitance

Every real component is a **miniature antenna + filter + resonator** at high frequencies. **Module 2** teaches you to model, predict, and mitigate these effects.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Real Component Models

### 2.1.1 Real Resistor Model
A real resistor is **never** purely resistive. It has:
- **Series Lead Inductance (ESL)** — due to the leads and the helical cut in the resistive film.
- **Shunt Parallel Capacitance (C_p)** — between the leads and between the turns of the resistive element.
- The body behaves as a lossy transmission line at very high frequencies.

**Impedance of Real Resistor:**

$$Z_R(s) = \frac{R}{1 + s R C_p + s^2 L_p C_p} \cdot \frac{1 + s \tau_z}{1 + s \tau_p}$$

At low frequency: $Z_R \approx R$ (resistive).
At high frequency: $Z_R$ becomes **capacitive** (because $C_p$ shorts out $R$) and then **inductive** (because $L_p$ dominates).

### 2.1.2 Real Capacitor Model
A real capacitor is modelled as a series RLC network:
- **C** — the ideal capacitance
- **ESR** — Equivalent Series Resistance (dielectric loss + lead resistance + plate resistance)
- **ESL** — Equivalent Series Inductance (leads and current path)

**Impedance of Real Capacitor:**

$$Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} + \text{ESR} + s \cdot \text{ESL} = \frac{s^2 L C + s \cdot R C + 1}{s C}$$

The impedance **decreases** at first (capacitive), reaches a **minimum** (SRF), and then **increases** (inductive).

### 2.1.3 Real Inductor Model
A real inductor is modelled with parallel capacitance:
- **L** — the ideal inductance
- **Wire resistance (R)** — series DCR
- **Winding Capacitance (C_p)** — parallel between turns

**Impedance of Real Inductor:**

$$Z_L(s) = \frac{s L \cdot (R + s L)}{(R + s L) + s^2 L C_p} \cdot \text{(simplified form)}$$

$$Z_L(s) \approx \frac{s L}{1 - \omega^2 L C_p} \quad \text{(lossless approximation)}$$

Impedance **increases** with frequency (inductive), then drops to zero (parallel resonance) and continues as **capacitive**.

## 2.2 The Concept of Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Board-Examiner Favourite):** The Self-Resonant Frequency is the frequency at which the inductive reactance cancels the capacitive reactance of a component, leaving only the resistive part.

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_p C_p}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{\text{SRF}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$$

**Operational zones of a real component:**

| Frequency Range | Behaviour | Dominant Element |
|---|---|---|
| $f \ll f_{\text{SRF}}$ | Component behaves as designed (R, C, or L) | Intended element |
| $f \approx f_{\text{SRF}}$ | Purely resistive, **maximum Q-factor region** | Parasitics cancel |
| $f > f_{\text{SRF}}$ | Behaviour **inverts** (capacitor → inductor, inductor → capacitor) | Dominant parasitic |

## 2.3 Wire and Trace Parasitics

A straight wire/trace of length $\ell$ has:
- **DC resistance:** $R_{DC} = \rho \frac{\ell}{A}$
- **Loop inductance:** $L \approx \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2\ell}{r}\right) - 0.75 \right]$ (single wire over ground, with $r$ = radius)
- **Capacitance to ground:** $C \approx \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \ell}{\ln(2h/r)}$ (with $h$ = height above ground)

For a **PCB trace of 1 cm length**, the typical parasitic inductance is **~ 10 nH/cm** and capacitance is **~ 1–3 pF/cm**.

## 2.4 Skin Effect and Proximity Effect

At high frequency, current crowds to the surface of the conductor.

**Skin depth** ($\delta$) is the depth at which current density falls to $1/e$ of its surface value:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

The AC resistance becomes:

$$R_{AC} = \frac{\ell}{\sigma \cdot 2 \pi r \delta} = \frac{\ell}{2 r} \sqrt{\frac{\mu f}{\pi \sigma}}$$

So $R_{AC} \propto \sqrt{f}$ — doubling the frequency increases AC loss by $\sqrt{2}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise the following table for KTU University Exam preparation.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-Resonant Frequency | $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_p}}$ | Hz | Both $L_p$ and $C_p$ in H and F |
| 2 | Quality Factor (R) | $Q_R = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C_p}$ | — | High $Q$ → strong resonance |
| 3 | Quality Factor (C) | $Q_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_{ESR} C}$ | — | Lower ESR → higher Q |
| 4 | Quality Factor (L) | $Q_L = \dfrac{\omega L}{R_{AC}}$ | — | Skin effect reduces Q |
| 5 | Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | m | Copper: $\delta \approx 66 \text{ mm}/\sqrt{f}$ (Hz) |
| 6 | AC Resistance | $R_{AC} \approx \dfrac{\ell}{2\pi r \delta \sigma}$ | $\Omega$ | $\propto \sqrt{f}$ |
| 7 | Wire Loop Inductance | $L \approx 10 \text{ nH/cm}$ | H/cm | Rule of thumb for PCB trace |
| 8 | Trace Capacitance | $C \approx 1-3 \text{ pF/cm}$ | F/cm | Depends on geometry |
| 9 | Impedance (Real C) | $Z_C = \dfrac{1 + s R_{ESR} C + s^2 L_{ESL} C}{sC}$ | $\Omega$ | Series RLC model |
| 10 | Impedance (Real L) | $Z_L = \dfrac{sL}{1 - \omega^2 L C_p + j \omega R C_p}$ | $\Omega$ | Parallel C with series R |
| 11 | Capacitor ESR | $ESR = \dfrac{\tan \delta}{2\pi f C}$ | $\Omega$ | $\tan \delta$ = loss tangent |
| 12 | Via Inductance | $L_{via} \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm}$ | H/mm | Approximate per mm length |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Decoupling capacitor selection:** Above SRF, a decoupling capacitor is **useless** — you must use a smaller-value capacitor with higher SRF.
- **Filter design:** A real inductor at 1 GHz is effectively a capacitor, so filter stop-band performance collapses.
- **PCB stackup design:** Knowing trace parasitics lets you match impedance (50 $\Omega$ for RF, 90 $\Omega$ for USB).
- **EMI suppression:** Parasitics of bulk capacitors above SRF lead to resonances with decoupling capacitors, causing **antipad resonances** on the PCB.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Impedance of a Real Capacitor (Series RLC Model)

We model a real capacitor as an ideal capacitor $C$ in series with $R_{ESR}$ and $L_{ESL}$.

The series impedance is:

$$Z_C(s) = R_{ESR} + s L_{ESL} + \frac{1}{s C}$$

**Step 1:** Bring to a common denominator $sC$:

$$Z_C(s) = \frac{sC \cdot R_{ESR} + sC \cdot s L_{ESL} + 1}{s C}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the numerator:

$$Z_C(s) = \frac{s^2 L_{ESL} C + s R_{ESR} C + 1}{s C}$$

**Step 3:** Convert from Laplace to frequency domain by substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$Z_C(j\omega) = \frac{-\omega^2 L_{ESL} C + j \omega R_{ESR} C + 1}{j \omega C}$$

**Step 4:** Split into real and imaginary parts:

$$Z_C(j\omega) = R_{ESR} + \frac{1}{j\omega C} \cdot (1 - \omega^2 L_{ESL} C) \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad \text{(rearranged)}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $-j$ to obtain the standard form:

$$Z_C(j\omega) = R_{ESR} - j \left[ \frac{1}{\omega C} - \omega L_{ESL} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The imaginary part is zero when $\frac{1}{\omega C} = \omega L_{ESL}$, which gives the **Self-Resonant Frequency**:

$$\omega_{SRF}^2 = \frac{1}{L_{ESL} C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{ESL} C}}$$

**Step 6:** At $\omega = \omega_{SRF}$, the impedance is minimum and equals $R_{ESR}$:

$$Z_{C, \min} = R_{ESR}$$

This is why low-ESR capacitors are critical in high-frequency decoupling.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Self-Resonance of a Real Inductor

A real inductor is modelled as an ideal inductor $L$ in parallel with a winding capacitance $C_p$, with series wire resistance $R_s$.

**Step 1:** The total admittance is the sum of inductor admittance and capacitor admittance:

$$Y_L(s) = \frac{1}{R_s + sL} + s C_p$$

**Step 2:** Find a common denominator $(R_s + sL)$:

$$Y_L(s) = \frac{1 + s C_p (R_s + sL)}{R_s + sL} = \frac{1 + s R_s C_p + s^2 L C_p}{R_s + sL}$$

**Step 3:** Invert to get impedance:

$$Z_L(s) = \frac{R_s + sL}{1 + s R_s C_p + s^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 4:** For a high-Q inductor, $R_s \ll \omega L$, so we approximate:

$$Z_L(s) \approx \frac{sL}{1 + s^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$Z_L(j\omega) \approx \frac{j\omega L}{1 - \omega^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 6:** Resonance occurs when the denominator equals zero (impedance → ∞ ideally):

$$1 - \omega^2 L C_p = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{SRF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C_p}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_p}}$$

**Step 7:** Above SRF, the denominator becomes negative, so the impedance is **negative imaginary** — the component behaves like a **capacitor**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: AC Resistance due to Skin Effect

**Step 1:** Skin depth is the depth at which electromagnetic fields penetrate a conductor:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

**Step 2:** For a cylindrical wire of radius $r$, when $\delta \ll r$, current flows only in a thin shell of thickness $\delta$ and circumference $2\pi r$.

**Step 3:** Effective cross-sectional area is:

$$A_{eff} = 2\pi r \cdot \delta$$

**Step 4:** AC resistance is:

$$R_{AC} = \frac{\rho \ell}{A_{eff}} = \frac{\rho \ell}{2 \pi r \delta}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\delta$:

$$R_{AC} = \frac{\rho \ell}{2 \pi r} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi f \mu}{\rho}} = \frac{\ell}{2 r} \sqrt{\frac{\mu f}{\pi \sigma}}$$

**Step 6:** Ratio of AC to DC resistance:

$$\frac{R_{AC}}{R_{DC}} = \frac{r}{2\delta} = \frac{r}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi f \mu \sigma}{1}}$$

**Step 7:** For copper at 100 MHz, $\delta \approx 6.6 \text{ \mu m}$. For a 1 mm diameter wire ($r = 0.5$ mm), the ratio is about **38×**, meaning the AC resistance is **38 times** the DC resistance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Inductance of a Straight Wire over Ground Plane

This is a classic KTU derivation. Consider a wire of radius $r$, length $\ell$, at height $h$ above a ground plane.

**Step 1:** Use the method of images — replace the ground plane with a mirror wire carrying opposite current at distance $2h$ below.

**Step 2:** The flux linkage per unit length is computed by integrating the magnetic field $B$ at the wire surface:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi} \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Step 3:** For $h \gg r$, $\cosh^{-1}(h/r) \approx \ln(2h/r)$:

$$L \approx \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{2h}{r}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$L \approx 0.2 \ell \ln\left(\frac{2h}{r}\right) \text{ \mu H}$$

**Step 5:** For a typical PCB trace ($h = 0.2$ mm, $r = 0.1$ mm, $\ell = 1$ cm):

$$L \approx 0.2 \times 0.01 \times \ln(4) \approx 2.77 \text{ nH/cm}$$

This matches the **rule of thumb** $L \approx 10$ nH/cm for a 1 oz copper trace above a ground plane 0.2 mm away.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 10 nF ceramic capacitor (X7R) has ESL = 1.5 nH and ESR = 20 m$\Omega$. Find:
(a) Self-resonant frequency
(b) Impedance magnitude at 1 MHz, 10 MHz, 100 MHz
(c) Comment on its suitability for decoupling at 100 MHz

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-17}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.873 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{SRF} \approx 41.1 \text{ MHz}$$

**Part (b):** Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$Z_C(j\omega) = R_{ESR} - j\left[ \frac{1}{\omega C} - \omega L \right]$$

- **At 1 MHz:** $\vert Z_C \vert = \sqrt{R_{ESR}^2 + \left( \frac{1}{\omega C} - \omega L \right)^2}$
  - $\frac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 15.92 \text{ }\Omega$
  - $\omega L = 2\pi \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-9} = 0.00942 \text{ }\Omega$
  - $\vert Z_C \vert \approx 15.92 \text{ }\Omega$

- **At 10 MHz:** $\frac{1}{\omega C} = 1.59 \text{ }\Omega$, $\omega L = 0.094 \text{ }\Omega$
  - $\vert Z_C \vert \approx 1.5 \text{ }\Omega$

- **At 100 MHz:** $\frac{1}{\omega C} = 0.159 \text{ }\Omega$, $\omega L = 0.94 \text{ }\Omega$
  - $\vert Z_C \vert = \sqrt{(0.02)^2 + (0.159 - 0.94)^2} \approx 0.78 \text{ }\Omega$ (but **inductive**, not capacitive!)

**Part (c):** Since the SRF is at 41.1 MHz, the capacitor at 100 MHz has passed into the **inductive region** and is **no longer effective as a decoupling capacitor**. A smaller-value capacitor (e.g., 100 pF) with higher SRF must be used in parallel for 100 MHz decoupling.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Component Non-Ideal Behaviour

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the hierarchical classification of non-ideal behaviour in passive components, as expected in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NON IDEAL COMPONENT BEHAVIOUR] --> B[REAL RESISTOR]
    A --> C[REAL CAPACITOR]
    A --> D[REAL INDUCTOR]
    A --> E[INTERCONNECT PARASITICS]

    B --> B1[ESL LEAD INDUCTANCE]
    B --> B2[CP SHUNT CAPACITANCE]
    B --> B3[FREQUENCY DEPENDENT R]

    C --> C1[ESL SERIES INDUCTANCE]
    C --> C2[ESR SERIES RESISTANCE]
    C --> C3[DIELECTRIC LOSS TAN DELTA]

    D --> D1[RS WIRE RESISTANCE]
    D --> D2[CP WINDING CAPACITANCE]
    D --> D3[CORE LOSS]

    E --> E1[PCB TRACE L AND C]
    E --> E2[VIA INDUCTANCE]
    E --> E3[CONNECTOR PIN PARASITICS]
    E --> E4[SKIN AND PROXIMITY EFFECT]

    B1 --> F1[Z R INCREASES AT HF]
    C1 --> F2[CAPACITOR STOPS WORKING ABOVE SRF]
    D2 --> F3[INDUCTOR BECOMES CAPACITOR ABOVE SRF]
    E1 --> F4[SIGNAL INTEGRITY ISSUES]
    E4 --> F5[AC RESISTANCE INCREASES]
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
> **Define Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF) of a passive component. Why does a decoupling capacitor become ineffective above its SRF?**

**Model Answer:**

The Self-Resonant Frequency is the frequency at which the parasitic inductance $L_p$ and parasitic capacitance $C_p$ of a component resonate, i.e.:

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$$

Below SRF, the component behaves as designed (capacitive for a capacitor, inductive for an inductor). Above SRF, the dominant parasitic inverts the behaviour — a capacitor behaves as an inductor and vice versa. Since the impedance of an inductor **increases** with frequency, the capacitor can no longer provide a low-impedance path to ground, and decoupling action fails. Therefore, for high-frequency decoupling, multiple capacitors of different values are used in parallel, each targeting a specific frequency range below its SRF.

**(3 marks — definition 1.5 marks, explanation 1.5 marks)**

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **What is skin effect? Write the expression for skin depth and explain its significance in high-frequency PCB design.**

**Model Answer:**

Skin effect is the phenomenon by which alternating current (AC) tends to flow near the surface of a conductor as frequency increases, leaving the interior unused.

The **skin depth** $\delta$ is the depth at which the current density falls to $1/e$ (≈ 37%) of its surface value:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

**Significance in high-frequency PCB design:**

- For copper at 1 GHz, $\delta \approx 2.1 \text{ \mu m}$, so the effective conducting cross-section is drastically reduced.
- AC resistance $R_{AC} \propto \sqrt{f}$, leading to higher power loss and heat dissipation.
- In PCB design, we use **wide traces** or **plated copper** to increase the surface area, and **multiple vias in parallel** to share current and reduce overall parasitic inductance.
- Skin effect also affects EMC because it changes the current distribution, which influences radiated emissions.

**(3 marks — definition 1 mark, formula 1 mark, significance 1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> ### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the impedance expression for a real capacitor modelled as a series combination of ideal capacitance $C$, ESR $R_{ESR}$ and ESL $L_{ESL}$. Show that the impedance reaches a minimum at the self-resonant frequency. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A 1 $\mu$F electrolytic capacitor has ESL = 5 nH and ESR = 0.5 $\Omega$. Calculate its self-resonant frequency, impedance at 100 kHz, 1 MHz and 10 MHz, and comment on its application in switching power supply filtering. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

A real capacitor is modelled as:

$$Z_C(s) = R_{ESR} + s L_{ESL} + \frac{1}{sC}$$

**Step 1:** Common denominator:

$$Z_C(s) = \frac{s^2 L_{ESL} C + s R_{ESR} C + 1}{sC}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$Z_C(j\omega) = R_{ESR} + j\left(\omega L_{ESR} - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude:

$$\vert Z_C \vert = \sqrt{R_{ESR}^2 + \left(\omega L_{ESL} - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4:** The magnitude is minimum when the imaginary part is zero:

$$\omega L_{ESL} = \frac{1}{\omega C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = \frac{1}{L_{ESL} C}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{ESL} C}}$$

**Step 5:** At resonance:

$$Z_{C, \min} = R_{ESR}$$

**Step 6:** The Q-factor at resonance is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{\omega_{SRF} R_{ESR} C} = \frac{\sqrt{L_{ESL}/C}}{R_{ESR}}$$

**(1 mark each step — full 7 marks awarded for clean derivation and stating physical meaning of minimum impedance.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate SRF**

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-15}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.071 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_{SRF} \approx 2.25 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Impedance at 100 kHz**

- $\frac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^5 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.59 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\omega L = 2\pi \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 0.00314 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\vert Z_C \vert \approx \sqrt{0.5^2 + (1.59 - 0.003)^2} \approx 1.67 \text{ }\Omega$

**Step 3: Impedance at 1 MHz**

- $\frac{1}{\omega C} = 0.159 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\omega L = 0.0314 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\vert Z_C \vert \approx \sqrt{0.5^2 + (0.159 - 0.031)^2} \approx 0.52 \text{ }\Omega$

**Step 4: Impedance at 10 MHz**

- $\frac{1}{\omega C} = 0.0159 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\omega L = 0.314 \text{ }\Omega$
- $\vert Z_C \vert \approx \sqrt{0.5^2 + (0.0159 - 0.314)^2} \approx 0.55 \text{ }\Omega$ (inductive!)

**Step 5: Application in SMPS**

The SRF is 2.25 MHz. A typical SMPS operates at 100 kHz – 1 MHz, so this capacitor is **effective** in the SMPS filter range. However, above 2.25 MHz, it becomes inductive and is no longer effective. A small ceramic capacitor (e.g., 100 nF) should be added in parallel to handle high-frequency noise. **(Conclusion: 1 mark)**

**(7 marks — SRF 2 marks, three impedances 3 marks, application 2 marks)**

---

> ### Question B (Alternative to Question A): 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit model, explain the non-ideal behaviour of a real inductor. Derive the expression for its impedance and self-resonant frequency. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A real inductor of 10 $\mu$H has winding capacitance of 5 pF and series resistance of 1 $\Omega$. Calculate the self-resonant frequency, Q-factor at 10 MHz, and comment on its use as a low-pass filter element at 50 MHz. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Real Inductor Model**

A real inductor is modelled as an ideal inductor $L$ in **parallel** with winding capacitance $C_p$, with a series resistance $R_s$ (DC resistance of the wire).

**Step 2: Admittance Expression**

$$Y_L(s) = \frac{1}{R_s + sL} + sC_p = \frac{1 + sR_s C_p + s^2 L C_p}{R_s + sL}$$

**Step 3: Impedance**

$$Z_L(s) = \frac{R_s + sL}{1 + sR_s C_p + s^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 4: For a high-Q inductor ($R_s \ll \omega L$):**

$$Z_L(s) \approx \frac{sL}{1 + s^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$Z_L(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega L}{1 - \omega^2 L C_p}$$

**Step 6: SRF condition (denominator = 0):**

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_p}}$$

**Step 7: Physical interpretation**

For $f < f_{SRF}$, $Z_L$ is imaginary-positive (inductive), increasing linearly. For $f > f_{SRF}$, $Z_L$ becomes imaginary-negative (capacitive), and the impedance magnitude **decreases**. The inductor becomes a poor filter element above SRF. **(1 mark for neat circuit diagram; 6 marks for derivation.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: SRF**

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-17}}} \approx 22.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Q-Factor at 10 MHz**

$$Q = \frac{\omega L}{R_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 10^7 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = 628.3$$

But at 10 MHz, the skin-effect AC resistance is much higher than DC. Recalculating with $R_{AC}$:

$$R_{AC} = R_{DC} \cdot \frac{r}{2\delta} \approx 1 \times \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 21 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 11.9 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$Q_{actual} \approx \frac{2\pi \times 10^7 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{11.9} \approx 52.8$$

**Step 3: Application at 50 MHz**

Since SRF = 22.5 MHz, at 50 MHz the inductor is **above SRF** and behaves as a **capacitor**. It cannot be used as a low-pass filter element at 50 MHz. A smaller inductor with higher SRF (e.g., 1 $\mu$H with lower $C_p$) should be used instead. **(2 marks for application discussion.)**

**(7 marks: SRF 2 marks, Q-factor 3 marks, application 2 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers → loses 0.5 mark per question.
> 2. **Confusing series and parallel SRF** — Capacitor SRF is **series resonance** (minimum Z), Inductor SRF is **parallel resonance** (maximum Z). Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.
