> 3. **Ignoring the boundary condition** $f = f_{SRF}$ where $Z = R$ for capacitor, $Z = R_p$ (parallel resistance) for inductor. Many students forget to substitute and find the actual impedance value at SRF.
> 4. **Not mentioning practical PCB parasitics** (10 nH/cm, 1–3 pF/cm) in design questions — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for engineering practical knowledge.
> 5. **Writing the wrong Q-factor formula** — Quality factor differs for capacitor (Q = 1/$\omega R C$) and inductor (Q = $\omega L / R$). Memorise both.
> 6. **Forgetting skin effect** in AC resistance — at 100 MHz, $R_{AC} \neq R_{DC}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Non-Ideal Behaviour of Components**

- [x] Every real component has **parasitic L, R, and C** in addition to its nominal value.
- [x] **SRF formula:** $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$ — applies to both real capacitors and inductors.
- [x] **Capacitor above SRF → inductor**; **Inductor above SRF → capacitor** (behaviour inverts).
- [x] **ESR** (Equivalent Series Resistance) and **ESL** (Equivalent Series Inductance) for capacitors.
- [x] **Wire/trace inductance** ≈ 10 nH/cm, **capacitance** ≈ 1–3 pF/cm on a typical PCB.
- [x] **Skin effect** causes $R_{AC} \propto \sqrt{f}$; skin depth $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / \pi f \mu}$.
- [x] **Via inductance** ≈ 1 nH/mm of length.
- [x] **Q-factor** is the inverse of loss; high-Q components are more frequency-selective.
- [x] Real resistors become **capacitive** at very high frequency due to shunt parasitic capacitance.
- [x] **Decoupling strategy:** use multiple capacitor values in parallel to cover a wide frequency range, each operating below its own SRF.
- [x] **Loss tangent (tan $\delta$)** quantifies dielectric loss in capacitors.
- [x] **EM consequence:** parasitic resonance can amplify noise, cause ringing, and produce radiated emissions.
- [x] **Frequency-domain analysis** (Bode plot of $\vert Z \vert$) is the standard way to visualise non-ideal behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *passive two-terminal electrical component* that implements electrical resistance as a circuit element. In the domain of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, a resistor is characterized not only by its DC resistance $R$ (in Ohms, $\Omega$) but also by its **parasitic inductance** ($L_p$), **parasitic capacitance** ($C_p$), and **frequency-dependent skin-effect resistance** ($R_{ac}$).

In high-speed digital and RF design, a resistor is mathematically modeled as a **lumped-element network** containing the desired resistance $R$ in parallel with a shunt capacitance $C_p$ and in series with a lead/trace inductance $L_p$. This makes the resistor a **frequency-selective component** rather than a pure resistive element, which is the central theme in KTU Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In EMC/SI, a resistor is studied for its **non-ideal behavior** at high frequencies. The bandwidth of a resistor is limited to a frequency at which the parasitic reactance equals the nominal resistance. This boundary frequency is sometimes called the **"resistor's usable bandwidth"** or **self-resonant frequency (SRF)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Model

Imagine water flowing through a **narrow garden hose**:

| Parameter | Electrical Equivalent | Water Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Resistance $R$ | Friction in pipe | Narrowing the hose diameter |
| Inductance $L_p$ | Water hammer / inertia | Sudden valve closure causes pressure spike |
| Capacitance $C_p$ | Sloshing / water storage | A bulge/bucket in the pipe stores fluid |
| Frequency $f$ | Rate of valve toggling | How fast you turn the tap on/off |

- At **low frequencies (DC)**: The water flows smoothly, and only the **narrowness** of the hose matters → only $R$ is effective.
- At **high frequencies (AC/RF)**: The **inertia** (water hammer) and **bulge** (capacitance) start to dominate, distorting the flow → parasitic elements dominate.

This analogy immediately tells us **why** a $100\ \Omega$ resistor stops acting like a $100\ \Omega$ resistor when the signal frequency approaches the **GHz** range — exactly the EMC/SI problem domain.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard resistance range:** $1\ \text{m}\Omega$ to $10^{12}\ \Omega$ (1 GΩ in special precision/high-voltage types)
- **Resistivity of common materials:** $\rho_{\text{nichrome}} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $\rho_{\text{carbon}} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- **Characteristic impedance of free space:** $Z_0 = 376.73\ \Omega$ (≈ $377\ \Omega$) — used as a **reference** for terminator resistor selection
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ — used in transmission-line delay calculations tied to SI
- **Standard tolerance codes:** $\pm 1\%$, $\pm 5\%$, $\pm 10\%$ (EIA E-series)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Non-Ideal Resistor Model — Three Parasitic Regimes

A real resistor exhibits **three distinct behavioral regimes** as frequency increases. This is the most important concept in this module for KTU examinations.

### Regime 1 — Low Frequency (Purely Resistive)
- Reactances $X_L = \omega L_p$ and $X_C = \tfrac{1}{\omega C_p}$ are negligible.
- $Z(f) \approx R$
- Applicable for: Power supplies, audio circuits, low-speed digital (< 10 MHz).

### Regime 2 — Mid Frequency (Capacitive Dominance)
- The shunt capacitance $C_p$ (typically $0.1$ to $1.5\ \text{pF}$ for SMD) creates a low-impedance path to ground.
- Impedance **decreases** below $R$ as frequency rises.
- The resistor behaves like an **RC low-pass filter**.

### Regime 3 — High Frequency (Inductive Dominance + Self-Resonance)
- Lead inductance $L_p$ (typically $1$ to $10\ \text{nH}$ for SMD 0402/0603) causes $X_L$ to rise.
- The $L_p$–$C_p$ combination forms a **series resonant circuit** (or parallel, depending on topology).
- At the **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)**, $\vert Z \vert$ peaks (or dips, depending on topology), and the resistor is no longer useful.
- Above SRF, the component behaves like a **pure inductor** (or capacitor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EMC:** At GHz frequencies, even a $0\ \Omega$ "jumper" resistor presents $\sim 1\ \text{nH}$ of parasitic inductance, giving $X_L \approx 6.28\ \Omega$ at 1 GHz — enough to cause **signal integrity violations, reflections, and radiated emissions** in transmission-line systems.

## 2.2 Constructional Types of Resistors (Relevant to EMC/SI)

| Type | Construction | Frequency Limit | EMC/SI Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wirewound** | Nichrome wire on ceramic | ~ 50 kHz | Poor at RF — high $L_p$ |
| **Carbon Composition** | Carbon granules + binder | ~ 1 MHz | Poor — noisy, high $L_p$ |
| **Carbon Film** | Thin carbon film on ceramic | ~ 100 MHz | Moderate — used in general circuits |
| **Metal Film** | NiCr film on ceramic | ~ 1 GHz | Good — low noise, moderate $C_p$ |
| **Thick-Film (Chip)** | Ruthenium oxide on alumina | ~ 10 GHz | Standard for SMD, balanced perf. |
| **Thin-Film (Chip)** | NiCr or TaN sputtered | ~ 10+ GHz | Best for precision high-freq. |
| **Foil (Bulk Metal)** | Constantan/NiCr foil | ~ 1 GHz | Ultra-precision, low TCR |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for KTU examination questions on resistors. **Memorize these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Power Dissipation | $P = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = V \cdot I$ | W | Derated for temperature |
| 3 | Resistivity | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | $\rho$: $\Omega\cdot$m, $\ell$: m, $A$: m² |
| 4 | Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | m | $f \uparrow \Rightarrow \delta \downarrow$ |
| 5 | AC Resistance | $R_{ac} \approx R_{dc} \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{2 \delta}$ (for $w \gg \delta$) | $\Omega$ | $w$: conductor width |
| 6 | Lead Inductance (approx.) | $L_p \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot \ell}{2\pi} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2\ell}{r}\right) - 0.75\right]$ | H | Wire resistor approximation |
| 7 | SMD Parasitic $L_p$ (typical) | $L_p \approx 0.5$ to $5\ \text{nH}$ | H | Package-size dependent |
| 8 | SMD Parasitic $C_p$ (typical) | $C_p \approx 0.05$ to $1.5\ \text{pF}$ | F | Smaller pkg = smaller $C_p$ |
| 9 | Self-Resonant Freq. (SRF) | $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_p}}$ | Hz | Lumped SRF of resistor |
| 10 | Total Impedance (Series $R$-$L_p$-$C_p$) | $Z(s) = R + s L_p + \dfrac{1}{s C_p}$ | $\Omega$ | Laplace form |
| 11 | Impedance Magnitude | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L_p - \tfrac{1}{\omega C_p}\right)^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Use $\vert\cdot\vert$ for magnitude |
| 12 | Voltage Divider | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | V | Ideal resistor case |
| 13 | Termination Reflection Coeff. | $\Gamma = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}$ | dimensionless | $R_L = Z_0 \Rightarrow \Gamma = 0$ |
| 14 | Thermal Noise (Johnson) | $V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 15 | Temperature Coefficient | $TCR = \dfrac{1}{R_0} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{\Delta T}$ | $\text{ppm}/^\circ\text{C}$ | Sign indicates direction |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Series Termination in High-Speed Digital:** A resistor $R_s$ placed at the driver output with $R_s = Z_0 - Z_{driver}$ absorbs reflections on transmission lines — the classic **series-damping topology** in PCIe, USB 3.0, HDMI.
2. **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors:** Used in I²C, SPI, and asynchronous buses to define logic levels when no driver is active; value selection involves a trade-off between **power dissipation** and **signal rise-time**.
3. **Impedance Matching Pads (L-Pad / T-Pad):** Attenuator networks in RF front-ends using resistors for inter-stage matching.
4. **Current Sensing:** Low-value ($< 1\ \Omega$) precision resistors with low TCR for current monitoring in power electronics.
5. **EMC Filtering:** RC snubbers across switching devices (relays, MOSFETs) to suppress EMI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impedance of a Real Resistor (Series R-L-C Model)

A real wire-ended or leaded resistor can be modeled as a **series combination** of $R$, $L_p$, and $C_p$ (the shunt $C_p$ is between the leads, leading to a parallel branch in some topologies; we derive the more common **series-equivalent** first).

**Step 1 — Start with the complex impedance of each element:**

$$Z_R = R$$

$$Z_{L_p} = j \omega L_p$$

$$Z_{C_p} = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_p} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C_p}$$

**Step 2 — Sum them in series:**

$$Z(\omega) = R + j \omega L_p - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_p}$$

$$Z(\omega) = R + j \left( \omega L_p - \dfrac{1}{\omega C_p} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the magnitude (this is what an LCR meter or VNA measures):**

$$\vert Z(\omega) \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left( \omega L_p - \dfrac{1}{\omega C_p} \right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF):**

At SRF, the imaginary part vanishes (the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel):

$$\omega_{SRF} L_p = \dfrac{1}{\omega_{SRF} C_p}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega_{SRF}$:**

$$\omega_{SRF}^2 = \dfrac{1}{L_p C_p}$$

$$\omega_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L_p C_p}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hz:**

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (typical 0805 SMD resistor):**

Given $L_p = 2\ \text{nH} = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{H}$ and $C_p = 0.5\ \text{pF} = 5 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F}$:

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-9})(5 \times 10^{-13})}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10^{-21}}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{1.987 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{SRF} \approx 5.03\ \text{GHz}}$$

**Conclusion:** A standard 0805 SMD resistor is usable as a pure resistor only **well below 1 GHz**. Above 1 GHz, it is an **RLC network**, not a resistor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skin Effect and AC Resistance

The DC resistance of a uniform conductor of length $\ell$, cross-section $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$R_{dc} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A}$$

At high frequencies, current is confined within one skin depth $\delta$ from the surface. The effective cross-sectional area shrinks to $A_{eff} = 2 \ell \delta$ (for a wide, flat conductor of width $w \gg \delta$).

**Step 1 — Write the effective resistance:**

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{2 \ell \delta} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \delta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the skin depth formula $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$:**

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi f \mu}{\rho}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ac} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\pi f \mu \rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio to DC resistance for a circular wire of radius $r$:**

For a round wire ($A = \pi r^2$):

$$\dfrac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \dfrac{r}{2 \delta} \quad \text{(for } r \gg \delta \text{)}$$

This is a key KTU result — AC resistance grows proportionally to the **square root of frequency**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reflection Coefficient for a Resistor Termination

Consider a transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated with a resistor $R_L$. A forward-traveling wave $V^+$ arrives at the termination.

**Step 1 — Define the reflection coefficient $\Gamma$:**

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{V^-}{V^+}$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (no transmission beyond termination):**

The total voltage is $V = V^+ + V^-$ and the total current is $I = (V^+ - V^-)/Z_0$ (current direction convention).

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law at the load ($V = I \cdot R_L$):**

$$V^+ + V^- = R_L \cdot \dfrac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $\Gamma = V^-/V^+$:**

$$V^+ + V^- = \dfrac{R_L}{Z_0} V^+ - \dfrac{R_L}{Z_0} V^-$$

$$V^- \left(1 + \dfrac{R_L}{Z_0}\right) = V^+ \left(\dfrac{R_L}{Z_0} - 1\right)$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{V^-}{V^+} = \dfrac{\dfrac{R_L}{Z_0} - 1}{1 + \dfrac{R_L}{Z_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $Z_0$:**

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}}$$

**Step 6 — Special cases (KTU must-know):**
- $R_L = Z_0 \Rightarrow \Gamma = 0$ (matched — no reflection)
- $R_L = \infty$ (open) $\Rightarrow \Gamma = +1$ (full positive reflection)
- $R_L = 0$ (short) $\Rightarrow \Gamma = -1$ (full negative reflection)
- $R_L \neq Z_0$ (mismatch) $\Rightarrow \vert \Gamma \vert < 1$ (partial reflection)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Resistance vs. Reactance vs. Frequency

| Frequency Range | Dominant Element | $\vert Z \vert$ Behavior | Phase $\angle Z$ | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f \ll f_a$ (very low) | $R$ | $\vert Z \vert \approx R$ | $0^\circ$ | Behaves as ideal resistor |
| $f_a < f < f_{SRF}$ | $C_p$ | $\vert Z \vert \downarrow$ (decreasing) | Slightly negative | RC low-pass action |
| $f = f_{SRF}$ | $L_p$ and $C_p$ resonate | $\vert Z \vert \approx R$ (peak/dip) | $0^\circ$ | Usable upper limit |
| $f > f_{SRF}$ | $L_p$ | $\vert Z \vert \uparrow$ (increasing) | Positive | Behaves as inductor |

Where the lower "capacitive corner" $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C_p}$ marks the onset of capacitance dominance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lumped Equivalent Circuit of a Real Resistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal 1] --> B[R: Nominal Resistance]
    B --> C[Lp: Parasitic Inductance]
    C --> D[Terminal 2]
    A -.-> E[Cp: Parasitic Capacitance]
    E -.-> D

    classDef resStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef indStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef capStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class B resStyle
    class C indStyle
    class E capStyle
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows a real resistor as a series $R$–$L_p$ branch (top path) with a shunt $C_p$ (dashed path). This is the canonical **lumped-element parasitic model** used in SPICE and VNA-based characterization.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Series Termination Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DRIVER [Driver Stage]
        A[ASIC/FPGA Output Buffer]
    end

    subgraph TERMINATION [Series Termination]
        B[Rs: Series Resistor]
    end

    subgraph TL [Transmission Line]
        C[PCB Microstrip / Stripline Z0 equals 50 ohm]
    end

    subgraph RECEIVER [Receiver End]
        D[Receiver Input High Impedance]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D

    classDef drv fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef tl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000

    class A drv
    class B term
    class C tl
    class D rx
```

**Interpretation:** A series resistor $R_s$ is placed between the driver and the transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$. The condition $R_s + Z_{driver} = Z_0$ absorbs the incident wave, eliminating the **secondary reflection** at the receiver.

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Resistor for EMC/SI Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Resistor Type] --> B{Frequency of Operation?}
    B -->|less than 1 MHz| C[Carbon Film or Wirewound acceptable]
    B -->|1 MHz to 100 MHz| D[Metal Film or Thick-Film SMD]
    B -->|greater than 100 MHz| E[Thin-Film SMD or Foil]
    C --> F{Power Requirement?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|less than 100 mW| G[0402 or 0603 SMD]
    F -->|100 mW to 1 W| H[1206 or 2512 SMD]
    F -->|greater than 1 W| I[Wirewound or Power SMD]

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    class A start
    class B,D,E,F dec
    class C,G,H,I end
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart guides a designer through the **decision tree** for selecting the right resistor type based on frequency and power constraints — a high-yield KTU topic.

## 4.4 Impedance vs. Frequency Curve (Conceptual Bode Plot)

```
|Z|  ↑            ___________
   |           /
   |         /     ← Inductive (L_p) region
   |        /
R -|-------◯ ← f_SRF (Self-Resonant Frequency)
   |     /
   |   /        ← Capacitive (C_p) region
   | /
   |/_______________→ Frequency f
   DC          f_a      f_SRF
   ↑
   Pure R region
```

**Key markers:**
- $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C_p}$ — start of capacitance dominance
- $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$ — usable upper limit
- Above $f_{SRF}$ — inductive behavior, **resistor no longer functions as a resistor**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define the self-resonant frequency (SRF) of a resistor and explain why it is the upper usable frequency limit.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **self-resonant frequency (SRF)** of a resistor is the frequency at which the parasitic inductance $L_p$ and parasitic shunt capacitance $C_p$ of the resistor resonate, making the net reactance zero.

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}$$

> Beyond the SRF, the resistor no longer behaves as a resistive element — it acts as a pure **inductor** (for a series-R-L-C model) or a pure **capacitor** (for a parallel-R-L-C model). The impedance magnitude **diverges** from the nominal resistance value $R$, and the phase angle of the impedance becomes significant. Therefore, the SRF is the **upper usable frequency limit** for the component.

> For typical 0805 SMD resistors, $L_p \approx 2\ \text{nH}$ and $C_p \approx 0.5\ \text{pF}$ give $f_{SRF} \approx 5\ \text{GHz}$, meaning such resistors are usable only up to **roughly 1 GHz** in practice (typically 1/3 of SRF for safety).

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: A PCB trace has a characteristic impedance of $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$. What value of termination resistor must be used to prevent signal reflections, and what is the resulting reflection coefficient?**

**Model Answer:**

> To prevent signal reflections on a transmission line, the termination resistor $R_L$ must equal the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of the line.

$$R_L = Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$$

> The reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ at the termination is:

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0} = \dfrac{50 - 50}{50 + 50} = \dfrac{0}{100} = 0$$

> $\Gamma = 0$ indicates **no reflection** — the entire incident wave is absorbed by the termination resistor as heat, ensuring **signal integrity**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**(a)** With the help of a suitable circuit model, derive the impedance of a real (non-ideal) resistor and obtain an expression for its self-resonant frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $50\ \Omega$ metal-film resistor has parasitic inductance $L_p = 3\ \text{nH}$ and shunt capacitance $C_p = 0.8\ \text{pF}$. Calculate: (i) the self-resonant frequency, (ii) the magnitude of impedance at $f = 2\ \text{GHz}$, and (iii) the phase angle at $f = 2\ \text{GHz}$. Comment on the resistor's suitability at 2 GHz. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Impedance and SRF

**Step 1 — State the Lumped Equivalent Circuit:**

A real resistor is modeled as a series combination of $R$, $L_p$ (parasitic inductance), and a shunt capacitance $C_p$ across the terminals.

**Step 2 — Express the impedance of each element in phasor form:**

- $Z_R = R$
- $Z_{L_p} = j \omega L_p$
- $Z_{C_p} = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_p}$

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain the total impedance:**

$$Z(\omega) = R + j \omega L_p + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_p}$$

$$Z(\omega) = R + j \left( \omega L_p - \dfrac{1}{\omega C_p} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the impedance:**

$$\vert Z(\omega) \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left( \omega L_p - \dfrac{1}{\omega C_p} \right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — SRF is where the imaginary part equals zero:**

$$\omega_{SRF} L_p - \dfrac{1}{\omega_{SRF} C_p} = 0$$

$$\omega_{SRF}^2 = \dfrac{1}{L_p C_p}$$

$$\boxed{f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p C_p}}}$$

**Step 6 — Explain the physical meaning:**

At $f_{SRF}$, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, and the resistor's impedance magnitude is approximately equal to $R$. **Below** $f_{SRF}$, the device behaves as a resistor with mild capacitive/inductive deviation. **Above** $f_{SRF}$, the impedance deviates substantially from $R$, and the device is no longer useful as a resistor. **(Full 7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $R = 50\ \Omega$, $L_p = 3\ \text{nH} = 3 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{H}$, $C_p = 0.8\ \text{pF} = 8 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F}$, $f = 2\ \text{GHz} = 2 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Hz}$.

**Sub-part (i) — Self-Resonant Frequency:**

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(3 \times 10^{-9})(8 \times 10^{-13})}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{2.4 \times 10^{-21}}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 4.899 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{3.078 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{SRF} \approx 3.25\ \text{GHz}}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Magnitude of impedance at 2 GHz:**

First, calculate the reactances at $f = 2 \times 10^{9}$ Hz:

$$X_L = \omega L_p = 2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{9} \times 3 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$X_L = 2\pi \times 6 = 12\pi = 37.70\ \Omega$$

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_p} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{0.01005} = 99.47\ \Omega$$

Net reactance: $X = X_L - X_C = 37.70 - 99.47 = -61.77\ \Omega$

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + (-61.77)^2}$$

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{2500 + 3815.5} = \sqrt{6315.5}$$

$$\boxed{\vert Z \vert \approx 79.5\ \Omega}$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Phase angle at 2 GHz:**

$$\angle Z = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \dfrac{X}{R} \right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \dfrac{-61.77}{50} \right)$$

$$\angle Z = \tan^{-1}(-1.2354)$$

$$\boxed{\angle Z \approx -51.0^\circ}$$

**Comment on Suitability at 2 GHz:**

At 2 GHz, the actual impedance is **$79.5\ \Omega$** (rather than the nominal $50\ \Omega$) with a phase angle of $-51^\circ$. This means:
- A **59% deviation** in magnitude from the specified value.
- Significant **phase error** (pure resistor should be $0^\circ$).
- The device is **operating in its capacitive region** (below SRF of 3.25 GHz, but too close for comfort).

**Verdict:** This resistor is **marginally suitable** at 2 GHz. For reliable SI/EMC performance, the operating frequency should be kept **below $f_{SRF}/3 \approx 1.08\ \text{GHz}$**. A thinner package (e.g., 0402) with smaller $L_p$ and $C_p$ would extend the useful bandwidth. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **skin effect** in conductors. Derive an expression for skin depth and AC resistance, and show that $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper trace on a PCB is $5\ \text{cm}$ long, $0.2\ \text{mm}$ wide, and $35\ \mu\text{m}$ thick. Given $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ and $\mu_{Cu} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$: (i) calculate the DC resistance, (ii) the skin depth at 1 GHz, and (iii) the AC resistance at 1 GHz, assuming the current flows uniformly within one skin depth. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Skin Effect Derivation

**Conceptual Explanation:**

> When alternating current flows through a conductor, the **self-induced eddy currents** in the conductor push the current density toward the outer surface. This is the **skin effect**. The effective cross-sectional area for current flow decreases as frequency increases, causing the AC resistance to rise.

**Step 1 — Maxwell's equation for the field penetration:**

For a semi-infinite conductor with a tangential electric field $E_0$ at the surface, the field inside decays as $E(x) = E_0 e^{-x/\delta}$, where $\delta$ is the skin depth.

**Step 2 — From Maxwell's equations, the skin depth is:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

**Derivation outline (qualitative for KTU):**
- From $\nabla^2 E = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial t}$, in phasor form $\nabla^2 \tilde{E} = j \omega \mu \sigma \tilde{E}$
- For plane-wave penetration: $\dfrac{d^2 E}{d x^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma E$
- Solution: $E(x) = E_0 e^{-(1+j)x/\delta}$
- Where $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}}$ (using $\rho = 1/\sigma$)

**Step 3 — Derive the AC resistance:**

For a flat conductor of width $w$ and length $\ell$, with $w \gg \delta$:

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{w \cdot \delta} = \dfrac{\rho \ell}{w} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\delta}$$

Substituting $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}}$:

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho \ell}{w} \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu}{2 \rho}} = \dfrac{\ell}{w} \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \rho}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ac} = \dfrac{\ell}{w} \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi f \mu \rho}{1}}}$$

This shows $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$, as required. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $\ell = 5\ \text{cm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $w = 0.2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $t = 35\ \mu\text{m} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$, $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $\mu_{Cu} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} \approx 1.257 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/m}$, $f = 1\ \text{GHz} = 10^9\ \text{Hz}$.

**Sub-part (i) — DC Resistance:**

$$R_{dc} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{w \cdot t}$$

$$R_{dc} = \dfrac{(1.72 \times 10^{-8})(0.05)}{(2 \times 10^{-4})(3.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$R_{dc} = \dfrac{8.6 \times 10^{-10}}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{dc} \approx 0.123\ \Omega}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Skin depth at 1 GHz:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.72 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 10^9 \times 1.257 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.72 \times 10^{-8}}{3.949 \times 10^{3}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{4.355 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 2.087\ \mu\text{m}}$$

**Sub-part (iii) — AC Resistance at 1 GHz:**

Since the trace thickness $t = 35\ \mu\text{m}$ and $w = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, with $\delta \approx 2.09\ \mu\text{m}$, we have $t \gg \delta$ and $w \gg \delta$. The current is confined to a skin-depth layer on **both** the top and bottom surfaces (and both side edges, but the side contribution is negligible for $w \gg t$).

Effective cross-sectional area: $A_{eff} \approx 2 \cdot w \cdot \delta = 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.087 \times 10^{-6}$

$$A_{eff} \approx 8.348 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A_{eff}} = \dfrac{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05}{8.348 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$R_{ac} = \dfrac{8.6 \times 10^{-10}}{8.348 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ac} \approx 1.030\ \Omega}$$

**Note:** $R_{ac}/R_{dc} \approx 1.030/0.123 \approx 8.4$ — the AC resistance is **more than 8× larger** than the DC resistance at 1 GHz, demonstrating the severity of skin effect in PCB traces at high frequencies. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Scenarios in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in SRF Calculation:** Students often forget to convert $\text{nH}$ to $\text{H}$ and $\text{pF}$ to $\text{F}$. One nano-failure here produces an error of $10^{21}$ in the answer. **Always** write units explicitly in the substitution step.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ vs. $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \rho}$ distinction:** The two equivalent forms of $\delta$ use $\omega = 2\pi f$. Confusing these gives a $\sqrt{2\pi} \approx 2.51\times$ error.
>
> 3. **Treating resistor as pure $R$ in high-frequency problems:** If a problem mentions GHz or mentions "transmission line" / "microstrip" / "stripline", you **must** include the parasitic $L_p$ and $C_p$ — full marks require the non-ideal model.
>
> 4. **Reflection Coefficient Sign Error:** $\Gamma = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}$ — students sometimes invert the numerator. **Trick:** If $R_L > Z_0$, $\Gamma$ must be positive (open-circuit-like).
>
> 5. **Skin Effect: $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$:** Examiners award 1 mark just for writing this proportionality explicitly. Don't forget it.
>
> 6. **Heat Derating:** When asked about power rating, never state the nominal wattage. Apply the derating curve: $P_{derated} = P_{rated} \times \text{derating factor}$ based on ambient temperature.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Resistors (Module 2)**

- [x] A real resistor is modeled as $R$ + parasitic $L_p$ (series) + parasitic $C_p$ (shunt).
- [x] The series parasitic inductance $L_p$ dominates at **high frequencies**.
- [x] The shunt parasitic capacitance $C_p$ dominates at **intermediate frequencies**.
- [x] **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF):** $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_p}}$ — upper usable frequency limit.
- [x] Usable bandwidth rule of thumb: $f_{op} \leq f_{SRF}/3$ for $< 5\%$ impedance deviation.
- [x] **Skin depth** $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ — decreases with frequency, increases with resistivity and permeability.
- [x] **AC resistance** $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$ — doubles when frequency quadruples.
- [x] **Reflection coefficient** $\Gamma = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}$ — zero when matched.
- [x] **Standard termination values:** $50\ \Omega$ (RF/coax), $75\ \Omega$ (video), $100\ \Omega$ (differential pairs: USB, Ethernet).
- [x] **Johnson-Nyquist noise** $V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — grows with resistance, temperature, and bandwidth.
- [x] **TCR (Temperature Coefficient of Resistance):** Expressed in $\text{ppm}/^\circ\text{C}$; positive for most metals, negative for carbon.
- [x] **Resistor selection for EMC/SI:**
  - Low-frequency (< 1 MHz): Carbon/metal film
  - High-frequency (> 100 MHz): Thin-film SMD or foil
  - High-power: Wirewound (but watch inductance!)
- [x] **Termination topologies:** Series (source-end, $R_s = Z_0 - Z_{driver}$), Parallel (load-end, $R_L = Z_0$), Thevenin (split between $V_{CC}$ and GND).
- [x] **Voltage divider** $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ — only valid for $R_L \gg R_2$ (no loading).
- [x] **Power dissipation** $P = I^2 R = V^2/R = VI$ — derate for ambient temperature.
- [x] **Pull-up/pull-down sizing trade-off:** Lower $R$ = faster edge, higher power; higher $R$ = slower edge, lower power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capacitors # Capacitors in EMC and Signal Integrity — Core Foundations ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) and Signal Integrity (SI)**, a capacitor is a two-terminal passive energy-storage element whose primary engineering utility extends far beyond its textbook role of "storing charge." A capacitor functions as a **frequency-selective impedance element** that exhibits high impedance to DC/low-frequency signals and low impedance to high-frequency noise. It is the most widely used component in **Power Distribution Networks (PDN)**, **decoupling/bypassing networks**, **EMI filters**, and **signal coupling stages**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A capacitor is a passive lumped element characterized by its capacitance $C$ (Farads), parasitic Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR), and Equivalent Series Inductance (ESL). Its behavior is frequency-dependent and critically important for controlling power rail noise, ground bounce, and radiated emissions in high-speed digital and RF systems. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **large overhead water tank** connected to your house: - The **water level** in the tank represents stored **charge (Q)**. - The **tank's volume** represents **capacitance (C)**. - The **water pressure (height of water column)** represents **voltage (V)**. - When you open a tap (load suddenly demands current), water rushes out instantly from the tank without delay, because it is already stored locally. The main pipeline (power supply) does not have to respond instantaneously. - A **larger tank** (bigger capacitor) holds more reserve and can serve sudden demand better. - However, the **pipe leading to the tank** has friction (ESR) and the water has inertia (ESL) — these limit how fast water can actually flow in/out, especially at high demand rates (high frequencies). In the **electrical world**: a decoupling capacitor placed close to an IC acts exactly like this local water tank — when a digital gate switches, the capacitor dumps its stored charge instantly to supply the transient current, preventing the supply voltage from collapsing (which would cause signal integrity failures and EMI). > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** A capacitor in EMC/SI is a *local energy reservoir* that fights voltage fluctuations. Its *effectiveness* is highly dependent on **frequency** — and this is where ESR, ESL, and Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF) become critical. ## 1.3 The Three Faces of a Capacitor A real-world capacitor is NOT a pure capacitance. It has three intrinsic parasitic behaviors: | Parasitic Element | Symbol | Physical Origin | Effect on Performance | |---|---|---|---| | **Equivalent Series Resistance** | ESR | Lead resistance, plate resistance, dielectric loss | Causes heating, limits Q-factor, dissipates noise as heat | | **Equivalent Series Inductance** | ESL | Lead/trace inductance, plate geometry | Causes self-resonance; capacitor becomes inductive above SRF | | **Dielectric Absorption** | DA | Polarization lag in dielectric molecules | Causes voltage "memory" — problematic in sample-and-hold circuits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of Capacitors in SI:** *A capacitor is capacitive only **below** its self-resonant frequency. Above the SRF, it behaves as an **inductor** — and an inductor at high frequency is essentially an open circuit for noise suppression. This is why PCB designers must carefully choose capacitor values for the frequency range of interest.* ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ - **Characteristic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 377 \, \Omega$ - **Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) $\varepsilon_r$**: ranges from ~2 (PTFE) to ~10,000 (high-K ceramics) --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ideal Capacitor — Fundamental Theory A parallel-plate capacitor consists of two conductive plates of area $A$ separated by a dielectric of thickness $d$ with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$. The **capacitance** is given by: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$$ The charge stored when voltage $V$ is applied across it: $$Q = C \cdot V$$ The energy stored in the electric field: $$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} QV$$ The current through a capacitor is the rate of change of charge: $$i_C(t) = C \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$ The voltage across the capacitor in terms of current: $$V_C(t) = V_C(0) + \frac{1}{C} \int_0^t i_C(\tau) \, d\tau$$ In the **frequency domain**, applying the phasor relationship $i = C \frac{dV}{dt} \Rightarrow I = j\omega C V$: $$Z_C = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -\frac{j}{\omega C}$$ This means an ideal capacitor has purely imaginary (reactive) impedance with magnitude that **decreases with frequency**. ## 2.2 The Real (Non-Ideal) Capacitor Model A real capacitor is modeled as a **series R-L-C circuit**: ``` ───[ESR]──[ESL]───( C )─── │ │ (V) │ │ │ ──────────────────────── ``` The total impedance of this series combination is: $$Z(f) = ESR + j\omega ESL + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = ESR + j\left(2\pi f \cdot ESL - \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C}\right)$$ ## 2.3 Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF) The **Self-Resonant Frequency** is the frequency at which the inductive reactance $X_L = 2\pi f \cdot ESL$ exactly cancels the capacitive reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C}$. At this frequency, the capacitor's impedance equals only its **ESR** (the minimum possible). Setting $X_L = X_C$: $$2\pi f_{SRF} \cdot ESL = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{SRF} \cdot C}$$ Solving for $f_{SRF}$: $$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{ESL \cdot C}}$$ At $f_{SRF}$: - The capacitor is **purely resistive** (only ESR remains). - $|Z|_{min} = ESR$. - Above $f_{SRF}$: the capacitor behaves as an **inductor** — useless for bypass/decoupling. - Below $f_{SRF}$: the capacitor behaves as a **capacitor** — effective bypass. > [!NOTE] > **Practical implication:** When selecting a decoupling capacitor, the SRF must be higher than the highest frequency component of the noise you want to suppress. A 0.1 µF X7R 0402 capacitor typically has SRF ≈ 16 MHz, while a 1 µF 0603 might have SRF ≈ 5 MHz. ## 2.4 Q-Factor and Dissipation Factor The **Quality Factor (Q)** measures how "pure" a capacitor is — how close it approaches ideal behavior: $$Q = \frac{X_C}{ESR} = \frac{1}{\omega C \cdot ESR} = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C \cdot ESR}$$ The **Dissipation Factor (DF)** is the reciprocal: $$DF = \frac{1}{Q} = \tan \delta = \omega C \cdot ESR$$ > A lower DF means a "better" capacitor (less loss, sharper resonance, more effective filtering). ## 2.5 Decoupling Capacitor Behavior — Target Impedance In a Power Distribution Network (PDN), the goal is to keep the **power rail impedance below a target value** $Z_{target}$ across the entire frequency band of interest (from DC to the highest harmonic of the clock). The target impedance is derived from the allowed voltage ripple and worst-case transient current: $$Z_{target} = \frac{V_{dd} \cdot (\text{ripple fraction})}{I_{transient}} = \frac{\Delta V}{I_{max}}$$ For a typical 3.3 V supply with 5% ripple and 1 A transient: $Z_{target} = 0.165 \, \Omega$. A single capacitor cannot maintain this low impedance over a wide frequency range, so designers **parallel multiple capacitors of different values** to achieve a flat low-impedance profile — this is the **Bulk–Mid–High-frequency decoupling strategy**. ## 2.6 Anti-Resonance in Parallel Capacitor Combinations When two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ (with their respective ESLs $L_1$ and $L_2$) are placed in parallel, an **anti-resonance (parallel resonance) peak** appears between the two SRFs. The peak frequency is approximately: $$f_{anti} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L_1 L_2 C_1 C_2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\text{...}}}$$ More precisely, treating each capacitor as a series RLC, the parallel combination forms a **parallel RLC** between them, creating a notch in impedance. The anti-resonance frequency can be approximated as: $$f_{anti} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_p \cdot C_p}}$$ where $L_p$ and $C_p$ are the parallel-equivalent values derived from the two RLC branches. This anti-resonance can cause **PDN noise peaks** that may exceed the target impedance — a critical EMC concern. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Anti-resonance peaks in multi-value decoupling networks are a common cause of EMC failures. Mitigation strategies include using capacitors with closely-spaced values, adding damping resistors, or using the same value capacitor in multiple packages (e.g., two 0.1 µF 0402 instead of one 0.1 µF + one 0.01 µF). ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Farads (F) | $A$: plate area; $d$: separation | | Charge stored | $Q = C \cdot V$ | Coulombs (C) | | | Energy stored | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Joules (J) | | | Current-voltage relation | $i_C = C \frac{dV}{dt}$ | A, V, s | | | Capacitive impedance (ideal) | $Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | | Capacitive reactance magnitude | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | | Real capacitor impedance | $Z = ESR + j\left(2\pi f L - \frac{1}{2\pi f C}\right)$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $L \equiv ESL$ | | Self-Resonant Frequency | $f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C}}$ | Hz | | | Quality factor | $Q = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C \cdot ESR}$ | Dimensionless | | | Dissipation factor | $DF = \tan \delta = \omega C \cdot ESR$ | Dimensionless | | | Target impedance | $Z_{target} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{I_{transient,max}}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | PDN design | | Time constant | $\tau = ESR \cdot C$ | Seconds (s) | RC discharge | | Inductive reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f \cdot L$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $L \equiv ESL$ | | Resonant angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s | | > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip (KTU):** Most numerical problems in Module 2 expect students to compute SRF, Q-factor, or impedance at a given frequency using the real-capacitor model. Always show the three-component series RLC model in your diagram before applying these formulas. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Capacitors are indispensable in: 1. **Decoupling/Bypassing** — Suppress power-rail noise in digital ICs (microcontrollers, FPGAs, ASICs). 2. **EMI Filters** — Pi-filters, LC-filters for conducted emissions compliance (CISPR 22 / CISPR 32). 3. **DC Blocking / AC Coupling** — Series capacitor blocks DC while passing AC in RF chains. 4. **Snubber Networks** — Across switching transistors (IGBTs, MOSFETs) to limit $dv/dt$. 5. **Sample-and-Hold Circuits** — In ADCs (low-DA capacitors required). 6. **Bypass for ESD/Transient Protection** — Across protection devices. 7. **Tuning and Matching** — In RF and antenna matching networks. 8. **Power Factor Correction** — In industrial EMC compliance for harmonic suppression. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor **Starting point:** Gauss's law for electric displacement field $\vec{D}$: $$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enclosed}$$ **Step 1:** Between the plates, the electric field is uniform and perpendicular to the plates. The flux through a Gaussian pillbox enclosing one plate is: $$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = D \cdot A = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E \cdot A$$ **Step 2:** Equating to enclosed charge $Q$: $$\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E \cdot A = Q$$ **Step 3:** The voltage between the plates is the integral of the electric field over the separation: $$V = \int_0^d E \, dx = E \cdot d \quad \text{(uniform field assumption)}$$ **Step 4:** Solving for $E$ from Step 3 and substituting into Step 2: $$E = \frac{V}{d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{V}{d} \cdot A = Q$$ **Step 5:** Recognizing that $Q = C \cdot V$, we divide both sides by $V$: $$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}}$$ This is the fundamental parallel-plate capacitance formula. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Self-Resonant Frequency of a Real Capacitor **Starting point:** A real capacitor is modeled as a series R-L-C circuit (R = ESR, L = ESL, and the ideal capacitance C). **Step 1:** The complex impedance of this series combination is: $$Z(f) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$ **Step 2:** Using $\frac{1}{j} = -j$, rewrite the capacitive term: $$Z(f) = R + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$ **Step 3:** At resonance (SRF), the imaginary part equals zero (the circuit is purely resistive): $$\omega_0 L - \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 0$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging: $$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$ **Step 5:** Converting to frequency in Hz using $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_{SRF}$: $$2\pi f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C}}}$$ **Step 6:** Verify the impedance at SRF: $$Z(f_{SRF}) = R + j(0) = R = ESR$$ Thus at SRF, the capacitor's impedance is **minimum and purely real** (equal to ESR). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quality Factor (Q) of a Series Resonant Circuit **Starting point:** Quality factor is defined as the ratio of energy stored per cycle to energy dissipated per cycle. For a series RLC at resonance: $$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C R}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ into $\frac{1}{\omega_0 C R}$: $$Q = \frac{1}{C R} \cdot \sqrt{LC} = \frac{\sqrt{LC}}{C R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$ **Step 2:** Alternatively, write in terms of frequency $f$: $$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{2\pi f_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C R}$$ **Step 3:** For a capacitor specifically, the quality factor (using $L \equiv ESL$ and $R \equiv ESR$): $$\boxed{Q = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C \cdot ESR} = \frac{1}{\omega C \cdot ESR}}$$ > **Interpretation:** Higher Q means narrower resonance peak (more selective filter), while lower Q means wider bandwidth but more loss. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Target Impedance for PDN Design **Given:** A digital IC switching causes transient current demand $I_{transient}$ for a duration $\Delta t$. The supply voltage must not drop by more than $\Delta V_{ripple}$ (typically 5% of $V_{DD}$). **Step 1:** The maximum charge drawn from the supply during the transient is: $$Q_{demand} = I_{transient} \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 2:** This charge must come from the local decoupling capacitor (otherwise it comes from the supply, causing voltage droop): $$Q_C = C \cdot \Delta V_{ripple}$$ **Step 3:** Equating the two: $$C \cdot \Delta V_{ripple} = I_{transient} \cdot \Delta t \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{I_{transient} \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V_{ripple}}$$ **Step 4:** The PDN impedance required to limit the voltage drop to $\Delta V$ for a current step $I$ is: $$\boxed{Z_{target} = \frac{\Delta V_{ripple}}{I_{transient,max}}}$$ **Step 5:** Example: For $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, 5% ripple, $I_{max} = 1$ A: $$Z_{target} = \frac{0.05 \times 3.3}{1} = 0.165 \, \Omega$$ The PDN must present impedance $\leq 0.165 \, \Omega$ from DC to the highest frequency component of interest. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Impedance of Two Capacitors in Parallel (Anti-Resonance) **Given:** Two capacitors with values $C_1$ and $C_2$ and ESLs $L_1$ and $L_2$ in parallel. Each branch has its own ESR ($R_1$ and $R_2$). The total admittance is: $$Y_{total} = Y_1 + Y_2$$ where for a series R-L-C branch: $$Y_1 = \frac{1}{R_1 + j\omega L_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}} = \frac{j\omega C_1}{1 - \omega^2 L_1 C_1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}$$ **Step 1:** For frequencies between the two SRFs (where each capacitor is approximately at its resonance), one branch is inductive and the other is capacitive. **Step 2:** Simplifying near the anti-resonance frequency by ignoring $R_1$ and $R_2$ (assuming ideal): $$Y_1 \approx \frac{1}{j\omega L_1} \quad \text{(inductive branch above its SRF)}$$ $$Y_2 \approx j\omega C_2 \quad \text{(capacitive branch below its SRF)}$$ **Step 3:** Total admittance: $$Y_{total} = j\omega C_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega L_1} = j\left(\omega C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega L_1}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Anti-resonance occurs when $Y_{total} = 0$ (infinite impedance — a **parallel resonance** peak): $$\omega C_2 = \frac{1}{\omega L_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{anti}^2 = \frac{1}{L_1 C_2}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\boxed{f_{anti} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_1 C_2}}}$$ This peak can cause PDN noise to exceed the target impedance and is a critical EMC/SI concern. ## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Choosing Capacitors | Application | Frequency Range | Typical Values | Preferred Type | Why | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bulk decoupling (low freq) | DC – 1 MHz | 10 µF – 1000 µF | Aluminum electrolytic, polymer | High capacitance, low cost | | Mid-frequency decoupling | 1 MHz – 50 MHz | 0.1 µF – 1 µF | X7R MLCC (0603/0805) | Good balance of C and SRF | | High-frequency decoupling | 50 MHz – 500 MHz | 1 nF – 10 nF | C0G/NP0 or small X7R (0402) | Low ESL, high SRF | | RF bypass | > 500 MHz | 10 pF – 100 pF | C0G/NP0 (0201/0402) | Very low ESL, controlled SRF | | Filter capacitors (EMI) | Conducted noise band | 100 nF – 1 µF | X2/Y2 safety rated | Safety, line-voltage rated | | Snubber (switching) | kHz – MHz | 0.1 µF – 1 µF | Film (PP, PEN) | High pulse current, low DF | | Coupling (AC) | RF band | 10 pF – 100 nF | C0G/NP0 | Low DA, stable value | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Real Capacitor Equivalent Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[ "TERMINAL A" ] --> nodeB[ "ESR
Equivalent Series Resistance" ] nodeB --> nodeC[ "ESL
Equivalent Series Inductance" ] nodeC --> nodeD[ "C
Ideal Capacitance" ] nodeD --> nodeE[ "TERMINAL B" ] nodeF[ "Parasitic Dielectric
Absorption DA" ] -.-> nodeD ``` **Interpretation:** This is the standard lumped-element model used in SPICE simulations. The ESR arises from lead and plate resistance; the ESL arises from lead geometry and current loop area; the ideal C stores charge in the dielectric field; the DA (dashed) represents polarization lag in the dielectric. ## 4.2 Impedance vs Frequency Behavior of a Real Capacitor ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[ "START: Real Capacitor" ] --> nodeB[ "f LESS THAN SRF
Capacitive Region
X_C dominates
Z decreases with f" ] nodeB --> nodeC[ "f EQUALS SRF
Resonance Point
X_C cancels X_L
Z equals ESR minimum" ] nodeC --> nodeD[ "f GREATER THAN SRF
Inductive Region
X_L dominates
Z increases with f" ] nodeD --> nodeE[ "END: Capacitor acts as INDUCTOR
Useless for bypass" ] nodeF[ "Design Rule" ] --> nodeG[ "Choose capacitor so that
operating frequency is
well BELOW SRF" ] ``` **Interpretation:** This is the *single most important* diagram for EMC/SI capacitor selection. A 0402 0.1 µF X7R capacitor has SRF ≈ 16 MHz. If you use it to decouple a 100 MHz signal, it is actually an **inductor** — failing its job. ## 4.3 Multi-Capacitor Decoupling Network (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[ "VRM
Voltage Regulator Module" ] --> nodeB[ "Bulk Capacitor
100 uF
Aluminum Polymer" ] nodeB --> nodeC[ "Mid Frequency
0.1 uF X7R 0402" ] nodeC --> nodeD[ "High Frequency
1 nF X7R 0201" ] nodeD --> nodeE[ "IC Power Pin
FPGA or ASIC" ] nodeF[ "PCB Power Plane
Low Impedance" ] -.-> nodeE nodeG[ "Frequency Coverage" ] --> nodeH[ "DC to 1 kHz" ] nodeG --> nodeI[ "1 kHz to 1 MHz" ] nodeG --> nodeJ[ "1 MHz to 100 MHz" ] nodeG --> nodeK[ "100 MHz to GHz" ] ``` **Interpretation:** Each capacitor value targets a specific frequency band. The bulk handles low-frequency transients (clock edges averaged over time), mid-frequency handles package lead resonances, and high-frequency handles die-level switching. The power plane provides the ultra-low-impedance path at GHz frequencies. ## 4.4 Decoupling Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[ "START: Need a decoupling capacitor" ] --> nodeB[ "Identify noise frequency range" ] nodeB --> nodeC[ "Compute target impedance Z_target" ] nodeC --> nodeD{ "Single cap enough?" } nodeD -->| YES | nodeE[ "Pick C with SRF above max noise freq" ] nodeD -->| NO | nodeF[ "Use multi-value parallel network" ] nodeF --> nodeG[ "Check anti-resonance peaks" ] nodeG --> nodeH{ "Peaks exceed Z_target?" } nodeH -->| YES | nodeI[ "Add damping or change values" ] nodeH -->| NO | nodeJ[ "Design OK" ] nodeE --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ ``` **Interpretation:** This is the systematic engineering design flow used in professional PCB layout for high-speed digital systems. The anti-resonance check (Step 7) is what separates competent EMC design from naive design. ## 4.5 Capacitor Type Comparison Chart (Schematic Overview) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[ "CAPACITOR TYPES" ] --> nodeB[ "Ceramic MLCC" ] nodeA --> nodeC[ "Tantalum" ] nodeA --> nodeD[ "Aluminum Electrolytic" ] nodeA --> nodeE[ "Film" ] nodeB --> nodeB1[ "C0G/NP0 stable
X7R general
X5R small size
Y5V poor stability" ] nodeC --> nodeC1[ "High CV per volume
Polarized
Failure mode open" ] nodeD --> nodeD1[ "Largest CV
Polarized
High ESR
Limited lifetime" ] nodeE --> nodeE1[ "Polypropylene PP
Polyester PET
High voltage
Low loss" ] ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A] **Q: Define Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF) of a capacitor. Why is it important in the design of decoupling networks for high-speed digital circuits?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Self-Resonant Frequency (SRF)** of a capacitor is the frequency at which the capacitor's capacitive reactance ($X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$) exactly cancels its inductive reactance ($X_L = 2\pi f L_{ESL}$). At this frequency, the capacitor's net impedance becomes purely resistive and equals its ESR (minimum value). Mathematically: $$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{ESL} \cdot C}}$$ **Importance in decoupling:** Below SRF, the capacitor behaves as a capacitor (effective bypass); above SRF, it behaves as an **inductor** (ineffective — actually blocks AC). In high-speed digital circuits, switching noise contains harmonics extending up to several GHz. A decoupling capacitor whose SRF is lower than the noise frequency will fail to suppress it. Therefore, the SRF of every decoupling capacitor must be selected to be **higher than the highest frequency component** of the noise spectrum it must suppress. *(3 marks for definition + 2 key points + formula)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q: What is Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) of a capacitor? How does it affect the Q-factor and the filtering performance of a capacitor?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)** is the lumped parasitic resistance that appears in series with the ideal capacitance in a real capacitor's model. It arises from the resistance of the leads, plates, terminations, and dielectric losses. **Effect on Q-factor:** The Quality factor is inversely related to ESR: $$Q = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C \cdot ESR}$$ Higher ESR → lower Q → wider, less selective resonance peak. **Effect on filtering performance:** A high ESR capacitor dissipates more noise energy as heat rather than shunting it to ground. It also broadens the resonance peak, reducing the filter's ability to selectively target a specific frequency. For sharp, low-loss filtering (e.g., in RF or PDN applications), low-ESR capacitors (such as C0G/NP0 ceramics or polymer aluminum) are preferred. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for Q relation + 1 mark for filtering effect)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. The model below presents both alternatives, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks. --- ### Part B — Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2] **Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the impedance of a real capacitor modeled as a series R-L-C circuit. Sketch and explain the impedance vs frequency characteristic. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 0.1 µF ceramic capacitor (X7R, 0402 package) has an ESL of 1 nH and ESR of 0.05 Ω. Calculate: (i) Self-resonant frequency (ii) Q-factor at 1 MHz (iii) Impedance magnitude at 100 MHz (iv) Comment on its effectiveness as a decoupling capacitor at 100 MHz. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1: Equivalent circuit model (1 mark)** A real capacitor is modeled as a series combination of: - An ideal capacitance $C$ - An Equivalent Series Inductance (ESL), denoted $L$ - An Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR), denoted $R$ **Step 2: Impedance derivation (3 marks)** The total impedance of the series R-L-C combination is: $$Z(f) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$ Using the identity $\frac{1}{j} = -j$: $$Z(f) = R + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C} = R + j\left(2\pi f L - \frac{1}{2\pi f C}\right)$$ **Step 3: Magnitude (1 mark)** $$|Z(f)| = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(2\pi f L - \frac{1}{2\pi f C}\right)^2}$$ **Step 4: Three frequency regions (2 marks)** | Region | Condition | Behavior | Impedance | |---|---|---|---| | Low frequency | $f \ll f_{SRF}$ | $X_C \gg X_L$ | Capacitive: $\vert Z \vert \approx X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | | At SRF | $f = f_{SRF}$ | $X_C = X_L$ | Resistive: $\vert Z \vert = R = ESR$ (minimum) | | High frequency | $f \gg f_{SRF}$ | $X_L \gg X_C$ | Inductive: $\vert Z \vert \approx X_L = 2\pi f L$ | **Sketch description for full marks:** The $\vert Z \vert$ vs $f$ curve is a V-shape with a sharp minimum at $f_{SRF}$. Below SRF, the curve slopes downward (capacitive region with slope -20 dB/decade). Above SRF, the curve slopes upward (inductive region with slope +20 dB/decade). The minimum value of $\vert Z \vert$ is the ESR. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $C = 0.1 \, \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ F - $L = ESL = 1 \, nH = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ H - $R = ESR = 0.05 \, \Omega$ **Sub-part (i): Self-resonant frequency (2 marks)** $$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(1 \times 10^{-9})(1 \times 10^{-7})}}$$ $$L \cdot C = 1 \times 10^{-16} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{LC} = 1 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{10^8}{2\pi} = 1.592 \times 10^7 \, Hz$$ $$\boxed{f_{SRF} \approx 15.92 \, MHz}$$ **Sub-part (ii): Q-factor at 1 MHz (2 marks)** $$Q = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C \cdot ESR} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.05}$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.03142}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 31.83}$$ **Sub-part (iii): Impedance at 100 MHz (2 marks)** $$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times 10^{-9} = 0.6283 \, \Omega$$ $$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^8 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{62.83} = 0.01592 \, \Omega$$ $$X = X_L - X_C = 0.6283 - 0.01592 = 0.6124 \, \Omega$$ $$|Z| = \sqrt{ESR^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (0.6124)^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.3750} = \sqrt{0.3775}$$ $$\boxed{|Z| \approx 0.614 \, \Omega}$$ **Sub-part (iv): Effectiveness at 100 MHz (1 mark)** Since 100 MHz $\gg$ $f_{SRF} = 15.92$ MHz, the capacitor is operating in the **inductive region** and presents a high impedance (~0.614 Ω). It is therefore **ineffective as a decoupling capacitor at 100 MHz**. A smaller-value capacitor (e.g., 1 nF or 100 pF) with higher SRF should be used for 100 MHz noise suppression. --- ### Part B — Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of target impedance in Power Distribution Network (PDN) design. For a system with $V_{DD} = 5$ V, allowed ripple = 5%, and maximum transient current = 2 A, calculate the target impedance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of anti-resonance in parallel decoupling capacitors. Two capacitors, $C_1 = 1 \, \mu F$ with $L_1 = 2$ nH, and $C_2 = 0.01 \, \mu F$ with $L_2 = 1$ nH, are placed in parallel. Calculate the anti-resonance frequency and explain the EMC implications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Conceptual explanation (4 marks):** In a Power Distribution Network (PDN), the power supply rail must present an impedance **below a certain target value** at all frequencies from DC up to the highest harmonic of the switching signals. If the PDN impedance is too high, voltage fluctuations occur on the rail during current transients, causing: - Logic-level errors (signal integrity violations) - Increased EMI radiation (power-rail ringing acts as an antenna) - Timing jitter in clocks **Definition of Target Impedance:** The target impedance $Z_{target}$ is the maximum impedance the PDN can present without causing the supply voltage to drop below the tolerance band: $$Z_{target} = \frac{V_{DD} \times (\text{ripple fraction})}{I_{transient,max}}$$ **Design philosophy:** - The PDN impedance curve (vs frequency) must lie **below** $Z_{target}$ across the entire frequency band of interest. - A single capacitor value cannot achieve this — multiple values must be paralleled. - The selection of capacitor values and their placement must consider anti-resonance peaks. **Numerical calculation (3 marks):** Given: - $V_{DD} = 5$ V - Ripple fraction = 5% = 0.05 - $I_{transient,max} = 2$ A $$\Delta V_{max} = 0.05 \times 5 = 0.25 \, V$$ $$Z_{target} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{I_{transient,max}} = \frac{0.25}{2}$$ $$\boxed{Z_{target} = 0.125 \, \Omega}$$ The PDN must present impedance $\leq 0.125 \, \Omega$ from DC to the highest frequency of interest. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Conceptual explanation (3 marks):** When two (or more) capacitors with different values are placed in parallel, the combination of one branch being inductive (above its SRF) and the other being capacitive (below its SRF) creates a **parallel resonant circuit** between them. This results in a **sharp peak in impedance** at the anti-resonance frequency, where the total admittance approaches zero. This anti-resonance peak is highly undesirable in PDN design because it can: - Cause the PDN impedance to **exceed the target impedance** at that frequency. - Create a **noise amplification** point. - Generate radiated emissions if the peak is sharp and high. **Mitigation strategies:** - Use capacitors with closely-spaced values (1:2 ratio rather than 1:10). - Add small damping resistors in series with each capacitor. - Use the same value in multiple package sizes (e.g., two 0.1 µF 0402 + one 0.1 µF 0603). - Use fewer distinct values and more capacitors of the same value. **Numerical calculation (4 marks):** Given: - $C_1 = 1 \, \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $L_1 = 2$ nH $= 2 \times 10^{-9}$ H - $C_2 = 0.01 \, \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-8}$ F, $L_2 = 1$ nH $= 1 \times 10^{-9}$ H The anti-resonance occurs when the inductive reactance of one branch cancels the capacitive reactance of the other. The dominant anti-resonance is: $$f_{anti} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_1 \cdot C_2}}$$ (Since $L_1$ is the ESL of the larger capacitor which becomes inductive above its SRF, and $C_2$ is the smaller capacitor which remains capacitive below its SRF in the relevant band.) $$L_1 \cdot C_2 = (2 \times 10^{-9})(1 \times 10^{-8}) = 2 \times 10^{-17}$$ $$\sqrt{L_1 C_2} = \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-8.5} = 1.414 \times 10^{-8.5}$$ Computing: $\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-17}} = 4.472 \times 10^{-9}$ $$f_{anti} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.472 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{2.809 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$\boxed{f_{anti} \approx 35.6 \, MHz}$$ **EMC implication (in-built in explanation above):** If the digital circuit has clock harmonics or switching transients near 35.6 MHz, the anti-resonance will cause voltage noise peaks on the power rail, potentially failing EMC compliance (e.g., conducted emissions per CISPR 22). --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in Capacitor problems (Module 2):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Most SRF/Q-factor errors arise from mixing nH, µF, MHz, and GHz without proper conversion. **Always write units explicitly in every step.** > > 2. **Confusing the series and parallel resonance formulas** — A single capacitor's SRF uses its own L and C in series RLC. Anti-resonance of two parallel capacitors uses a *cross* L-C product (e.g., $L_1 C_2$), not the product of their own. > > 3. **Drawing incomplete equivalent circuits** — Always include ESR, ESL, AND C in your diagram. Omitting ESR costs 1–2 marks; omitting ESL invalidates the entire SRF calculation. > > 4. **Treating capacitors as ideal at high frequencies** — Writing $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ alone for a 100 MHz calculation loses 2 marks. You must use the full real-capacitor impedance formula. > > 5. **Missing the physical interpretation** — Numerical answers without a concluding sentence (e.g., "this frequency is above SRF, so the capacitor is inductive and ineffective for decoupling") lose 1–2 marks for "engineering judgment." > > 6. **Sign errors in reactance subtraction** — $X = X_L - X_C$ is correct, not $X_C - X_L$. At $f > f_{SRF}$, $X$ is positive (inductive); at $f < f_{SRF}$, $X$ is negative (capacitive). > > 7. **Incorrectly applying target impedance formula** — Some students write $Z = \Delta V / I_{avg}$ instead of $Z = \Delta V / I_{max}$. The denominator must be the **peak (worst-case) transient current**. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **A real capacitor = series (ESR + ESL + C)**, NOT a pure capacitance. - **SRF** = $\frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{ESL} \cdot C}}$ — beyond this, capacitor becomes inductor. - **Q-factor** = $\frac{1}{\omega C \cdot ESR}$ — higher Q = sharper filter, lower loss. - **Target impedance** = $\frac{\Delta V_{max}}{I_{transient,max}}$ — must be met across entire frequency band. - **Anti-resonance** in parallel multi-value decoupling is a key EMC failure mechanism. - **Bulk, Mid, High-frequency** capacitors are paralleled to cover DC-to-GHz. - **Ceramic types**: C0G/NP0 (stable, low-loss) > X7R (general purpose) > Y5V (avoid in EMC). - **Mounting inductance** (via/trace) is often the dominant ESL — minimize loop area. - **Anti-resonance mitigation**: use capacitors with 1:2 or 1:3 value ratio, not 1:10. - **Dielectric absorption** is critical for sample-and-hold; less so for power decoupling. - **Voltage coefficient** of X5R/X7R causes capacitance drop at high DC bias — derate in design. - **ESR decreases with frequency** for ceramic capacitors (dielectric loss dominant) but is flat for electrolytics. - **Temperature coefficient**: C0G = 0 ppm/°C, X7R = ±15% over -55 to +125 °C, Y5V = +22/-82%. - **PCB layout rule**: place decoupling cap within 1–2 mm of IC power pin; use short, wide traces/vias. - **Module 2 connection**: Capacitors are the *workhorse* of EMC — they appear in filters, decoupling, snubbers, and bypass networks across all later modules. ---

topics.title: Inductors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inductors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Inductors in Electromagnetic Compatibility & Signal Integrity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inductor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A passive two-terminal electrical component that stores energy in a **magnetic field** when current flows through it. In the context of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, an inductor is fundamentally a manifestation of the **Faraday–Neumann–Lenz law**, where any conductor carrying a time-varying current generates a magnetic flux, and any time-varying magnetic flux linking a closed loop induces an electromotive force (EMF) opposing the source of the flux change.

The constitutive relation governing an ideal inductor is governed by **Faraday's Law of Induction**:

$$v_L(t) = L \, \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$

where the constant of proportionality **L** is the **self-inductance**, measured in **Henries (H)**. The energy stored in the magnetic field is:

$$W_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L \, i^2$$

In the **KTU 2024 EMC & SI framework**, inductors are not studied merely as circuit elements but as **sources, victims, and coupling paths** of electromagnetic interference (EMI). They are the dual of capacitors, but unlike the near-field E-field dominance of capacitors, inductors are dominated by **H-field (magnetic field) coupling** in the near field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for EMC Engineers:** Capacitors are **high-pass devices** and primarily couple through **electric fields**, while inductors are **low-pass devices** and primarily couple through **magnetic fields**. This duality governs filter design, PCB layout, and shielding strategy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — The Water Flywheel:**
Imagine a heavy mechanical flywheel attached to a water pipe. When you suddenly try to push water through, the flywheel's inertia resists the change and takes time to spin up. Once spinning, it stores rotational kinetic energy. If you suddenly stop pushing, the flywheel keeps pushing water through for a moment. An inductor behaves identically with **electrical current** — it resists *changes* in current (not current itself) and stores energy in its **magnetic field**, just as a flywheel stores energy in its **rotational inertia**.

**Analogy 2 — PCB Trace = Hidden Inductor:**
Every straight copper trace on a printed circuit board is, electrically speaking, a *tiny invisible inductor*. A trace just **10 mm long** and **0.2 mm wide** typically has about **7–10 nH of parasitic inductance**. When signals transition at sub-nanosecond speeds (rise times < 1 ns), this seemingly trivial inductance creates voltage spikes that can corrupt logic levels — a phenomenon called **ground bounce** or **switching noise**. This is the central reason EMC engineers obsess over inductors: they are *everywhere*, whether designed in or not.

**Analogy 3 — The Transformer Whisper:**
Place two coils close together. Speak (apply current) into one, and the other "hears" the signal through the magnetic field even though no wire connects them. This is **mutual inductance** — the foundation of both intentional coupling (transformers) and unintentional coupling (crosstalk between adjacent PCB traces).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlights for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Self-Inductance (L):** Henries (H); typical PCB range = **nH to μH**
> - **Mutual Inductance (M):** Henries (H)
> - **Coupling Coefficient (k):** dimensionless, **0 ≤ k ≤ 1**
> - **Inductive Reactance (X_L):** Ohms (Ω); X_L = ωL
> - **Quality Factor (Q):** dimensionless; Q = ωL/R
> - **Permeability of free space (μ₀):** 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m ≈ **1.2566 μH/m**
> - **Relative permeability (μᵣ):** for ferrite materials, typically **10 to 5000**

### 1.3 Classification of Inductors in EMC Context

From the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective, inductors are classified as follows:

| Class | Typical Use in EMC/SI |
|---|---|
| **Air-core inductors** | High-frequency RF, broadband, low loss |
| **Iron-core / Ferrite inductors** | Power filtering, common-mode chokes |
| **Toroidal inductors** | EMI suppression, contained magnetic flux |
| **Parasitic (unintended) inductance** | PCB traces, vias, bond wires, lead frames |
| **Mutual inductance** | Crosstalk, transformer action, common-mode coupling |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

The behavior of an inductor is rooted in three coupled physical laws. A KTU board answer that does not cite these is incomplete.

- **Step 1 — Ampère's Law (current creates magnetic field):** A current *I* flowing through a closed loop of *N* turns produces a magnetic flux density **B** that is proportional to *I*.
- **Step 2 — Flux Linkage (Φ_L = N∫B·dA):** The total flux linking all *N* turns defines the **flux linkage λ = NΦ**.
- **Step 3 — Faraday's Law (changing flux induces voltage):** The induced EMF is **v = dλ/dt**. Substituting λ = LI gives **v = L(dI/dt)**. The minus sign in Lenz's law is why v opposes di/dt.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Law

- **Why does L oppose current changes?** Because any change in current creates a changing magnetic flux, which by Lenz's law induces a back-EMF that *opposes* the change. The inductor therefore acts as an *inertial* element for current — the electrical analog of mass.
- **Why is energy stored in the magnetic field?** Because building up current requires work against this back-EMF. The work done is permanently stored in the magnetic field and is released back into the circuit when the field collapses.
- **Why do inductors block high frequencies?** Because **X_L = ωL**, so as frequency rises, impedance rises linearly. At DC, the inductor is a short circuit; at high frequency, it approaches an open circuit. This makes it a natural **low-pass / high-frequency blocker**.

### 2.3 Self-Inductance vs. Mutual Inductance

- **Self-inductance L** describes the flux a current produces in *its own* loop: **λ₁₁ = L₁·i₁**.
- **Mutual inductance M** describes the flux produced in *another* loop by current in the first: **λ₂₁ = M·i₁**.
- The coupling coefficient **k** quantifies how much of the flux from one coil links the other:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

with **0 ≤ k ≤ 1**. k = 1 means perfectly coupled (ideal transformer); k = 0 means no coupling (magnetically isolated).

### 2.4 Inductor Models in EMC/SI

In real EMC work, no inductor is ideal. The high-frequency model includes:

- **Series resistance R_s** (DC winding resistance + skin-effect losses)
- **Parallel capacitance C_p** (turn-to-turn and inter-winding capacitance)
- **Magnetic core losses** (hysteresis + eddy currents, captured in complex permeability μ = μ' − jμ'')

This gives a **non-ideal inductor model**:

```
   L (series)
   o────[####]────o
   │              │
  [R_s]         [C_p]
   │              │
   o──────────────o
```

The **self-resonant frequency (SRF)** is where L and C_p resonate:

$$f_{SRF} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_p}}$$

Above the SRF, the inductor stops behaving like an inductor and behaves like a **capacitor** — a critical failure mode in EMI filter design.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for any KTU 2024 question on inductors. Memorize all entries.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage across inductor | $v_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Back-EMF from changing flux |
| 2 | Energy stored | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ | J (or W·s) | Energy in magnetic field |
| 3 | Inductive reactance | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ | Ω | Frequency-dependent impedance |
| 4 | Self-inductance (flux link) | $L = \dfrac{N\Phi}{I}$ | H | Turns × flux per ampere |
| 5 | Mutual inductance | $M = \dfrac{N_2 \Phi_{12}}{i_1}$ | H | Flux linkage between two coils |
| 6 | Coupling coefficient | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of flux shared |
| 7 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\omega L}{R_s}$ | dimensionless | Selectivity / loss inverse |
| 8 | Self-resonant frequency | $f_{SRF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_p}}$ | Hz | Where L stops being L |
| 9 | Straight wire inductance | $L \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 l}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2l}{r}\right) - 0.75\right]$ | H | Long thin wire approximation |
| 10 | PCB trace inductance | $L \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 l}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2l}{w+t}\right) + 0.5\right]$ | H | Approx. for rectangular trace |
| 11 | Parallel inductors | $L_{eq} = \dfrac{L_1 L_2 - M^2}{L_1 + L_2 \mp 2M}$ | H | (−) aiding, (+) opposing |
| 12 | Series inductors | $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 \pm 2M$ | H | (+) aiding, (−) opposing |
| 13 | Permeability (free space) | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | Fundamental constant |
| 14 | Relative permeability | $\mu_r = \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_0}$ | dimensionless | Material magnetic gain |
| 15 | Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | m | Conductor effective thickness |
| 16 | Coil inductance (solenoid) | $L = \dfrac{\mu N^2 A}{l}$ | H | N turns, area A, length l |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that in formulas 11 and 12, the **M term carries the SAME sign convention as current direction**. If currents enter dotted terminals in the same sense, the M term is positive (aiding). If opposite, M is negative (opposing). This is the most common KTU marking error.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Supply Decoupling:** Inductor-capacitor (LC) low-pass filters on DC rails attenuate switching noise above the cutoff frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **Common-Mode Chokes:** Two windings on a single core with k ≈ 1. Differential signals see nearly zero impedance; common-mode noise sees the full L.
- **Ferrite Beads:** Lossy inductors with high R_s at high frequency; they convert unwanted RF energy to heat.
- **PCB Trace Routing:** Designers minimize loop area to reduce parasitic L, which determines ground bounce and EMI radiation.
- **Crosstalk Mitigation:** Spacing parallel traces apart reduces M, hence reducing inductive (and capacitive) coupling.
- **Wireless Power Transfer:** Operates entirely on intentional M between loosely coupled coils (k ≈ 0.1–0.3).
