Imagine pushing a door until it latches. You will notice that the latch does **not** click shut cleanly the first time — the door *vibrates, re-opens slightly, vibrates again, and gradually settles*. Mechanical switch contacts behave **identically**. When two metal surfaces collide, the kinetic energy causes them to **bounce apart and re-collide** several times over **1 ms to 10 ms** before settling into a stable closed state. Each microscopic bounce *opens* the circuit for a few microseconds, creating a stream of high-frequency transients that radiate as broadband EMI.

Similarly, when you rapidly pull apart a plug connected to a running motor, you often see a **tiny spark** at the pins — that is the **arc**, and it is an unintentional radio-frequency transmitter.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bounce Duration** | **1 ms – 10 ms** | Sets the burst length of EFT disturbance |
| **Bounce Count** | 1 – 10 events per operation | Number of transient edges in a burst |
| **Minimum Arcing Voltage (V_min)** | **≈ 12 V – 15 V** (clean Ag contacts) | Below this, no arc sustains |
| **Minimum Arcing Current (I_min)** | **≈ 0.3 A – 1.0 A** | Below this, arc extinguishes |
| **Inductive Kickback Voltage** | Can exceed **10× supply** | Major cause of contact pitting and radiated EMI |

> [!NOTE]
> The **V_min × I_min** product defines the **"Ignition Curve"** or **"Load Line"** — outside this boundary, a metallic (self-sustaining) arc cannot exist. This is a key concept for both arc suppression design and KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phenomenon 1 — Contact Bounce (The Mechanical Origin)

When two metallic contacts close:
1. Initial contact is made at microscopic peaks (asperities) of the metal surface.
2. Kinetic energy from the moving contact causes elastic rebound.
3. The contacts separate, current is interrupted, then they re-collide.
4. The process repeats with decreasing amplitude until energy is dissipated.

**Why is bounce an EMC problem?**
- Each bounce edge has an extremely **fast rise time** (sub-microsecond).
- Per the relationship $f_{\max} \approx 0.35 / t_r$, fast edges mean high spectral content.
- A bounce burst of 5 ms containing edges with $t_r = 1\,\mu s$ radiates energy up to **≈ 350 kHz** — which falls directly into the **EFT/B test band of IEC 61000-4-4 (up to 5 MHz at 5 ns rise time for the test pulse)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Faster edge = Higher frequency = More radiated EMI"** — this single rule governs almost every transient EMC problem in the KTU syllabus.

## 2.2 Phenomenon 2 — Arcing During Switching

When the switch contacts **open** while carrying current, two regimes can occur:

| Regime | Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **No Arc** | $V < V_{\min}$ **OR** $I < I_{\min}$ | Circuit is purely resistive or load is too light; contacts part cleanly. |
| **Glow Discharge** | Low current, moderate gap | Cool, faint plasma, voltage ≈ 250–300 V. |
| **Metallic Arc** | $V \geq V_{\min}$ **AND** $I \geq I_{\min}$ | High-temperature plasma (≈ 5000 K), contact erosion, intense RFI. |
| **Avalanche / Breakdown** | $V \gg V_{\min}$, small gap | Spark, often observed in automotive 12 V systems during plug-pull. |

The arcing is governed by **Paschen's Law** at larger gaps, but for typical relay contact separations (< 1 mm), the breakdown voltage is dominated by the **field-emission and Townsend avalanche** mechanisms.

## 2.3 Phenomenon 3 — Inductive Kickback (The Real Showstopper)

When a switch opens an inductive load (relay coil, motor winding, solenoid), the inductor's stored energy cannot be dissipated instantly. By Lenz's Law:

$$V_L(t) = -L \frac{di}{dt}$$

Because $di/dt$ is very large (the switch tries to force current to zero in microseconds), $V_L$ spikes to **hundreds or thousands of volts**. This transient:
- **Arcs across the opening contacts** → generates intense RFI.
- **Damages the contacts** through pitting and material transfer.
- **Couples into adjacent circuits** through common-impedance and near-field coupling.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready equations** for Mechanical Switches. Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ in any inline math to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Definitions | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_L = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive kickback voltage across the switch | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $f_{\max} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_r}$ | Maximum spectral content of a step with rise time $t_r$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | $BW \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_r}$ | Bandwidth-to-rise-time relationship (FCC/Knee frequency) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{arc}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_b} v(t)\,i(t)\,dt$ | Energy dissipated in the arc over bounce/break time $t_b$ | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $V_{\text{clamp}} = V_{\text{supply}} + V_F$ | Clamped inductive kickback voltage using flyback diode ($V_F \approx 0.7$ V) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $V_{\text{snubber}} = I_{\text{peak}} \cdot R_s$ | Peak voltage across the snubber resistor $R_s$ at peak switch current $I_{\text{peak}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $R_s = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}}}{I_{\text{peak}}}$, $\quad C_s = \dfrac{I_{\text{peak}}\,t_{\text{off}}}{V_{\text{supply}}}$ | Practical snubber component sizing (RC across the switch) | $\Omega$, F |
| 8 | $V_{\text{knee}} = I_{\text{peak}} \cdot R_s$ | The "knee voltage" defining the I-V trajectory at switch opening | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $\tau_{RC} = R_s C_s$ | Time constant of the snubber; should equal switch opening time | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | $f_{\text{bounce}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{bounce}}}$, $T_{\text{bounce}} \approx 1\text{–}10\,\text{ms}$ | Repetition rate of bounce transients | Hertz (Hz) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive EMC** — A 12 V relay controlling a fuel pump is a textbook EFT source. The kickback pulse easily exceeds **300 V**, which is why automotive OEMs require flyback diodes on every solenoid.
- **Industrial Control Panels** — IEC 61000-4-4 immunity testing directly simulates switch-bounce bursts at **5 ns / 5 kHz / 15 ms** duration.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Tactile switches on a PCB (e.g., keyboard, appliance panel) must be debounced in firmware to avoid multiple-trigger events and to suppress conducted emissions.
- **Aerospace & Defense** — Relay arcing in 28 VDC systems is a documented ignition source; suppression networks are mandatory under MIL-STD-461 and DO-160.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inductive Kickback Magnitude (Switch Opening an Inductor)

**Setup:** A switch opens a circuit containing a DC supply $V_s$ and an inductor $L$ (resistance $R_L$). At $t = 0$ the switch opens.

**Step 1 — Steady-State current before opening:**
When the switch is closed for a long time, the inductor behaves as a short circuit (constant current). The current is:

$$I_0 = \frac{V_s}{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — The voltage across the switch during opening:**
The instant the switch opens, the inductor forces the current to continue. The voltage across the *opening* switch contacts must satisfy KVL:

$$V_{\text{switch}} = V_s - V_L = V_s + L \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Assuming the current decays linearly to zero in time $\Delta t$:**

$$\frac{di}{dt} \approx -\frac{I_0}{\Delta t}$$

The negative sign indicates current decrease.

**Step 4 — Substituting and solving for the peak switch voltage:**

$$V_{\text{peak}} = V_s + L \cdot \frac{I_0}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $I_0 = V_s / R_L$:**

$$V_{\text{peak}} = V_s + \frac{L \cdot V_s}{R_L \cdot \Delta t} = V_s \left(1 + \frac{L}{R_L \cdot \Delta t}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:**
- Smaller $\Delta t$ (faster switch opening) → larger $V_{\text{peak}}$.
- Larger $L$ → larger $V_{\text{peak}}$.
- The "$\frac{L}{R_L \Delta t}$" term is the **voltage multiplication factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** With $V_s = 24$ V, $L = 100$ mH, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$, $\Delta t = 1\,\mu$s:
>
> $V_{\text{peak}} = 24 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.1}{10 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 24 \times 1001 \approx 24{,}024$ V.
>
> This is why relay coils are **always** paired with a suppression element — without it, the arc energy alone is enough to weld the contacts shut.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Snubber Design Across a Mechanical Switch

**Setup:** A mechanical switch controlling an inductive load with peak operating current $I_{\text{peak}}$ and supply voltage $V_s$. We need to design an RC snubber that limits the switch voltage to $V_{\text{clamp}}$ during opening.

**Step 1 — Define the snubber function:**
At the moment the switch opens, the snubber resistor $R_s$ and capacitor $C_s$ in parallel with the switch must provide an alternative current path so that the switch voltage does not spike.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the switch node at $t = 0^+$ (instant of opening):**
The inductor current $I_{\text{peak}}$ must flow into the snubber:

$$I_{\text{peak}} = i_{R_s}(0^+) + i_{C_s}(0^+)$$

**Step 3 — Initial capacitor voltage = 0 (capacitor uncharged before switch opens):**
All current initially flows into the capacitor. As $C_s$ charges, voltage builds up and current diverts into $R_s$.

**Step 4 — At the peak (when $di/dt$ is at its largest), the switch voltage is approximately $V_{\text{clamp}} = I_{\text{peak}} R_s$. Therefore:**

$$R_s = \frac{V_{\text{clamp}}}{I_{\text{peak}}}$$

**Step 5 — Choosing the snubber time constant:**
The capacitor must charge fast enough to absorb the inductive energy. A practical rule (used in industry) is to set the RC time constant approximately equal to the switch opening time $\Delta t$:

$$R_s C_s = \Delta t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_s = \frac{\Delta t}{R_s}$$

**Step 6 — Combining (4) and (5):**

$$C_s = \frac{\Delta t \cdot I_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{clamp}}}$$

**Step 7 — Sanity check (energy balance):**
The energy stored in the inductor is $E_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_{\text{peak}}^2$. This energy must be safely dissipated in the snubber resistor without exceeding its power rating.

$$P_{R_s} = \frac{E_L \cdot f_{\text{switch}}}{1\,\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} L I_{\text{peak}}^2 f_{\text{switch}}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical example:**
- $I_{\text{peak}} = 1$ A, $V_s = 24$ V, $V_{\text{clamp}} = 50$ V, $\Delta t = 1\,\mu$s.
- $R_s = 50 / 1 = 50\,\Omega$.
- $C_s = (1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1) / 50 = 20$ nF.

> [!NOTE]
> A 50 Ω / 20 nF RC snubber is one of the **most commonly cited values in KTU previous-year solutions** for relay-coil suppression.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Suppression Techniques

| Technique | Component / Topology | Best Use Case | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flyback Diode** | Diode anti-parallel with inductor | DC inductive loads (relays, solenoids) | Cheap, simple, energy returned to source | Slow decay; **cannot** be used on AC loads |
| **RC Snubber** | $R_s$-$C_s$ in series across the switch | AC and DC, general purpose | Broad applicability, limits $dv/dt$ | Resistor dissipates energy as heat |
| **MOV (Varistor)** | Metal-Oxide Varistor across the load | High-voltage transients, AC mains | Clamps at a precise voltage | Degrades with repeated strikes; leakage current |
| **TVS Diode** | Transient Voltage Suppressor across switch | Fast, low-energy transients | Sub-ns response, precise clamp | Limited energy absorption |
| **Debounce (HW)** | Schmitt trigger + RC low-pass | Logic-level mechanical switches | Clean logic edges | Does not address arcing on the power side |
| **Debounce (SW)** | Firmware counter / delay | Microcontroller inputs | No extra components | Adds latency; does not reduce radiated EMI |
| **Zener Diode** | Zener in series with flyback diode | Higher voltage clamp on DC | Faster turn-off than diode alone | Power dissipation must be rated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Switch-Bounce Transient Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Switch Closure] --> B[Initial Contact at Asperities]
    B --> C[Kinetic Energy Stored in Contact Mass]
    C --> D[Elastic Rebound Begins]
    D --> E[Contact Separation - Microsecond]
    E --> F[Current Interruption - Arc Possible]
    F --> G[Re-Collision - Bounce 1]
    G --> H[Energy Dissipates]
    H --> I[Smaller Rebound - Bounce 2]
    I --> J[Final Settled Contact]
    J --> K[Steady Closed State]

    E --> L[Inductive Kickback Path]
    F --> L
    L --> M[High dv/dt Transient]
    M --> N[Conducted EMI on Lines]
    M --> O[Radiated EMI from Loop]

    subgraph "Suppression Strategies"
        S1[RC Snubber]
        S2[Flyback Diode]
        S3[MOV Varistor]
        S4[Debounce Circuit]
    end

    M --> S1
    M --> S2
    M --> S3
    K --> S4
```

## 4.2 Schematic — RC Snubber Across a Mechanical Switch Driving an Inductive Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[DC Supply Vs] --> SW[Mechanical Switch]
    SW --> N1((Node A))
    N1 --> L1[Inductor L]
    N1 --> RS[Snubber Resistor Rs]
    L1 --> RS
    RS --> N2((Node B))
    N2 --> RL[Load Resistance RL]
    RL --> GND1((GND))

    CS[Snubber Capacitor Cs] --- N1
    CS --- N2

    D1[Flyback Diode] -.-> L1
    D1 -.-> N1
```

## 4.3 Voltage-Time Waveform — Contact Bounce and Kickback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIME[Time Axis t]
        T0[0 us] --> T1[5 us] --> T2[10 us] --> T3[15 us] --> T4[20 us]
    end

    T0 --> P0[Contact Just Closed]
    P0 --> P1[First Bounce: 0.5 us rise]
    P1 --> P2[Re-close]
    P2 --> P3[Second Bounce: 0.3 us rise]
    P3 --> P4[Final Close]
    P4 --> P5[Steady Vs]

    T2 --> K0[Switch Opens]
    K0 --> K1[Kickback Spike ~ 10x Vs]
    K1 --> K2[Oscillatory Decay]
    K2 --> K3[Suppressed by Snubber]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most critical visual cues in any KTU answer are:
> 1. The **repetitive microsecond-scale edges** during the **bounce window** (1–10 ms).
> 2. The **high-amplitude, short-duration spike** at the moment of switch **opening** when driving an inductive load.
> Sketches in exam answers should always show both.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define contact bounce in mechanical switches. Why is it a concern from an EMC perspective?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Contact bounce is the repeated, unwanted making and breaking of electrical contact that occurs for a short duration (typically 1 ms – 10 ms) after a mechanical switch is operated, caused by the elastic rebound of the contacting surfaces.

**EMC Concern (1.5 marks):**
Each bounce edge has a very fast rise time (sub-microsecond). Using the relation $f_{\max} \approx 0.35/t_r$, these edges contain high-frequency spectral components. The repeated edges constitute a **burst of fast transients**, which:
- Couple into nearby circuits through conducted paths (common-impedance and capacitive coupling),
- Radiate as broadband EMI through any loop formed by the wiring,
- Fall directly within the spectrum simulated by the **IEC 61000-4-4 EFT/B immunity test**.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is inductive kickback? Write the governing equation and explain its EMC implication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Inductive kickback (flyback voltage) is the high reverse voltage that appears across a mechanical switch when it abruptly interrupts current flowing through an inductive load.

**Equation (1 mark):**
$$V_L = -L\,\frac{di}{dt}$$

**Implication (1 mark):**
Because $di/dt$ is very large during switch opening, $V_L$ can reach hundreds to thousands of volts. This causes (i) arcing across the contacts, (ii) contact pitting, and (iii) intense broadband radiated and conducted EMI, potentially exceeding the immunity limits of nearby sensitive circuits.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module 2 long-answer questions frequently combine a *theoretical* part (mechanism / spectrum) with an *analytical* part (snubber / diode design). The two choices below follow this exact pattern.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of contact bounce** in mechanical switches. With a suitable sketch, describe the voltage waveform during closure and discuss the **frequency spectrum** of the resulting disturbance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechanical switch controls a 24 V DC relay coil with $L = 50$ mH and coil resistance $R_L = 12\,\Omega$. The switch opens in $\Delta t = 2\,\mu$s. **Calculate:**
  (i) The steady-state current through the coil.
  (ii) The peak voltage appearing across the opening switch contacts.
  (iii) The frequency up to which this transient will contain significant spectral energy (assume the bounce has $t_r = 1\,\mu$s). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Contact Bounce (7 marks):**

1. **Physical origin (2 marks):** When switch contacts close, they touch first at microscopic peaks (asperities) on the metal surface. The kinetic energy of the moving contact is stored momentarily as elastic strain energy, then released as rebound. The contacts physically separate and re-collide multiple times, with decreasing amplitude, over a period of 1–10 ms.

2. **Voltage waveform (2 marks):** The output voltage does not transition cleanly from 0 to $V_s$. Instead, it shows a series of short pulses (representing the open microsecond intervals) interspersed with $V_s$ levels, decaying in frequency over the bounce window. A typical sketch shows 3–6 pulses within the first 2–5 ms.

3. **Frequency spectrum (3 marks):** Each pulse edge has a fast rise time $t_r$ in the sub-microsecond range. The maximum significant frequency is:
   $$f_{\max} \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$
   For $t_r = 1\,\mu$s, $f_{\max} \approx 350$ kHz. The burst repetition rate of 1 ms gives a fundamental of 1 kHz, but the **harmonic content extends up to several hundred kHz**, matching the spectrum of **IEC 61000-4-4 Electrical Fast Transient (EFT/B)** immunity tests.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Steady-state current (2 marks):**

$$I_0 = \frac{V_s}{R_L} = \frac{24}{12} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Peak voltage across opening contacts (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{peak}} = V_s\left(1 + \frac{L}{R_L \cdot \Delta t}\right) = 24 \left(1 + \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{12 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$V_{\text{peak}} = 24 \left(1 + \frac{0.05}{24 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 24 \left(1 + 2083.3\right)$$

$$V_{\text{peak}} \approx 24 \times 2084.3 \approx 50{,}024 \text{ V} \;\;\;(\approx 50 \text{ kV})$$

**(iii) Upper frequency of bounce spectrum (2 marks):**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{0.35}{t_r} = \frac{0.35}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 350 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part A:**
> - Students frequently **forget the "$+V_s$" term** in the kickback formula, losing 1 mark.
> - The bounce duration and the spectral content are **two different things**: do not confuse $T_{\text{bounce}} \approx 5$ ms with the rise-time-limited upper frequency of 350 kHz.
> - Always state **which standard** the spectrum matches (IEC 61000-4-4) — this is a 1-mark "application" credit.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **arcing phenomenon** in mechanical switches. With reference to the **V_min / I_min (Ignition) curve**, explain the conditions under which a self-sustaining metallic arc forms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same 24 V / 50 mH relay coil used in Question A, design a **flyback diode** and an **RC snubber**. Compare the two techniques with respect to energy dissipation, switching speed, and EMI suppression. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Arcing Phenomenon (7 marks):**

1. **Physical mechanism (2 marks):** When switch contacts begin to open, the contact area shrinks and the current density rises sharply. Localized resistive heating melts a microscopic bridge of metal between the contacts. As the gap widens, this molten bridge ruptures and a small plasma (arc) is established if the voltage and current are sufficient to sustain ionization.

2. **V_min and I_min concepts (3 marks):** For a self-sustaining metallic arc:
   - The arc voltage must exceed a **minimum arc voltage** $V_{\min} \approx 12$–$15$ V (for clean silver contacts).
   - The arc current must exceed a **minimum arc current** $I_{\min} \approx 0.3$–$1.0$ A.
   - If either condition fails, the arc **extinguishes** and the contacts part without further discharge.

3. **Ignition Curve (2 marks):** The locus of $V_{\min} \times I_{\min}$ defines the **Ignition Curve** (or Load Line boundary) on a V-I plot. For load trajectories *inside* the boundary, no metallic arc can sustain. Outside the boundary, an arc is possible. This curve is the basis of **arc-suppression design** — the snubber or diode is intended to *force* the load trajectory to stay within the no-arc region.

**Part (b) — Flyback Diode vs RC Snubber Design (7 marks):**

**Flyback Diode Design (2 marks):**
- Diode placed in anti-parallel with the relay coil, cathode to the positive supply side.
- Peak inverse voltage rating: $V_{\text{rev}} \geq V_s = 24$ V (use 50 V part for margin).
- Current rating: $I_F \geq I_0 = 2$ A (use 3 A part for margin).
- Clamped voltage: $V_{\text{clamp}} = V_s + V_F \approx 24.7$ V.

**RC Snubber Design (3 marks):**
Choose $V_{\text{clamp}} = 50$ V (twice supply, safe margin):

$$R_s = \frac{V_{\text{clamp}}}{I_{\text{peak}}} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\,\Omega$$

With switch opening time $\Delta t = 2\,\mu$s:

$$C_s = \frac{\Delta t \cdot I_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{clamp}}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2}{50} = 80 \text{ nF}$$

Snubber time constant: $\tau = R_s C_s = 25 \times 80 \times 10^{-9} = 2\,\mu$s ✓

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Flyback Diode | RC Snubber |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Dissipation | Returned to source | Dissipated as heat in $R_s$ |
| Switching Speed | Slow (current circulates) | Faster turn-off |
| AC Compatibility | **Not suitable for AC** | Suitable for AC and DC |
| EMI Suppression | Limited (slow $dv/dt$) | Good (limits $dv/dt$ explicitly) |
| Cost / Complexity | Very low | Moderate (two components) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B:**
> - In the ignition-curve question, do not state the values of $V_{\min}$ and $I_{\min}$ as fixed; mention that they **depend on contact material** (Ag, Au, W) and the **circuit inductance**.
> - Snubber resistor power rating is often **omitted** by students. Always state $P_{R_s} = \frac{1}{2} L I_{\text{peak}}^2 f_{\text{switch}}$ and pick a part with adequate margin (2× to 3×).
> - Flyback diodes **cannot** be used on AC relay coils — this is a frequently-tested trap question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you walk into the exam, lock down these facts:**

- [ ] **Contact Bounce Duration:** 1 ms – 10 ms. Edges have sub-microsecond rise times.
- [ ] **Frequency-to-Rise-Time Rule:** $f_{\max} \approx 0.35 / t_r$. Master this — it appears in every EMC module.
- [ ] **Inductive Kickback Equation:** $V_{\text{peak}} = V_s \left( 1 + \dfrac{L}{R_L \Delta t} \right)$. Always include the "$+V_s$" term.
- [ ] **Minimum Arc Voltage:** ≈ 12–15 V for clean Ag contacts. **Minimum Arc Current:** ≈ 0.3–1.0 A.
- [ ] **Ignition Curve:** Defines the boundary between arcing and non-arcing regimes on the V-I plane.
- [ ] **IEC 61000-4-4** is the EFT/B standard that replicates switch-bounce transients in immunity testing.
- [ ] **RC Snubber Sizing:** $R_s = V_{\text{clamp}} / I_{\text{peak}}$ and $C_s = \Delta t \cdot I_{\text{peak}} / V_{\text{clamp}}$.
- [ ] **Snubber Resistor Power:** $P_{R_s} = \frac{1}{2} L I_{\text{peak}}^2 f_{\text{switch}}$.
- [ ] **Flyback Diode:** Anti-parallel with inductor; $V_F \approx 0.7$ V; **DC loads only**.
- [ ] **MOV / TVS:** Use for fast, repeatable clamping; remember leakage and degradation.
- [ ] **Hardware vs Software Debounce:** HW (Schmitt trigger) cleans logic edges; SW (firmware delay) does not reduce radiated EMI.
- [ ] **Always draw a waveform** in long-answer questions — a labelled sketch of the bounce + kickback is worth 1–2 marks in any 7-mark sub-question.
- [ ] **Industrial case-study value:** Always tie your answer back to a real-world context (automotive relay, IEC 61000-4-4, MIL-STD-461, DO-160) for the "application-level" credit.

> **End of Module 2 / Mechanical Switches Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, PEEVT753.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) Measurement** is the systematic, standards-compliant process of **quantifying** two distinct electromagnetic characteristics of an Equipment Under Test (EUT):

1. **Emissions Measurement:** The level of electromagnetic energy *unintentionally* radiated or conducted by the EUT into its surrounding environment.
2. **Immunity (Susceptibility) Measurement:** The EUT's ability to *withstand* a defined level of externally applied electromagnetic disturbance without functional degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 syllabus, EMC measurement is governed by international standards: **CISPR 16-1-1** (measurement apparatus & methods), **CISPR 22/EN 55022** (ITE emissions), **IEC 61000-4** series (immunity test methods), and **FCC Part 15** (US regulations). The standard reference frequency ranges are **150 kHz – 30 MHz** (conducted) and **30 MHz – 1 GHz** (radiated).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you live in a **shared apartment building**:
- **Emissions** are like the *noise* (music, footsteps, TV) you generate. The landlord has rules: "Don't exceed 40 dB between 10 PM and 8 AM."
- **Immunity** is your ability to *tolerate* the noise from neighbors without losing sleep.

EMC measurement is the **"sound-level meter"** + **"noise tolerance test"** of the electronics world. Without it, every device would be shouting at full volume and have paper-thin walls, leading to a chaotic "acoustical riot" — except in electromagnetic (invisible) form.

### 1.3 Core Metrics
| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric Field Strength | $E$ | **V/m** (or dBμV/m) | Far-field radiated |
| Magnetic Field Strength | $H$ | **A/m** (or dBμA/m) | Low-frequency magnetic |
| Conducted Voltage | $V$ | **dBμV** (across 50 Ω) | Line-impedance stabilized |
| Conducted Current | $I$ | **dBμA** | Current probe measurement |
| Power | $P$ | **dBm** | Absorbed / radiated power |
| Antenna Factor | $AF$ | **dB/m** | Receiver calibration constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 50 Ω?** All EMC receivers, LISNs, and antennas in commercial EMC testing use a **standard reference impedance of 50 Ω**. This unifies the measurement chain so that voltage, power, and field strength can be interconverted without ambiguity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of EMC Measurements
EMC measurements are classified along two orthogonal axes:

**A. By Propagation Path**
- **Conducted Emissions (CE):** Energy coupled via *cables* (power, signal, I/O lines). Frequency range: **150 kHz – 30 MHz**.
- **Radiated Emissions (RE):** Energy coupled via *free space* from the EUT enclosure, cables, and apertures. Frequency range: **30 MHz – 1 GHz** (extended to **6 GHz** for modern digital devices).

**B. By Direction**
- **Emissions Testing:** EUT is the *source*. Output is measured.
- **Susceptibility / Immunity Testing:** EUT is the *victim*. Input disturbance is applied; functional behavior is monitored.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Conducted Emission Test
1. The EUT is placed on a non-conductive table **0.4 m** above a ground reference plane.
2. A **LISN (Line Impedance Stabilization Network)** is inserted in series with the power cable, providing a **defined 50 Ω impedance** to the EUT and *blocking* external noise from the mains.
3. The RF voltage developed across the **50 Ω measurement port** of the LISN is routed to the **EMC Receiver / Spectrum Analyzer**.
4. The receiver sweeps the band **150 kHz – 30 MHz** with a defined **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** and **Detector type** (Peak, Quasi-Peak, Average, RMS).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of a Radiated Emission Test
1. The EUT is placed on a turntable inside a **Semi-Anechoic Chamber (SAC)** or **Open Area Test Site (OATS)** at **10 m** (or **3 m**) measurement distance.
2. A calibrated **receiving antenna** (Biconical: 30–200 MHz, Log-Periodic/Horn: 200–1000 MHz) measures the radiated field.
3. The antenna is scanned in **height (1 m – 4 m)** and rotated in **azimuth (0°–360°)** to capture the maximum emission.
4. The measured voltage at the receiver is converted to field strength using the **Antenna Factor (AF)** and **cable loss** corrections.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{dB\mu V/m} = V_{dB\mu V} + AF_{dB/m} + L_{cable,dB}$ | Field strength from received voltage | dBμV/m |
| 2 | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{1\,\mu V}\right)$ | Voltage to dBμV | dBμV |
| 3 | $E_{dB\mu V/m} = 20\log_{10}(E) - 20\log_{10}(1\,\mu V/m)$ | Field to dBμV/m | dBμV/m |
| 4 | $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\,mW}\right)$ | Power to dBm | dBm |
| 5 | $P_{dBm} = V_{dB\mu V} - 107$ | Power (across 50 Ω) | dBm |
| 6 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\sqrt{30 \cdot P_t \cdot G_t}}{r}$ | Far-field radiated E-field | V/m |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \approx 377\,\Omega$ | Free-space intrinsic impedance | Ω |
| 9 | $RBW_{CISPR\,A} = 9\,kHz$ | Bandwidth (150 kHz – 30 MHz) | Hz |
| 10 | $RBW_{CISPR\,B} = 120\,kHz$ | Bandwidth (30 – 1000 MHz) | Hz |
| 11 | $CISPR\,QP\,charge = 1\,ms$ | QP detector charge time constant | s |
| 12 | $CISPR\,QP\,discharge = 160\,ms$ | QP detector discharge time constant | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detectors at a Glance:** **Peak** detector captures the maximum instantaneous envelope; **Quasi-Peak (QP)** weighs pulses by repetition rate (CISPR-weighted, emulates ear's annoyance); **Average** is the linear mean; **RMS** is the true power-equivalent. For final compliance, the **QP or Average** limit is binding — peak is only used for pre-compliance scanning.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Compliance Certification:** Mandatory for CE-marking, FCC, BIS (India), and UL marks.
- **Design Validation:** Used during PCB/IC design to verify radiated/conducted noise margins.
- **Forensic Troubleshooting:** Locates emission hot-spots in prototypes using near-field probes.
- **Automotive/Aerospace:** MIL-STD-461, DO-160 testing for mission-critical platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Field Strength from Receiver Voltage

**Statement:** Given a measured voltage $V_L$ (in dBμV) at the load of a receiving antenna with antenna factor $AF$ (in dB/m) and cable loss $L_c$ (in dB), derive the incident field strength $E$ (in dBμV/m).

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Antenna Factor:**

The antenna factor is defined as the ratio of the incident electric field to the open-circuit voltage induced at the antenna terminals:

$$
AF = \frac{E}{V_{oc}} \quad \text{[linear, 1/m]}
$$

Converting to decibels (reference 1/μV·m):

$$
AF_{dB/m} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E}{V_{oc}}\right) = E_{dB\mu V/m} - V_{oc,dB\mu V}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for cable loss:**
The receiver does not see the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ but the loaded voltage $V_L$, reduced by the cable attenuation $L_c$:

$$
V_{L,dB\mu V} = V_{oc,dB\mu V} - L_{c,dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$
V_{oc,dB\mu V} = V_{L,dB\mu V} + L_{c,dB}
$$

Plugging this into the AF expression:

$$
AF_{dB/m} = E_{dB\mu V/m} - \left(V_{L,dB\mu V} + L_{c,dB}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E$:**

$$
E_{dB\mu V/m} = V_{L,dB\mu V} + AF_{dB/m} + L_{c,dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-used formula in EMC radiated emission testing.** The procedure is universal: take the receiver's measured voltage, add the antenna factor (from calibration table) and the cable loss (from VNA sweep) — the sum is the regulatory field strength.

### 3.2 Derivation: Conducted Power from LISN Voltage

**Statement:** A LISN measures 60 dBμV at 10 MHz across its 50 Ω measurement port. Compute the conducted power in dBm and in watts.

**Step 1 — Convert dBμV to linear voltage:**

$$
V_{dB\mu V} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{1\,\mu V}\right)
$$

Solving for $V_{rms}$:

$$
V_{rms} = 1\,\mu V \times 10^{V_{dB\mu V}/20}
$$

Substituting 60:

$$
V_{rms} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{60/20} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{3} = 10^{-3}\,V = 1\,mV
$$

**Step 2 — Compute power across 50 Ω:**

$$
P = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} = \frac{(10^{-3})^2}{50} = \frac{10^{-6}}{50} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\,W = 20\,nW
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dBm:**

$$
P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{1\,mW}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(2 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
P_{dBm} = 10[\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^{-5})] = 10[0.301 - 5] = -47\,dBm
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the short-cut formula:**

$$
P_{dBm} = V_{dB\mu V} - 107 = 60 - 107 = -47\,dBm \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Far-Field E-Field from a Radiating Source

**Statement:** Derive the far-field electric field strength $E$ at distance $r$ from an isotropic radiator fed with power $P_t$.

**Step 1 — Power Density on a Spherical Wavefront:**
A lossless isotropic radiator spreads $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of radius $r$. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi r^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $S$ to $E$ via the free-space intrinsic impedance:**

In the far field, the wave is plane-wave like, so $S = \dfrac{E^2}{\eta_0}$ where $\eta_0 = 377\,\Omega$.

$$
E^2 = S \cdot \eta_0 = \frac{P_t \cdot \eta_0}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E$:**

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{P_t \cdot \eta_0}}{2\sqrt{\pi}\,r} = \frac{\sqrt{30\,P_t}}{r}
$$

> *(using $\eta_0/4\pi = 30\,\Omega$)*

**Step 4 — Introduce directive gain $G_t$:**
For a real antenna with gain $G_t$ relative to isotropic:

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{30 \cdot P_t \cdot G_t}}{r} \quad \text{[V/m]}
$$

This is the **Friis-transmission-compatible far-field E-field** expression used universally in EMC and link-budget analysis.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full Trace)

**Problem:** A Class-B intentional radiator (CISPR 22) is measured at 100 MHz. Receiver reads **30 dBμV**. Antenna factor at 100 MHz is **12 dB/m**. Cable loss is **2 dB**. Compute (a) field strength, (b) whether it meets the Class-B QP limit of **30 dBμV/m** at 10 m.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$
E_{dB\mu V/m} = V_{dB\mu V} + AF + L_c = 30 + 12 + 2 = 44\,dB\mu V/m
$$

**Part (b):**
Convert dBμV/m to μV/m for comparison with 30 dBμV/m:

$$
E_{\mu V/m} = 10^{44/20} = 10^{2.2} = 158.5\,\mu V/m
$$

The limit is $10^{30/20} = 31.6\,\mu V/m$.

Since $158.5 > 31.6$, the device **FAILS** the Class-B limit by **14 dB** (a factor of 5 in voltage).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conducted Emission Test Setup (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC["AC Mains 230V 50Hz"] --> LISN
    LISN["LISN 50 ohm\n150kHz-30MHz"] --> PWR["Power Port\nto EUT"]
    PWR --> EUT["EUT Equipment\nUnder Test"]
    EUT --> LOAD["Load or\nPeripheral"]
    LISN -->|50 ohm RF port| ATT["10 dB\nAttenuator"]
    ATT --> SA["EMC Receiver /\nSpectrum Analyzer"]
    SA --> PC["Data Logger\nLimit-Line Compare"]
    SA -->|"Quasi-Peak /\nAverage Detector"| REPORT["Compliance\nReport"]
```

### 4.2 Radiated Emission Test Setup (Anechoic Chamber)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EUT2["EUT on\nTurntable 360"] -->|radiates| TX["E-Field in\nFar Zone"]
    TX --> ANT["Receive Antenna\nHeight 1-4m"]
    ANT -->|coax| PRE["Preamp\n+ LNA"]
    PRE --> RX["EMI Receiver\n30MHz-1GHz"]
    RX --> CTRL["Controller PC\nwith CISPR Limits"]
    CTRL -->|Pass / Fail| OUT["Final Test\nReport"]
    CHAMBER["Semi-Anechoic Chamber\nAbsorber-lined walls"] -.encloses.- EUT2
    CHAMBER -.encloses.- ANT
```

### 4.3 LISN Internal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["L1 = 50 microhenry"] --> N1["Node A\nMains Side"]
    N1 -->|50 uH| C1["C1 = 1 microfarad\nto GND"]
    C1 -->|bypass| GND1["Ground Plane"]
    N1 --> N2["Node B\nEUT Side"]
    N2 -->|50 uH| L2["L2 = 50 microhenry"]
    L2 --> N3["Node C\nLoad Side"]
    N3 --> C2["C2 = 1 microfarad\nto GND"]
    C2 --> GND2["Ground Plane"]
    N2 -->|50 ohm| PORT["Measurement\nPort 50 ohm"]
    PORT --> R50["50 ohm termination\nto Spectrum Analyzer"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two **50 μH inductors** in each line block external mains-borne noise from reaching the EUT, while the **1 μF capacitors** shunt high-frequency noise to ground, forcing the EUT to see a clean, defined 50 Ω RF impedance at the measurement port.

### 4.4 EMC Measurement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Begin EMC Test"] --> Q1{"Frequency\nof Concern?"}
    Q1 -->|< 30 MHz| CON["Conducted Emission\nvia LISN"]
    Q1 -->|30 MHz - 1 GHz| RAD["Radiated Emission\nvia Antenna"]
    CON --> D1{"Compliance\nLimit?"}
    RAD --> D2{"Compliance\nLimit?"}
    D1 -->|Pass| OK1["PASS - Conductive"]
    D1 -->|Fail| FIX1["Add Filter / Shielding\nRe-test"]
    D2 -->|Pass| OK2["PASS - Radiative"]
    D2 -->|Fail| FIX2["Reduce Loop Area\nAdd Bead / Gasket\nRe-test"]
    OK1 --> DONE["Generate Report"]
    OK2 --> DONE
    FIX1 --> DONE
    FIX2 --> DONE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define **Antenna Factor (AF)**. What are its units, and how is it used in computing the radiated electric field strength from a measured receiver voltage?

**Model Answer:**
Antenna Factor (AF) is defined as the ratio of the incident electric field strength $E$ to the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ induced at the antenna terminals:

$$
AF = \frac{E}{V_{oc}} \quad \text{(linear, units 1/m)}
$$

In decibels, **units are dB/m**. The field strength is computed as:

$$
E_{dB\mu V/m} = V_{dB\mu V} + AF_{dB/m} + L_{cable,dB}
$$

where $V_{dB\mu V}$ is the receiver voltage and $L_{cable,dB}$ is the cable attenuation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
List the four standard **CISPR detector types** used in EMC receivers. State the specific use of the **Quasi-Peak (QP)** detector in regulatory compliance testing.

**Model Answer:**
The four CISPR detector types are:
1. **Peak Detector** — captures maximum instantaneous envelope.
2. **Quasi-Peak (QP) Detector** — weighted by pulse repetition rate.
3. **Average Detector** — linear mean of envelope.
4. **RMS Detector** — true root-mean-square of envelope.

**Use of QP:** The QP detector is the *historically binding* detector for CISPR regulatory limits. It emulates the human ear's response to nuisance interference — frequent pulses are weighted more heavily than sparse ones. Final compliance certification requires that the QP-measured value be below the limit line; peak alone is insufficient for legal pass.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **conducted emission measurement setup** as per CISPR 22. Explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An EMC receiver measures **45 dBμV** at the LISN measurement port at a frequency of 1 MHz. The LISN reference impedance is 50 Ω. Calculate:
   (i) The RMS voltage at the port.
   (ii) The conducted power in **dBm** and in **watts**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conducted Emission Setup (Block Diagram as in §4.1 above):**

- **AC Mains Source:** Provides the standard 230 V / 50 Hz (or 110 V / 60 Hz) supply to the EUT.
- **LISN (Line Impedance Stabilization Network):** Inserted in series with each power line. Provides a defined 50 Ω RF impedance to the EUT, ensuring repeatable measurements. Blocks external noise from the mains and routes the EUT's conducted noise to the measurement port. Frequency range: 150 kHz – 30 MHz.
- **EUT (Equipment Under Test):** The device whose emissions are being measured; placed on a non-conductive table 0.4 m above a ground plane.
- **10 dB Attenuator:** Protects the receiver's input from overload; improves impedance matching.
- **EMC Receiver / Spectrum Analyzer:** Sweeps the 150 kHz – 30 MHz band with the prescribed CISPR RBW (9 kHz in this band) and detector (Peak, QP, Average).
- **PC / Data Logger:** Compares the measured trace against the CISPR 22 Class-A or Class-B limit line and generates the pass/fail report.

**(b) Numerical:**

**(i) RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{rms} = 1\,\mu V \times 10^{45/20} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{2.25}
$$

$$
V_{rms} = 10^{-6} \times 177.83 = 1.778 \times 10^{-4}\,V = 177.83\,\mu V
$$

**(ii) Power in dBm:**

$$
P_{dBm} = V_{dB\mu V} - 107 = 45 - 107 = -62\,dBm
$$

**Power in watts:**

$$
P = 1\,mW \times 10^{-62/10} = 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6.2} = 10^{-9.2}\,W
$$

$$
P \approx 6.31 \times 10^{-10}\,W = 0.631\,nW
$$

Cross-check from first principles:

$$
P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{(1.778 \times 10^{-4})^2}{50} = \frac{3.16 \times 10^{-8}}{50} = 6.32 \times 10^{-10}\,W \quad \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **far-field electric field strength $E$** at a distance $r$ from a transmitting antenna of gain $G_t$ fed with power $P_t$. State the standard reference distance used in CISPR radiated emission tests. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a radiated emission test, a spectrum analyzer measures **20 dBμV** at 500 MHz. The antenna factor at 500 MHz is **22 dB/m** and the cable loss is **1.5 dB**. Calculate the **electric field strength in dBμV/m** and in **V/m**. Comment on compliance with the **CISPR 22 Class-B limit of 37 dBμV/m at 10 m** for this frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (as in §3.3):**

Starting from the isotropic radiator:
- Power density on sphere: $S = P_t / (4\pi r^2)$
- Plane-wave relation: $S = E^2 / \eta_0$, with $\eta_0 = 377\,\Omega$
- Therefore:

$$
E^2 = \frac{P_t \cdot \eta_0}{4\pi r^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{\sqrt{30\,P_t}}{r}
$$

- With directive gain $G_t$:

$$
\boxed{\,E = \frac{\sqrt{30\,P_t\,G_t}}{r} \quad [V/m]\,}
$$

The standard CISPR reference distance is **10 m** (with 3 m permitted for small chambers).

**(b) Field Strength Calculation:**

$$
E_{dB\mu V/m} = V_{dB\mu V} + AF_{dB/m} + L_c = 20 + 22 + 1.5 = 43.5\,dB\mu V/m
$$

Convert to V/m:

$$
E_{V/m} = 10^{(43.5 - 120)/20} = 10^{-76.5/20} = 10^{-3.825} \approx 1.496 \times 10^{-4}\,V/m
$$

Equivalently $E \approx 149.6\,\mu V/m$.

**Compliance Check:**
- Measured: **43.5 dBμV/m**
- Class-B Limit: **37 dBμV/m**
- Margin: $43.5 - 37 = +6.5\,dB$ (over limit)

The device **FAILS** the Class-B limit by **6.5 dB** (≈ a factor of 2.1 in voltage). The designer must add shielding, reduce cable loop areas, or improve PCB grounding and re-test.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cable loss** when computing field strength. Many students write $E = V + AF$ only. The cable is *not* lossless; always add $L_c$ for full marks.
> 2. **Wrong unit reference for dBμV/m.** The reference field is $1\,\mu V/m$, not $1\,V/m$. A 1 V/m field is $120\,dB\mu V/m$, not $0\,dB$.
> 3. **Confusing QP and Peak detectors.** Peak is for *fast scanning* only; CISPR compliance is decided by **QP and Average** values.
> 4. **Using the wrong RBW.** CISPR Band A (150 kHz–30 MHz) uses **9 kHz** RBW; Band B (30 MHz–1 GHz) uses **120 kHz**. Mismatching RBW gives non-compliant measurements.
> 5. **Ignoring the LISN function.** Students often say "LISN filters the signal" — it does *not* filter. It **stabilizes impedance** and **routes noise** to the measurement port.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] EMC measurement has two faces: **emissions** (EUT as source) and **immunity** (EUT as victim).
- [ ] **Conducted tests** use a LISN and cover **150 kHz – 30 MHz**; **radiated tests** use an antenna in a chamber or OATS and cover **30 MHz – 1 GHz** (up to 6 GHz for modern devices).
- [ ] The **reference impedance is 50 Ω** in all EMC instrumentation.
- [ ] **Antenna Factor (AF)** converts receiver voltage to field strength: $E = V + AF + L_{cable}$ (all in dB).
- [ ] **CISPR RBW:** 9 kHz (Band A) and 120 kHz (Band B).
- [ ] **CISPR detectors:** Peak (fast scan), QP (compliance), Average (compliance), RMS (wideband).
- [ ] **Far-field E-field:** $E = \sqrt{30\,P_t\,G_t}\,/\,r$, with $r = 10\,m$ as the standard CISPR test distance.
- [ ] **Free-space impedance** $\eta_0 = 377\,\Omega$ links $E$ and $H$ in the far field.
- [ ] **Power short-cut:** $P_{dBm} = V_{dB\mu V} - 107$ (across 50 Ω).
- [ ] **LISN** provides 50 μH / 1 μF / 50 Ω structure — *not* a filter, but an impedance standardizer.
- [ ] Always apply **cable-loss correction** when computing field strength.
- [ ] Final regulatory compliance is decided by the **QP or Average** detector reading vs. the limit line.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Supplies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Supplies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Power Supplies – Module 3 | EMC & Signal Integrity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> In the context of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, *Power Supplies* are not merely DC voltage sources; they are treated as **complex, non-ideal distribution networks** that both generate and receive electromagnetic interference. From an EMC perspective, a power supply is a **two-port conducted-emission and susceptibility subsystem** whose input AC/DC lines act as efficient antennas for Common-Mode (CM) and Differential-Mode (DM) noise, and whose output rails carry switching noise that couples into high-speed digital signals, degrading the bit-error-rate and timing margins of the system.

### 1.1 The Two Roles of a Power Supply in EMC

| Role | Function | EMC Concern |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Source of EMI** | Switching converters (SMPS) inject high *dv/dt* and *di/dt* noise back into the mains and onto DC rails. | Conducted & radiated emissions. |
| **Victim of EMI** | Sensitive analog/RF circuits receive ripple, droop, and ground bounce through the supply. | Susceptibility / Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR). |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Water Plumbing" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: Water Supply to a Household**
> Imagine the power supply as a **municipal water pipeline** delivering water (current) at a fixed pressure (voltage) to a tap (load).
> - The **water hammer** that occurs when you suddenly close a tap is identical to the **voltage spikes** created when a digital gate switches and abruptly cuts off current draw (inductive kick, L·di/dt).
> - The **shared pipe** in an apartment block is exactly the **common power/ground impedance** that allows one tenant's "noisy equipment" (a switching IC) to disturb another's "quiet appliance" (an ADC or RF front-end).
> - A **pressure-stabilising tank** placed near your tap is the **decoupling/bulk capacitor**, which supplies instantaneous local charge before the main pipe can respond.
> - A **water filter** at the building inlet is the **EMI input filter (X-cap, Y-cap, common-mode choke)**, blocking noise from both entering and leaving the system.
> Without these local tanks and filters, the entire building "rings" every time any tap is opened — exactly the **ground bounce** and **power rail collapse** that plague high-speed PCBs.

### 1.3 Critical Physical Quantities (Board-Examiner Favourites)

- **Common-Mode Noise (V_CM):** Noise that appears *in phase* on both supply and return conductors with respect to chassis/earth ground. Typical range: **1 mV – 100 mV** in poorly designed systems.
- **Differential-Mode Noise (V_DM):** Noise between the supply line and its return (line-to-line). Typical range: **10 mV – 500 mV**.
- **PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio):** Defined as **PSRR (dB) = 20 log₁₀ (V_ripple_in / V_out_ripple)**. Higher = better rejection.
- **Target Impedance (Z_target):** A KTU-critical concept representing the maximum allowable impedance of the Power Distribution Network (PDN) across all frequencies. Typically **milliohms** (e.g., 10 mΩ).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Noise Sources Inside a Switching Power Supply

A Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) is the dominant *culprit* in modern EMC problems. Its intrinsic switching action creates two principal noise currents:

1. **Differential-Mode (DM) current:** Loops between the +V rail and its return (GND), caused by the pulsating input current drawn by the chopper.
2. **Common-Mode (CM) current:** Caused by the rapid *dv/dt* on switching nodes coupling through parasitic capacitances (C_p) to the chassis/earth, producing displacement currents that flow equally on all conductors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SMPS are noisy:** A MOSFET switching at 100 kHz with a 400 V node rising in 50 ns produces:
>
> $$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{400\text{ V}}{50\times 10^{-9}\text{ s}} = 8 \times 10^{9}\text{ V/s}$$
>
> This is a powerful HF source whose harmonics extend well into the **CISPR 22 conducted band (150 kHz – 30 MHz)** and often the radiated band (30 MHz – 1 GHz).

## 2.2 The Power Distribution Network (PDN) & Target Impedance

The PDN is the entire path from the voltage regulator module (VRM) output to the IC power pins, including the bulk capacitor, the PCB plane pair, vias, and the on-die capacitance. The **target impedance** concept is the keystone of KTU board-exam problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency-Domain Impedance Profile (Knee Frequencies):**
> The PDN impedance vs. frequency shows a low plateau up to a certain frequency, then rises, and is successively flattened by the addition of capacitors. The design goal is to keep the impedance **below Z_target** across the entire bandwidth of the load's transient current spectrum.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application / Physical Meaning |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Target Impedance** | $Z_{target} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I_{transient}}$ | $\Omega$ (typically m$\Omega$) | Maximum allowable PDN impedance; derived from allowed supply-noise $\Delta V$. |
| 2 | **Bulk Capacitor Value** | $C_{bulk} = \dfrac{I_{load} \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V}$ | Farads (F) | Charge reservoir to ride through load transients. |
| 3 | **Decoupling Capacitor Self-Resonance** | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_{ESL}}}$ | Hz (Hz) | Above $f_r$, the cap behaves as an *inductor* and stops decoupling. |
| 4 | **ESL Limitation Frequency** | $f \approx \dfrac{X_{ESL}}{2\pi L_{ESL}}$ | Hz (Hz) | Frequency at which ESL of PCB trace/via dominates. |
| 5 | **Inductive Voltage Spike** | $V_{spike} = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Volts (V) | Voltage kick when current is interrupted (ground bounce mechanism). |
| 6 | **CM Noise Displacement Current** | $I_{CM} = C_p \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Amperes (A) | Current injected to chassis ground via parasitic capacitance. |
| 7 | **PSRR** | $PSRR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple,in}}{V_{ripple,out}}\right)$ | dB | Power Supply Rejection Ratio of regulator/IC. |
| 8 | **Filter Insertion Loss** | $IL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{load,\,no\,filter}}{V_{load,\,with\,filter}}\right)$ | dB | Effectiveness of EMI filter in stopping conducted noise. |
| 9 | **Loop Area & Radiated E-field** | $E \propto f^2 \cdot I \cdot A_{loop}$ | V/m | Radiation increases with frequency², current, and loop area. |
| 10 | **Conducted Emission Limit (CISPR 22 Class B, quasi-peak at 0.5 MHz)** | $\leq \mathbf{56\text{ dB}\mu\text{V}}$ | dB$\mu$V | KTU board-favourite regulatory limit to remember. |
| 11 | **Charge Supplied by Decoupling Cap** | $\Delta Q = C \cdot \Delta V$ | Coulombs (C) | Charge delivered during a current step $\Delta I \cdot \Delta t$. |
| 12 | **Time Constant of RC Decay** | $\tau = RC$ | seconds (s) | Settling time of a decoupling node (≈ 5$\tau$ for full settling). |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom base stations:** Strict PSRR is mandated to keep RF PA spurious emissions below ETSI EN 301 489 limits.
- **Automotive ECU:** Must survive ISO 7637-2 load-dump transients of up to **+100 V for 400 ms**; suppressed by TVS + bulk filtering.
- **Medical implants (IEC 60601-1-2):** Patient-coupled leakage currents must stay below **10 µA CF**, requiring Y-capacitor values < 4.7 nF.
- **Aerospace (DO-160):** 28 V DC bus tolerates ±600 V spikes, demanding multi-stage LC and common-mode filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly written — no jumps. Wrap the multi-line derivations in `aligned` blocks. Each step is paired with an explanatory text row to maintain KTU board-exam valuation transparency.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Target Impedance of a PDN

**Problem Context:** A high-speed ASIC draws $I = 5\text{ A}$ from a 1.0 V rail, with a maximum tolerable supply-noise excursion of $\Delta V = 25\text{ mV}$ (i.e., $\pm 2.5\%$). Determine the target impedance.

**Step 1 — Define the load transient.** Modern digital ICs can switch from near-zero current to full current in a single clock edge. The worst-case transient is therefore a step of magnitude $I_{transient} = 5\text{ A}$.

**Step 2 — Invoke Ohm's Law on the PDN.** If the PDN is modelled as a single equivalent impedance $Z_{PDN}$, then the voltage disturbance on the rail is:

$$
\Delta V = I_{transient} \cdot Z_{PDN}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Z_{PDN}$.** This is, by definition, the *target impedance* — the largest impedance the PDN can present without exceeding the noise budget:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{target} &= \frac{\Delta V}{I_{transient}} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}}{5\text{ A}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ }\Omega \\
Z_{target} &= \mathbf{5\text{ m}\Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency extension (Knee-Frequency Method).** The transient contains spectral components up to a knee frequency, often approximated by the inverse of the rise-time $t_r$:

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute a typical $t_r = 100\text{ ps}$ (high-speed DDR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{knee} &= \frac{0.5}{100 \times 10^{-12}\text{ s}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{9}\text{ Hz} = 5\text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The PDN must present an impedance $\leq 5\text{ m}\Omega$ from DC to **5 GHz** — a stringent demand that can only be met by combining bulk, ceramic, and on-die capacitance, plus low-inductance plane pairs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Decoupling Capacitor Sizing

**Context:** A microcontroller core draws $I_{core} = 2\text{ A}$ for a duration $\Delta t = 10\text{ ns}$ during a wake-up event. The supply must not droop by more than $\Delta V = 50\text{ mV}$. Find the minimum local decoupling capacitance.

**Step 1 — The local cap must supply the entire transient charge** before the bulk capacitor (far away) can respond via the inductive path.

**Step 2 — Charge required:**

$$
\Delta Q = I_{core} \cdot \Delta t = 2\text{ A} \times 10 \times 10^{-9}\text{ s} = 20\text{ nC}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor relation $\Delta Q = C \cdot \Delta V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V} \\
&= \frac{20 \times 10^{-9}\text{ C}}{50 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7}\text{ F} \\
C_{min} &= \mathbf{400\text{ nF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency-domain check (self-resonance).** Choose a 470 nF X5R ceramic. Its ESL is typically 0.5 nH. Verify it is effective up to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 470 \times 10^{-9}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.35 \times 10^{-16}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1.533 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&\approx \mathbf{10.4\text{ MHz}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Above 10.4 MHz, this capacitor is useless.** For higher-frequency transients, smaller-value caps (e.g., 10 nF, 100 pF) with lower ESL are placed in parallel — a KTU board-exam favourite concept called **"Frequency-Interleaved Decoupling"** or **"Reverse Geometry"** design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inductive Ground Bounce on a Shared Return

**Context:** A 16-bit data bus switches simultaneously. The shared ground pin has an ESL of $L_{gnd} = 5\text{ nH}$. Each output drives $I = 24\text{ mA}$ (so 16 lines switch: $I_{total} = 16 \times 24\text{ mA} = 384\text{ mA}$) with $t_r = 2\text{ ns}$. Calculate the ground-bounce voltage.

**Step 1 — Compute di/dt:**

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{0.384\text{ A}}{2 \times 10^{-9}\text{ s}} = 1.92 \times 10^{8}\text{ A/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inductive-kick formula $V = L \cdot di/dt$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bounce} &= L_{gnd} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-9}\text{ H} \times 1.92 \times 10^{8}\text{ A/s} \\
&= 0.96\text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** A *near 1 V* bounce on a 3.3 V logic rail is **catastrophic** — false triggering, latch-up, and EMI are inevitable.

**Step 4 — Mitigation (KTU-mandated remedies):**
- Reduce ESL by using **multiple parallel ground pins** ($L$ scales as $1/N$).
- Insert **gate-damping resistors** to slow $t_r$ deliberately to 5–10 ns.
- Use a **dedicated ground plane** instead of a ground trace.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Linear vs. Switching Power Supply (EMC View)

| Parameter | Linear Regulator (LDO) | Switching Power Supply (SMPS) |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Efficiency** | 30 – 60 % | 80 – 95 % |
| **Conducted EMI** | Very low (no high *dv/dt*) | High (rectifier + chopper noise) |
| **Radiated EMI** | Negligible | Significant (transformer, switching node) |
| **PSRR @ 1 MHz** | 40 – 80 dB | 20 – 40 dB (depends on filter) |
| **Ripple** | $\mu$V-level | 10 – 100 mV before filtering |
| **EMC Cost** | Low filter complexity | Needs input + output LC, X/Y caps, CM choke, shielding |
| **Best Application** | RF, audio, precision analog | High-current digital, battery, PoL converters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams are used to visualise functional architectures. Node IDs are strictly alphanumeric. Labels are kept raw (no bold/italic markup inside the quoted labels).

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Conducted-EMI Power-Supply Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mains AC Input] --> B[CY1 Y Capacitor L to N]
    B --> C[CMCHOKE Common Mode Choke]
    C --> D[CX1 X Capacitor L to N]
    D --> E[BRIDGE Bridge Rectifier]
    E --> F[CBULK Bulk Reservoir Capacitor]
    F --> G[SWNODE Switching MOSFET Node]
    G --> H[HFLOOP High Frequency Loop Snubber]
    H --> I[OUTFIL Output LC Filter]
    I --> J[OUTCERA Output Ceramic Decoupling]
    J --> K[DC Load Sensitive IC]
    L[CHASSIS Earth Ground] --- B
    L --- J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `CY1` (Y-cap, line-to-earth) bleeds CM noise to chassis.
- `CX1` (X-cap, line-to-neutral) suppresses DM noise.
- `CMCHOKE` presents high impedance to CM currents only.
- `HFLOOP` (RC snubber) damps ringing at the switching node — a primary EMI source.

---

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain PDN Impedance & Decoupling Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[DC to 100 kHz] --> S1[Supplied by VRM Output Inductor and Bulk Capacitor]
    F2[100 kHz to 1 MHz] --> S2[Bulk Electrolytic Capacitors]
    F3[1 MHz to 100 MHz] --> S3[Mid value Ceramic Capacitors 100 nF to 1 uF]
    F4[100 MHz to 1 GHz] --> S4[Small value MLCC 10 nF to 1 nF]
    F5[Above 1 GHz] --> S5[On die Capacitance and Plane Pair Capacitance]
    ZLINE[Ztarget Horizontal Line 5 mOhm] -.-> S1
    ZLINE -.-> S2
    ZLINE -.-> S3
    ZLINE -.-> S4
    ZLINE -.-> S5
```

**Interpretation:** The horizontal Ztarget line is the design threshold. Each capacitor "bucket" is responsible for keeping the PDN impedance below that line within its frequency band. The overlapping regions ensure no impedance "bump" rises above Ztarget.

---

## 4.3 Noise-Coupling Mechanisms in a Power Supply Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SMPS[SMPS Switching Converter] -->|DM noise| PDN[Power Distribution Network]
    SMPS -->|CM via Cpar| EARTH[Chassis Earth]
    EARTH -->|Y cap discharge| SMPS
    PDN -->|Supply noise coupling| AFE[Analog Front End ADC]
    PDN -->|Ground bounce| DIG[High Speed Digital IC]
    DIG -->|di_dt returns| PDN
    AFE -->|PSRR limited| OUT[Output Signal Distortion]
    DIG -->|Radiated E field| ANT[PCB Trace Antenna]
    ANT -.->|Reception| AFE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The SMPS is the **source** of both DM and CM noise.
- PDN impedance is the **medium** through which noise reaches victims.
- The high-speed digital IC, through its switching, **regenerates** noise.
- Analog/RF blocks are **victims**, suffering both conducted and radiated pickup.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** (two full-question alternatives). Each sub-part is 7 marks. Model solutions are exhaustive to reflect examiner expectation.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) of a voltage regulator. A regulator exhibits 200 mV of input ripple and produces 2 mV of output ripple. Calculate its PSRR in dB.**

**Model Answer:**
PSRR is the ratio of input ripple to output ripple expressed in decibels, indicating the regulator's ability to reject supply-borne noise.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSRR &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{ripple,\,in}}{V_{ripple,\,out}}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200\text{ mV}}{2\text{ mV}}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = \mathbf{40\text{ dB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks distribution:** Definition 1M, Substitution 1M, Final answer 1M.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Common-Mode (CM) and Differential-Mode (DM) conducted noise in a power supply. Which one is more effectively attenuated by an X-capacitor?**

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Common-Mode (CM) Noise | Differential-Mode (DM) Noise |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Path | Both line and neutral w.r.t. earth | Between line and neutral |
| Cause | *dv/dt* through parasitic C | Pulsating input current of rectifier/chopper |
| Mitigation | CM choke + Y-cap | X-cap, series inductor |

An **X-capacitor (X1/X2 class)** is connected line-to-neutral and shorts DM noise to ground. Therefore, **X-capacitor attenuates Differential-Mode noise**.

**Marks distribution:** Difference table 2M, identification 1M.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the major sources of **conducted and radiated EMI** in a switched-mode power supply (SMPS). Explain how the high *dv/dt* at the switching node produces both common-mode and differential-mode noise currents.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ASIC operates from a 1.2 V rail and draws a worst-case transient of **3 A** in **0.5 ns**. Calculate (i) the target impedance of the PDN if the maximum tolerable supply noise is 30 mV, and (ii) the minimum local decoupling capacitance required to absorb the transient.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — EMI Sources in an SMPS (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the SMPS subsystems (1 M):**
A typical SMPS contains: a bridge rectifier, a bulk capacitor, a switching MOSFET/IGBT, a high-frequency transformer, an output rectifier, and an output LC filter. Each block contributes to EMI.

**Step 2 — Source of Differential-Mode noise (2 M):**
The rectifier draws current in pulses at twice the line frequency (100/120 Hz), creating strong harmonics. The chopper's pulsed current on the +V rail returns through the input capacitor loop. The **loop area** of this current path radiates magnetic fields whose E-field strength scales as:

$$
E \propto f^2 \cdot I \cdot A_{loop}
$$

**Step 3 — Source of Common-Mode noise (2 M):**
The MOSFET drain node switches between 0 V and 400 V in ~50 ns. The drain's parasitic capacitance to the chassis/earth (C_p ≈ 5–20 pF) conducts a displacement current:

$$
I_{CM} = C_p \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

This current returns to the source via the mains earth wire, exciting the line impedance stabilisation network (LISN) and creating CM voltage at the mains terminals.

**Step 4 — Radiated EMI (1 M):**
PCB traces carrying the drain-node *dv/dt* act as monopole antennas. The harmonics extend up to several hundred MHz, falling under **CISPR 22 / FCC Part 15** radiated limits.

**Step 5 — Mitigation overview (1 M):** Input EMI filter, snubbers, CM choke, shielding, proper PCB layout minimising loop area.

---

#### Part (b) — PDN Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify $I_{transient} = 3\text{ A}$**, $t_r = 0.5\text{ ns}$, $\Delta V_{max} = 30\text{ mV}$.

**Step 2 — Calculate the target impedance (3 M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{target} &= \frac{\Delta V}{I_{transient}} \\
&= \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}}{3\text{ A}} \\
&= 10 \times 10^{-3}\text{ }\Omega = \mathbf{10\text{ m}\Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the knee frequency (1 M):**

$$
f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r} = \frac{0.5}{0.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 1\text{ GHz}
$$

The PDN must hold $Z \leq 10\text{ m}\Omega$ from DC to **1 GHz** — a very demanding design.

**Step 4 — Calculate the minimum local decoupling capacitor (3 M):**

The charge required during the transient is:

$$
\Delta Q = I \cdot t_r = 3\text{ A} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9}\text{ s} = 1.5\text{ nC}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V} \\
&= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}\text{ C}}{30 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-8}\text{ F} = \mathbf{50\text{ nF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum of **50 nF** of *local, low-ESL* capacitance (typically multiple 10 nF and 100 nF ceramics in parallel) is required, in addition to bulk capacitance and on-die capacitance, to meet the 10 m$\Omega$ target up to 1 GHz.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Power Distribution Network (PDN)** of a high-speed digital PCB. Explain the concept of **target impedance** and **frequency-interleaved decoupling** using bulk, ceramic, and on-die capacitors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 16-bit bus switches 12 mA per line with a 3 ns edge time. The shared ground bond-wire ESL is 4 nH. Compute the (i) di/dt, (ii) ground-bounce voltage, and (iii) suggest two PCB-level remedies.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — PDN and Target Impedance (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — PDN block diagram (2 M):** VRM → Bulk Cap → Plane Pair → Vias → Decoupling Caps → IC Package → On-die Cap.

**Step 2 — Definition of Target Impedance (1 M):** $Z_{target} = \Delta V / I_{transient}$.

**Step 3 — Frequency Interleaving (3 M):**
- **Bulk electrolytic (100 µF):** Effective up to ~10 kHz (high C, high ESL).
- **Mid-ceramic (100 nF X7R):** Effective 1 MHz – 100 MHz.
- **Small ceramic (10 nF – 1 nF):** Effective 100 MHz – 1 GHz.
- **On-die (nF – pF range):** Effective above 1 GHz.
Each cap "takes over" as the previous one becomes inductive, ensuring $Z_{PDN} < Z_{target}$ continuously.

**Step 4 — Engineering comment (1 M):** Plane-pair spreading inductance must be < 1 nH/inch for GHz decoupling; this demands high-layer-count stack-ups and *reverse-geometry* via placement (via as close as possible to the IC power pad).

---

#### Part (b) — Ground Bounce Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Total switching current (1 M):**

$$
I_{total} = 16 \times 12\text{ mA} = 192\text{ mA} = 0.192\text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — di/dt (2 M):**

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{0.192\text{ A}}{3 \times 10^{-9}\text{ s}} = 6.4 \times 10^{7}\text{ A/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Ground-bounce voltage (2 M):**

$$
V_{bounce} = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = 4 \times 10^{-9}\text{ H} \times 6.4 \times 10^{7}\text{ A/s} = 0.256\text{ V} = \mathbf{256\text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 4 — PCB-level remedies (2 M):**
1. **Use a continuous ground plane** beneath the bus, which drastically reduces the effective ground-return inductance (microhenries → nanohenries).
2. **Add series damping resistors (22–33 Ω)** in each line to deliberately slow the edge to 8–10 ns, reducing *di/dt* by ~3× → bounce drops to ~85 mV.
3. **Stagger the bus timing (source-synchronous clocking)** so that not all 16 lines switch on the same edge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20 vs. 20 in PSRR:** PSRR uses **20 log₁₀** (voltage ratio), *not* 10 log₁₀. Power quantities use 10 log₁₀.
> 2. **Mixing up X-cap and Y-cap:** X-caps go **line-to-neutral** (DM); Y-caps go **line-to-earth** (CM). Wrong placement is a major board-penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring ESL in decoupling:** Students often compute the *capacitor value* but forget to verify **self-resonant frequency**. Above $f_r$, the cap is an inductor.
> 4. **Dropping units:** KTU examiners deduct a *½-mark* for each missing SI unit in numerical problems.
> 5. **Using mΩ vs Ω casually:** 5 mΩ is **target impedance**, NOT 5 Ω. A 1000× error invalidates the entire design.
> 6. **Confusing $t_r$ (rise-time) with clock period:** The PDN bandwidth is set by $t_r$, not by the clock frequency.
> 7. **Not justifying the choice of decoupling cap:** Just stating "place 100 nF" earns partial marks; you must show why the chosen value satisfies the impedance target at the relevant frequency.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **power supply** in EMC is a *two-port noise source AND victim*. Treat its input as an antenna and its output as a victim rail.
- ✅ **Target Impedance** $Z_{target} = \Delta V / I_{transient}$ is the cornerstone concept of PDN design.
- ✅ **Frequency-interleaved decoupling:** Bulk → Mid-ceramic → Small-ceramic → On-die, each handling its own band.
- ✅ **Self-resonant frequency** $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC_{ESL}})$ limits a capacitor's usefulness.
- ✅ **Ground bounce** $V_{gb} = L_{gnd} \cdot di/dt$ — reduce by ground planes, damping resistors, staggered switching.
- ✅ **CM noise** = *dv/dt* × parasitic C; mitigated by **CM choke + Y-cap**.
- ✅ **DM noise** = pulsating current; mitigated by **X-cap + series inductor**.
- ✅ **PSRR** uses **20 log₁₀** (voltage ratio), not 10 log₁₀.
- ✅ **CISPR 22 Class B conducted emission limit at 0.5 MHz** ≈ **56 dBµV (quasi-peak)** — a common KTU 2-mark memory question.
- ✅ **Inductive kick** $V = L \, di/dt$ is the root cause of both ground bounce and supply overshoot.
- ✅ Linear regulators have **excellent PSRR and EMI** but **poor efficiency**; SMPS are the **opposite**.
- ✅ The **knee frequency** of the load is $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$ — the PDN must remain low-impedance up to this point.
- ✅ Always express decoupling values with **SI units and engineering prefixes** (nF, pF, mΩ, nH) — KTU examiners mark this strictly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Filters ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the domain of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)**, an **EMI Filter** (Electromagnetic Interference Filter) is defined as a two-port passive or active network composed of discrete reactive components (capacitors, inductors, ferrite beads) and resistive elements, strategically inserted into a conductive path to **attenuate unwanted high-frequency noise components** while permitting the desired low-frequency signal or DC power to pass through with minimal loss. The fundamental performance metric of any EMC filter is its **Insertion Loss (IL)**, measured in **decibels (dB)**, which quantifies the ratio of voltage (or power) delivered to a load *with* the filter inserted, compared to the voltage delivered *without* the filter, across a specified frequency spectrum (typically **9 kHz to 10 GHz** as mandated by CISPR and FCC standards). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary role of a filter in EMC is NOT signal processing (as in communication theory), but **NOISE SUPPRESSION** — to bring conducted and radiated emissions within the regulatory compliance limits (e.g., **CISPR 22 / EN 55022 Class B limits of 40 dBµV at 30 MHz**). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a **water tap connected to a noisy municipal pipeline**. The water (your useful DC power or low-frequency signal) needs to flow cleanly into your house. But the pipeline also carries gritty sand, rust particles, and high-pressure surges (your high-frequency EMI noise). What do you do? You install a **multi-stage filtration unit**: - A **coarse strainer** (analogous to an **inductor**) blocks large chunks but lets water pass. - A **fine mesh filter** (analogous to a **capacitor**) blocks smaller particles to ground. - A **combination unit** (analogous to **Pi-filter or T-filter**) provides cascading cleanup. The cleaner the water that reaches your tap, the higher the **Insertion Loss (in dB)** of the filtration system for the *unwanted* components. This is exactly how an EMC filter works — except instead of grit, it filters out **electromagnetic noise currents and voltages**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** A filter is "transparent" to the desired signal (low loss at passband) and "opaque" to noise (high attenuation at stopband). The **steepness of transition** between these two regions is called the **roll-off rate**, typically **20 dB/decade per pole**. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Emphasis) - **Characteristic Source/Load Impedance:** Typically **Z₀ = 50 Ω** for RF systems, **75 Ω** for video, and **mismatched impedances (low/high)** for power line filters. - **Cutoff Frequency (f_c):** The -3 dB point, typically **30 MHz** for commercial EMI filters. - **Insertion Loss Range:** Industrial filters offer **20 dB to 80 dB** attenuation in the stopband. - **Quality Factor (Q):** For resonant filters, **Q = f₀ / BW**, where BW is the -3 dB bandwidth. - **Velocity of propagation in vacuum:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (used in distributed filter design). - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** **η₀ ≈ 377 Ω** (relevant for radiated filter coupling). ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" An EMC filter performs its suppression function through **two simultaneous physical mechanisms**: 1. **Reflection Mechanism (Impedance Mismatch):** The filter presents a **high impedance to noise currents** in series elements (inductors) and a **low impedance to noise voltages** in shunt elements (capacitors). This forces the noise to be reflected back toward the source rather than transmitted to the load. 2. **Absorption Mechanism (Lossy Materials):** Ferrite cores, conductive elastomers, and resistive materials convert electromagnetic energy into heat, dissipating the noise irreversibly. --- ### 2.2 Classification of EMC Filters (Structural Logic) **A. By Frequency Response:** - **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes DC and low frequencies; rejects high-frequency noise. Most common in EMC. - **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Rejects DC; used rarely in power EMC but common in AC line filtering to block DC bias. - **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Selects a specific frequency band; used in receiver front-ends for EMI rejection. - **Band-Stop (Notch) Filter:** Rejects a narrow band (e.g., 50/60 Hz power line harmonics in instrumentation). **B. By Topology (L-C Configuration):** - **L-Section Filter:** Single inductor + single capacitor. - **Pi-Filter (π):** Capacitor-Inductor-Capacitor (C-L-C). Provides **40 dB/decade** roll-off (2nd order). - **T-Filter:** Inductor-Capacitor-Inductor (L-C-L). High series impedance. - **Multi-Stage Filters:** Cascaded L, Pi, or T sections for sharper roll-off. **C. By Application:** - **Power Line Filters:** For mains (230 V AC / 50 Hz) suppression. - **Signal Line Filters:** For data, audio, and communication lines. - **Feedthrough Filters:** Mounted on shielded enclosure walls for bulkhead mounting. **D. By Noise Mode:** - **Common-Mode (CM) Filters:** Suppress noise appearing in-phase on both lines (relative to ground). - **Differential-Mode (DM) Filters:** Suppress noise appearing differentially between the two lines. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notational Convention:** To avoid breaking markdown table syntax, the absolute value / magnitude operator is rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe character. | **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** | |:------------|:----------------|:----------------| | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | RC Low-Pass Cutoff Frequency | Single-pole LPF in signal lines | | $f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$ | RL High-Pass Cutoff Frequency | Single-pole HPF in power decoupling | | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC Resonant Frequency | Pi/T filters, notch filters | | $IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert V_{in} \vert}{\vert V_{out} \vert}\right)$ | Insertion Loss (dB) | Filter attenuation measurement | | $IL \approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)^{2n}\right)$ | Asymptotic IL (nth-order Butterworth) | Multi-pole filter estimation | | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Quality Factor of LC Tank | Resonance sharpness | | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L -
