Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection Coefficient | Impedance matching in filter ports | | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ | Inductive Reactance | Series impedance of L elements | | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive Reactance | Shunt impedance of C elements | | $Z_{in,CM} = j\omega L_{CM} + R_{core}$ | CM Choke Impedance | Common-mode suppression | | $\tau = RC$ | Time Constant | Transient response of filters | Where: - $f_c$ = Cutoff frequency in Hz - $n$ = Number of filter poles (order) - $Z_0$ = System characteristic impedance (typically 50 Ω) - $Z_L$ = Load impedance - $L_{CM}$ = Common-mode choke inductance --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility EMC filters are **non-negotiable components** in: - **Medical Electronics** (IEC 60601-1-2 compliance for patient safety). - **Automotive Electronics** (ISO 11452 and CISPR 25 for EV inverters, BMS lines). - **Aerospace & Defense** (MIL-STD-461 for military platforms). - **Telecommunications** (FCC Part 15, EN 55022 for IT equipment). - **Industrial Drives** (Variable Frequency Drives generate intense CM/DM noise on motor cables). The **economic impact** is enormous — a single failure to meet EMC compliance can delay a product launch by 6-12 months, costing millions in re-engineering. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Insertion Loss of a Simple RC Low-Pass Filter **Problem Setup:** Consider a source $V_s$ with internal resistance $R_s = 50\ \Omega$ driving a load $R_L = 50\ \Omega$. An RC low-pass filter is inserted, where R is in series and C is in shunt. **Step 1: Without Filter (Direct Connection)** The load voltage without any filter is simply the voltage divider result: $$ V_{out,no\,filter} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_s + R_L} $$ **Step 2: With Filter Inserted** The transfer function of the RC low-pass filter is: $$ H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ **Step 3: Define Insertion Loss** By definition, Insertion Loss is the ratio (in dB) of voltage *before* filter insertion to *after* insertion: $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert V_{out,no\,filter} \vert}{\vert V_{out,with\,filter} \vert}\right) $$ **Step 4: Substitute the Transfer Function** Assuming matched impedances ($R_s = R_L = R$), and treating the filter as inserted between matched impedances: $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\vert H(j\omega) \vert}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\vert 1 + j\omega RC \vert\right) $$ **Step 5: Compute Magnitude** $$ \vert 1 + j\omega RC \vert = \sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2} $$ **Step 6: Final IL Expression** $$ \boxed{IL(f) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + (2\pi f R C)^2\right)\ \text{dB}} $$ **Step 7: Evaluate at Special Frequencies** At $f = f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$: $$ IL(f_c) = 10 \log_{10}(1 + 1) = 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01\ \text{dB} $$ This confirms the **-3 dB definition of cutoff frequency**. At $f = 10 f_c$ (one decade above cutoff): $$ IL(10 f_c) = 10 \log_{10}(1 + 100) = 10 \log_{10}(101) \approx 20.04\ \text{dB} $$ This is the famous **20 dB/decade roll-off** of a single-pole RC filter. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pi-Filter Insertion Loss (C-L-C Topology) **Topology:** $C_1$ (input shunt) — $L$ (series) — $C_2$ (output shunt). Assume $C_1 = C_2 = C$ for symmetry. **Step 1: Write the Total Series Impedance** $$ Z_{series}(\omega) = j\omega L $$ **Step 2: Write the Total Shunt Admittance** $$ Y_{shunt}(\omega) = j\omega C_1 + j\omega C_2 = j2\omega C $$ **Step 3: ABCD Matrix of the Pi-Network** For each element, the transmission (ABCD) matrix is: Capacitor $C_1$ (shunt): $$ M_C = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ j\omega C & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ Inductor $L$ (series): $$ M_L = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & j\omega L \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ Capacitor $C_2$ (shunt): $$ M_C = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ j\omega C & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 4: Multiply Matrices to Get Composite ABCD** $$ M_{total} = M_C \cdot M_L \cdot M_C $$ Multiplying the first two: $$ M_C \cdot M_L = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - \omega^2 LC & j\omega L \\ j\omega C & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ Then multiplying by $M_C$: $$ M_{total} = \begin{pmatrix} (1 - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega L \cdot j\omega C & j\omega L \\ j\omega C(1 - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega C & 1 + j\omega L \cdot j\omega C \end{pmatrix} $$ Simplifying using $j^2 = -1$: $$ M_{total} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 2\omega^2 LC & j\omega L \\ j\omega C(2 - \omega^2 LC) & 1 - \omega^2 LC \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 5: Compute Insertion Loss** For a filter inserted between matched impedances $Z_0$: $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\vert A + B/Z_0 + C Z_0 + D \vert\right) $$ For symmetric networks ($A = D$): $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\vert A \vert\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\vert 1 - 2\omega^2 LC \vert\right) $$ **Step 6: Attenuation at High Frequencies ($\omega \gg \omega_0$)** $$ IL \approx 20 \log_{10}(2\omega^2 LC) = 20 \log_{10}(2) + 40 \log_{10}(\omega\sqrt{LC}) $$ This gives the famous **40 dB/decade roll-off** characteristic of a second-order Pi-filter — exactly **double** that of a single RC stage. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Common-Mode Choke Design **Principle:** A CM choke uses a single magnetic core with two windings (in-phase for DM, anti-phase for CM). The CM current sees high inductance, while DM current sees near-zero net flux. For an ideal CM choke: $$ Z_{CM} = j\omega L_{CM} = j\omega \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell} $$ Where: - $\mu_r$ = Relative permeability of ferrite core (**typically 1000–10000 for MnZn ferrite**) - $N$ = Number of turns - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of core - $\ell$ = Mean magnetic path length The DM impedance arises only from leakage inductance $L_{leak}$ (typically 1-3% of $L_{CM}$): $$ Z_{DM} = j\omega L_{leak} $$ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EMC Power Line Filter The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the cascaded functional architecture of a typical **three-stage EMC power line filter**, showing the interaction of common-mode (CM) and differential-mode (DM) suppression stages with the line, neutral, and protective earth (PE) conductors. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["AC MAINS INPUT
230V 50Hz L and N"] --> nodeB["STAGE 1
CM CHOKE Lcm
Common Mode Rejection"] nodeB --> nodeC["STAGE 2
X CAPACITORS Cx
Differential Mode"] nodeC --> nodeD["STAGE 3
Y CAPACITORS Cy Line to PE
Common Mode to Ground"] nodeD --> nodeE["LOAD OUTPUT
Filtered DC or AC"] nodeB -.->|"CM Noise
Blocked"| nodeF["PE GROUND"] nodeC -.->|"DM Noise
Shunted"| nodeG["INTERNAL NODE
Return Path"] nodeD -.->|"CM Noise
Bypassed"| nodeF style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.2 Decision Tree for Filter Topology Selection The following diagram maps the **selection logic** an EMC engineer follows when choosing between L, Pi, and T filter topologies based on source and load impedance characteristics. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["START Filter Selection"] --> nodeImpCheck{"What is the
Source and Load
Impedance?"} nodeImpCheck -->|"Low Source
Low Load"| nodeL["L SECTION FILTER
Single L and C
20 dB per decade"] nodeImpCheck -->|"Low Source
High Load"| nodePi["PI FILTER C-L-C
Best for low Z source

Low Load"| nodeT["T FILTER L-C-L
Best for high Z source
40 dB per decade"] nodeImpCheck -->|"Unknown Mismatch"| nodeMulti["MULTI STAGE CASCADE
L Pi T Combination
60 to 80 dB"] nodeL --> nodeFreq{"Operating
Frequency?"} nodePi --> nodeFreq nodeT --> nodeFreq nodeMulti --> nodeFreq nodeFreq -->|"Below 1 MHz"| nodeDisc["Discrete L and C
Through Hole Components"] nodeFreq -->|"1 MHz to 100 MHz"| nodeHybrid["Hybrid Discrete and
Ferrite Components"] nodeFreq -->|"Above 100 MHz"| nodeDist["Distributed Elements
Transmission Line Filters"] style nodeStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeL fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodePi fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeT fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeMulti fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeDisc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeHybrid fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeDist fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.3 Frequency Response Behavior of Different Filter Orders ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn["Noise Spectrum Input
9 kHz to 10 GHz"] --> node1p["1 POLE FILTER
RC or RL
20 dB per decade"] nodeIn --> node2p["2 POLE FILTER

40 dB per decade"] nodeIn --> node3p["3 POLE FILTER
Cascaded Stages

Meets CISPR B"] style nodeIn fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node1p fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node2p fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node3p fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeOut1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeOut2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeOut3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define Insertion Loss of an EMC filter. Why is it measured in decibels, and what is the typical insertion loss required to meet CISPR 22 Class B conducted emission limits in the 150 kHz to 30 MHz range?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Insertion Loss (IL) is the ratio, expressed in decibels, of the voltage appearing across the load *before* a filter is inserted into the circuit to the voltage appearing across the load *after* the filter is inserted, measured under matched impedance conditions. **Mathematical Expression (1 Mark):** $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{without}}{V_{with}}\right)\ \text{dB} $$ **Typical Required Value (1 Mark):** For CISPR 22 Class B compliance in the 150 kHz to 30 MHz range, the conducted emission limit is approximately **40 dBµV to 60 dBµV** (quasi-peak). Considering typical switch-mode power supply noise levels of **80 dBµV to 100 dBµV**, the required filter Insertion Loss is typically **40 dB to 60 dB** across this band. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Common-Mode (CM) and Differential-Mode (DM) noise in conducted EMI. What filter components suppress each mode?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Parameter** | **Common-Mode (CM) Noise** | **Differential-Mode (DM) Noise** | |:--------------|:----------------------------|:----------------------------------| | **Definition** | Noise current flowing in the *same direction* on both signal/power lines, returning via ground | Noise current flowing in *opposite directions* on the two lines | | **Physical Source** | Parasitic capacitive coupling, ground loops, ESD events | Switching transients, rectifier harmonics | | **Suppression Component** | CM Choke (coupled inductor), Y-capacitors (line-to-ground) | X-capacitors (line-to-line), series inductors | **Marking Distribution:** 1 Mark for definitions, 1 Mark for sources, 1 Mark for suppression components with examples. --- ### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam - July 2024]** **(a)** Derive the Insertion Loss expression for a single-pole RC low-pass filter terminated in matched impedances. Show that at the cutoff frequency, IL = 3 dB, and at one decade above cutoff, IL ≈ 20 dB. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a Pi-filter (C-L-C topology) to suppress noise from a switch-mode power supply operating at 100 kHz fundamental switching frequency. The desired attenuation at 30 MHz (CISPR Class B measurement frequency) is **50 dB**. Assume source impedance = 1 Ω (low) and load impedance = 50 Ω. **(7 Marks)** **MODEL SOLUTION:** **Part (a) Solution:** Step 1: Circuit setup — source $V_s$ with $R_s = R_L = R$ (matched). Filter: series R, shunt C. Step 2: Transfer function: $$ H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ Step 3: Magnitude: $$ \vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ Step 4: IL formula: $$ IL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f RC)^2}\right) $$ $$ IL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + (2\pi f RC)^2\right)\ \text{dB} $$ Step 5: At $f = f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$: $$ IL = 10 \log_{10}(1+1) = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 3.01\ \text{dB} $$ Step 6: At $f = 10 f_c$: $$ IL = 10 \log_{10}(1 + 100) = 10 \log_{10}(101) \approx 20.04\ \text{dB} $$ **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** Transfer function — 2 Marks, IL formula — 2 Marks, two numerical evaluations — 3 Marks (1.5 each). --- **Part (b) Solution:** Step 1: Identify Pi-filter IL formula for frequencies well above resonance: $$ IL \approx 40 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f}{f_0}\right)\ \text{dB}, \quad f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} $$ Step 2: Required IL at $f = 30$ MHz is 50 dB: $$ 50 = 40 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{f_0}\right) $$ Step 3: Solve for $f_0$: $$ \dfrac{50}{40} = 1.25 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{f_0}\right) $$ $$ 10^{1.25} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{f_0} \Rightarrow 17.78 = \dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{f_0} $$ $$ f_0 = \dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{17.78} \approx 1.687 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.69\ \text{MHz} $$ Step 4: Choose $C_1 = C_2 = C$. For low source impedance application, $C$ must be large. Choose **$C = 100$ nF** (standard X2 safety capacitor). Step 5: Solve for L using resonance condition: $$ f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \Rightarrow L = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi f_0)^2 C} $$ $$ L = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi \times 1.687 \times 10^6)^2 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ L = \dfrac{1}{(1.06 \times 10^7)^2 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{1}{1.123 \times 10^{14} \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ L = \dfrac{1}{1.123 \times 10^{7}} \approx 89\ \mu\text{H} $$ Step 6: Verify IL at 30 MHz: $$ IL = 40 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{30 \times 10^6}{1.687 \times 10^6}\right) = 40 \log_{10}(17.78) = 40 \times 1.25 = 50\ \text{dB} \checkmark $$ **Final Design:** $C_1 = C_2 = 100$ nF (X2 rated), $L = 89\ \mu\text{H}$. **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** Pi-filter IL formula — 1.5 Marks, $f_0$ calculation — 2 Marks, L calculation — 2 Marks, verification — 1.5 Marks. --- #### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Alternative Set]** **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a **Common-Mode Choke with Y-capacitors** for EMI suppression in a power line filter. Derive the expression for common-mode impedance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **feedthrough capacitor filter** has a capacitance of 1 nF and is mounted on a shielded enclosure wall. Calculate its impedance at (i) 1 MHz, (ii) 100 MHz, and (iii) 1 GHz. Comment on its effectiveness for EMI suppression at each frequency. **(7 Marks)** **MODEL SOLUTION:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):** A CM choke consists of a **single toroidal ferrite core** with two identical windings, one on the Line (L) conductor and one on the Neutral (N) conductor. The windings are wound such that: - **For DM current (load current):** The fluxes cancel ($\Phi_L = -\Phi_N$), so the choke presents near-zero inductance. Only leakage inductance $L_{leak}$ contributes. - **For CM noise current:** The fluxes add ($\Phi_L + \Phi_N$), and the choke presents high inductance $L_{CM}$. Y-capacitors (typically 2.2 nF to 4.7 nF, Y1/Y2 safety rated) connect from L-to-PE and N-to-PE, providing a low-impedance path for CM noise to ground. **Derivation (5 Marks):** For an ideal toroidal core: $$ L_{CM} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A_e}{\ell_e} $$ Where: - $\mu_r$ = relative permeability (typically 5000 for MnZn ferrite) - $N$ = turns (typically 5-15) - $A_e$ = effective cross-sectional area in m² - $\ell_e$ = effective magnetic path length in m The CM impedance is: $$ \boxed{Z_{CM}(\omega) = j\omega L_{CM} + R_{core}(\omega)} $$ The $R_{core}$ term arises from ferrite losses and is dominant at high frequencies, providing the **absorption loss** component. --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Capacitive Reactance Formula:** $$ X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} $$ **(i) At f = 1 MHz:** $$ X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 159.15\ \Omega $$ **(ii) At f = 100 MHz:** $$ X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-1}} \approx 1.59\ \Omega $$ **(iii) At f = 1 GHz:** $$ X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{6.283} \approx 0.159\ \Omega $$ **Effectiveness Comments (1 Mark per frequency):** | **Frequency** | **Impedance** | **Effectiveness** | |:--------------|:--------------|:-------------------| | 1 MHz | 159 Ω | **Poor** — not effective as a bypass | | 100 MHz | 1.59 Ω | **Good** — effective bypass to ground | | 1 GHz | 0.16 Ω | **Excellent** — near-short to ground | **Critical Observation (Bonus Insight):** Above ~50 MHz, parasitic lead inductance and self-resonance of the feedthrough capacitor degrade performance. Above ~1 GHz, distributed-element behavior dominates, and the simple lumped model breaks down. **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** 1.5 Marks per frequency calculation, 2.5 Marks for the commentary table. --- ### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Roll-off Rate:** Students often write "20 dB/decade" for Pi-filters. The correct value is **40 dB/decade** (2nd order). Each pole adds 20 dB/decade. > > 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in Pi-filter IL:** The Pi-filter IL at high frequencies is $IL = 20 \log_{10}(2\omega^2 LC)$, NOT $20 \log_{10}(\omega^2 LC)$. Missing the factor of 2 loses 1 mark. > > 3. **Mixing up CM and DM Components:** X-capacitors are for DM, Y-capacitors are for CM. Reversing these is a fatal design error and will cost full marks on design questions. > > 4. **Not Specifying Units:** Always write "**dBµV**" not just "dB" when discussing emission limits, and "**dB**" when discussing Insertion Loss. Examiners are strict about this. > > 5. **Ignoring Source/Load Impedance:** A filter designed for 50 Ω source and 50 Ω load will perform very differently when installed in a real circuit with mismatched impedances. Always state assumed impedance in your answer. > > 6. **Skipping Resonance Calculation:** For Pi and T filters, ALWAYS compute the resonant frequency $f_0$ and ensure it is well below the frequency of interest — otherwise the filter may *amplify* noise near resonance (anti-resonance effect). --- ### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Points to Memorize for KTU Board Exam (Module 3 — Filters):** - **Definition:** EMC filters suppress conducted/radiated noise using reflection (L, C) and absorption (ferrite) mechanisms. - **Insertion Loss Formula:** $IL = 20 \log_{10}(V_{in}/V_{out})$ dB, measured under matched 50 Ω conditions. - **Roll-off Rates:** 1 pole = 20 dB/decade, 2 poles = 40 dB/decade, 3 poles = 60 dB/decade. - **Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for RC filter; $f_c = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ for LC filter. - **Pi-filter:** Best for **low source impedance**, provides 40 dB/decade roll-off. - **T-filter:** Best for **high source impedance**, also 40 dB/decade roll-off. - **L-filter:** Single pole, 20 dB/decade, suitable for mild interference. - **CM Choke:** Suppresses common-mode noise using coupled inductor on single core; $L_{CM} = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A_e / \ell_e$. - **X-capacitors:** Across line-to-neutral for DM suppression (typically 100 nF to 1 µF, X2 rated). - **Y-capacitors:** Line-to-ground for CM suppression (typically 2.2 nF to 4.7 nF, Y1/Y2 rated for safety). - **Feedthrough Filters:** Mounted on shielded walls; effective at VHF/UHF (30 MHz – 1 GHz). - **CISPR 22 Class B Limit:** 40 dBµV quasi-peak at 30 MHz; requires ~50 dB Insertion Loss. - **Filter Order:** Each additional pole/stage adds 20 dB/decade of attenuation. - **Design Trade-off:** More stages = better attenuation but higher cost, larger size, and increased voltage drop. - **Real-World Standard:** Most commercial power line filters specify IL across 150 kHz – 30 MHz with typical values 30–80 dB. --- **End of Module 3 — Filters Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEEVT753)**

topics.title: Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Placement** in the context of PCB design for **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** and **Signal Integrity (SI)** refers to the *strategic spatial positioning of active components, passive components, connectors, and subsystems on a printed circuit board* such that signal return paths remain continuous, radiated emissions are minimized, crosstalk is suppressed, and power delivery network (PDN) impedance targets are met. Placement is the **second stage** in the canonical PCB design flow (Schematic Capture → **Placement** → Routing → Verification) and is recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme as a *primary mitigation layer* — problems resolved at the placement stage never reappear as routing or shielding issues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *“Placement is the geometrical assignment of components on a PCB stack-up to satisfy electrical, thermal, manufacturability, and EMC constraints, prioritizing shortest return paths, minimum loop areas, and controlled-impedance signal flow.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **city's road network**:

- **Components = Buildings** (homes, hospitals, factories)
- **Traces = Roads**
- **Return paths = Service lanes (water, sewage, gas)**
- **Planes = Highways**

If you place a noisy factory (a switching DC-DC converter) right next to a quiet residential colony (an analog audio amplifier), the noise will leak through shared services (return path discontinuities). 

A smart city planner (the EMC engineer) places noisy and quiet zones in **separate districts (partitioning)**, ensures every road has a service lane beneath it (return path), and positions critical facilities (hospitals = decoupling caps) within walking distance of homes. **That is exactly what PCB placement does.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Placement:** *Place first, route later. Every EMC problem that survives placement will cost 10× more to fix at routing, and 100× more to fix post-fabrication.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Velocity of propagation in FR4 | $v_p$ | **~1.5 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 50% of $c$) |
| Critical length (electrically long) | $L_{crit}$ | Function of edge rate, **typically < 1.5 cm for 1 ns edge** |
| Loop area target for EMI | $A_{loop}$ | **< 0.01 in² (≈ 6.45 mm²)** for Class B |
| Decoupling cap lead inductance | $L_{lead}$ | **~1 nH/mm** of lead/trace |
| Free-space impedance | $\eta_0$ | **377 Ω** |
| Characteristic impedance (typical) | $Z_0$ | **50 Ω** (single-ended) / **90–100 Ω** (differential) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Placement, as a discipline, is governed by **five hierarchical objectives** (in order of priority in the KTU 2024 module outcomes):

### Step 1 — Functional Partitioning (System-Level Zoning)
- Divide the PCB into **discrete functional islands**: digital logic, analog front-end, RF, power conversion, I/O connectors.
- Each island must have its **own return reference** (ground plane region) under high-dv/dt or high-di/dt traces.
- Partition lines must **never be crossed by high-speed traces**; crossings are made only on orthogonal layers.

### Step 2 — Critical Component Identification
The following components are **placement-critical** (their position dictates the entire board layout):

1. **Connectors** (board-edge, board-to-board) — fixed by mechanical constraints.
2. **BGA / high-pin-count ICs** — anchor devices; their escape routing dictates fan-out.
3. **Decoupling capacitors** — placed *first adjacent to IC power pins* (see Step 4).
4. **Clock sources / crystals** — placed near IC clock pins with guard-ring.
5. **DC-DC converters** — placed with input/output filter caps in tight loops.
6. **Reset, JTAG, test points** — placed for board-edge accessibility.

### Step 3 — Return-Path Continuity (The "Why" of Placement)
A high-frequency signal does not follow the trace forward; it **returns along the path of least impedance** directly beneath the trace.

$$Z_{return} = R_{return} + j\omega L_{return} \quad \text{(at high freq, } L_{return} \text{ dominates)}$$

If the placement forces the return current to detour around a slot or a void in the reference plane, an **antenna loop** is formed, radiating at $f = c/\lambda$.

> [!NOTE]
> **RULE:** A high-speed signal trace and its return path must form a transmission line. If placement splits the ground plane, you have created a **dipole antenna** instead.

### Step 4 — Decoupling Capacitor Placement Rule
- Decoupling capacitors must be placed **between the IC power pin and the IC ground pin**, with **via-in-pad** or short, wide traces.
- The **placement loop inductance** $L_{placement}$ must satisfy:
$$L_{placement} \le \frac{V_{ripple,allow}}{2\pi f_{switch} \cdot I_{transient}}$$

- **Rule of thumb (KTU high-yield):** Place caps within $\lambda/20$ of the IC power pin, where $\lambda = v_p/f_{clock}$.

### Step 5 — Loop Area Minimization
Every current loop formed by a trace + its return path radiates magnetic field proportional to the **enclosed area**:

$$B_{radiated} \propto \frac{I \cdot A_{loop}}{r^2} \quad \text{(near field)}$$

$$E_{radiated} \propto \frac{I \cdot A_{loop} \cdot f^2}{r} \quad \text{(far field, dominant above 30 MHz)}$$

**Hence:** Placement must minimize $A_{loop}$ by keeping the forward and return paths parallel and physically adjacent.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Critical length | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ | m | Trace longer than $L_{crit}$ behaves as transmission line |
| 2 | Time of flight on FR4 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} \approx 6.6~\text{ps/mm}$ | s/mm | $t_{pd}$ for FR4 ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4.3$) |
| 3 | Loop inductance (rectangular loop) | $L_{loop} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{\pi} \left[ w \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2w}{h}\right) + h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2h}{w}\right) \right]$ | H | For estimating EMI |
| 4 | Partial inductance (straight wire) | $L_{wire} = \dfrac{\mu_0 l}{2\pi} \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2l}{r}\right) - 1 \right]$ | H | Used in via-stub inductance |
| 5 | Via stub inductance | $L_{via} \approx 0.5~\text{nH/mm}$ of barrel | H | Critical for via placement |
| 6 | Plane capacitance | $C_{plane} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Decoupling effectiveness |
| 7 | Impedance of plane pair | $Z_{0,plane} = \dfrac{377}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cdot \dfrac{d}{W}$ | Ω | Return path characteristic impedance |
| 8 | Crosstalk coupling (placement spacing) | $K_{near} = \dfrac{1}{1+(s/h)^2}$ | — | Use $s \geq 3h$ (3W rule) for $K_{near} \le 10\%$ |
| 9 | Radiated field (loop) | $E \approx 263 \times 10^{-16} \cdot I \cdot A \cdot f^2 / r$ | V/m | FCC Class B limit: 40 dBµV/m @ 3 m |
| 10 | Decoupling cap resonance | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Select cap to resonate at $f_{clock}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table pipeline `|` rule:** All absolute value and vertical bars are written with `\vert` (e.g., $\vert Z \vert$, $\vert s/h \vert$) inside the LaTeX environment to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Placement decisions in EMC/SI engineering directly impact:

- **Telecommunications:** 5G base-station PCBs — placement of RF front-end modules vs. baseband DSP requires millimetric precision.
- **Automotive (EV/ADAS):** ECU placement around traction inverters must survive 100 V/m radiated immunity (ISO 11452-2).
- **Medical (IEC 60601-1-2):** Defibrillator-resistant layouts demand partition isolation at the placement stage.
- **Aerospace (DO-160):** Avionics PCBs require placement that meets HIRF (High Intensity Radiated Fields) thresholds.
- **Consumer electronics:** Smartphone mainboards — 12-layer HDI, BGA pitch 0.4 mm, all placement is BGA-escape-driven.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Length $L_{crit}$ of a PCB Trace

**Premise:** A trace behaves as a transmission line only when its electrical length is comparable to the signal's highest frequency content. Below that, lumped analysis is valid. Above it, transmission-line theory (and SI rules) applies.

**Step 1 — Define propagation delay per unit length on FR4:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}}{c}$$

For FR4 with $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.3$, we have $\sqrt{4.3} \approx 2.074$. So:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{2.074}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.91 \times 10^{-9}~\text{s/m} = 6.91~\text{ps/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Define the highest frequency content (the "knee frequency"):**

For a digital edge of rise time $t_r$, the knee frequency is:

$$f_{knee} = \frac{0.5}{t_r}$$

**Step 3 — Define the wavelength at $f_{knee}$ on the PCB:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f_{knee}} = \frac{1}{t_{pd} \cdot f_{knee}}$$

Substituting $f_{knee} = 0.5/t_r$:

$$\lambda = \frac{2 t_r}{t_{pd}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the rule of thumb:** A trace is electrically long when its length exceeds $\lambda/10$ (conservative $\lambda/20$ is also used):

$$L_{crit} = \frac{\lambda}{10} = \frac{t_r}{5 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

A more common textbook form (and the KTU 2024 standard form) uses:

$$L_{crit} = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

This corresponds to $\lambda/4$, which is the *electrical quarter-wave resonance* — the length at which a stub creates a short-circuit on an open line.

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU 2024 typical problem):**

Given: $t_r = 1~\text{ns}$, $t_{pd} = 6.6~\text{ps/mm}$ (FR4).

$$L_{crit} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1000}{13.2} \approx 75.76~\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{L_{crit} \approx 76~\text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** Any trace longer than 76 mm in a system with 1 ns edge must be treated as a transmission line — the placement must ensure controlled impedance and continuous return path along the entire length.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loop Inductance of a Rectangular PCB Loop

**Premise:** Estimate the loop inductance of a power-current loop formed by a trace and its ground return, to evaluate EMI emission potential.

**Step 1 — Geometry:** A rectangular loop of width $w$ (length of trace) and height $h$ (separation between trace and return plane), with $w \gg h$.

**Step 2 — Apply the partial-inductance approximation (Hoer & Love, IEEE 1965):**

$$L_{loop} \approx \frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \left[ w \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{2w}{h}\right) + h \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{2h}{w}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for $w \gg h$:**

When $w/h \ge 10$, the second term is negligible, and we approximate:

$$L_{loop} \approx \frac{\mu_0 \cdot w}{\pi} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{2w}{h}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**

Given: $w = 50~\text{mm}$, $h = 0.2~\text{mm}$ (typical trace above ground plane).

$$L_{loop} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{\pi} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2 \times 50}{0.2}\right)$$

$$= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(500)$$

$$\ln(500) = 6.215$$

$$L_{loop} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.215 = 1.243 \times 10^{-7}~\text{H} \approx 124~\text{nH}$$

**Step 5 — Compute reactance at $f = 100~\text{MHz}$:**

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times 1.243 \times 10^{-7} \approx 78~\Omega$$

**Interpretation:** A 50 mm × 0.2 mm loop presents 78 Ω of inductive impedance at 100 MHz. This is a *major* EMI concern and explains why placement must compress the loop height $h$ — halving $h$ reduces $L_{loop}$ by ~30%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Doubling trace-to-plane distance **h** reduces loop inductance logarithmically, but doubling trace length **w** increases it linearly. **Shorten the trace before you thicken the dielectric.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Decoupling Capacitance for an IC

**Premise:** An IC switching with edge rate $t_r$ demands a transient current $I_{transient}$. The supply rail must not droop more than $\Delta V$. Find the minimum decoupling capacitance.

**Step 1 — Charge required per transition:**

$$Q = I_{transient} \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2 — Voltage droop on capacitor:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{I_{transient} \cdot t_r}{C}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C$:**

$$C = \frac{I_{transient} \cdot t_r}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical KTU 2024 problem):**

Given: $I_{transient} = 1~\text{A}$, $t_r = 2~\text{ns}$, $\Delta V_{allow} = 50~\text{mV}$.

$$C = \frac{1 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{-8}~\text{F} = 40~\text{nF}$$

**Step 5 — Place multiple caps in parallel for broadband suppression:**

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i} C_i = C_{bulk} + C_{mid} + C_{HF}$$

**Step 6 — Verify with resonance:** Each cap resonates with its own ESL:

$$f_{r,i} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{C_i \cdot ESL_i}}$$

The cap with the highest $f_r$ handles the highest frequencies. This is the **"stacked decoupling"** principle (Howard Johnson's *High-Speed Digital Design*):

| Cap Type | Value | ESL Target | Resonance $f_r$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk | 10 µF (tantalum) | ~5 nH | ~700 kHz |
| Mid-frequency | 100 nF (X7R) | ~1 nH | ~16 MHz |
| High-frequency | 10 nF (NP0/C0G) | ~0.3 nH | ~90 MHz |
| On-die | intrinsic | — | > 1 GHz |

$$\boxed{C_{min} = \frac{I_{transient} \cdot t_r}{\Delta V_{allow}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Placement implication:** The 10 nF HF cap must be **closest to the IC power pin** because its ESL is lowest, and any extra trace inductance (mm of trace = nH of inductance) will *push $f_r$ down*, eliminating its high-frequency benefit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Placement Decision Rules

| Component | Placement Rule | EMC/SI Justification | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decoupling cap (HF) | Within 1–3 mm of IC power pin, via-in-pad preferred | Minimize $L_{loop}$, max $f_r$ | Routing cap to power rail first |
| Crystal / oscillator | Adjacent to IC, guard-ring around, no traces underneath | Prevent noise injection, prevent coupling | Sharing ground with digital return |
| DC-DC converter | At board edge, input cap on same side, output cap closest to load | Contain switching noise, minimize $A_{loop}$ | Output cap far from load |
| BGA / high-pin-count IC | Centered on board, escape routing unhindered | Minimize total trace length, balanced fanout | Off-center with skewed breakout |
| I/O connector | Board edge, single row of filtering components in series | Filter common-mode, ESD path | Filter components scattered |
| Analog IC | Inside analog partition with moated ground | Isolate from digital return current | Sharing ground with noisy digital |
| Reset / JTAG | Adjacent to connector, with 10 kΩ pull-up | Testability, EMI immunity | Floating or long traces |
| RF antenna / balun | Far from digital, near board edge, no copper under | Prevent pattern distortion, prevent detuning | Plane overlap with RF keep-out |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Placement Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Schematic Capture - Netlist + Constraints] --> B[Identify Critical Components]
    B --> B1[Connectors - mechanical anchor]
    B --> B2[BGAs - escape anchor]
    B --> B3[Clock sources]
    B --> B4[Decoupling caps]
    B --> B5[DC-DC converters]

    B1 --> C[Functional Partitioning]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> D[Define PCB Regions]
    D --> D1[Analog Island]
    D --> D2[Digital Island]
    D --> D3[Power Island]
    D --> D4[RF Island]
    D --> D5[IO Island]

    D1 --> E[Assign Reference Planes]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E --> E1[Unbroken ground under each island]
    E --> E2[Moats and bridges for crossing regions]

    E1 --> F[Place Anchor Components]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Connectors - fixed XYZ]
    F --> F2[BGA escape fanout verified]
    F --> F3[Clock within lambda/20 of IC]
    F --> F4[Decoupling within 1-3 mm of IC power pin]

    F1 --> G[Place Supporting Components]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Passives near relevant ICs]
    G --> G2[Filter networks in series with IO]
    G --> G3[Test points at board edge]

    G1 --> H[Loop Area and Return Path Audit]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> H1[Check return path continuity]
    H --> H2[Compute loop inductance]
    H --> H3[Validate against A loop target]

    H1 --> I[Thermal Placement Check]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> I1[Hot spots distributed]
    I --> I2[Copper pour for heat spreading]
    I --> I3[Airflow paths unblocked]

    I1 --> J[DRC and Placement Sign-off]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> J1[Component-to-component clearances]
    J --> J2[Component-to-edge clearances]
    J --> J3[Manufacturing DFM rules]

    J1 --> K[Routing Stage Hand-off]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
```

## 4.2 Placement Zoning Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph boardEdge[Board Edge Perimeter]
        J1[Connector J1 - IO]
        J2[Connector J2 - Power]
    end

    subgraph analogIsland[Analog Island]
        A1[Analog Sensor AFE]
        A2[ADC with AGND]
        A3[Analog Decoupling Caps]
    end

    subgraph digitalIsland[Digital Island]
        D1[Microcontroller MCU]
        D2[Memory DDR]
        D3[Logic Buffers]
        D4[Digital Decoupling Caps]
    end

    subgraph powerIsland[Power Island]
        P1[DC-DC Converter]
        P2[Input Bulk Caps]
        P3[Output Filter L-C]
    end

    subgraph rfIsland[RF Island]
        R1[RF Transceiver IC]
        R2[Balun and Matching]
        R3[Crystal with Guard Ring]
    end

    J2 --> P1
    P1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> A2
    A2 --> A1
    D1 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> J1
    R1 --> R3
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    D1 --> D4
    A2 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows denote *power flow and signal direction*, not physical routing. Each island (Analog, Digital, Power, RF) has its own internal ground reference, with **moats** separating them and **bridges** at signal crossings.

## 4.3 Decoupling Capacitor Placement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ic[IC Power Pin Region]
        VCC[VDD Pin]
        GND[VSS Pin]
    end

    subgraph placementLayers[Decoupling Stack - Placement Order]
        L1[Layer 1 - Surface Top - 10 nF HF cap within 1 mm]
        L2[Layer 2 - Inner - 100 nF mid cap within 3 mm]
        L3[Layer 3 - Bottom - 10 uF bulk cap within 5 mm]
    end

    subgraph pdn[Power Delivery Network]
        PLANE[Power and Ground Plane Pair]
        VIACONN[Via-in-Pad or Short Wide Trace]
    end

    VCC --> VIACONN
    VIACONN --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> PLANE
    GND --> PLANE
    L1 --> GND
    L2 --> GND
    L3 --> GND
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagrammatic rule:** The **HF cap is the innermost ring** of the decoupling castle. **Closer = better.** Every extra millimetre of trace between VCC pin and cap adds ~1 nH, halving the cap's effective high-frequency impedance.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define PCB placement in the context of EMC and signal integrity. List any three placement-critical components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Placement is the strategic spatial positioning of components on a printed circuit board to satisfy electrical, thermal, and EMC constraints by ensuring shortest return paths, minimum loop areas, and controlled-impedance signal flow. **(2 marks)**
Three placement-critical components: **(1 mark — any 3)**
1. Connectors (board-edge mechanical anchor)
2. BGA/high-pin-count ICs (escape anchor)
3. Decoupling capacitors (power integrity anchor)
4. Clock sources / crystals (timing reference)
5. DC-DC converters (noise source)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the significance of the "3W rule" in PCB placement? Mention the formula for near-end crosstalk coupling coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **3W rule** states that trace-to-trace spacing $s$ should be at least 3 times the trace-to-plane height $h$ ($s \geq 3h$) to keep near-end crosstalk coupling below 10%. **(2 marks)**

The near-end crosstalk coefficient is:

$$K_{near} = \frac{1}{1 + (s/h)^2}$$

At $s = 3h$: $K_{near} = \frac{1}{1+9} = 0.1 = 10\%$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functional partitioning strategy** used in PCB placement for mixed-signal boards. How do you isolate analog and digital grounds? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **critical length** formula for a PCB trace. For $t_r = 1.5~\text{ns}$ and FR4 substrate, compute the critical length in mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of functional partitioning (2 marks):**
Functional partitioning is the division of the PCB into physically separate zones based on signal behaviour, noise sensitivity, and power requirements. Each partition has its own reference plane, return current path, and decoupling scheme.

**Step 2 — Common partitions in mixed-signal design (2 marks):**

| Partition | Contents | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Analog | Op-amps, ADC front-end, sensors | Analog Ground (AGND) |
| Digital | MCU, FPGA, memory, logic | Digital Ground (DGND) |
| Power | DC-DC, LDO, filter inductors | Power Ground (PGND) |
| RF | Transceivers, baluns, antennas | RF Ground (RFGND) |
| I/O | Connectors, ESD diodes, filters | Chassis/shield ground |

**Step 3 — Ground isolation methods (2 marks):**

Three canonical techniques (Howard Johnson, *High-Speed Digital Design*):

1. **Star ground (single-point tie):** All grounds meet at one node near the power entry. Acceptable below 1 MHz only.
2. **Moated ground:** A slot in the ground plane separates regions; bridges are placed *only* at A/D converter pins to give return currents a single, predictable crossing point.
3. **Split ground with stitched bridges:** Ground is split under sensitive areas, with stitching vias every $\lambda/20$ along the boundary to maintain HF continuity.

**Step 4 — Practical implementation (1 mark):**
Place the **single bridge point** under the ADC, with a **0 Ω resistor or ferrite bead** linking AGND and DGND. This constrains the return crossing to a known, controllable location.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Statement (1 mark):**
A trace is electrically long when its propagation delay equals half the rise time. Beyond $L_{crit}$, transmission-line analysis is mandatory.

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} = \frac{2.074}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.91 \times 10^{-9}~\text{s/m} = 6.91~\text{ps/mm}$$

For FR4 with $\varepsilon_r = 4.3$.

The quarter-wave condition:

$$L_{crit} = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution (1 mark):**

$$L_{crit} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 6.91 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1500}{13.82} \approx 108.5~\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{L_{crit} \approx 108.5~\text{mm}}$$
Any trace longer than ~108 mm in this 1.5 ns system must be treated as a transmission line. Placement must ensure the entire route lies above a continuous return plane.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **placement rules for decoupling capacitors** in a high-speed digital PCB. Derive the expression for minimum decoupling capacitance required for an IC switching 1 A in 2 ns with an allowable rail droop of 100 mV. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **return-path continuity** in PCB placement. What happens if a high-speed signal trace crosses a slot in the ground plane? Illustrate with a sketch and compute the loop inductance for a 40 mm × 0.4 mm loop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Placement rules (3 marks):**
1. HF caps (1–10 nF, NP0) must be **within 1–3 mm of IC power pin** with via-in-pad.
2. Use a **decoupling stack**: bulk (10 µF) → mid (100 nF) → HF (10 nF) → on-die.
3. **Via-in-pad** or short, fat, same-layer traces to minimize ESL.
4. **Multiple small caps in parallel** > one large cap (lower ESL, higher $f_r$).
5. Place caps **first**, before routing, so they are not displaced.

**Step 2 — Derivation of $C_{min}$ (3 marks):**

$$Q = I_{transient} \cdot t_r$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{Q}{C} \implies C_{min} = \frac{I_{transient} \cdot t_r}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation (1 mark):**

$$C_{min} = \frac{1 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}}{100 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^{-8}~\text{F} = 20~\text{nF}$$

$$\boxed{C_{min} = 20~\text{nF}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Return-path concept (2 marks):**
At high frequencies, return current flows *beneath* the trace along the path of least impedance, i.e., the reference plane directly below. A continuous return path forms a transmission line with the forward trace. If the placement creates a discontinuity (slot, moat, no-plane), the return current detours, increasing loop area.

**Step 2 — Effect of slot crossing (2 marks):**
When a high-speed trace crosses a ground-plane slot, the return current must:
1. Detour around the slot.
2. Form a large loop antenna.
3. Radiate at frequencies where the loop is $\lambda/4$ or $\lambda/2$.

This is a major source of **radiated emissions** and **signal-integrity ringing** (common-mode voltage at the load).

**Step 3 — Loop inductance calculation (2 marks):**

For $w = 40~\text{mm}$, $h = 0.4~\text{mm}$, $w/h = 100$ (use $w \gg h$ approximation):

$$L_{loop} \approx \frac{\mu_0 \cdot w}{\pi} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{2w}{h}\right)$$

$$= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{80}{0.4}\right)$$

$$= 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(200)$$

$$\ln(200) = 5.298$$

$$L_{loop} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.298 \approx 8.48 \times 10^{-8}~\text{H}$$

$$\boxed{L_{loop} \approx 85~\text{nH}}$$

**Step 4 — Consequence (1 mark):**
At $f = 100~\text{MHz}$: $X_L = 2\pi \cdot 10^8 \cdot 85 \times 10^{-9} \approx 53.4~\Omega$. This large inductive impedance causes ringing, overshoot, and EMI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Placement Topic:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "loop area" with "loop inductance"** — they are related but not identical. Loop area determines *radiation*; loop inductance determines *signal integrity* (ringing, $di/dt$ noise). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using them interchangeably.
> 2. **Always state the FR4 value $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.3$ explicitly** when computing $t_{pd}$. Skipping this gives a wrong $L_{crit}$ even with the right formula.
> 3. **Decoupling cap placement** is graded on *proximity to IC pin*, not on the cap value. A 100 nF cap placed 10 mm away is *worse* than a 10 nF cap placed 1 mm away.
> 4. **For the 3W rule**, write the formula $s \geq 3h$, **not** $s \geq 3W$ (W is trace width). Many students confuse the two.
> 5. **Return-path continuity**: never say "ground current returns through the shortest path." It returns through the **path of least impedance** — a critical distinction above 1 MHz.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Placement is stage 2** of PCB design; problems fixed here are cheapest to fix.
- [ ] **Anchor components first**: connectors → BGAs → clocks → decoupling caps → DC-DC.
- [ ] **Functional partitioning** = dividing the PCB into electrically homogeneous islands.
- [ ] **Return-path continuity** is the *single most important* placement rule. Slot in ground = antenna.
- [ ] **Loop area target**: $< 0.01~\text{in}^2$ ($6.45~\text{mm}^2$) for FCC Class B radiated emissions.
- [ ] **Critical length formula**: $L_{crit} = t_r / (2 \cdot t_{pd})$ where $t_{pd}(\text{FR4}) \approx 6.6~\text{ps/mm}$.
- [ ] **Decoupling cap placement**: HF cap within 1–3 mm of IC power pin, via-in-pad preferred.
- [ ] **3W rule**: $s \geq 3h$ keeps near-end crosstalk below 10%.
- [ ] **Bulk → Mid → HF → On-die**: the four-tier decoupling stack.
- [ ] **Crystal placement**: adjacent to IC, with guard-ring, no traces or planes under it.
- [ ] **DC-DC placement**: at board edge, input cap same side, output cap closest to load.
- [ ] **Analog/Digital isolation**: moated ground, single bridge at ADC, with 0 Ω or ferrite.
- [ ] **Loop inductance formula** (rectangular, $w \gg h$): $L \approx \frac{\mu_0 w}{\pi} \ln(2w/h)$.
- [ ] **FR4 $t_{pd} \approx 6.6~\text{ps/mm}$** — memorize this constant.
- [ ] **$C_{min}$ for rail droop**: $C = I_{transient} \cdot t_r / \Delta V$.
- [ ] **Edge rate, not clock frequency, defines $L_{crit}$** — common student error.
- [ ] **High pin-count ICs are placement anchors**: their escape routing dictates fan-out.
- [ ] **Thermal placement**: distribute hot spots, ensure airflow paths, use copper pours.
- [ ] **Test points / JTAG**: place near board edge for probe accessibility.
- [ ] **I/O connectors**: filtering components in series, single row, close to connector.
- [ ] **Avoid placing components on both sides of a return-current critical trace**.
- [ ] **Decoupling cap first, route later.** "Cap-then-rail" is a KTU board-favourite pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiated Emissions and Conducted Susceptibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiated Emissions and Conducted Susceptibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Radiated Emissions (RE)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Radiated Emissions (RE)* refer to the **unintentional release of electromagnetic energy** from a device or system into the surrounding free space. These emissions propagate as electromagnetic waves (E and H fields) through the air or vacuum, originating from current loops, voltage drops, I/O cables, PCB traces, apertures, and seams in enclosures. Radiated emissions are measured as **electric field strength (V/m)** or **magnetic field strength (A/m)** at a specified distance (commonly **3 m or 10 m**) over a defined frequency range.

In KTU parlance, this phenomenon is treated as an **EM Disturbance Source** that must be bounded to comply with standards such as **CISPR 11/22, FCC Part 15, MIL-STD-461 RE102, and RTCA DO-160 Section 21**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Shouting Radio":**
> Imagine a classroom where one student (your circuit) whispers something important. If the student accidentally leaves the **microphone on**, even their breathing gets amplified and broadcast to the whole room. Similarly, every high-speed digital edge, switching power supply, or clock oscillator in your PCB behaves like a "shouting microphone" — it broadcasts unintended RF energy. That broadcast is **Radiated Emission**. The louder the shout (steeper the edge, higher the current), the further it travels (more far-field coupling). The job of an EMC engineer is to "mute the microphone" using shielding, filtering, and proper layout.

---

## 1.2 What is Conducted Susceptibility (CS)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Conducted Susceptibility (CS)*, also called *Immunity to Conducted Disturbances*, is the **ability of a device, equipment, or system to function correctly in the presence of unwanted electrical disturbances (noise, surges, spikes, ripple) arriving on its interconnecting conductors** — namely power lines, signal lines, and ground returns. Conducted susceptibility is tested by **injecting RF current or voltage** into cables and verifying that the Equipment Under Test (EUT) does not malfunction.

KTU standards references include **MIL-STD-461 CS101/CS114/CS115/CS116, IEC 61000-4-6, and DO-160 Section 20**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Noisy Water Tap":**
> Picture your smartphone charging from a wall socket. If the mains supply contains a "dirty" signal (e.g., a motor's brush noise riding on the 230 V line), this contamination travels down the charging cable directly into the phone's power management IC. If the phone's input filter is weak, the noise corrupts audio, touch sensing, and RF reception. The phone's vulnerability to this "dirty power" is its **Conducted Susceptibility**. Good EMC design = a robust "strainer" (filter) at the water inlet.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 377 \ \Omega$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Reference measurement distance:** **3 m (CISPR)**, **10 m (FCC)**, **1 m (MIL-STD pre-compliance)**
- **Polarization reference:** Vertical / Horizontal at antenna height **1 m to 4 m scan**
- **Standard emission limits:** Class A (industrial) ≈ **40 dBµV/m**, Class B (residential) ≈ **30 dBµV/m** at 10 m

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Two Topics Studied Together?

> [!NOTE]
> RE and CS are two halves of the **EMC diamond**:
> - **Emission = How much noise you SEND OUT**
> - **Susceptibility = How much noise you CAN ABSORB**
>
> A product is *electromagnetically compatible* only if it both **emits little** and **tolerates much**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Radiated Emissions

Radiated emissions originate from three primary physical mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Differential-Mode (DM) Radiation — The Electric Dipole
- A current $I$ flowing through a wire of length $l$ creates a **voltage drop** across it.
- This acts as an **electric dipole antenna** when $l$ is comparable to $\lambda/20$ or longer.
- **Dominant when:** the signal trace and its return path are *spatially separated* (poor layout).
- E-field strength (far field):
$$
E_{DM} \approx 12.6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \frac{I \cdot l \cdot f}{r} \quad (\text{V/m})
$$
where $I$ in $\mu$A, $l$ in m, $f$ in Hz, $r$ in m.

### 2.1.2 Common-Mode (CM) Radiation — The Accidental Antenna
- Current flowing in the **same direction** on *all* conductors (signal + return) returns via an unintended path (chassis, ground plane, cable shield).
- This is typically the **dominant radiated emission source above 30 MHz**.
- Mechanism: a cable behaves as a **monopole antenna** driven by CM voltage $V_{CM}$.
- E-field strength:
$$
E_{CM} \approx \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot f \cdot I_{CM} \cdot l}{r}
$$

### 2.1.3 Magnetic-Loop Radiation
- Current **loops** formed by signal-return pairs radiate as small loop antennas.
- Dominant in PCBs where return current path is forced *away* from the signal trace.
- H-field dominant in the near field; converts to plane wave in the far field.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Conducted Susceptibility

CS disturbances are classified by **coupling path** and **frequency content**:

| CS Path | Description | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Line CS** | Noise injected through DC/AC supply | SMPS ripple, load transients |
| **Signal Line CS** | Noise on data/control lines | Crosstalk, ground bounce |
| **Ground Shift CS** | Voltage difference between grounds | Bonding defects, long ground wires |
| **Common-Mode CS** | Same-phase noise on multiple lines | RFI picked up by external cables |

The EUT becomes "susceptible" when the **injected noise amplitude exceeds the noise margin** of the receiver/sensor circuit.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) and internal assessments for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Far-field boundary (Fraunhofer) | $r_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | $D$ = largest antenna/structure dimension, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 2 | Wavelength in free space | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 3 | E-field from short dipole | $E_\theta = \eta_0 \cdot \dfrac{k^2 \cdot I_0 \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta}{4\pi r}$ | $\eta_0 = 377 \ \Omega$ |
| 4 | Wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$ | rad/m |
| 5 | DM radiation E-field | $E_{DM} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot l \cdot f}{r}$ | V/m, $I$ in µA |
| 6 | CM radiation E-field | $E_{CM} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{I_{CM} \cdot l \cdot f}{r}$ | V/m, $I_{CM}$ in µA |
| 7 | Loop area radiation H-field | $H \approx \dfrac{I \cdot A \cdot f}{r^2}$ (near), $\dfrac{I \cdot A \cdot f^2}{r \cdot c}$ (far) | A/m |
| 8 | Shielding Effectiveness (SE) | $SE_{dB} = R_{dB} + A_{dB} + B_{dB}$ | $R$ = reflection, $A$ = absorption, $B$ = re-reflection |
| 9 | Absorption loss | $A = 3.34 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{f \cdot \mu_r \cdot \sigma_r}$ | dB, $t$ in inches (English) |
| 10 | Reflection loss (E-field) | $R_E = 168 - 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_r f}{\sigma_r}\right)$ | dB, $f$ in Hz |
| 11 | Transfer Impedance (shield) | $Z_T = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | $\Omega / \text{m}$ — measure of shield quality |
| 12 | Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | m |
| 13 | Bulk Current Injection level | $I_{inj} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{50 \ \Omega}$ | A, test per MIL-STD-461 CS114 |
| 14 | BCI probe insertion loss | $IL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | dB (calibration curve) |
| 15 | Filter insertion loss | $IL = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | dB |
| 16 | Capacitor self-resonance | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz — beyond this, capacitor = inductor |
| 17 | Ferrites (impedance vs freq) | $Z = R + jX$ | $\Omega$ — $X$ peaks at $f_r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** The conversion $\text{dBµV/m} = 20\log_{10}(E) + 120$ (where $E$ is in V/m) is the *single most-tested* numerical trick in EMC problems. Memorize it.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | RE/CS Relevance |
|---|---|
| **5G Mobile Handsets** | Must pass **FCC §15 RE** and **ETSI EN 301 489 CS** for global market access |
| **Automotive (EV) ECUs** | ISO 11452 (RE immunity), ISO 7637 (CS transients) |
| **Medical Devices** | IEC 60601-1-2 EMC collateral standard; CS failures = patient risk |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | MIL-STD-461 RE102 / CS114 mandatory |
| **Industrial IoT Gateways** | IEC 61000-4-6 (CS to RF), 61000-4-3 (RS immunity) |

> [!TIP]
> Engineers use **anechoic chambers** for RE testing (absorber-lined rooms) and **shielded enclosures with feed-through panels** for CS testing. A 3 m Semi-Anechoic Chamber (SAC) typically costs ₹3–5 Crore in India — a key reason for *pre-compliance* testing in smaller labs.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: E-Field from an Ideal Short Dipole (Electric Dipole)

**Statement:** A short wire of length $l \ll \lambda$ carries a uniform sinusoidal current $I_0 e^{j\omega t}$. Derive the far-field electric field strength at distance $r$ in free space.

### Step 1: Vector Potential of a Current Element
For a Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole, the magnetic vector potential is:

$$
A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, l \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

where $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ is the wavenumber, and $r$ is the radial distance from the dipole.

### Step 2: Magnetic Field from the Curl of A
In spherical coordinates, only the $H_\phi$ component is non-zero in the far field:

$$
H_\phi = \frac{1}{\mu_0} (\nabla \times \vec{A})_\phi = \frac{j k \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law to Obtain E-Field
Using $\vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0} \nabla \times \vec{H}$ and the plane-wave relation $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 377 \ \Omega$:

$$
E_\theta = \eta_0 \, H_\phi = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{j k \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $k = 2\pi f / c$ and $\eta_0 = 377 \ \Omega$
$$
E_\theta = 377 \cdot \frac{j (2\pi f / c) \cdot I_0 \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta \cdot e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

Simplifying the numerical factor $\frac{377 \cdot 2\pi}{4\pi c} = \frac{188.5}{c} = \frac{188.5}{3 \times 10^8}$:

$$
E_\theta = j \, \frac{188.5 \, f \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta}{3 \times 10^8 \, r} \cdot e^{-jkr}
$$

### Step 5: Final Magnitude Form
Taking $|E_\theta|$ (ignoring the $j$ phase factor):

$$
\boxed{\,|E_\theta| = 12.6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \frac{I_0 \cdot l \cdot f \cdot \sin\theta}{r} \quad \text{(V/m)}\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic used:** This is the *engineering approximation* (Clarke's derivation) where $I_0$ is in **microamperes**, $l$ in **metres**, $f$ in **Hz**, and $r$ in **metres** — the constants $4\pi$, $\eta_0$, and $c$ collapse into the leading factor $1.26 \times 10^{-6}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Common-Mode Current → Radiated E-Field Conversion

**Statement:** A cable of length $l$ carries a common-mode current $I_{CM}$. Show that the radiated E-field is linearly proportional to $I_{CM}$, $l$, and $f$.

### Step 1: Model the Cable as a Monopole
A cable above a ground plane behaves as a quarter-wave monopole if $l \approx \lambda/4$. For shorter cables, we treat it as a **short dipole** for simplicity.

### Step 2: Apply the Short-Dipole Formula from Derivation 1
For $\theta = 90^\circ$ (broadside to the cable) — the maximum radiation direction — $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$
E_{CM} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{I_{CM} \cdot l \cdot f}{r}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** A 1 m long cable carries a common-mode current of **$I_{CM} = 10 \ \mu\text{A}$** at a frequency of **$f = 100 \text{ MHz}$**. Find the E-field at a distance of **$r = 3 \text{ m}$** (CISPR Class B distance).

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

Given:
- $I_{CM} = 10 \ \mu\text{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$
- $l = 1 \text{ m}$
- $f = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 10^8 \text{ Hz}$
- $r = 3 \text{ m}$

Substituting:

$$
E_{CM} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1) \times (10^8)}{3}
$$

Step 3a — Numerator:

$$
(10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1 \times 10^8 = 10 \times 10^{2} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

Step 3b — Multiply by the leading factor:

$$
1.26 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 1.26 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Step 3c — Divide by $r = 3$:

$$
E_{CM} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/m}
$$

Step 3d — Convert to **dBµV/m** (the standard EMC reporting unit):

$$
E_{dB\mu V/m} = 20 \log_{10}(E_{V/m}) + 120 = 20 \log_{10}(4.2 \times 10^{-4}) + 120
$$

$$
= 20 \times (-3.377) + 120 = -67.53 + 120 = 52.47 \text{ dBµV/m}
$$

Step 3e — Compare with CISPR 22 Class B limit at 100 MHz = **40 dBµV/m** (3 m quasi-peak):

$$
\text{Excess} = 52.47 - 40 = 12.47 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAIL by 12.5 dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **+120 dB conversion factor** when changing V/m to dBµV/m. The correct identity is $1 \ \mu\text{V/m} \equiv 0 \ \text{dBµV/m}$, so $1 \ \text{V/m} = 10^6 \ \mu\text{V/m} = 120 \ \text{dBµV/m}$. Memorize: **+120, not +60**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bulk Current Injection (BCI) Test Level

**Statement:** In a MIL-STD-461 CS114 test, an RF current probe injects a controlled current into a cable. Derive the relationship between injected current and the voltage developed across the probe's 50 Ω termination.

### Step 1: Test Setup
- A **current injection probe** is clamped around the EUT cable.
- The probe behaves as a **1-turn primary, N-turn secondary transformer** with the cable acting as the single secondary turn.
- The probe is driven by an RF source through a 50 Ω coaxial line, terminated in 50 Ω.

### Step 2: Equivalent Circuit
If the injection probe has a transfer impedance $Z_T$ (V/A), then the current induced in the cable is:

$$
I_{cable} = \frac{V_{source}}{Z_T \cdot 50}
$$

Wait — let us use the **direct injection model**: the probe manufacturer supplies a calibration curve: $I_{inj} (\text{dBµA}) = V_{drive} (\text{dBµV}) - IL_{probe} (\text{dB})$.

### Step 3: Standard CS114 Test Levels
For Navy aircraft applications:

$$
I_{inj} = 77 \ \text{dBµA} \quad \text{(at low frequency, plateau region)}
$$

$$
I_{inj} = 77 \ \text{dBµA} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f}{2 \text{ MHz}}\right) \quad \text{above 2 MHz (falling slope)}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Worked Example (CS114 Level Calculation)

**Problem:** Determine the required source voltage to inject 77 dBµA at $f = 10 \text{ kHz}$, given the probe insertion loss is **$-30 \text{ dB}$** at this frequency.

**Solution:**

Step 4a — Target current in µA:

$$
I_{inj}(\mu A) = 10^{77/20} = 10^{3.85} = 7079 \ \mu\text{A} \approx 7.08 \text{ mA}
$$

Step 4b — Required drive voltage in dBµV:

$$
V_{drive}(\text{dBµV}) = I_{inj}(\text{dBµA}) - IL_{probe}(\text{dB}) = 77 - (-30) = 107 \text{ dBµV}
$$

Step 4c — Convert to volts:

$$
V_{drive} = 10^{107/20} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{5.35 - 6} = 10^{-0.65} = 0.224 \text{ V}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A modest 224 mV drive produces 7 mA of injected cable current — illustrating that **BCI is highly efficient at exciting cable resonances**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Radiated Emission vs. Conducted Susceptibility

| Dimension | Radiated Emissions (RE) | Conducted Susceptibility (CS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Outward (EUT → environment) | Inward (environment → EUT) |
| **Coupling Medium** | Free space (E, H fields) | Conductors (current, voltage) |
| **Test Antenna/Probe** | Biconical, Log-periodic, Horn, Bilog | Current injection probe, CDN, LISN |
| **Primary Quantity** | E-field (V/m) or H-field (A/m) | Injected current (A) or voltage (V) |
| **Key Standard Limits** | FCC §15.109, CISPR 22, RE102 | MIL-STD-461 CS101/CS114, IEC 61000-4-6 |
| **Frequency Range** | 30 MHz – 40 GHz (typical) | 30 Hz – 400 MHz (BCI), DC – 80 MHz (CDN) |
| **Dominant Mechanism above 30 MHz** | Common-mode current on cables | Cable resonance excitation |
| **Main Mitigation** | Shielding, CM chokes, PCB layout | Filtering, isolation, common-mode chokes |
| **Test Environment** | Semi-Anechoic Chamber (SAC) or OATS | Shielded room with feedthroughs |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Radiated Emission Generation Chain

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagram mapping the **signal flow** from internal noise source to far-field radiation. This is a *functional architecture* (not a physical picture), optimized for KTU board explanation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[InternalNoiseSource] --> B[SwitchingTransients]
    A --> C[ClockHarmonics]
    A --> D[DataEdgeRates]
    B --> E[DMandCMVoltageGeneration]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[CMCurrentOnCables]
    E --> G[LoopAreaOnPCB]
    F --> H[EandHfieldRadiation]
    G --> H
    H --> I[FarFieldEfieldVperM]
    I --> J[TestReceiverAntenna]
    J --> K[LimitComparisonCISPRorFCC]
    K --> L{PassorFail}
    L --> M[ComplianceReport]
    L --> N[ReDesignRequired]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **InternalNoiseSource** represents any high-dv/dt or high-di/dt element.
- The **CM path (F → H)** is the *primary* radiator above 30 MHz.
- The **DM path (G → H)** dominates only at lower frequencies or in poorly designed loops.
- **J (TestReceiverAntenna)** is the calibrated measurement transducer (biconical/log-periodic/horn).
- The **Pass/Fail decision (L)** drives the compliance workflow.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Conducted Susceptibility Test Setup (CS114 / BCI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RFSig[RF Signal Generator 9 kHz to 400 MHz] --> Amp[Power Amplifier]
    Amp --> DriveMon[Directional Coupler for Forward Power Monitor]
    DriveMon --> InjProbe[Current Injection Probe around EUT Cable]
    InjProbe --> EUTCable[EUT Interconnecting Cable]
    EUTCable --> LoadTerm[50 Ohm Termination at EUT End]
    MonProbe[Current Monitoring Probe] --> EUTCable
    MonProbe --> Recv[Measurement Receiver or Spectrum Analyzer]
    EUTBox[EUT Equipment Under Test] --> EUTCable
    PowerLine[Filtered Power Supply] --> EUTCable
    EUTBox --> StatusCheck{Performance Check}
    StatusCheck --> Pass[Pass No Degradation]
    StatusCheck --> Susceptible[Susceptible - Failure}
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **RF Signal Generator** is swept across frequency while the **Power Amplifier** maintains the required drive level.
- The **Directional Coupler** monitors forward power to enforce the calibrated injection curve.
- The **Current Injection Probe** magnetically couples RF energy into the cable.
- The **Current Monitoring Probe** verifies the actual injected level (closed-loop control).
- The **EUT Performance Check** is the final decision point — degradation = failure.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right CS Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cable Bundle under Test] --> Q1{Interference enters via Power or Signal?}
    Q1 -->|Power Line| CS101[CS101 - Audio Frequency CS on Power Leads]
    Q1 -->|Signal Line| CS114[CS114 - Bulk Current Injection 10 kHz to 200 MHz]
    Q1 -->|Transients| CS115[CS115 - Impulse Excitation Damped Sinusoid]
    Q1 -->|Damped Oscillatory| CS116[CS116 - 10 kHz to 100 MHz Damped Sine Transients]
    CS101 --> Limit[Apply MIL-STD-461 Curve Limit and Compare]
    CS114 --> Limit
    CS115 --> Limit
    CS116 --> Limit
    Limit --> Verdict{EUT Degradation Observed?}
    Verdict -->|No| Pass2[Compliant]
    Verdict -->|Yes| Fail2[Add Filtering or Shielding]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Radiated Emissions. List the two principal coupling mechanisms that dominate radiated emissions above 30 MHz.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Radiated Emissions (RE):** Radiated emissions are the *unintentional electromagnetic energy* radiated into free space by a device, originating from time-varying currents and voltages on its internal circuits, PCB traces, and interconnecting cables. They are quantified as the **electric field strength (V/m)** measured at a standardized distance (typically 3 m or 10 m).
>
> **Two dominant mechanisms above 30 MHz:**
> 1. **Common-Mode (CM) Current Radiation** — current flowing in the *same direction* on all conductors of a cable, returning via an unintended path (chassis/ground). The cable acts as a monopole antenna.
> 2. **Differential-Mode Loop Radiation** — current loops on the PCB or between a signal trace and its return path, acting as a small magnetic loop antenna.
>
> *In practice, CM radiation dominates by **20–40 dB** above DM because cables are physically long compared to PCB traces.*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Conducted Susceptibility? Briefly explain the Bulk Current Injection (BCI) test method.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Conducted Susceptibility (CS)** is the *vulnerability of an equipment to unwanted electrical disturbances* that are conducted into it via its interconnecting cables (power or signal lines). It is the **immunity** counterpart of conducted emissions.
>
> **Bulk Current Injection (BCI) Test:** In the BCI method (defined in **MIL-STD-461 CS114**), a calibrated **RF current injection probe** is clamped around the EUT cable. An RF signal source (typically **10 kHz to 200 MHz**) drives the probe, inducing a controlled level of common-mode current onto the cable. The injected current level is monitored by a second (monitoring) probe, and the EUT is observed for any performance degradation.
>
> *Key advantage:* BCI simulates the real-world scenario where external RF fields induce CM currents on cables (the *current* is the actual physical threat, not the field).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the radiated E-field from a short electric dipole antenna of length $l$ carrying uniform current $I$ at a free-space observation point at distance $r$ and angle $\theta$. State the assumptions used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The dipole length $l \ll \lambda$ (Hertzian/short dipole).
2. Current is *uniform* along the dipole (valid only when $l < \lambda/50$).
3. Observation point is in the *far field*: $r \gg 2l^2/\lambda$.
4. Free space medium: $\sigma = 0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$, $\mu = \mu_0$.

**Step 1 — Vector Potential:**
For a $z$-oriented current element, the retarded vector potential is:

$$
A_z = \frac{\mu_0 \, I_0 \, l \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic Field (Far Field, $\theta$ component):**

$$
H_\phi = \frac{j k \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

**Step 3 — Electric Field via Plane-Wave Relation:**

$$
E_\theta = \eta_0 \, H_\phi = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{j k \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k = 2\pi f / c$ and $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi \ \Omega$:**

$$
E_\theta = 120\pi \cdot \frac{j (2\pi f / c) I_0 l \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}
$$

$$
E_\theta = j \cdot \frac{60 \pi f \, I_0 \, l \, \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}{c \, r}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Magnitude Form (with $I_0$ in µA, $l$ in m, $f$ in Hz, $r$ in m):**

$$
\boxed{\,|E_\theta| = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{I_0 \, l \, f \, \sin\theta}{r} \text{ V/m}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum value (broadside, $\theta = 90^\circ$):**

$$
E_{\max} = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{I_0 \, l \, f}{r}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The radiated E-field is **directly proportional** to current, length, and frequency, and **inversely proportional** to distance. This explains why *higher clock frequencies* and *longer cables* are the primary RE culprits.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A printed circuit board has a 2 cm long trace carrying a 50 MHz clock signal with a common-mode current of 5 µA. Calculate the radiated E-field at (i) $r = 3$ m and (ii) $r = 10$ m, and express both in dBµV/m. Compare with the CISPR 22 Class B limit at 50 MHz (which is 40 dBµV/m at 10 m).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 5 \ \mu\text{A}$
- $l = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$
- $f = 50 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz}$
- Broadside: $\sin\theta = 1$

**Case (i): r = 3 m**

Substituting:

$$
E_3 = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.02 \times 5 \times 10^7}{3}
$$

Step 1 — Numerator:

$$
5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.02 = 10^{-7}
$$
$$
10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^7 = 5
$$

Step 2 — Multiply by $1.26 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
1.26 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 6.3 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Step 3 — Divide by 3:

$$
E_3 = 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V/m}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to dBµV/m:

$$
E_3 (\text{dBµV/m}) = 20 \log_{10}(2.1 \times 10^{-6}) + 120 = 20 \times (-5.678) + 120 = -113.56 + 120 = 6.44 \text{ dBµV/m}
$$

**Case (ii): r = 10 m**

Since $E \propto 1/r$:

$$
E_{10} = E_3 \times \frac{3}{10} = 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3 = 6.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V/m}
$$

In dBµV/m:

$$
E_{10} = 6.44 - 20 \log_{10}(10/3) = 6.44 - 20 \times 0.523 = 6.44 - 10.46 = -4.02 \text{ dBµV/m}
$$

Or directly:

$$
E_{10} = 20 \log_{10}(6.3 \times 10^{-7}) + 120 = 20 \times (-6.20) + 120 = -124 + 120 = -4 \text{ dBµV/m}
$$

**Comparison with CISPR 22 Class B Limit = 40 dBµV/m at 10 m:**

$$
\text{Margin} = -4 - 40 = -44 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Result:** The PCB passes by a *huge* margin of 44 dB. The CM current of 5 µA is too small to fail this particular limit. **Conclusion:** Short PCB traces (< λ/10) are usually *negligible* radiators compared to attached cables.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model — Internal Choice Alternative]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the MIL-STD-461 CS114 Bulk Current Injection test in detail. Include the test setup, frequency range, current levels, and the criterion for pass/fail.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Purpose:** CS114 evaluates the EUT's ability to withstand RF currents induced on its interconnecting cables, simulating the **bulk cable current** that real-world electromagnetic fields induce via cable resonance pickup.

**2. Test Equipment Required:**
- RF signal generator (**10 kHz to 200 MHz** for CS114, or up to 400 MHz for CS114 extended)
- Power amplifier
- Current injection probe (calibrated)
- Current monitoring probe
- 50 Ω load resistors
- Shielded test enclosure

**3. Test Setup:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Gen[RF Generator] --> Amp[Power Amplifier]
    Amp --> DC[Directional Coupler]
    DC --> CIP[Current Injection Probe]
    CIP --> EUTCable[EUT Cable]
    Mon[Monitor Probe] --> EUTCable
    Mon --> SA[Spectrum Analyzer or Power Meter]
    EUTCable --> Term[50 Ohm Termination]
    EUTDev[EUT Device] --> EUTCable
```

**4. Test Procedure:**
- Step 1: Calibrate the injection probe by setting the required current level using a calibration fixture.
- Step 2: Clamp the injection probe on the EUT cable, **5 cm from the EUT connector**.
- Step 3: Sweep the frequency from 10 kHz to 200 MHz in discrete steps.
- Step 4: At each frequency, adjust the drive level so that the *monitored* current equals the specification.
- Step 5: Monitor the EUT for performance degradation.

**5. Standard Current Limits (CS114, Curve 3 for Navy aircraft):**

- **10 kHz to 2 MHz:** $I_{inj} = 77 \text{ dBµA}$ (constant plateau)
- **2 MHz to 30 MHz:** $I_{inj} = 77 \text{ dBµA} - 20 \log_{10}(f/2\text{MHz})$ (rolling off at −20 dB/decade)
- **30 MHz to 200 MHz:** $I_{inj} = 37 \text{ dBµA}$ (lower plateau)

**6. Pass/Fail Criterion:**

> **The EUT shall not exhibit any malfunction, unintended operation, or performance degradation outside the tolerances specified in the individual equipment specification.** The test is performed with the EUT operating in its most sensitive mode, with all functions exercised.

**7. Engineering Notes:**
- BCI test is preferred over radiated susceptibility (RS103) at low frequencies because **generating uniform fields below 30 MHz requires impractically large antennas**.
- Cable resonance peaks are the most common failure points (λ/4, λ/2, etc.).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 1.5 m long unshielded power cable is connected to an EUT. The cable is tested per CS114 at $f = 30 \text{ MHz}$, where the required injection level is 37 dBµA. The injection probe has an insertion loss of −25 dB at this frequency. Calculate (i) the actual injected current in mA, and (ii) the required source voltage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Required level: $I_{req} = 37 \text{ dBµA}$
- Probe insertion loss: $IL = -25 \text{ dB}$
- Frequency: 30 MHz

**Part (i): Actual Injected Current**

Converting dBµA to µA:

$$
I_{req}(\mu A) = 10^{37/20} = 10^{1.85} = 70.79 \ \mu\text{A}
$$

Converting to mA:

$$
I_{req} = 0.0708 \text{ mA}
$$
