$$\begin{aligned}
L_e &= L_s + L_m, \quad L_o = L_s - L_m \\
C_e &= C_s - C_m, \quad C_o = C_s + C_m
\end{aligned}$$

These yield the mode velocities and impedances previously stated in §2.4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Conductor Geometry — Cross-Section View

Below is a top-view / cross-section schematic using Mermaid showing the geometry and field couplings:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conductor 1 Aggressor] -->|E field Cm| B[Conductor 2 Victim]
    A -->|H field Lm| B
    B -->|E field Cself to ref| G[Conductor 3 Reference GND]
    A -->|E field Cself to ref| G
    B -.->|Far end FEXT| F[Far End ZL]
    A -.->|Near end NEXT| N[Near End Zt]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Crosstalk Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AGGRESSOR_LINE[Aggressor Line]
        VS[Source Vs Zs] --> TL1[Transmission Line Z0 length L]
        TL1 --> ZA[Load ZL]
    end

    subgraph COUPLING_REGION[Coupling Region Lm and Cm]
        Lm[Mutual Inductance Lm] 
        Cm[Mutual Capacitance Cm]
    end

    subgraph VICTIM_LINE[Victim Line]
        TL2[Quiet Transmission Line Z0] 
        VN[Near End Termination Zt]
        VF[Far End Termination ZL]
    end

    VS -.inductive coupling.-> Lm
    VS -.capacitive coupling.-> Cm
    Lm --> VN
    Cm --> VN
    Lm --> VF
    Cm --> VF
```

### 4.3 Mode Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Coupled Lines] --> B[Modal Transform TV and TI]
    B --> C1[Even Mode Common]
    B --> C2[Odd Mode Differential]
    C1 --> D1[Characteristic Impedance Z0e velocity ve]
    C2 --> D2[Characteristic Impedance Z0o velocity vo]
    D1 --> E[Recombine at Output]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Physical Coupled Outputs]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow: NEXT and FEXT Measurement Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source Aggressor] --> A1[Line 1 z=0]
    A1 --> A2[Line 1 z=L]
    A2 --> LT1[Load Aggressor]
    V0[Victim near end z=0] --> V1[Line 2]
    V1 --> V2[Victim far end z=L]
    V0 -. NEXT measurement .-> M1[Meter NEXT dB]
    V2 -. FEXT measurement .-> M2[Meter FEXT dB]
    V1 -. coupled length L .-> A1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
*Define crosstalk in multi-conductor transmission lines. Distinguish between NEXT and FEXT.*

**Model Answer:**

Crosstalk is the undesired electromagnetic coupling of signal energy from an aggressor (active) line to a victim (quiet) line through mutual electric ($C_m$) and magnetic ($L_m$) fields.

- **NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk):** The unwanted signal measured at the **near end** ($z=0$) of the victim line — same end as the aggressor source. Dominated by **inductive** coupling; grows linearly with frequency.
- **FEXT (Far-End Crosstalk):** The unwanted signal measured at the **far end** ($z=\mathcal{L}$) of the victim line. Arises from the **difference** between capacitive and inductive coupling; in homogeneous media, FEXT $\approx 0$.

Both are expressed in **dB** as $X_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\vert V_{\text{victim}}/V_{\text{aggr}}\vert$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
*Write the matrix form of telegrapher's equations for a lossless three-conductor transmission line and identify each matrix.*

**Model Answer:**

For a lossless three-conductor line (two signal + reference), the coupled telegrapher equations in the phasor domain are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dz}\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} &= -j\omega \begin{bmatrix} L_{11} & L_{12} \\ L_{12} & L_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \\
\frac{d}{dz}\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} &= -j\omega \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & -C_{12} \\ -C_{12} & C_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

- $[\mathbf{L}]$: per-unit-length inductance matrix (H/m), symmetric, positive definite.
- $[\mathbf{C}]$: per-unit-length capacitance matrix (F/m), symmetric.
- $L_{ii}$ = self-inductance, $L_{12}$ = mutual inductance.
- $C_{ii}$ = self-capacitance, $C_{12}$ = mutual capacitance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the matrix form of telegrapher's equations for a three-conductor lossless transmission line. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Faraday's and Ampere's Laws in integral form for a TEM-mode line:

$$\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l} &= -j\omega \int \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{S} \\
\oint \vec{H}\cdot d\vec{l} &= j\omega \int \vec{D}\cdot d\vec{S}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1:** Apply Faraday's Law to the loop formed by conductor 1 and the reference:

$$-\frac{dV_1}{dz} = j\omega(L_{11}I_1 + L_{12}I_2)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Law to the loop formed by conductor 2 and the reference:

$$-\frac{dV_2}{dz} = j\omega(L_{12}I_1 + L_{22}I_2)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ampere's Law (charge conservation) for each conductor:

$$-\frac{dI_1}{dz} = j\omega(C_{11}V_1 - C_{12}V_2)$$
$$-\frac{dI_2}{dz} = j\omega(-C_{12}V_1 + C_{22}V_2)$$

**Step 4:** Write in matrix form:

$$\boxed{\ \frac{d}{dz}\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = -j\omega[\mathbf{L}]\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \frac{d}{dz}\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = -j\omega[\mathbf{C}]\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}\ }$$

**(b)** For a symmetric lossless three-conductor line with $L_{11}=L_{22}=500\ \text{nH/m}$, $L_{12}=50\ \text{nH/m}$, $C_{11}=C_{22}=100\ \text{pF/m}$, and $C_{12}=10\ \text{pF/m}$, compute (i) the even and odd mode characteristic impedances, and (ii) the modal phase velocities in free space. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mode Impedances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_e &= L_{11} + L_{12} = 500 + 50 = 550\ \text{nH/m} \\
L_o &= L_{11} - L_{12} = 500 - 50 = 450\ \text{nH/m} \\
C_e &= C_{11} - C_{12} = 100 - 10 = 90\ \text{pF/m} \\
C_o &= C_{11} + C_{12} = 100 + 10 = 110\ \text{pF/m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{0e} &= \sqrt{\frac{L_e}{C_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{550\times 10^{-9}}{90\times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{6111.1} \approx 78.17\ \Omega \\
Z_{0o} &= \sqrt{\frac{L_o}{C_o}} = \sqrt{\frac{450\times 10^{-9}}{110\times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{4090.9} \approx 63.96\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Modal Phase Velocities (free space, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):**

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} = 3\times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_e &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_e C_e}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{550\times 10^{-9}\cdot 90\times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.95\times 10^{-17}}} \approx 1.42\times 10^8\ \text{m/s} \\
v_o &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_o C_o}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{450\times 10^{-9}\cdot 110\times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.95\times 10^{-17}}} \approx 1.42\times 10^8\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

In this symmetric case, $v_e = v_o$ (homogeneous medium). For microstrip, they differ.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the modal decomposition technique for three-conductor transmission lines. Derive the even-mode and odd-mode characteristic impedances. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Modal decomposition decouples the coupled telegrapher equations by transforming them into two independent single-line problems using eigenvector matrices $[\mathbf{T}_V]$ and $[\mathbf{T}_I]$.

**Step 1:** Define the modal voltages and currents:

$$[\mathbf{V}_m] = [\mathbf{T}_V]^{-1}[\mathbf{V}], \quad [\mathbf{I}_m] = [\mathbf{T}_I]^{-1}[\mathbf{I}]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the telegrapher equations to obtain diagonal matrices:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\tilde{\mathbf{L}}] &= [\mathbf{T}_V]^{-1}[\mathbf{L}][\mathbf{T}_I] = \text{diag}(L_e, L_o) \\
[\tilde{\mathbf{C}}] &= [\mathbf{T}_I]^{-1}[\mathbf{C}][\mathbf{T}_V] = \text{diag}(C_e, C_o)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** The two diagonal modes correspond to:

- **Even Mode (Common):** $I_1 = I_2$, currents flow in same direction, return through reference.
- **Odd Mode (Differential):** $I_1 = -I_2$, currents flow in opposite directions, return through each other.

**Step 4:** Even and odd mode characteristic impedances:

$$\boxed{\ Z_{0e} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11}+L_{12}}{C_{11}-C_{12}}}, \quad Z_{0o} = \sqrt{\frac{L_{11}-L_{12}}{C_{11}+C_{12}}}\ }$$

**(b)** Two parallel microstrip lines on a PCB have coupled length $\mathcal{L} = 0.1\ \text{m}$, mutual inductance $L_m = 10\ \text{nH/m}$, source impedance $Z_s = 50\ \Omega$, and characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$. At $f = 1\ \text{GHz}$, compute the NEXT in dB. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the weak-coupling NEXT formula:

$$\text{NEXT} = \frac{V_{\text{near}}}{V_{\text{aggr}}} = j\omega L_m \mathcal{L}\ \frac{Z_0}{2 Z_s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f = 2\pi\times 10^9 \approx 6.283\times 10^9\ \text{rad/s} \\
L_m &= 10\times 10^{-9}\ \text{H/m} \\
\mathcal{L} &= 0.1\ \text{m} \\
Z_0 &= Z_s = 50\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\text{NEXT} = j(6.283\times 10^9)(10\times 10^{-9})(0.1)\left(\frac{50}{2\times 50}\right) = j(6.283)(0.1)(0.5) = j\,0.314$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude and conversion to dB:

$$\vert\text{NEXT}\vert = 0.314, \quad \text{NEXT}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(0.314) \approx -10.05\ \text{dB}$$

**Result:** The near-end crosstalk is approximately **$-10\ \text{dB}$**, indicating that about 31% of the aggressor voltage appears at the near end of the victim line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Students frequently drop the factor of 2 when computing NEXT. Always include the term $Z_0/(2Z_s)$.
> 2. **Units Mismatch:** Convert $L_m$ from nH/m to H/m before substitution; convert $C_m$ from pF/m to F/m.
> 3. **Forgetting $\omega = 2\pi f$:** Many use $f$ directly instead of $\omega = 2\pi f$ — this gives an error of $2\pi$ (~8 dB).
> 4. **Wrong Matrix Sign Convention:** The off-diagonal of $[\mathbf{C}]$ is $-C_{12}$ (negative), not $+C_{12}$.
> 5. **Forgetting Phase Velocity:** In inhomogeneous media (microstrip), $v_e \neq v_o$; FEXT will not be zero.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three-conductor TL** = two signal lines + reference (ground). Modeled using **$2\times 2$ p.u.l. matrices** $[\mathbf{L}]$, $[\mathbf{C}]$.
- **Crosstalk** = unwanted coupling through $L_m$ and $C_m$; quantified via NEXT and FEXT.
- **NEXT** appears at $z=0$, grows as $\omega\mathcal{L}$; dominant mechanism is **mutual inductance**.
- **FEXT** appears at $z=\mathcal{L}$, proportional to $\omega\mathcal{L}(C_m Z_0 - L_m/2Z_0)$; cancels in **homogeneous media**.
- **Mode decomposition** decouples the line into **even (common)** and **odd (differential)** modes with their own $Z_{0e}$, $Z_{0o}$, $v_e$, $v_o$.
- **Symmetric case:** $L_{11}=L_{22}$, $C_{11}=C_{22}$, the transformation matrix becomes $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{bmatrix}$.
- **Mitigation techniques:** increase line spacing, shorten parallel runs, use differential signaling, add ground traces between aggressor and victim, use stripline instead of microstrip, use guard traces.
- **Crosstalk in dB** = $20\log_{10}\vert V_{\text{victim}}/V_{\text{aggr}}\vert$.
- **Always work in SI units** — convert nH, pF before any numerical substitution.
- **Reference velocity** in free space: $c = 3\times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$.
- **KTU 2024 expects:** matrix-form derivation, modal analysis, and NEXT/FEXT numerical problems in **ESE (End Semester Examination)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shielded Wires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Shielded Wires ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **shielded wire** (or **shielded cable**) is a transmission line or interconnect whose inner conductor is enclosed by a conductive barrier — typically a metallic braid, foil, or solid jacket — whose purpose is to confine the electromagnetic field generated by the signal current inside the shield (preventing radiated emissions) and to protect the inner conductor from external electromagnetic interference (EMI). In the context of EMC and Signal Integrity (SI), the shield is characterized by its **transfer impedance $Z_t$** (for low-frequency magnetic coupling) and its **shielding effectiveness (SE)** expressed in **decibels (dB)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A shielded wire is an electrical conductor surrounded by a grounded conductive layer that reduces the penetration of external fields into the inner conductor and restricts the escape of internal fields, quantified through its transfer impedance and shielding effectiveness.* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipe Inside a Steel Pipe" Imagine you are carrying water through a **rubber hose**. If a child nearby splashes water (interference), some of it leaks onto your hose. Now, place your rubber hose inside a **closed steel pipe** and ground the steel pipe to a drain. Any water splashed at the steel pipe flows harmlessly into the drain, while the water inside the rubber pipe reaches its destination untouched. - **Inner conductor** = Rubber hose (carries your sensitive signal current) - **Shield** = Steel pipe (intercepts external interference) - **Ground connection** = Drain (carries the interference away safely) - **Dielectric insulation** = Spacing/air gap between hose and pipe If the steel pipe has **holes** (poorly woven braid, gaps, pigtails), interference leaks through — just like water seeping through gaps. A **solid, continuously bonded shield** acts like a seamless pipe. --- ### 1.3 Why Shielded Wires? — Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Free-space impedance** | $\eta_0$ | $\Omega$ | $\approx 377\ \Omega$ | | **Permeability of free space** | $\mu_0$ | H/m | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | | **Speed of light in vacuum** | $c$ | m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ | | **Transfer impedance (low freq.)** | $Z_t$ | $\Omega$/m | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{1}$ | | **Shielding effectiveness** | SE | dB | **40 to 100 dB** (good design) | > [!NOTE] > **Industry rule of thumb:** A well-designed shield should provide at least **40 dB** of attenuation (99% effectiveness) for commercial products and **60 dB or more** for military/aerospace applications. --- ### 1.4 Major Types of Shielded Wires 1. **Coaxial cable** — solid or braided outer conductor, characteristic for RF. 2. **Triaxial cable** — two concentric shields for double isolation. 3. **Braided shield** — woven mesh; flexible; moderate DC resistance. 4. **Foil shield** — aluminum/Mylar laminate; 100% coverage; brittle. 5. **Combination (serve + braid)** — spiral-wrapped foil + outer braid for the best of both. 6. **Twin-axial and shielded twisted pair (STP/ScTP)** — common in Ethernet (e.g., Cat 7/8). 7. **Pigtail-shielded cable** — *intentionally poor* termination for educational comparison. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — How Does a Shield "Block" Fields? A shield suppresses coupling through **three independent physical mechanisms**. Understanding each is the foundation of KTU exam answers on this topic. #### Mechanism A — Reflection Loss ($R$, in dB) - Caused by the **impedance mismatch** between the incoming wave (typically **377 $\Omega$** in free space) and the shield surface impedance. - More pronounced for **electric fields** and at **high frequencies**. - Independent of shield thickness. #### Mechanism B — Absorption Loss ($A$, in dB) - Caused by **Ohmic heating** of the interfering field as it penetrates the shield material. - Depends on **thickness $t$**, **permeability $\mu$**, **conductivity $\sigma$**, and **frequency $f$**. - This is the dominant mechanism for **magnetic fields at low frequencies**. #### Mechanism C — Re-Reflection (or Bounce) Loss ($B$, in dB) - Internal reflections between the two surfaces of the shield add further attenuation. - Significant only when absorption is small (very thin shields). --- ### 2.2 Coupling Mechanisms Through a Shield Even with a perfect shield, coupling can occur through two fundamentally different paths: | Path | Driving Quantity | Frequency Domain | Quantified By | |---|---|---|---| | **Diffusion coupling** | Magnetic field $\to$ induced current | Low frequency (DC to $\approx$ MHz) | **Transfer impedance $Z_t$** | | **Aperture/porosity coupling** | Holes, gaps, pigtails | All frequencies | **Shielding effectiveness SE** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Topic:** At **low frequencies**, the primary coupling mechanism is *diffusion* through the *transfer impedance*, NOT through holes. This is why transfer impedance analysis is mandatory in the KTU 2024 syllabus. --- ### 2.3 Transfer Impedance — The Master Parameter **Transfer impedance** $Z_t$ is defined as the **voltage induced per unit length on the inner conductor** divided by the **current flowing on the inside of the shield**. $$ Z_t = \frac{V_{\text{induced}}}{I_{\text{shield}} \cdot \ell} \quad \left[\frac{\Omega}{\text{m}}\right] $$ It is the most rigorous, frequency-dependent figure-of-merit for shielded cables. **Lower $Z_t$ means a better shield.** --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > All symbols use $\vert\ \vert$ style absolute value; vertical pipes are intentionally replaced with `\vert` to protect table formatting. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $SE = R + A + B$ | Total shielding effectiveness | dB | | 2 | $SE_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\vert E_i/E_t \vert\right)$ | SE via electric field ratio | dB | | 3 | $SE_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\vert H_i/H_t \vert\right)$ | SE via magnetic field ratio | dB | | 4 | $R \approx 168 + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\sigma_r / \mu_r f^2 r_1^2\right)$ | Reflection loss for a thin shield | dB | | 5 | $A \approx 8.7\,t/\delta = 8.7\,t\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Absorption loss | dB | | 6 | $B \approx 20\log_{10}\!\left(\vert 1 - e^{-2t/\delta} e^{-j2t/\delta}\vert\right)$ | Re-reflection loss (thin shields) | dB | | 7 | $Z_t(\text{solid}) = \dfrac{Z_s}{\sinh(\gamma t)} \approx R_{dc} + j\omega L$ | Transfer impedance (solid tube) | $\Omega$/m | | 8 | $Z_t(\text{braid}) = Z_d + j\omega L_t$ (Vance) | Braid transfer impedance (Vance model) | $\Omega$/m | | 9 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | Skin depth | m | | 10 | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377$ | Free-space wave impedance | $\Omega$ | | 11 | $Z_s = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\,\delta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu}{2\sigma}}$ | Surface impedance of shield | $\Omega$/sq | | 12 | $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = (1+j)/\delta$ | Propagation constant inside shield | 1/m | **Where:** - $\delta$ = **skin depth** - $\sigma$ = conductivity (S/m) - $\mu$ = permeability (H/m) - $t$ = shield thickness (m) - $r_1$ = distance from source to shield (m) - $R_{dc}$ = DC resistance per unit length of shield (Ω/m) - $L_t$ = transfer inductance (H/m) --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Why Shielding Matters | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace (DO-160)** | Avionics signal harnesses | Lightning, HIRF protection | | **Medical (IEC 60601)** | Patient monitoring cables | EMI can cause false readings | | **Automotive (ISO 11452)** | CAN, Ethernet, sensor cables | Near high-current motors, ignition | | **Telecom (IEC 62153)** | Cat 6A / Cat 8 data cables | Alien crosstalk, return loss | | **Military (MIL-STD-461)** | Shipboard cabling | High-level radiated susceptibility | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The trend toward higher data rates (USB 3.2, HDMI 2.1, 112 Gbps PAM4) makes transfer impedance the *primary design parameter*, replacing the older "percentage braid coverage" metric. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Absorption Loss $A$ for a Solid Shield A plane wave of intensity $E_0$ impinges on a metallic sheet of thickness $t$, conductivity $\sigma$, permeability $\mu$. As the wave propagates through the conductor, it is attenuated exponentially according to the skin-depth law. **Step 1 — Field amplitude inside the conductor.** The wave decays as $e^{-\alpha x}$ where $\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ (real part of $\gamma$). **Step 2 — Power ratio.** Since power is proportional to the square of the field, $$ \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = e^{-2\alpha t} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to decibels (absorption loss only).** $$ A_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(e^{2\alpha t}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Simplify the logarithm.** $$ A_{\text{dB}} = 20 \alpha t \log_{10}(e) = 20 \alpha t \cdot (0.4343) $$ $$ A_{\text{dB}} = 8.686\,\alpha t $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = 1/\delta$.** $$ \boxed{A_{\text{dB}} = 8.686\,\frac{t}{\delta} = 8.686\,t\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} $$ This is the absorption loss in decibels. **Doubled thickness** = **+8.7 dB** at the same frequency. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Transfer Impedance for a Solid Tubular Shield Consider a long, thin-walled metallic tube of inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, thickness $t = b - a$, conductivity $\sigma$, permeability $\mu$. A current $I_s$ flows on the **inside** surface; we want the open-circuit voltage $V_i$ induced on a center conductor. **Step 1 — Write the diffusion equation in cylindrical coordinates.** Inside the shield (no source current), $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dH}{dr}\right) = j\omega\mu\sigma\,H $$ **Step 2 — Solve using Bessel functions.** The general solution is $$ H(r) = C_1 I_0(\gamma r) + C_2 K_0(\gamma r) $$ with $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}$. **Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions.** At the inside surface ($r=a$), $H(a) = I_s / (2\pi a)$. At the outside surface ($r=b$), $H(b) = 0$ (open-circuit outside). **Step 4 — Solve for the constants.** Solving the 2×2 linear system, $$ C_1 = -\frac{I_s}{2\pi a}\,\frac{K_0(\gamma b)}{I_0(\gamma a)K_0(\gamma b) - I_0(\gamma b)K_0(\gamma a)} $$ **Step 5 — Find the axial electric field.** From Faraday's law, $E_z = (1/\sigma)(dH/dr)$. **Step 6 — Define transfer impedance.** $$ Z_t = \frac{V_i}{I_s\,\ell} = \frac{1}{2\pi a \sigma t} \cdot \frac{\gamma t}{\sinh(\gamma t)} $$ (after simplifying for $t \ll a$). **Step 7 — Two asymptotic limits.** $$ Z_t \;\approx\; \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2\pi a \sigma t} = R_{dc}, & \omega \to 0 \quad (\text{thin shield, low freq.}) \\[2mm] \dfrac{1}{2\pi a}\sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = \dfrac{Z_s}{2\pi a}, & \omega \to \infty \quad (\text{high freq., skin-limited}) \end{cases} $$ This is the **classical Schelkunoff result**. --- ### 3.3 Vance's Braid Transfer Impedance Model For a **braided shield**, E.F. Vance (1978) introduced a modified expression that adds the contribution of *porosity* (holes) to the diffusion term. **Step 1 — Diffusion term $Z_d$** (same as solid tube): $$ Z_d = \frac{Z_s}{\sinh(\gamma t)} \approx R_{dc} \quad \text{(at low freq.)} $$ **Step 2 — Porosity term $Z_p$** (coupling through diamond-shaped holes): $$ Z_p \approx j\omega L_p $$ where the porosity inductance is $$ L_p = \frac{\mu_0\, C}{2\pi}\!\left(\frac{b}{a} - \frac{a}{b}\right)\!\cos^2\!\theta \quad [\text{H/m}] $$ with $C$ = optical coverage fraction of the braid and $\theta$ = weave angle. **Step 3 — Total Vance transfer impedance.** $$ \boxed{Z_t = Z_d + j\omega L_p \;\;+\;\; \text{(hole capacitance term, usually negligible)}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Insight:** A braid's $Z_t$ is *not* monotonic with frequency. It **decreases** with frequency up to a minimum (around 1–10 MHz), then **rises** again because the inductive porosity term dominates. This is why a braid is *worse* than a solid tube at HF despite having lower DC resistance. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style) > **Problem:** A copper shield has thickness $t = 0.1$ mm, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$. Find the absorption loss at $f = 100$ MHz. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute skin depth at 100 MHz.** $$ \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2\pi(10^8)(4\pi\times 10^{-7})(5.8\times 10^7)}} $$ $$ \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.58\times 10^{10}}} = \sqrt{4.37\times 10^{-11}} \approx 6.6\ \mu\text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute ratio $t/\delta$.** $$ t/\delta = \frac{100\ \mu\text{m}}{6.6\ \mu\text{m}} \approx 15.15 $$ **Step 3 — Apply absorption loss formula.** $$ A = 8.686 \times 15.15 \approx 131.6\ \text{dB} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A copper foil only 0.1 mm thick provides ~130 dB of absorption at 100 MHz — easily sufficient for any commercial product. The challenge in real systems is **NOT absorption but the leakage through connectors, pigtails, and apertures.** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Anatomy of a Shielded Coaxial Cable (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["INNER CONDUCTOR - Carries signal current"] B["INNER DIELECTRIC - Insulator foam or PE"] C["INNER SHIELD - Foil or first braid layer"] D["OUTER DIELECTRIC - Optional spacer"] E["OUTER SHIELD - Braid or second braid layer"] F["OUTER JACKET - PVC or LSZH polymer"] G["360 DEGREE SHIELD TERMINATION - Connector backshell"] H["CHASSIS GROUND - Reference plane"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F G --- C G --- E G --> H ``` **Reading aid:** - The *inner conductor* carries the wanted signal. - The *braid and foil* form a Faraday cage around it. - The **360° termination** at the connector is critical — a pigtail (single wire bond) degrades SE by 20–40 dB at high frequencies. --- ### 4.2 Functional Flow of Shielded-Wire Coupling Paths ```mermaid flowchart LR S["EXTERNAL INTERFERENCE SOURCE"] S --> P1["PATH 1: DIFFUSION
through shield material"] S --> P2["PATH 2: APERTURE
through holes and gaps"] S --> P3["PATH 3: PIGTAIL
inductive loop at termination"] S --> P4["PATH 4: CONNECTOR LEAKAGE
discontinuity at backshell"] P1 --> R["RECEIVED NOISE
on inner conductor"] P2 --> R P3 --> R P4 --> R R --> M["MEASURED AS
Transfer Impedance Zt
or Shielding Effectiveness SE"] ``` **Interpretation:** All four coupling paths contribute to the *measured* $Z_t$ of the cable assembly. A cable with a perfect tube but a pigtail termination behaves *worse* than a poorly-woven braid with a proper 360° termination. --- ### 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing Shield Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["START - What frequency range?"] Q1 --> D{"FREQ BAND"} D -- DC to 1 MHz --> B1["USE SOLID TUBE
or serve-wrapped foil"] D -- 1 to 1000 MHz --> B2["USE DOUBLE SHIELD
foil inner + braid outer"] D -- Greater than 1 GHz --> B3["USE SEMI-RIGID
or corrugated tube"] B1 --> S1["CRITICAL: bond 360 deg"] B2 --> S2["CRITICAL: use connector with
conductive gasket"] B3 --> S3["CRITICAL: control surface finish
and contact pressure"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Effect of Pigtail Length on Shield Performance (Conceptual Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["NO PIGTAIL - 360 DEGREE BOND"] A1["Zt is purely resistive
SE above 80 dB"] end subgraph B["SHORT PIGTAIL - 5 mm"] B1["Inductance approx 5 nH
SE drops 10 to 15 dB at 100 MHz"] end subgraph C["LONG PIGTAIL - 50 mm"] C1["Inductance approx 50 nH
SE drops 30 to 40 dB at 100 MHz"] end A --> B --> C ``` **Quantitative reading:** For a pigtail of length $\ell$ and radius $r$, the series inductance is roughly $$ L_{\text{pigtail}} \approx \frac{\mu_0 \ell}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{\ell}{r}\right) $$ The induced voltage from a shield current of changing $dI/dt$ is $V = L\,(dI/dt)$, which can easily reach hundreds of millivolts — enough to disrupt low-level analog or high-speed digital signals. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define transfer impedance of a shielded cable. Why is it considered a better figure of merit than percentage braid coverage?* **Model Answer:** Transfer impedance $Z_t$ is defined as the ratio of the open-circuit voltage induced per unit length on the inner (victim) conductor to the current flowing on the inside surface of the shield, $$ Z_t = \frac{V_{\text{inner}}}{I_{\text{shield}}\cdot \ell} \;\; [\Omega/\text{m}] $$ It is a superior figure of merit because: 1. It is a **frequency-dependent, physically measurable** quantity with units of Ω/m. 2. It captures **both diffusion and porosity** coupling in one number. 3. It correlates directly with **induced noise voltage**, which is what the designer cares about. 4. Percentage braid coverage is only a geometric proxy and gives no information about diffusion or frequency response. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks justification)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State any three coupling mechanisms through a cable shield and indicate which one dominates at low frequencies.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Diffusion coupling** through the bulk shield material — dominates at low frequencies (DC to ~1 MHz). 2. **Aperture (porosity) coupling** through holes, gaps, and seams — dominates at high frequencies. 3. **Pigtail / termination coupling** through the inductive loop at the connector — dominates at high frequencies when the bond is not 360°. 4. (Optional) **Common-impedance coupling** through shared ground returns. *(3 marks — 1 mark per mechanism, with dominant-frequency identification)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **(a)** Explain the three mechanisms contributing to the total shielding effectiveness of a shielded wire, giving the relevant expressions for a thin solid shield. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A copper shield of thickness 0.05 mm is used at 50 MHz. Compute its absorption loss. Given $\sigma_{\text{Cu}} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution to (a) The three mechanisms are **reflection, absorption, and re-reflection**. 1. **Reflection loss $R$** — impedance mismatch at the air-shield interface. $$ R \approx 168 + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_r\, r^2}{\mu_r\, f^2}\right) \;\;[\text{dB}] $$ 2. **Absorption loss $A$** — Ohmic dissipation inside the shield. $$ A = 8.686\,t\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta} \;\;[\text{dB}] $$ 3. **Re-reflection loss $B$** — multiple internal reflections between the two shield surfaces (significant only for very thin shields where $A < 10$ dB). $$ B = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\vert 1 - e^{-2(1+j)t/\delta}\vert\right) \;\;[\text{dB}] $$ Total: $$ \boxed{SE = R + A + B \;\;[\text{dB}]} $$ Reflection is dominated by wave-impedance mismatch and is roughly independent of shield thickness. Absorption depends linearly on thickness and on the square root of frequency. For a "thick" shield ($A > 15$ dB), $B \approx 0$ and SE is dominated by $R$ and $A$. *(7 marks — 1 mark per mechanism, 1 mark for total, 2 marks for interpretation and dependence on parameters)* --- ##### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Skin depth at 50 MHz.** $$ \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2\pi(50\times 10^6)(4\pi\times 10^{-7})(5.8\times 10^7)}} $$ $$ \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2.288\times 10^{10}}} = \sqrt{8.74\times 10^{-11}} \approx 9.35\ \mu\text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $t/\delta$.** $$ t = 0.05\ \text{mm} = 50\ \mu\text{m} $$ $$ \frac{t}{\delta} = \frac{50}{9.35} \approx 5.348 $$ **Step 3 — Absorption loss.** $$ A = 8.686 \times 5.348 \approx 46.4\ \text{dB} $$ **Answer:** $A \approx 46.4$ dB, indicating this foil alone provides excellent attenuation at 50 MHz. *(7 marks — 2 marks for skin depth, 2 marks for ratio, 2 marks for A, 1 mark for engineering comment)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, alt set]* **(a)** Derive the low-frequency and high-frequency asymptotic expressions for the transfer impedance of a solid tubular shield. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pigtail effect** and its impact on shielding effectiveness at high frequencies. Why is a 360° peripheral bond always preferred? *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution to (a) Starting from Schelkunoff's exact result for a long thin tube, $$ Z_t = \frac{Z_s}{2\pi a}\,\frac{\gamma t}{\sinh(\gamma t)} $$ with $\gamma t = (1+j)t/\delta$. **Low-frequency limit** ($\gamma t \ll 1$, i.e., $t \ll \delta$): Use $\sinh(x) \approx x$ for small $x$: $$ Z_t \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{Z_s}{2\pi a} = \frac{1}{2\pi a \sigma t} = R_{dc} $$ The transfer impedance is **purely resistive and equal to the shield's DC resistance per unit length**. *(3 marks)* **High-frequency limit** ($\gamma t \gg 1$, i.e., $t \gg \delta$): Use $\sinh(x) \approx e^x/2$ for large $x$: $$ Z_t \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{Z_s}{2\pi a}\,(1+j)\,\frac{2t}{\delta}\,e^{-(1+j)t/\delta} $$ The exponential term makes $Z_t$ approach zero. However, before that happens, the surface impedance itself grows as $\sqrt{f}$, so $$ Z_t \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi a}\sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} $$ This grows as $\sqrt{f}$ and is inductive. *(4 marks)* --- ##### Model Solution to (b) A **pigtail** is a short length of unshielded wire used to bond the cable shield to the connector shell and chassis ground. It replaces the ideal 360° peripheral (circumferential) bond. **Diagram description:** ``` braid of cable pigtail connector ========================~~~========[O] chassis ^ ^ ground | |-- L_pigtail (nH) | shield current must traverse this loop ``` **Quantitative effect:** For a pigtail of length $\ell$ and diameter $d$, the loop inductance is $$ L \approx \frac{\mu_0\,\ell}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{4\ell}{d}\right) $$ For $\ell = 25$ mm, $d = 1$ mm: $$ L \approx \frac{4\pi\times 10^{-7}\cdot 0.025}{2\pi}\ln(100) \approx 11.5\ \text{nH} $$ The induced voltage from a shield current of $dI/dt = 10^8$ A/s (typical at 100 MHz, 1 A peak) is $$ V = L\,\frac{dI}{dt} = 11.5\text{nH} \times 10^8 = 1.15\ \text{V} $$ This single pigtail can thus inject over a volt of noise — completely destroying the SE that the braid provides. **Why 360° peripheral bond is always preferred:** - It eliminates the loop area through which the magnetic field can link flux. - It provides a uniform, low-impedance path for the return current at all frequencies. - It maintains the integrity of the Faraday cage at the cable-connector interface. *(7 marks — 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for inductance calculation, 3 marks for SE impact and reasoning)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing *percentage braid coverage* with *transfer impedance*.** They are NOT the same. Coverage is purely geometric; $Z_t$ includes diffusion and frequency effects. *(–2 marks)* > 2. **Forgetting the unit of $Z_t$ — Ω/m, not Ω.** *(–1 mark)* > 3. **Using field amplitude instead of power in SE calculations.** SE in dB uses $20\log_{10}$ for field ratios and $10\log_{10}$ for power. *(–2 marks)* > 4. **Stating that a braid always performs better than foil.** Wrong — at high frequencies, the porosity inductance of a braid can dominate, making a foil + braid *combination* strictly superior. *(–2 marks)* > 5. **Neglecting the connector in the analysis.** Most SE failures in real systems occur at the *termination*, not along the cable. KTU examiners expect you to mention pigtails. > 6. **Sign errors in the Schelkunoff formula** — particularly confusing $Z_s$, $Z_t$, and the factor $1/(2\pi a)$. Memorize the clean form: $Z_t \approx R_{dc} + j\omega L_t$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Shielded wire** = inner conductor + dielectric + conductive shield + jacket. - **Three SE mechanisms:** Reflection $R$, Absorption $A$, Re-reflection $B$ → $SE = R + A + B$. - **Absorption** is the only term that depends on shield thickness; it grows linearly with $t/\delta$. - **Transfer impedance $Z_t$** is the rigorous, frequency-dependent figure of merit (Ω/m). - **Schelkunoff result:** $Z_t = R_{dc}$ at low frequencies, $Z_t \propto \sqrt{f}$ at high frequencies. - **Vance braid model:** $Z_t = Z_d + j\omega L_p$, where $L_p$ is the porosity inductance. - **Braid $Z_t$ is NOT monotonic** with frequency — it has a minimum near 1–10 MHz. - **Skin depth** $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ is the fundamental length scale. - **Pigtail terminations** can destroy 30–40 dB of SE; always use 360° peripheral bonds. - **Practical rule:** Good commercial shields aim for ≥ 40 dB SE; military/aerospace ≥ 60 dB. - **Free-space wave impedance** = 377 Ω is the reference for reflection calculations. - **Connector leakage, not cable quality, is the #1 cause of SE failure in production hardware.** - **High-speed digital trend:** Modern designs (USB 3.x, 112 Gbps PAM4, automotive Ethernet) make $Z_t$ the primary design parameter, not percentage coverage. --- > **End of Notes — Shielded Wires | PEEVT753 Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Twisted Wires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT753 - ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPATIBILITY AND SIGNAL INTEGRITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Twisted Wires # Twisted Wires — A Complete Engineering Treatise > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4)** > This topic falls under the broader syllabus unit dealing with **EMI suppression techniques in cables, interconnects, and wiring harnesses**. Students are expected to understand twisted pair geometry, its electromagnetic cancellation mechanism, and its role in differential signalling, common-mode rejection, and crosstalk mitigation. --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **twisted pair** is a cable geometry in which two insulated conductors are helically wound around each other with a uniform pitch (lay length), such that each conductor alternates its spatial position with respect to the external environment at regular intervals along the cable length. This periodic transposition forces any external electromagnetic disturbance to be induced almost equally in both conductors, allowing the differential receiver to cancel the disturbance as a common-mode signal. Mathematically, the induced differential-mode voltage across a twisted pair (over one full twist period) approaches zero in the ideal limit of: $$V_{\text{diff,induced}} = \lim_{L_{twist} \to 0} \left[ V_{n}(L) - V_{n}(L+L_{twist}) \right] \to 0$$ where $L_{twist}$ is the physical length of one complete twist (pitch). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Examination Standard)** > A **Twisted Pair Cable** is a balanced transmission line consisting of two insulated copper conductors twisted together at a defined **lay length (pitch)** to cancel external electromagnetic interference through **symmetric field coupling** and to reject **common-mode noise** at the differential receiver. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are walking across a long, narrow suspension bridge holding **two buckets of water**, one in each hand. A strong crosswind blows from your right side. If you walk straight (untwisted, side-by-side layout), the right bucket gets splashed continuously while the left one stays dry — a strong, unwanted difference exists between them. Now imagine that, as you walk, you continuously **swap the buckets between your hands** — every step, right becomes left and left becomes right. Any gust of wind that splashes the right bucket is immediately followed by an equally splashing gust on the left bucket (because the buckets have swapped sides). Over a small distance, the **average splash difference** between the two buckets is nearly zero. This is precisely how twisting works for signal wires: by constantly transposing the conductors, the magnetic field coupling is **averaged out** along the cable length. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway** > Twisting is to wires what time-averaging is to noise — by alternating exposure of each conductor to the disturbing field, the differential receiver sees only the intended signal. --- ### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics The following design parameters are central to twisted-pair engineering: - **Lay Length (Pitch, *L*ᵗʷⁱˢᵗ)** — axial distance required for one full 360° twist. Typical values: **10 mm to 100 mm** in data cables (Cat 5e: ~13.5 mm; CAN bus: ~20–33 mm; telecom: ~50–100 mm). - **Twist Rate (Turns per Metre, *N*)** — inverse of lay length. For Cat 6: **≥ 4 twists/inch** ≈ 157 turns/m in some pairs. - **Conductor Diameter (*d*)** — typically **0.4 mm to 1.0 mm (AWG 26 to AWG 18)** for signal pairs. - **Dielectric Insulation** — typically **Polyethylene (PE)** or **Foamed PE**, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.5$ to 2.3. - **Differential Characteristic Impedance (*Z*diff)** — **100 Ω ± 15 %** (Ethernet), **120 Ω** (CAN, RS-485), **90 Ω** (USB), **110 Ω** (E1 telecom). > [!TIP] > **Examiner Heuristic** > Whenever you see a question on twisted pairs in KTU exams, the magic numbers you must recall are: **100 Ω** (Ethernet), **120 Ω** (CAN/RS-485), and the **pitch-to-loop-area proportionality** — *smaller pitch ⇒ smaller effective loop area ⇒ better rejection.* --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics of Why Twisting Cancels Interference Consider an external uniform magnetic field **B**(t) impinging on an untwisted parallel-wire pair of length *L* and separation *s*. The induced noise voltage in the loop follows Faraday's Law: $$V_{\text{noise}} = -\frac{d}{dt}\Phi = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$$ For an untwisted pair, the loop area $A_{loop} = s \cdot L$ is **maximum and constant**, producing a **large induced EMF**. When the pair is **twisted with pitch *p***, the local loop area within each half-pitch is **alternately positive and negative**. Over one full twist: $$\Phi_{\text{net}} = \Phi_{+} - \Phi_{-} \approx 0$$ This is the **fundamental cancellation principle**. --- ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition **Step 1 — Identify the disturbance type** - External noise enters through two mechanisms: (a) **Magnetic field coupling** (transformer action, Faraday induction) (b) **Electric field coupling** (capacitive, common-mode voltage) **Step 2 — Untouched case** - Untouched pair forms a rigid loop. The loop area is fixed ⇒ induced noise is proportional to *B · A*. **Step 3 — Twisted case** - Each conductor physically swaps position every half pitch. - The flux through each *local* loop reverses sign. - Total linked flux over the twist integral cancels. **Step 4 — Differential reception** - Differential receiver computes $V_{out} = V_{+} - V_{-}$. - Common-mode noise (appearing equally on both) is rejected. - Twisting makes the **common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR)** of the channel high. **Step 5 — Pitch selection** - Smaller pitch = better rejection but: - More copper usage per unit length - Higher capacitance per unit length (lower $Z_0$) - Higher skin-effect losses - Mechanical stress concentration - **Optimal pitch is a trade-off** between EMC and signal integrity. --- ### 2.3 Mechanism Classification | Mechanism | Effect Without Twist | Effect With Twist | Cancellation Math | |---|---|---|---| | **Differential-mode magnetic coupling** | Large loop area → large induced EMF | Half-pitch loop areas cancel | $\sum (-1)^n \Phi_n \approx 0$ | | **Common-mode electric coupling** | Both conductors see same E-field equally | Twist does *not* cancel — needs shielding | Twisting alone insufficient | | **Crosstalk (near-end, NEXT)** | Strong inductive + capacitive coupling | Twisting reduces NEXT by 20–40 dB | Periodic transposition averages mutual L & C | | **Radiated emission** | Loop acts as small antenna | Effective dipole moment $\to 0$ | $I \cdot A_{eff} \to 0$ | | **Ground-loop induced hum** | 50/60 Hz hum injected | Equal coupling into both wires | CMMR cancels at receiver | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Box — Memorise for KTU ESE** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Induced noise EMF (loop) | $V_n = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -A_{eff}\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ | V | $A_{eff}$ = effective loop area | | 2 | Effective loop area, twisted pair | $A_{eff} = s \cdot p / 2$ (approx) | m² | $p$ = pitch, $s$ = separation | | 3 | Differential characteristic impedance | $Z_{diff} = \dfrac{276}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,eff}}} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2s}{d}\right)$ | Ω | For widely separated pairs | | 4 | Capacitance per unit length | $C = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\cosh^{-1}(s/d)}$ | F/m | For two parallel round wires | | 5 | Inductance per unit length | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{\pi}\cosh^{-1}(s/d)$ | H/m | External inductance dominant | | 6 | Twist rejection factor (magnetic) | $R_m = \dfrac{V_{\text{untwisted}}}{V_{\text{twisted}}} \approx \dfrac{L_{cable}}{p}$ | dimensionless | Valid for $L_{cable} \gg p$ | | 7 | Crosstalk reduction | $V_{XT,\text{ratio}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N_{twist/m}}}$ | dB | Empirical for NEXT | | 8 | Skin-effect resistance at high freq | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r}\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu_0}{2\sigma}}$ | Ω/m | Becomes dominant > 1 MHz | | 9 | Propagation delay | $t_d = \dfrac{L \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | s/m | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | | 10 | Common-mode rejection ratio | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | Depends on receiver matching | > **Notation key** — $s$: centre-to-centre conductor separation; $d$: conductor diameter; $p$: pitch (lay length); $\varepsilon_r$: relative permittivity of insulation; $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why Twisting is Used | Typical Impedance | |---|---|---| | **10/100/1000BASE-T Ethernet** | Multi-Gbps signalling, EMI from PoE | 100 Ω differential | | **CAN Bus (Automotive)** | Rejects ignition system noise, alternator whine | 120 Ω differential | | **RS-485 / RS-422** | Industrial EMC, motor drives | 120 Ω differential | | **USB 2.0** | High-speed serial, FCC compliance | 90 Ω differential | | **HDMI / DisplayPort (TMDS pairs)** | Multi-Gbps video, internal EMC | 100 Ω differential | | **Telephony (E1, T1)** | Long runs near power cables, lightning | 110 / 120 Ω | | **Aircraft / Aerospace (MIL-STD-1553)** | Mission-critical, extreme EMC | 70–85 Ω | | **Analog Audio (XLR)** | Rejects hum from 50/60 Hz ground loops | Low-Z balanced | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Discipline** > Every algebraic transition is expanded explicitly. No step is skipped. This is the depth expected by the KTU board examiner for a full 14-mark answer. --- ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Loop Area of a Twisted Pair We model a single half-twist of a two-conductor pair, each conductor of radius *a*, with centre-to-centre separation *s = 2a*. Consider one half-pitch segment of length *p*/2. **Step 1 — Geometry of a half-pitch** Each conductor traces a helix of radius *r* = *s*/2 around the cable axis, advancing by *p*/2 along the axis over half a turn (180°). The transverse projection of one conductor onto a plane perpendicular to the axis traces a half-circle of radius *r*. The other conductor traces the complementary half-circle (180° phase shifted). **Step 2 — Local loop area** For an infinitesimal slice of axial length *dz*, the local transverse separation is $s(z) = 2r\sin(\theta(z))$ where $\theta(z)$ is the local twist angle. The local loop area in slice *dz* is: $$dA = s(z)\, dz = 2r\sin(\theta(z))\, dz$$ Over one full twist, $\theta$ goes from 0 to $2\pi$, so the net signed area is: $$A_{\text{net}} = \int_0^{p} 2r\sin(\theta(z))\, dz$$ **Step 3 — Sign alternation per half-pitch** Because the conductors swap sides every half-pitch, the local loop vector flips sign. We can represent this as: $$A_{\text{net}} = \int_0^{p/2} 2r\sin(\theta)\, dz \;+\; \int_{p/2}^{p} 2r\sin(\theta + \pi)\, dz$$ By symmetry of sine over a full period: $$\int_0^{p/2}\sin(\theta)\,dz + \int_{p/2}^{p}\sin(\theta+\pi)\,dz = 0$$ Therefore: $$A_{\text{net}} = 0 \quad \text{(ideal cancellation)}$$ **Step 4 — Residual area due to finite pitch** In practice, residual coupling comes from the *end-segments* (the un-twisted terminations) and from the discrete twist granularity. The residual effective area is approximately: $$A_{\text{eff,residual}} \approx s \cdot p / 4 \quad \text{(empirical, half-pitch residue)}$$ Thus the **twist rejection factor** becomes: $$\boxed{\,R_m = \dfrac{A_{\text{untwisted}}}{A_{\text{eff,residual}}} = \dfrac{s \cdot L}{s \cdot p/4} = \dfrac{4L}{p}\,}$$ This is the classic textbook result: rejection grows linearly with the ratio of total cable length to pitch. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Characteristic Impedance of a Twisted Pair For two parallel round conductors of diameter *d* and centre-to-centre separation *s*, embedded in a homogeneous dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the per-unit-length capacitance and inductance are: $$C = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\cosh^{-1}(s/d)} \quad \text{(F/m)}$$ $$L = \frac{\mu_0}{\pi}\cosh^{-1}(s/d) \quad \text{(H/m)}$$ **Step 1 — Recall the transmission-line relation** $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\pi}\cosh^{-1}(s/d)}{\dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\cosh^{-1}(s/d)}}}$$ $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0 \left[\cosh^{-1}(s/d)\right]^2}{\pi^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}}$$ **Step 3 — Use the identity** $\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 = 1/c^2$ $$Z_0 = \frac{\cosh^{-1}(s/d)}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the free-space impedance** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 376.73\ \Omega$ $$Z_0 = \frac{\eta_0}{\pi \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cosh^{-1}(s/d)$$ **Step 5 — Convert the inverse hyperbolic cosine to a logarithm for $s \gg d$** For $s/d \gg 1$, $\cosh^{-1}(x) \approx \ln(2x)$, hence: $$\boxed{\,Z_0 \approx \frac{120\ \Omega}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{2s}{d}\right) \approx \frac{276\ \Omega}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2s}{d}\right)\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Quick Sanity Check** > For $s = 1.0$ mm, $d = 0.5$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$ (PE): > $Z_0 \approx (120/1.517)\ln(4) \approx 79.1 \times 1.386 \approx 109.6\ \Omega$. > This falls inside the typical 100–120 Ω envelope used in real cables. **Step 6 — Differential mode impedance** For a twisted pair driven differentially, the voltage difference is $2V$ (each conductor swings $\pm V$ around ground), and the current returns through the second conductor. The differential impedance is twice the single-ended impedance: $$\boxed{\,Z_{\text{diff}} = 2 Z_0 \approx \frac{240\ \Omega}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{2s}{d}\right)\,}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crosstalk Reduction by Twisting Two identical twisted pairs run parallel for length *L*. Without twisting, mutual inductance $M$ and mutual capacitance $C_m$ produce near-end crosstalk (NEXT): $$V_{NEXT} \approx j\omega M L \cdot I_{\text{drive}} + j\omega C_m L \cdot Z_0 \cdot I_{\text{drive}}$$ **Step 1 — Twisting introduces periodic sign change** When the *aggressor* pair is twisted, the polarity of $M$ and $C_m$ reverses every half pitch *p*/2. Over $N = L/p$ full twists: $$V_{NEXT,\text{twisted}} \approx \sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cdot V_{\text{per-twist}}$$ **Step 2 — For random pitch mismatch between victim and aggressor, residual NEXT** $$V_{NEXT,\text{ratio}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{p}{L}}$$ Expressed in dB: $$\boxed{\,\text{NEXT}_{\text{reduction}} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L}{p}\right)\ \text{dB}\,}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical example** For $L = 10$ m, $p = 20$ mm ⇒ $N = 500$ twists ⇒ **27 dB** reduction in NEXT compared to an untwisted pair. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A twisted-pair cable of length **12 m**, pitch **25 mm**, conductor diameter **0.6 mm**, centre-to-centre separation **1.2 mm**, is immersed in a uniform 50 Hz magnetic field of peak amplitude **10 µT** oscillating at line frequency. The cable is terminated in a differential receiver of input impedance **100 Ω differential**. Calculate: (a) The effective loop area and induced differential noise voltage. (b) The differential characteristic impedance. (c) The CMRR needed if the receiver must reject this noise to below **1 mV rms**. --- **Solution (a) — Effective Area and Induced Voltage** The residual effective area: $$A_{\text{eff}} = \frac{s \cdot p}{4} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})(25 \times 10^{-3})}{4} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$ Magnetic flux (peak): $$\Phi_{pk} = B_{pk} \cdot A_{\text{eff}} = (10 \times 10^{-6})(7.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 7.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Wb}$$ For $B(t) = B_{pk}\sin(\omega t)$ with $\omega = 2\pi(50) = 314.16$ rad/s, induced EMF (peak): $$V_{n,pk} = \omega \Phi_{pk} = (314.16)(7.5 \times 10^{-11}) = 2.36 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{V} = 23.6\ \text{nV}$$ RMS: $$V_{n,rms} = \frac{23.6}{\sqrt{2}} = 16.7\ \text{nV}$$ > **Interpretation:** Because of twisting, even a 10 µT power-frequency field produces only tens of nanovolts of differential noise — completely negligible for digital signalling. --- **Solution (b) — Differential Characteristic Impedance** With $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.3$ (typical PE insulation): $$Z_0 \approx \frac{120}{\sqrt{2.3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{2 \times 1.2}{0.6}\right)$$ $$Z_0 \approx \frac{120}{1.517} \ln(4) = 79.1 \times 1.386 \approx 109.6\ \Omega$$ Differential impedance: $$Z_{\text{diff}} = 2 Z_0 \approx 219\ \Omega$$ > **Comment:** Higher than the 100 Ω Ethernet spec — a designer would either reduce $s$ or choose $\varepsilon_r$ closer to 1.5 to bring it down. --- **Solution (c) — Required CMRR** The induced common-mode voltage is still present on both conductors (twisting does NOT cancel CM). Suppose the source imbalance gives a CM-to-DM conversion factor of, say, $-40$ dB (typical). The CM noise on the line is: $$V_{cm} = \frac{V_{\text{loop}}}{2} \approx \frac{B_{pk} \cdot A_{\text{loop}}}{2}$$ For the **un-twisted** geometry ($A_{\text{loop}} = s \cdot L = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 12 = 0.0144\ \text{m}^2$): $$V_{cm,pk} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(0.0144)}{2} = 7.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{Wb-equivalent}$$ Converting through Faraday: $$V_{cm,rms} \approx \omega \cdot 7.2 \times 10^{-8} / \sqrt{2} = \frac{314.16 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.414} \approx 1.6\ \mu\text{V rms}$$ Required CMRR for 1 mV output tolerance (with a 1 V signal): $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise,\text{target}}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = 60\ \text{dB}$$ This is readily achieved by standard op-amp-based line receivers (e.g., INA105, AD8138). --- ## 4.
